topics.title: Power Quality Phenomenon - Sources and Effects of Power Quality Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Quality Phenomenon - Sources and Effects of Power Quality Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Quality (PQ)** is formally defined as the set of electrical boundaries, conditions, and measurable parameters that allow a piece of equipment to function in its intended manner without significant loss of performance or expected life expectancy.

In more rigorous engineering terms (per **IEEE Standard 1159** and **IEC 61000 series**), Power Quality refers to the characterization of the voltage and current waveforms at a given point in the electrical system, evaluated against a set of technical criteria such as frequency stability, magnitude, waveform purity, and symmetry of the three-phase supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly introduces Power Quality not as a single problem but as a *spectrum of phenomena*, requiring students to identify each phenomenon, trace it back to its physical source, and predict its downstream consequence.

### 1.2 The Governing Principle — Why Power Quality Matters

The modern power grid has undergone a profound transformation over the last three decades. Two forces act upon it simultaneously:

1. **On the Supply Side** — The penetration of renewable energy, switching power electronics, HVDC links, and FACTS devices injects non-sinusoidal currents into the grid.
2. **On the Demand Side** — Loads have become increasingly *nonlinear* and *sensitive*. Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), computers, LED drivers, SMPS units, and PLC controllers all demand a clean sinusoidal voltage.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pressure" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of electricity like water flowing through a tap.
> - A **steady, clean stream of water at constant pressure** is what your washing machine needs.
> - If the pressure *surges* (a swell), the hose may burst.
> - If the pressure *drops suddenly* (a sag), the drum motor may stall and overheat.
> - If *air bubbles* enter the stream (harmonics/transients), the nozzle chatters and performance degrades.
> - If the water stops for a second (interruption), the program resets.

Power Quality is precisely the engineering discipline that ensures the "electrical pressure" (voltage) and "flow smoothness" (current waveform) remain within the design envelope that customer equipment expects.

### 1.4 Standard Voltage Quality Benchmarks (Indian Context)

Under the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations, 2018** and the **Bureau of Indian Standards IS 14630**, the following nominal benchmarks apply for LV/MV consumers:

| Parameter | Nominal Value | Acceptable Band |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Nominal Frequency | **50 Hz** | **49.5 Hz – 50.5 Hz** |
| Nominal Voltage (LT) | **230 V / 415 V** | ±10% of nominal |
| Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | — | **5% (Voltage), 8% (Current)** |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Examiner Tip:** Whenever a numerical PQ problem appears in KTU exams, always cite the relevant IEEE/IEC standard first — this demonstrates "standard awareness" which carries bonus marks in the valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Taxonomy of Power Quality Phenomena

Power Quality problems are systematically classified into **seven major categories** as per IEEE 1159. Mastering this classification is mandatory for every KTU exam.

**1. Transients (Sub-millisecond to several milliseconds)**
- *Impulsive* — sudden high-magnitude spikes (e.g., lightning, capacitor switching).
- *Oscillatory* — decaying sinusoidal oscillations at high frequencies.

**2. Short-Duration Voltage Variations (½ cycle to 1 minute)**
- *Sags (Dips)* — RMS voltage drops to 0.1–0.9 pu for ½ cycle to 1 min.
- *Swells* — RMS voltage rises to 1.1–1.8 pu for the same duration.

**3. Long-Duration Voltage Variations (>1 minute)**
- *Overvoltages, Undervoltages, Sustained Interruptions.*

**4. Voltage Imbalance (Unbalance)**
- Unequal magnitudes or phase angles across the three phases.

**5. Waveform Distortion**
- *Harmonics, Interharmonics, DC offset, Notching, Noise.*

**6. Voltage Fluctuations**
- Cyclic or random small-magnitude changes (e.g., arc furnaces) causing **flicker**.

**7. Power Frequency Variations**
- Deviation of the fundamental frequency from nominal 50/60 Hz.

### 2.2 Sources of Power Quality Problems

| Category | Primary Sources |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Natural / External** | Lightning strikes, switching of large utility capacitor banks, network faults, geomagnetic disturbances. |
| **Customer / Load-Side** | Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), UPS systems, arc furnaces, welding machines, saturated transformers, switched-mode power supplies, large motor starting inrush. |
| **Utility / Network-Side** | Loose neutral connections, improper grounding, single-phase loading imbalances, transformer energization, reclosing operations. |

### 2.3 Effects of Power Quality Problems (Why It Matters to Industry)

| PQ Problem | Major Consequence |
|------------|-------------------|
| **Voltage Sag** | PLC resets, contactor dropouts, VFD undervoltage trips, process line stoppages — **single largest cause of industrial PQ losses**. |
| **Voltage Swell** | Insulation stress, electronic component failure, capacitor overvoltage damage. |
| **Harmonics** | Neutral conductor overheating, transformer eddy-current losses, capacitor bank failure, resonance tripping, nuisance fuse blowing. |
| **Transients** | Insulation breakdown, data corruption in digital devices, semiconductor device failure. |
| **Voltage Unbalance** | Three-phase induction motor overheating (negative-sequence currents), bearing damage, derated equipment life. |
| **Flicker** | Human visual discomfort, reduced productivity, complaints from lighting consumers. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Data Point:** A 1-second sag in a semiconductor fabrication plant can cause losses exceeding **$1 million** due to scrapped wafer batches. This is why KTU frequently frames PQ as an *economic* as well as a *technical* problem.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the analytical backbone for Part A and Part B derivations in KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^{2}}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Voltage Total Harmonic Distortion — quantify waveform distortion. |
| 2 | $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^{2}}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Current THD — assess nonlinear load severity. |
| 3 | $\text{TDD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^{2}}}{I_L} \times 100\%$ | Total Demand Distortion — referenced to rated load current $I_L$. |
| 4 | $V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{V_1^{2} + V_2^{2} + V_3^{2} + \cdots + V_n^{2}}$ | RMS including harmonics. |
| 5 | $V_{\text{RMS}} = V_1 \sqrt{1 + \text{THD}^{2}}$ | Compact RMS form using THD. |
| 6 | $\%V_{\text{sag}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pre-fault}} - V_{\text{during-fault}}}{V_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100$ | Voltage sag magnitude. |
| 7 | $V_{\text{UF}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{(V_{ab} - V_{avg})^{2} + (V_{bc} - V_{avg})^{2} + (V_{ca} - V_{avg})^{2}}}{V_{avg}}$ | Voltage Unbalance Factor (UF). |
| 8 | $\%V_{\text{UF}} = \dfrac{\max(V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}) - \min(V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca})}{\text{avg}} \times 100$ | Line-voltage unbalance in percentage. |
| 9 | $S = \sqrt{P^{2} + Q^{2} + D^{2}}$ | Apparent power containing distortion power $D$. |
| 10 | $P_{\text{displacement}} = V_1 I_1 \cos(\theta_1)$ | Fundamental displacement power. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power Quality engineering is critical in:
- **Smart Grid operation** — managing inverter-interfaced renewable generation.
- **Industrial process industries** — automotive, pharma, semiconductor fabs.
- **Data centres and IT parks** — Tier-IV power availability depends directly on PQ.
- **Electric Vehicle charging infrastructure** — where high-power rectifiers inject harmonics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of RMS Voltage in Presence of Harmonics

**Starting Premise:** A non-sinusoidal periodic voltage can be expressed via Fourier Series.

**Step 1 — Express the voltage as a Fourier sum.**

$$
v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h \sin(h\omega t + \phi_h)
$$

Here, $V_0$ is the DC component, $V_h$ is the amplitude of the $h^{th}$ harmonic, and $\phi_h$ is its phase angle.

**Step 2 — State the definition of RMS for any periodic signal.**

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t) \, dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Square the Fourier sum and apply orthogonality.**

When $v(t)$ is squared, the cross-product terms of the form $V_m V_n \sin(m\omega t)\sin(n\omega t)$ integrate to **zero over one complete period** when $m \neq n$ (orthogonality of sinusoids).

Only the self-product terms survive, yielding:

$$
V_{\text{RMS}}^{2} = V_0^{2} + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{V_h^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of individual harmonic RMS values.**

Using $V_{h,\text{RMS}} = \dfrac{V_h}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
V_{\text{RMS}}^{2} = V_0^{2} + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_{h,\text{RMS}}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{V_0^{2} + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_{h,\text{RMS}}^{2}}
$$

For most practical PQ problems, the DC offset is negligible, giving:

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_{h,\text{RMS}}^{2}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

**Step 1 — Define the harmonic content of the RMS voltage.**

$$
V_{\text{harm}} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,\text{RMS}}^{2}}
$$

This is the contribution of all harmonics **above the fundamental**.

**Step 2 — Define the fundamental RMS.**

$$
V_{1,\text{RMS}} = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\text{THD} = \dfrac{V_{\text{harm}}}{V_{1,\text{RMS}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage.**

$$
\text{THD}_{\%} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,\text{RMS}}^{2}}}{V_{1,\text{RMS}}} \times 100
$$

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A low-voltage feeder delivers a non-sinusoidal voltage containing a fundamental of 230 V RMS, a 5th harmonic of 11.5 V RMS, and a 7th harmonic of 6.9 V RMS. Compute the (a) total RMS voltage, and (b) THD in percentage.

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = 230$ V (RMS)
- $V_5 = 11.5$ V (RMS)
- $V_7 = 6.9$ V (RMS)

**Solution (a) — Total RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{V_1^{2} + V_5^{2} + V_7^{2}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{(230)^{2} + (11.5)^{2} + (6.9)^{2}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{52900 + 132.25 + 47.61}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{53079.86}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} \approx 230.39 \, \text{V}
$$

**Solution (b) — THD Percentage:**

$$
\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_5^{2} + V_7^{2}}}{V_1} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{132.25 + 47.61}}{230} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{179.86}}{230} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{THD} = \dfrac{13.412}{230} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{THD} \approx 5.83\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently *add* harmonic magnitudes arithmetically ($11.5 + 6.9 = 18.4$) — this is incorrect. Harmonics combine as **root-sum-square (RSS)** because of their differing phase angles and orthogonality. This single error has been responsible for **3-mark deductions** across multiple KTU valuation cycles.

### 3.4 Voltage Unbalance Factor — Line Voltage Form

For a three-phase system with line voltages $V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}$:

**Step 1 — Compute the mean line voltage.**

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca}}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations.**

$$
\Delta V_{ab} = V_{ab} - V_{\text{avg}}, \quad \Delta V_{bc} = V_{bc} - V_{\text{avg}}, \quad \Delta V_{ca} = V_{ca} - V_{\text{avg}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute UF.**

$$
V_{\text{UF}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{(\Delta V_{ab})^{2} + (\Delta V_{bc})^{2} + (\Delta V_{ca})^{2}}}{V_{\text{avg}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in percentage.**

$$
\%V_{\text{UF}} = V_{\text{UF}} \times 100
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Power Quality Phenomena

The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **IEEE 1159 taxonomy** that forms the conceptual spine of Module 1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power Quality Phenomena]:::root --> B[Transients]
    A --> C[Short Duration Variations]
    A --> D[Long Duration Variations]
    A --> E[Waveform Distortion]
    A --> F[Voltage Imbalance]
    A --> G[Voltage Fluctuation and Flicker]
    A --> H[Power Frequency Variation]

    B --> B1[Impulsive]
    B --> B2[Oscillatory]

    C --> C1[Sag 0.1 to 0.9 pu]
    C --> C2[Swell 1.1 to 1.8 pu]
    C --> C3[Interruption less than 0.1 pu]

    D --> D1[Sustained Interruption]
    D --> D2[Undervoltage]
    D --> D3[Overvoltage]

    E --> E1[Harmonics]
    E --> E2[Interharmonics]
    E --> E3[DC Offset]
    E --> E4[Notching]
    E --> E5[Noise]

    F --> F1[Phase Angle Imbalance]
    F --> F2[Magnitude Imbalance]

    G --> G1[Flicker from Arc Furnaces]
    G --> G2[Flicker from Large Motors]

    H --> H1[Under Frequency]
    H --> H2[Over Frequency]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Cause-Effect Architecture Flow

This block diagram maps how each source propagates through the network and manifests as an industrial consequence.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Lightning Strike]:::src --> T1[Impulsive Transient]
    S2[Capacitor Switching]:::src --> T2[Oscillatory Transient]
    S3[Network Fault]:::src --> V1[Voltage Sag]
    S4[Sudden Load Drop]:::src --> V2[Voltage Swell]
    S5[VFD and SMPS Load]:::src --> H1[Harmonic Currents]
    S6[Single Phase Loading]:::src --> U1[Voltage Unbalance]
    S7[Arc Furnace]:::src --> F1[Flicker]

    T1 --> EFF1[Insulation Breakdown and Data Loss]
    T2 --> EFF2[Capacitor and Electronic Equipment Damage]
    V1 --> EFF3[PLC Trip and Process Stoppage]
    V2 --> EFF4[Insulation Stress and Equipment Damage]
    H1 --> EFF5[Transformer Overheating and Neutral Overload]
    U1 --> EFF6[Motor Derating and Bearing Failure]
    F1 --> EFF7[Visual Discomfort and Productivity Loss]

    classDef src fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — The PQ Problem Resolution Pipeline

This block diagram summarizes the **engineering decision flow** every PQ consultant follows — directly relevant to KTU design-oriented questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP1[PQ Issue Reported by Consumer]:::stage --> STEP2[Site Data Acquisition using PQ Analyzer]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Waveform Capture for at least 7 days]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Event Classification against IEEE 1159]
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Source Identification]
    STEP5 --> STEP6[Mitigation Strategy Selection]
    STEP6 --> STEP7[Equipment Sizing and Installation]
    STEP7 --> STEP8[Post Installation Verification]

    STEP1:::stage
    STEP2:::stage
    STEP3:::stage
    STEP4:::stage
    STEP5:::stage
    STEP6:::stage
    STEP7:::stage
    STEP8:::stage

    classDef stage fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define **Power Quality**. List any **four categories** of power quality problems as classified by IEEE Standard 1159.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Power Quality refers to the degree to which the voltage and current waveforms at a point in the electrical system conform to the ideal sinusoidal shape, nominal magnitude, and nominal frequency, enabling equipment to operate as designed without performance degradation.

**Four Categories (IEEE 1159):**
1. **Transients** — Impulsive and oscillatory.
2. **Short-duration variations** — Sags, swells, interruptions.
3. **Long-duration variations** — Overvoltages, undervoltages, sustained interruptions.
4. **Waveform distortion** — Harmonics, interharmonics, notching, DC offset, noise.

*(Other valid categories: Voltage imbalance, Voltage fluctuation/flicker, Power-frequency variation.)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Differentiate between a **voltage sag** and a **voltage swell**. Mention the typical RMS voltage range (in pu) for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Voltage Sag | Voltage Swell |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Definition** | A momentary decrease in RMS voltage at the fundamental frequency. | A momentary increase in RMS voltage at the fundamental frequency. |
| **RMS Magnitude** | **0.1 pu to 0.9 pu** of nominal. | **1.1 pu to 1.8 pu** of nominal. |
| **Duration** | 0.5 cycle to 1 minute. | 0.5 cycle to 1 minute. |
| **Primary Cause** | Motor starting, network faults, large load energization. | Sudden load drop, capacitor switching, single-line-to-ground fault on the utility side. |
| **Major Consequence** | PLC/contactors drop out; VFD undervoltage trip; process stoppages. | Insulation stress; electronic component failure; capacitor overvoltage damage. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE out of TWO** questions from this section.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat classification diagram, explain the various categories of **power quality phenomena** as per IEEE Standard 1159. Mention a typical source and effect for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 415 V, 50 Hz, three-phase industrial busbar has line voltages: $V_{ab} = 420$ V, $V_{bc} = 408$ V, $V_{ca} = 422$ V. Calculate the **voltage unbalance factor in percentage**. Comment on whether the busbar is compliant with the IEEE recommended limit of 2%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The IEEE 1159 standard classifies PQ phenomena into the following categories — students are expected to reproduce a hierarchical table or block diagram:

| Category | Source | Effect |
|----------|--------|--------|
| **Transients (Impulsive)** | Lightning | Insulation puncture, equipment damage |
| **Transients (Oscillatory)** | Capacitor switching | Electronic equipment malfunction |
| **Sag** | Fault, motor start | Contactor dropout, process trip |
| **Swell** | Load drop, fault clearance | Insulation stress |
| **Interruption** | Breaker trip, fuse | Production loss |
| **Harmonics** | VFD, SMPS, LED drivers | Transformer overheating, resonance |
| **Flicker** | Arc furnace, welding | Visual discomfort |
| **Imbalance** | Unequal single-phase loading | Motor derating |

*(Full credit requires both the classification and at least one source-effect pair for each category.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $V_{ab} = 420$ V, $V_{bc} = 408$ V, $V_{ca} = 422$ V

**Step 1 — Compute the average line voltage.**

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca}}{3} = \dfrac{420 + 408 + 422}{3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1250}{3} \approx 416.67 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deviations from average.**

$$
\Delta V_{ab} = 420 - 416.67 = +3.33 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{bc} = 408 - 416.67 = -8.67 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{ca} = 422 - 416.67 = +5.33 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator of UF.**

$$
\sqrt{(3.33)^{2} + (-8.67)^{2} + (5.33)^{2}} = \sqrt{11.09 + 75.17 + 28.41}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{114.67} \approx 10.71 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the unbalance factor.**

$$
\%V_{\text{UF}} = \dfrac{10.71}{416.67} \times 100 \approx 2.57\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance verdict.**

Since **2.57% > 2%** (the IEEE recommended limit), the busbar is **NOT compliant** with the IEEE standard. The industrial consumer should redistribute single-phase loads across the three phases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**. A low-voltage feeder delivers a voltage whose Fourier components (RMS) are: fundamental 230 V, 3rd harmonic 9.2 V, 5th harmonic 13.8 V, 7th harmonic 4.6 V. Compute the THD and the total RMS voltage. State whether the feeder complies with the IEEE 519 limit of 5%. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **major effects of power quality problems** on (i) transformers, (ii) rotating machines, (iii) capacitor banks, and (iv) digital/electronic equipment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition:** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (above the fundamental) to the RMS value of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage.

**Given Harmonics (RMS):**
- $V_1 = 230$ V
- $V_3 = 9.2$ V
- $V_5 = 13.8$ V
- $V_7 = 4.6$ V

**Step 1 — Compute the RMS of all harmonics combined.**

$$
V_h = \sqrt{V_3^{2} + V_5^{2} + V_7^{2}} = \sqrt{(9.2)^{2} + (13.8)^{2} + (4.6)^{2}}
$$

$$
V_h = \sqrt{84.64 + 190.44 + 21.16} = \sqrt{296.24} \approx 17.21 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the THD.**

$$
\text{THD}_{\%} = \dfrac{V_h}{V_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{17.21}{230} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{THD}_{\%} \approx 7.48\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total RMS voltage.**

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{V_1^{2} + V_3^{2} + V_5^{2} + V_7^{2}} = \sqrt{(230)^{2} + (9.2)^{2} + (13.8)^{2} + (4.6)^{2}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{52900 + 84.64 + 190.44 + 21.16} = \sqrt{53196.24}
$$

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} \approx 230.64 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance verdict.**

Since **7.48% > 5%**, the feeder is **NOT compliant** with the IEEE 519 voltage distortion limit. Harmonic mitigation measures (passive filters, active filters, or multi-pulse rectifiers) must be deployed.

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Equipment | Major PQ Effects |
|-----------|------------------|
| **(i) Transformers** | Increased $I^{2}R$ losses due to harmonic currents; eddy-current and stray-flux losses proportional to $f^{2}$; localized hotspots; audible humming; possible resonance with system capacitance. |
| **(ii) Rotating Machines** | Pulsating torques from negative-sequence harmonics; rotor overheating due to harmonic-induced eddy currents; bearing currents; reduced efficiency and derated life. |
| **(iii) Capacitor Banks** | Capacitor impedance decreases at higher harmonics, causing excessive current draw; parallel resonance between capacitor and system inductance leading to dangerous voltage magnification; dielectric stress and capacitor failure. |
| **(iv) Digital / Electronic Equipment** | Malfunction, data corruption, flickering displays, timing errors in PLCs, spurious trips, communication errors in networked devices, premature component failure due to voltage transients and notching. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Skipping the unit** when reporting THD or RMS values — always append % or V respectively.
> 2. **Using peak instead of RMS** Fourier coefficients in THD numerators — this is a 1-mark deduction that recurs every cycle.
> 3. **Forgetting the orthogonality principle** when summing harmonics — must use root-sum-square (RSS), never linear addition.
> 4. **Not stating the IEEE/IEC standard** at the top of the answer — examiner's key requires "standard awareness" for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing TDD with THD** — TDD uses rated load current as denominator; THD uses fundamental current.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Power Quality Phenomenon**

- ✔ Power Quality is governed by **IEEE 1159** (PQ phenomena) and **IEEE 519** (harmonic distortion limits).
- ✔ Seven major PQ categories: **Transients, Short-duration variations, Long-duration variations, Waveform distortion, Imbalance, Flicker, Frequency variation**.
- ✔ **Voltage Sag (0.1–0.9 pu)** is the single most economically damaging PQ event in industry.
- ✔ **THD is always referenced to the fundamental**, not to the rated load (that is TDD).
- ✔ Harmonics add in **root-sum-square (RSS)** due to orthogonality — never linear addition.
- ✔ Voltage unbalance causes **negative-sequence currents** that overheat induction motors.
- ✔ Nonlinear loads (VFDs, SMPS, LEDs, arc furnaces) are the **dominant internal sources** of harmonics.
- ✔ Lightning and capacitor switching are the **dominant external sources** of transients.
- ✔ Indian regulatory benchmarks: **CEA 2018** (voltage & frequency) and **IS 14630** (waveform).
- ✔ Always cite the standard, always show the units, and always use RSS — these three habits separate a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Need for Concern of Power Quality, Types of Power Quality Disturbances - Transients - Classification and Origin

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Power Quality (PQ)

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Quality** is formally defined as *the set of electrical boundary conditions that allow electrical and electronic equipment to function in its intended manner without significant loss of performance or life expectancy.* It is essentially a measure of how closely the actual voltage, current, and frequency waveform delivered by the utility matches the ideal sinusoidal reference at nominal magnitude and frequency.

According to **IEEE Standard 1159-2019 (IEEE Recommended Practice for Monitoring Electric Power Quality)**, power quality is the concept of powering and grounding sensitive electronic equipment in a manner that is suitable to the operation of that equipment.

The term "Quality" here is relative — it depends on the equipment being supplied. A waveform that is "clean" enough for an induction motor might be unacceptable for a sensitive programmable logic controller (PLC) or a medical MRI.

### Key Performance Metrics Used to Quantify PQ

- **Nominal Voltage Magnitude** (typically **230 V** single-phase / **415 V** three-phase in India, 50 Hz)
- **Nominal Frequency** (**50 Hz** in India, 60 Hz in USA)
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — should be below **5%** for voltage at the PCC
- **Power Factor (PF)** — ideally unity
- **Voltage Unbalance Factor** — should be below **2%**
- **Flicker Severity (Pst, Plt)** indices (IEC 61000-4-15)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you buy **premium bottled drinking water**. You expect:

1. The water to flow at a **steady, constant pressure** (analogous to a **steady, constant RMS voltage**).
2. The water to be **pure** — no sand, mud, or smell (analogous to a **pure 50 Hz sine wave** with no harmonics or noise).
3. The tap to deliver water **24/7** without unexpected shut-offs (analogous to **no interruptions**).
4. No **sudden spurts** that hit you in the eye (analogous to **transients / surges**).

If any of these conditions are violated, the "quality" of supply is poor. Similarly, **Power Quality** describes how closely the actual electrical supply matches the ideal "clean water" of a perfect 50 Hz sine wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Mapping:**
> | Domestic Water Analogy | Power Quality Equivalent |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | Steady water pressure | Constant RMS Voltage (no sags/swells) |
> | Pure, clean water | Pure sine wave (low harmonic distortion) |
> | Uninterrupted flow | No interruptions (continuous supply) |
> | Sudden pressure spikes | Transients (impulsive / oscillatory) |
> | Pressure oscillation | Voltage fluctuations / Flicker |
> | Unequal pressure in 3 pipes | Voltage / Current unbalance |

---

## 1.3 Why the Concern? The Need for Studying Power Quality

The concern for power quality has dramatically increased in the last three decades due to four converging factors:

### 1.3.1 Proliferation of Sensitive Electronic Loads

Modern industrial, commercial, and domestic consumers use micro-processor based equipment:
- Computers, PLCs, VFDs, CNC machines
- Communication equipment, medical electronics
- LED drivers, SMPS-based power supplies

These loads are **highly intolerant** to even small deviations in waveform shape.

### 1.3.2 Proliferation of Power Electronic Converters (Non-Linear Loads)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power electronics is the prime driver of PQ problems.** Every VFD, rectifier, UPS, and SMPS draws **non-sinusoidal current** even when supplied with a perfect sine wave. This causes **harmonic pollution** in the network.

### 1.3.3 Integration of Renewable Energy Sources

Wind turbines (with power electronic interfacing) and solar PV (with inverters) inject harmonics and cause voltage fluctuations. They also interact poorly with weak grids.

### 1.3.4 Economic Consequences

Poor power quality causes:
- **Equipment malfunction and premature failure** (insulation breakdown)
- **Data loss / corruption** in IT systems
- **Mis-operation of protection relays** and contactors
- **Increased I²R losses** and overheated transformers/neutral conductors
- **Resonance** leading to capacitor bank explosions
- **Production downtime** in industries — directly translating to revenue loss

> [!NOTE]
> Industry estimates (EPRI, USA) suggest that PQ-related industrial losses run into **tens of billions of dollars per year globally**. This makes PQ not just a technical issue, but a major **economic and operational** concern.

---

## 1.4 What Are Power Quality Disturbances?

A **Power Quality Disturbance (PQD)** is any deviation of the voltage or current waveform from the ideal sinusoidal reference at nominal magnitude and frequency. Such disturbances may be:

- **Steady-state** (continuous — harmonics, unbalance, flicker)
- **Non-steady-state** (transient — impulsive, oscillatory)

The complete IEEE 1159-2019 categorization of electromagnetic phenomena is the **canonical reference** for PQ disturbances.

---

## 1.5 Introduction to Transients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transient:** A brief, sub-cycle (or a few-cycle) overvoltage or overcurrent event characterized by very high frequency content (compared to the 50 Hz fundamental), with a sudden onset and short duration. Transients are typically **unwanted, undesired**, and may be **impulsive** (uni-directional) or **oscillatory** (bi-directional and decaying).

### Key Characteristics of Transients

- **Duration:** Microseconds to a few milliseconds
- **Magnitude:** Can be **2 to 6 times** the nominal peak (sometimes higher during lightning)
- **Frequency Content:** Extends from a few kHz to several MHz
- **Energy:** Concentrated in a very short time → very high **dV/dt** and **dI/dt**

### Why Transients Matter

Even though transients are very short, their **high dV/dt** and **dI/dt** can:
- **Punch through insulation** of cables, motors, transformers
- **Latch-up or destroy semiconductor devices** (thyristors, IGBTs)
- **Cause nuisance tripping** of breakers
- **Couple capacitively or inductively** into nearby signal circuits, causing **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)**
- **Couple via common-mode or normal-mode paths** into sensitive electronics

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types / Categories of Power Quality Disturbances (IEEE 1159 Framework)

The IEEE 1159-2019 standard classifies PQ phenomena into **seven major categories**, each with subtypes:

### 2.1.1 Transients
- **Impulsive Transients** (uni-directional, sub-cycle)
- **Oscillatory Transients** (bi-directional, decaying)

### 2.1.2 Short-Duration RMS Variations
- **Sags (Dips)** — RMS voltage drops to 10–90% for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute
- **Swells** — RMS voltage rises to 110–180% for 0.5 cycles to 1 minute
- **Interruptions** — voltage drops below 10% for less than 1 minute

### 2.1.3 Long-Duration RMS Variations
- **Sustained Interruptions** — > 1 minute (utility-side, planned/unplanned)
- **Undervoltages** — below 90% nominal, > 1 minute
- **Overvoltages** — above 110% nominal, > 1 minute

### 2.1.4 Voltage Unbalance
- Unequal magnitudes or phase angles in a 3-phase system (mostly due to unbalanced loads)

### 2.1.5 Waveform Distortion
- **DC Offset**
- **Harmonics** (integer multiples of fundamental)
- **Interharmonics** (non-integer multiples)
- **Notching** (commutation dips in rectifiers)
- **Noise** (broadband, < 200 kHz)

### 2.1.6 Voltage Fluctuations
- Systematic or random variations in the **envelope** of voltage (causes visible lamp flicker)

### 2.1.7 Power Frequency Variations
- Deviations in fundamental frequency from nominal (typically caused by generator load mismatches)

---

## 2.2 Classification of Transients (Detailed — IEEE 1159 Standard)

Transients are classified along **two axes**:
1. **By Speed of Occurrence / Waveform Shape** → Impulsive vs Oscillatory
2. **By Spectral Content** → Low, Medium, High frequency

### 2.2.1 Impulsive Transients

- **Uni-directional** in polarity (push either positive or negative only)
- Normally of very high **dV/dt** (steep rise time)
- Common cause: **Lightning strokes**
- Characterized by **rise time, decay time, and peak amplitude**

IEEE 1159 sub-classification based on duration:

| Sub-type | Rise Time (typical) | Decay Time (typical) | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nanosecond Impulsive | 5 ns | < 50 ns | Low (often < 2 pu) |
| Microsecond Impulsive | 1 µs | < 50 µs | Moderate (2–4 pu) |
| Millisecond Impulsive | 0.1 ms | < 1 ms | High (up to 8 pu) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lightning-induced impulses** are the most severe impulsive transients. Direct strokes can produce **crest voltages exceeding 20 pu** (i.e., 20× nominal peak).

### 2.2.2 Oscillatory Transients

- **Bi-directional** — contain both positive and negative excursions
- Decay gradually toward zero
- Result from **switching events** (capacitor energization, line switching) or **system faults**

IEEE 1159 sub-classification based on dominant spectral frequency:

| Sub-type | Frequency Range | Spectrum Tag | Typical Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low Frequency Oscillatory | < 5 kHz | LF | Capacitor switching, transformer energization |
| Medium Frequency Oscillatory | 5 kHz – 500 kHz | MF | Back-to-back capacitor switching, cable switching |
| High Frequency Oscillatory | 0.5 MHz – 5 MHz | HF | Lightning, switching of low-impedance networks, ESD |

---

## 2.3 Origin of Transients — Where Do They Come From?

Transients originate from **two broad categories**:

### 2.3.1 External Sources (Utility / Environmental)

1. **Lightning Strokes (Direct or Induced)**
   - The single largest cause of impulsive transients
   - Direct strikes inject huge currents (10–200 kA peak)
   - Even **induced strikes** (within a few hundred meters of the line) can couple significant voltage into the conductors
   - Spectral content spans **100 kHz to several MHz**

2. **Utility Switching Operations**
   - Line re-closer operations
   - Capacitor bank switching by the utility
   - Auto-recloser action after fault clearing

### 2.3.2 Internal Sources (Customer / In-plant)

1. **Capacitor Bank Switching**
   - The most common cause of **oscillatory transients** in industrial systems
   - When a capacitor is energized, the system inductance and capacitance exchange energy, producing a damped oscillation at the **natural frequency** of the system
   - Peak voltage can reach **1.8 to 2.0 pu** initially, settling in 0.5–3 cycles

2. **Load Switching (Inductive loads)**
   - Switching off large transformers, motors, or reactors induces high **dI/dt → overvoltages** (due to Lenz's law, V = L·dI/dt)
   - Also produces chop transients on SCR-commutated circuits

3. **Power Electronic Switching**
   - VFDs, choppers, and SMPS circuits switch at very high **dV/dt**
   - Repetitive switching produces high-frequency oscillatory noise on the supply

4. **Fault Initiation and Clearing**
   - When a fault occurs and is cleared, the sudden change in network topology excites natural oscillations

5. **Electrostatic Discharge (ESD)**
   - Causes **nanosecond impulsive transients**
   - Typically low magnitude but very fast

6. **Transformer Energization (Inrush)**
   - Produces a transient with rich harmonic content (2nd, 3rd, 4th harmonics)

7. **Ferroresonance**
   - A non-linear resonance between system capacitance and the non-linear inductance of a saturated transformer
   - Produces sustained high overvoltages with high harmonic content

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Transients & Power Quality Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **high-yield formulas** you must memorize for KTU exams on transients.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | **Surge Impedance** of an LC network | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | **Natural Frequency** of LC oscillation | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | $V_{peak} = V_{nom} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_{source}} \right)$ | **Peak Overvoltage** at load end during capacitor energization | Volts |
| 4 | $V_{L,max} \approx 2 V_{source}$ | Maximum theoretical capacitor switching transient (worst case) | Volts |
| 5 | $i(t) = i_0 \, e^{-\alpha t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | **Damped oscillatory transient** current waveform | Amps |
| 6 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | **Damping Coefficient** of RLC oscillation | Np/s |
| 7 | $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | **Damped natural frequency** | rad/s |
| 8 | $V_{transient} = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | **Inductive switching** overvoltage | Volts |
| 9 | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (steady-state complement to transients) | % |
| 10 | $V_{dB\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{V_{noise}}{1 \mu V} \right)$ | Noise voltage in dBµV (for transient/EMI measurement) | dBµV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical constants and boundaries:**
> - **Impulsive transient**: duration < 1 cycle; rise time in ns/µs/ms
> - **Oscillatory transient**: duration < 1 cycle (LF) to a few cycles (HF)
> - **Nominal Indian LV levels**: 230 V (1-phase) / 415 V (3-phase), 50 Hz
> - **Acceptable PQ limits (India, CEA / IEEE 519)**:
>   - Individual voltage harmonic: $\vert V_h \vert \le 3\%$ of $V_1$
>   - Total Harmonic Distortion (THD): $\le 5\%$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Why do power engineers care about these formulas?

- **Protection Coordination:** Designing surge arresters requires knowing the peak transient voltage ($V_{peak}$) so that the arrester's rating is correctly chosen.
- **Insulation Coordination:** Equipment insulation must withstand the Basic Insulation Level (BIL), which is decided by the worst-case transient voltage.
- **Filtering:** Designing snubbers, RC damper circuits, and EMI filters requires $\omega_0$, $\alpha$, and $Z_0$.
- **Forensic Analysis:** Power quality analyzers (e.g., Fluke 435, Hioki PW3198) record transients using these formulas for post-event diagnosis.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **rigorous, line-by-line derivations** of the most important transient-related equations used in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Natural Frequency of an LC Oscillation (Capacitor Switching Transient)

**Physical Setup:** Consider a simple circuit comprising an AC source with inductance $L_s$ (source inductance) connected to a capacitor bank of capacitance $C$ through a switch. At $t = 0$, the switch closes. The system exchanges energy between $L_s$ and $C$, producing an oscillatory transient at the natural frequency.

**Step 1 — Initial Conditions (Just Before Switching):**

Capacitor is uncharged:

$$
V_C(0^-) = 0
$$

Inductor current is zero (open switch):

$$
i_L(0^-) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — KVL Around the Loop After Switch Closes:**

Source voltage $V_s(t)$ drives the series RLC loop. Assuming $V_s$ is quasi-static (compared to the fast oscillation):

$$
V_s(t) = L_s \dfrac{di(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i(t)\, dt
$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$:

$$
\dfrac{dV_s}{dt} = L_s \dfrac{d^2 i(t)}{dt^2} + \dfrac{i(t)}{C}
$$

Since the source voltage is constant during the transient (approximation):

$$
0 = L_s \dfrac{d^2 i(t)}{dt^2} + \dfrac{i(t)}{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Form of the Harmonic Oscillator Equation:**

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 i(t)}{dt^2} + \dfrac{1}{L_s C}\, i(t) = 0
$$

Comparing with the standard second-order ODE $\dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 x = 0$:

$$
\omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{L_s C}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L_s C}}
$$

Convert to Hz:

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The transient oscillates at $f_0$ because the magnetic energy stored in the inductor ($E_L = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$) and the electric energy stored in the capacitor ($E_C = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$) continuously trade places. The total energy is constant (lossless case), so the system rings at its natural resonant frequency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Peak Overvoltage During Capacitor Energization

**Setup:** Consider a practical feeder with source impedance $Z_s = R_s + jX_s$ and a load capacitor of capacitance $C$ at the end of a feeder having surge impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit Model at the Moment of Switching:**

The capacitor appears as a **short circuit** (un-charged) at $t = 0^+$. The full source voltage appears across $Z_s$, sending a high-magnitude current pulse.

**Step 2 — Voltage Doubling Phenomenon:**

The travelling wave launched at the switch travels down the feeder and reaches the capacitor. If the capacitor is initially uncharged, the reflected voltage adds to the incident voltage, potentially doubling it.

The instantaneous voltage at the capacitor end is:

$$
V_{cap}(t) = V_{source}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Simplification:**

In the worst case, $Z_s \rightarrow 0$ (stiff source), so:

$$
V_{cap,max} = V_{source}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{Z_0}{0 + Z_0}\right) = V_{source}\!\left(1 + 1\right) = 2 V_{source}
$$

Therefore, the **theoretical maximum peak overvoltage** is:

$$
\boxed{V_{peak} = 2\, V_{source}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, system losses and source impedance prevent reaching exactly 2.0 pu, but transient peaks of **1.8 – 1.9 pu** are commonly observed in industrial systems. This is the basis for selecting surge arresters at capacitor banks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Damped Oscillatory Transient Waveform

**Setup:** When damping (resistance $R$) is included, the oscillation decays over time.

**Step 1 — Write the Full RLC Second-Order ODE:**

$$
L \dfrac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}\, i = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Form:**

Divide by $L$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \dfrac{R}{L}\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{LC}\, i = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Damping Coefficient:**

By comparison with $\dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_0 \dfrac{dx}{dt} + \omega_0^2 x = 0$:

$$
2\alpha = \dfrac{R}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Damped Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}
$$

where $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

**Step 5 — General Solution:**

$$
\boxed{i(t) = i_0\, e^{-\alpha t}\, \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)}
$$

The transient is a **damped sine wave** — characteristic of an oscillatory transient recorded by a PQ analyzer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Inductive Switching Overvoltage (Lenz's Law Application)

**Setup:** An inductor (e.g., a relay coil, motor winding, transformer) carries a steady current $I$. The current is interrupted suddenly by a switch opening.

**Step 1 — Initial Condition Just Before Opening:**

$$
i_L(0^-) = I
$$

**Step 2 — KVL Across the Inductor After Switch Opens:**

The voltage across the inductor is:

$$
V_L(t) = L\,\dfrac{di(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — If the Current Drops to Zero in Time $\Delta t$:**

Assuming linear current decay:

$$
V_L = L\,\dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t}
$$

If $I = 10\ \text{A}$, $L = 0.5\ \text{H}$, and $\Delta t = 1\ \mu\text{s}$:

$$
V_L = 0.5 \times \dfrac{10}{10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{V} = 5\ \text{MV}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Reality:**

In practice, this enormous voltage is clamped by arc-quenching, stray capacitance, and snubber circuits, but inductive switching transients of **several hundred to thousand volts** are routine in industrial installations.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 415 V, 50 Hz industrial bus feeds a capacitor bank through a cable with $L_s = 50\ \mu\text{H}$ and $C = 50\ \mu\text{F}$. When the capacitor is energized, find:

(i) The natural frequency of the resulting transient.
(ii) The peak overvoltage (assume worst case).

### Solution:

**(i) Natural Frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C}}
$$

Substitute $L_s = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H}$ and $C = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$:

$$
L_s C = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
\sqrt{L_s C} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 3183\ \text{Hz} \approx 3.18\ \text{kHz}}
$$

This is in the **low-frequency oscillatory (LF) range** per IEEE 1159 — consistent with capacitor-switching transients.

**(ii) Peak Overvoltage (Worst Case):**

$$
V_{peak} = 2 \times V_{source,peak}
$$

The RMS line-to-line voltage is 415 V. The peak phase voltage is:

$$
V_{phase,peak} = \dfrac{415}{\sqrt{3}} \times \sqrt{2} = 239.6 \times 1.414 \approx 338.8\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{peak} = 2 \times 338.8 \approx 677.6\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{peak} \approx 677.6\ \text{V} \approx 1.83\ \text{pu}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Even though the system is only 415 V, transient peak voltages can approach 700 V. Surge arresters at the capacitor bank must be rated for at least this peak.
