**Voltage Unbalance** (also called Voltage Imbalance) is formally defined as a power quality condition in a three-phase (polyphase) electrical power system in which the **magnitudes of the phase voltages are unequal**, and/or the **phase angles between them deviate from the ideal balanced condition of 120° electrical separation**.

A perfectly balanced three-phase system has:
- Three sinusoidal voltages of **equal RMS magnitude**.
- A phase displacement of exactly **120°** between each pair of phases.
- Identical positive-sequence component and **zero negative-sequence** component.

When this ideal symmetry is disturbed, the system is said to be "unbalanced."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET751 Module 1):** Voltage unbalance is studied as a *steady-state* power quality disturbance, distinct from transient disturbances like sags, swells, or harmonics. It is typically quantified using standardized definitions given by **NEMA (MG-1)**, **IEEE 141 (Red Book)**, and **IEEE Standard 112**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine three runners tied together at the waist in a **three-legged race**:
- In a *balanced* system, all three runners step in perfect synchrony — they move forward smoothly at full speed.
- In an *unbalanced* system, **one runner stumbles or runs faster than the others**. The whole team slows down, twists, and strains.

Similarly, in a three-phase motor or transformer, if one phase voltage drops (say, due to a heavy single-phase load on that phase), the rotating magnetic field becomes elliptical instead of perfectly circular. The motor **runs hotter, vibrates more, and delivers less torque** — exactly like the twisting, slowing three-legged team.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Visual Rule of Thumb:** If you walk past an industrial site and hear an induction motor *humming loudly* or *vibrating* even under no load, the supply is very likely **voltage-unbalanced**.

---

### 1.3 Standardized Quantitative Metrics

Voltage unbalance is *not* defined by a single universal formula. Engineers use **three major definitions** depending on the application:

| Standard | Metric Name | Formula Basis |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **NEMA MG-1** | Percent Voltage Unbalance (LVUR) | Line Voltage Unbalance Ratio |
| **IEEE 141 / 112** | Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF) | Symmetrical Components |
| **CIGRE / IEC 61000-2-2** | True Unbalance (PVU / LVUF) | Magnitude and Angle of Phasors |

The most commonly accepted and KTU-recommended definition is the **IEEE Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)**, expressed as the ratio of the **negative-sequence voltage** to the **positive-sequence voltage**:

$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$$

where:
- $\vert V_1 \vert$ = magnitude of **positive-sequence** component.
- $\vert V_2 \vert$ = magnitude of **negative-sequence** component.
- $V_0$ = zero-sequence component (typically ignored in 3-wire systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Reference Values:**
> - **NEMA permissible unbalance:** **5 %** for motors (derating required above this).
> - **IEEE 141 recommended maximum:** **2 %** for a healthy power system.
> - **IEC 61000-2-2 compatibility level:** **2 %** for low-voltage public networks.

---

### 1.4 Why Voltage Unbalance Matters (Engineering Relevance)

Voltage unbalance is one of the **most common and damaging** power quality issues in industrial distribution networks because:

1. **It causes severe overheating** in three-phase induction motors — even a **2 %** unbalance can raise motor losses by ~8 %, and a **5 %** unbalance can double them.
2. **It produces negative-sequence currents** that rotate *opposite* to the rotor, inducing double-frequency currents in the rotor cage and causing **torque pulsations**.
3. **It stresses power electronics** — variable frequency drives (VFDs), rectifiers, and UPS systems may mis-trigger or fail.
4. **It reduces the lifespan** of transformers, capacitors, and rotating machines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Major Definitions of Voltage Unbalance (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### (a) NEMA MG-1 Definition — Line Voltage Unbalance Ratio (LVUR)

The **NEMA** (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) definition is the *simplest* and was historically the first widely used. It operates only on the **magnitudes** of line-to-line voltages.

**Step 1:** Calculate the *average* line-to-line voltage:
$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca}}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the *maximum deviation* of any line voltage from this average:
$$\Delta V_{\text{max}} = \max \big( \vert V_{ab} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert,\ \vert V_{bc} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert,\ \vert V_{ca} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert \big)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Line Voltage Unbalance Ratio (LVUR):
$$\text{LVUR (\%)} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{max}}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **NEMA only considers magnitude**, not phase angle. It is a fast, approximate method used by motor manufacturers for derating curves.

---

#### (b) IEEE 112 Definition — Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)

The **IEEE** definition is the *most accurate* and uses **symmetrical components** (Fortescue's Theorem, 1918). It captures *both* magnitude and angular unbalance.

**Step 1:** Measure the three line-to-line voltages as phasors: $V_{ab}$, $V_{bc}$, $V_{ca}$.

**Step 2:** Convert to phase voltages (line-to-neutral) using the relation:
$$V_a = \frac{V_{ab} - V_{ca}}{3}, \quad V_b = \frac{V_{bc} - V_{ab}}{3}, \quad V_c = \frac{V_{ca} - V_{bc}}{3}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Fortescue's symmetrical component transformation:
$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$$
$$V_1 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a V_b + a^2 V_c \big)$$
$$V_2 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a^2 V_b + a V_c \big)$$

where $a = 1 \angle 120° = e^{j 2\pi/3} = -0.5 + j\,0.866$.

**Step 4:** Calculate VUF:
$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $V_2$ matters:** The negative-sequence voltage $V_2$ produces a *backward-rotating* magnetic field in a motor. This is the *true physical cause* of motor heating and vibration under unbalance. Hence, VUF directly reflects the stress on rotating machines.

---

#### (c) CIGRE / True Definition (PVU — Phase Voltage Unbalance)

This definition uses both **magnitude and angle** of phase voltages, giving a single-number representation that agrees with VUF for small unbalances.

$$\text{PVU (\%)} = \sqrt{ \frac{1 - \sqrt{3 - 6\beta}}{1 + \sqrt{3 - 6\beta}} } \times 100$$

where the auxiliary parameter $\beta$ is:
$$\beta = \frac{\vert V_a \vert^4 + \vert V_b \vert^4 + \vert V_c \vert^4}{(\vert V_a \vert^2 + \vert V_b \vert^2 + \vert V_c \vert^2)^2}$$

---

### 2.2 Causes of Voltage Unbalance (Structured Logic)

Voltage unbalance is generated by **asymmetrical loading** or **asymmetrical network impedances**. The most common causes are:

- **Unequal single-phase loads** distributed across the three phases (e.g., lighting, residential feeders, welding machines).
- **Open phase** conditions (blown fuse, broken conductor, tripped single-pole breaker).
- **Unbalanced three-phase loads** (e.g., arc furnaces during melt-down, railway traction).
- **Asymmetrical transformer impedances** (e.g., open-delta or open-wye connections).
- **Faults on the power system** (single line-to-ground, line-to-line faults).
- **Unequal spacing of transmission line conductors** (causing mutual coupling differences).
- **Blown capacitor fuses** in power-factor correction banks.

---

### 2.3 Effects of Voltage Unbalance (Engineering Consequences)

| Equipment | Effect of Voltage Unbalance |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Induction Motor** | Increased losses (8 % per 1 % VUF), vibration, bearing wear, ~25 % loss of life per 1 % VUF |
| **Transformer** | Increased neutral current, K-rated heating in one phase |
| **VFD / Rectifier** | DC link ripple, harmonic injection, misfiring of thyristors/IGBTs |
| **Cables** | Unequal current sharing, neutral conductor overload |
| **Capacitors** | Voltage rise on least-loaded phase, possible dielectric failure |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU University Exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{LVUR} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{max}}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$ | NEMA unbalance | % |
| 2 | $\text{VUF} = \dfrac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$ | IEEE unbalance | % |
| 3 | $V_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + a V_b + a^2 V_c)$ | Positive sequence | V (phasor) |
| 4 | $V_2 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + a^2 V_b + a V_c)$ | Negative sequence | V (phasor) |
| 5 | $V_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$ | Zero sequence | V (phasor) |
| 6 | $a = 1\angle 120°$ | Fortescue operator | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Derating factor} = 1 - k \cdot \text{VUF}$ | Motor derating (NEMA) | p.u. |
| 8 | $P_{\text{loss}} \propto (1 + k \cdot \text{VUF}^2)$ | Motor losses | W |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

In a **substation or industrial plant**, the symmetric-component-based VUF is the *gold standard* for:
- Auditing the health of three-phase supply at the PCC (Point of Common Coupling).
- Designing **passive filters** to compensate unbalance via Steinmetz circuits.
- Determining **motor derating** before commissioning.
- Ensuring compliance with **IEEE 519** harmonic and unbalance limits for utility interconnection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Practice Tip:** Modern PQ analyzers (Fluke 435, Hioki PW3198, Schneider ION7650) compute VUF in *real time* and display it on the dashboard, alarming when it exceeds **2 %** (IEEE) or **5 %** (NEMA) thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: NEMA LVUR Calculation

**Problem:** A three-phase supply has the following line-to-line voltages:
$$V_{ab} = 410 \text{ V}, \quad V_{bc} = 400 \text{ V}, \quad V_{ca} = 390 \text{ V}$$
Calculate the **NEMA Percent Voltage Unbalance**.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the average line-to-line voltage:**

$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca}}{3}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{410 + 400 + 390}{3} = \frac{1200}{3} = 400 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each deviation from the average:**

$$\Delta V_{ab} = \vert 410 - 400 \vert = 10 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{bc} = \vert 400 - 400 \vert = 0 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{ca} = \vert 390 - 400 \vert = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum deviation:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{max}} = \max(10,\ 0,\ 10) = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the NEMA formula:**

$$\text{LVUR (\%)} = \frac{10}{400} \times 100 = 2.5\ \text{\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 2.5 % NEMA unbalance is **acceptable** by NEMA standards (limit 5 %) but **exceeds** the IEEE recommended 2 %. Motor derating is advised.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: IEEE VUF Using Symmetrical Components

**Problem:** The three line-to-line voltages are:
$$V_{ab} = 415 \angle 30° \text{ V}, \quad V_{bc} = 400 \angle -90° \text{ V}, \quad V_{ca} = 405 \angle 150° \text{ V}$$
Compute the **IEEE Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)**.

---

**Step 1 — Convert line-to-line voltages to phase voltages:**

The relation between line-to-line ($\vec{V}_{ab}$, $\vec{V}_{bc}$, $\vec{V}_{ca}$) and line-to-neutral ($\vec{V}_a$, $\vec{V}_b$, $\vec{V}_c$) voltages, with **no neutral connection**, is given by the standard conversion:

$$\vec{V}_a = \frac{\vec{V}_{ab} - \vec{V}_{ca}}{3}$$

$$\vec{V}_b = \frac{\vec{V}_{bc} - \vec{V}_{ab}}{3}$$

$$\vec{V}_c = \frac{\vec{V}_{ca} - \vec{V}_{bc}}{3}$$

Substituting the phasors:

$$\vec{V}_a = \frac{415\angle 30° - 405\angle 150°}{3}$$

Converting to rectangular form:
$$415\angle 30° = 415(\cos 30° + j\sin 30°) = 359.4 + j\,207.5$$
$$405\angle 150° = 405(\cos 150° + j\sin 150°) = -350.7 + j\,202.5$$

Subtracting:
$$\vec{V}_{ab} - \vec{V}_{ca} = (359.4 - (-350.7)) + j(207.5 - 202.5) = 710.1 + j\,5.0$$

Magnitude:
$$\vert \vec{V}_{ab} - \vec{V}_{ca} \vert = \sqrt{710.1^2 + 5.0^2} \approx 710.12 \text{ V}$$

Therefore:
$$\vec{V}_a \approx \frac{710.12}{3} \approx 236.7 \angle 0.4° \text{ V}$$

By symmetry (we will skip intermediate steps for brevity but show the method):

$$\vec{V}_b \approx 233.3 \angle -120.4° \text{ V}$$
$$\vec{V}_c \approx 235.0 \angle 119.6° \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fortescue's symmetrical component transformation:**

Define the Fortescue operator: $a = 1 \angle 120° = -0.5 + j\,0.866$, and $a^2 = 1 \angle 240° = -0.5 - j\,0.866$.

Compute $V_1$ (positive sequence):
$$\vec{V}_1 = \frac{1}{3}\big( \vec{V}_a + a \vec{V}_b + a^2 \vec{V}_c \big)$$

In rectangular form, using $V_a = 236.7 + j\,1.65$, $V_b = -116.6 - j\,202.0$, $V_c = -116.0 + j\,206.0$:

- $a \vec{V}_b = (-0.5 + j\,0.866)(-116.6 - j\,202.0) = 58.3 + j\,101.0 + j\,100.0 - 175.0 \approx 233.3 \angle 0°$
- $a^2 \vec{V}_c = (-0.5 - j\,0.866)(-116.0 + j\,206.0) = 58.0 - j\,103.0 + j\,100.0 + 178.5 \approx 236.7 \angle -2°$

Summing:
$$\vec{V}_1 \approx \frac{1}{3}(236.7 + j\,1.65 + 233.3 + j\,1.0 + 236.7 - j\,3.0)$$
$$\vec{V}_1 \approx \frac{1}{3}(706.7 - j\,0.35) \approx 235.6 \angle -0.03° \text{ V}$$

Compute $V_2$ (negative sequence):
$$\vec{V}_2 = \frac{1}{3}\big( \vec{V}_a + a^2 \vec{V}_b + a \vec{V}_c \big)$$

- $a^2 \vec{V}_b = (-0.5 - j\,0.866)(-116.6 - j\,202.0) = 58.3 + j\,101.0 + j\,100.0 - 175.0 \approx 233.3 \angle 180°$
- $a \vec{V}_c = (-0.5 + j\,0.866)(-116.0 + j\,206.0) = 58.0 - j\,103.0 + j\,100.0 + 178.5 \approx 236.7 \angle 178°$

Summing:
$$\vec{V}_2 \approx \frac{1}{3}(236.7 + j\,1.65 - 233.3 + j\,1.0 - 236.7 + j\,(-3.0))$$
$$\vec{V}_2 \approx \frac{1}{3}(-233.3 - j\,0.35) \approx 77.8 \angle 180.3° \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the VUF formula:**

$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert \vec{V}_2 \vert}{\vert \vec{V}_1 \vert} \times 100 = \frac{77.8}{235.6} \times 100 \approx 3.30\ \text{\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A VUF of 3.3 % **exceeds** the IEEE 141 recommended limit of 2 % and would require a 4–5 % derating of connected induction motors (NEMA derating curve). The plant engineer should redistribute single-phase loads to balance the supply.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Table

| Aspect | NEMA (LVUR) | IEEE (VUF) | CIGRE (PVU) |
|--------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| Input data | Magnitudes of line voltages | Phasors of phase voltages | Phasors of phase voltages |
| Uses angles? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Computational complexity | Very low | High (matrix math) | Medium |
| Accuracy for motor analysis | Approximate | Very accurate | Accurate |
| KTU/Industry preference | Common in motor specs | Most widely cited | Research / advanced |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Flow Diagram: Causes, Effects, and Mitigation of Voltage Unbalance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    source1[Asymmetrical Loading] --> vunbal[Voltage Unbalance]
    source2[Open Phase Fault] --> vunbal
    source3[Blown Capacitor Fuse] --> vunbal
    source4[Unbalanced Transformer Bank] --> vunbal
    source5[Single Line to Ground Fault] --> vunbal
    vunbal --> calc1{NEMA LVUR Calculation}
    vunbal --> calc2{IEEE VUF via Symmetrical Components}
    vunbal --> calc3{CIGRE PVU True Definition}
    calc1 --> effect1[Motor Overheating and Derating]
    calc2 --> effect1
    calc3 --> effect1
    calc1 --> effect2[Negative Sequence Currents in Rotor]
    calc2 --> effect2
    calc3 --> effect2
    calc1 --> effect3[VFD Misfiring and DC Link Ripple]
    calc2 --> effect3
    calc3 --> effect3
    calc1 --> effect4[Transformer Neutral Overload]
    calc2 --> effect4
    calc3 --> effect4
    effect1 --> mit1[Redistribute Single Phase Loads]
    effect2 --> mit1
    effect3 --> mit2[Install Active Power Filter]
    effect4 --> mit2
    effect1 --> mit3[Use Delta Wye Transformer with Tap Changer]
    effect2 --> mit3
    effect3 --> mit4[Replace with Inverter Duty Motor]
    effect4 --> mit4
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Symmetrical Component Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Phase Voltage Va] --> sum1[Matrix Multiplication Node A]
    inpB[Phase Voltage Vb] --> sum1
    inpC[Phase Voltage Vc] --> sum1
    op1[Fortescue Operator a] --> sum1
    op2[Fortescue Operator a squared] --> sum1
    sum1 --> v0[Zero Sequence V0]
    sum1 --> v1[Positive Sequence V1]
    sum1 --> v2[Negative Sequence V2]
    v1 --> div[VUF Computation V2 over V1 times 100]
    v2 --> div
    div --> result[Voltage Unbalance Factor percent]
    result --> comp{Compare with Standard}
    comp --> ieee2[IEEE Limit 2 percent]
    comp --> nema5[NEMA Limit 5 percent]
    ieee2 --> flag1[Alarm or Trip Signal]
    nema5 --> flag2[Derating Curve Activated]
```

---

### 4.3 Phasor Visualization of Symmetrical Components in an Unbalanced System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Unbalanced 3 Phase Supply Va Vb Vc] --> split[Resolve into 3 Balanced Systems]
    split --> seqZ[Zero Sequence Set: All in Phase]
    split --> seqP[Positive Sequence Set: a b c order]
    split --> seqN[Negative Sequence Set: a c b order]
    seqZ --> recons[Reconstruct Original Unbalanced Phasors]
    seqP --> recons
    seqN --> recons
    recons --> harm[Harmful Effect: Backward Rotating Field in Motor]
    harm --> remedy[Solution: Reduce Negative Sequence via Load Balancing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define voltage unbalance in a three-phase system. List any two causes of voltage unbalance in industrial distribution networks. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Voltage unbalance is a condition in a three-phase power system in which the **magnitudes of the phase voltages are unequal** and/or the **phase angles between them deviate from the ideal 120° separation**. A perfectly balanced system has equal magnitudes and exactly 120° displacement between phases.

**Two major causes:**
1. **Unequal distribution of single-phase loads** across the three phases (e.g., lighting, residential feeders, welding sets).
2. **Open-phase conditions** due to a blown fuse, broken conductor, or a tripped single-pole circuit breaker.

*(Acceptable: asymmetrical transformer impedances, single line-to-ground faults, blown capacitor fuses, unbalanced traction loads.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
State the IEEE definition of Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF). What is the recommended maximum VUF according to IEEE 141? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IEEE defines the **Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)** as the ratio of the **negative-sequence voltage magnitude** to the **positive-sequence voltage magnitude**, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$$

where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are obtained from the Fortescue symmetrical component transformation of the three phase voltages.

The **recommended maximum VUF** as per **IEEE 141 (Red Book)** is **2 %** for a healthy industrial power system. Exceeding this value causes excessive heating in induction motors and degradation of power-electronic equipment.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **NEMA definition** of voltage unbalance. A three-phase supply has line-to-line voltages of $V_{ab} = 408$ V, $V_{bc} = 400$ V, $V_{ca} = 388$ V. Calculate the **percent voltage unbalance** as per NEMA. Comment on its acceptability for an induction motor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)** using symmetrical components. Explain why the negative-sequence component $V_2$ is responsible for motor heating under unbalance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — State the NEMA definition:**

The NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) defines the **percent voltage unbalance** (also called **Line Voltage Unbalance Ratio, LVUR**) as the ratio of the *maximum deviation* of any line-to-line voltage from the *average line-to-line voltage*, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{LVUR (\%)} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{max}}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$$

where
$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca}}{3}, \quad \Delta V_{\text{max}} = \max \big( \vert V_{ab} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert,\ \vert V_{bc} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert,\ \vert V_{ca} - V_{\text{avg}} \vert \big)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average:**

$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{408 + 400 + 388}{3} = \frac{1196}{3} = 398.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the deviations:**

$$\Delta V_{ab} = \vert 408 - 398.67 \vert = 9.33 \text{ V}$$
$$\Delta V_{bc} = \vert 400 - 398.67 \vert = 1.33 \text{ V}$$
$$\Delta V_{ca} = \vert 388 - 398.67 \vert = 10.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the maximum deviation:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{max}} = \max(9.33,\ 1.33,\ 10.67) = 10.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the NEMA formula:**

$$\text{LVUR (\%)} = \frac{10.67}{398.67} \times 100 \approx 2.68\ \text{\%}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on acceptability:**

Since the calculated unbalance (2.68 %) is **less than the NEMA limit of 5 %**, the supply is *technically acceptable* for an induction motor. However, since it **exceeds the IEEE 141 recommended maximum of 2 %**, the connected motors should be **derated by approximately 4–5 %** of their rated horsepower, as per the NEMA derating curve. Continued operation without derating will reduce motor insulation life.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Statement of symmetrical components (Fortescue's Theorem):**

Any unbalanced set of three phasors $V_a$, $V_b$, $V_c$ can be uniquely decomposed into three balanced sets:
- **Zero-sequence** $V_0$ (all three phasors in phase and equal),
- **Positive-sequence** $V_1$ (a, b, c phase order, equal magnitudes, 120° apart),
- **Negative-sequence** $V_2$ (a, c, b phase order, equal magnitudes, 120° apart).

**Step 2 — Write the Fortescue transformation:**

Define the operator $a = 1 \angle 120° = e^{j2\pi/3}$, so that $a^2 = 1 \angle 240°$, and $a^3 = 1$, with $1 + a + a^2 = 0$.

The symmetric components are:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$$
$$V_1 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a V_b + a^2 V_c \big)$$
$$V_2 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a^2 V_b + a V_c \big)$$

The original (unbalanced) phasors are recovered by the inverse transformation:

$$V_a = V_0 + V_1 + V_2$$
$$V_b = V_0 + a^2 V_1 + a V_2$$
$$V_c = V_0 + a V_1 + a^2 V_2$$

**Step 3 — Define VUF:**

The IEEE 112 / IEEE 141 definition of the Voltage Unbalance Factor is:

$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Why $V_2$ causes motor heating:**

In a three-phase induction motor, the stator windings produce a **rotating magnetic field** when energized by balanced three-phase voltages. The positive-sequence voltage $V_1$ produces a **forward-rotating field** (synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$) that interacts with the rotor to produce useful mechanical torque.

The negative-sequence voltage $V_2$ produces a **backward-rotating magnetic field** of the same synchronous speed $N_s$ but in the **opposite direction**. This backward field:
1. **Induces double-frequency currents** (2 × slip frequency) in the rotor cage, causing severe **I²R losses** and **localized hot spots** in the rotor bars and end rings.
2. Produces a **braking torque** that reduces the net shaft torque.
3. Creates **mechanical vibration and acoustic noise** at twice the supply frequency.
4. Causes the stator to draw a **highly unbalanced current**, even when the motor is mechanically unloaded.

Since heating loss is proportional to $I^2 R$, and the rotor impedance to negative-sequence currents is small (only rotor resistance, not magnetizing reactance), even a small $\vert V_2 \vert$ produces a large negative-sequence current. This is why VUF is the **most physically meaningful** measure of unbalance stress on rotating machines.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **causes and effects of voltage unbalance** on three-phase induction motors. Discuss the **NEMA derating curve** for motors operating under unbalance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz supply has phase voltages:
$$V_a = 240 \angle 0° \text{ V}, \quad V_b = 230 \angle -120° \text{ V}, \quad V_c = 245 \angle 120° \text{ V}$$
Compute the **positive, negative, and zero sequence components** and the **Voltage Unbalance Factor**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Causes of Voltage Unbalance (with block diagram reference):**

| Sl. No. | Cause | Mechanism |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Unequal single-phase loads | Causes unequal voltage drop in each phase impedance |
| 2 | Open phase (blown fuse) | One phase is severely under- or over-voltage |
| 3 | Unbalanced 3-phase loads (arc furnace, traction) | Continuous stochastic unbalance |
| 4 | Asymmetrical transformer winding impedances | Different per-phase short-circuit impedances |
| 5 | Single line-to-ground fault | Severe temporary unbalance |
| 6 | Blown capacitor bank fuse | Asymmetric VAR injection |
| 7 | Untransposed transmission lines | Different mutual inductances between conductors |

**Effects on Three-Phase Induction Motor:**

1. **Increased Stator Current:** Even a 1 % VUF can increase stator current by 6–10 %.
2. **Increased Rotor Heating:** Rotor $I^2R$ losses rise approximately as the **square** of VUF. A 3.5 % VUF can **double** the rotor losses.
3. **Reduced Torque:** The backward-rotating field creates a braking torque, reducing the net developed torque.
4. **Mechanical Vibration:** Pulsating torque at 2× supply frequency causes bearing wear and acoustic noise.
5. **Reduced Bearing Life:** Vibration and shaft deflection lead to premature bearing failure.
6. **Reduced Insulation Life:** Hot-spot heating in the rotor reduces insulation life by approximately **50 % for every 10 °C rise**.

**NEMA Derating Curve:**

The NEMA MG-1 standard specifies that for a motor operating on an unbalanced supply, the **output power must be reduced** according to the empirical relationship:

$$\text{Derating Factor} \approx 1 - 0.6 \times \text{LVUR (\%/100)}$$

Practical derating table (NEMA MG-1 Part 14):

| Percent Voltage Unbalance | Derating Factor (kW output as fraction of rated) |
|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 0 % | 1.00 |
| 1 % | 0.97 |
| 2 % | 0.94 |
| 3 % | 0.89 |
| 4 % | 0.83 |
| 5 % | 0.75 |

For example, a 10 kW motor supplied with 4 % unbalance must be loaded only up to **8.3 kW** to prevent insulation damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering practice:** NEMA derating is conservative. IEC and IEEE methods using VUF give similar but slightly different curves. Always cross-check with the motor manufacturer's data sheet.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the operator $a$:**

$$a = 1 \angle 120° = -0.5 + j\,0.866, \quad a^2 = 1 \angle 240° = -0.5 - j\,0.866$$

**Step 2 — Express phase voltages in rectangular form:**

$$V_a = 240 \angle 0° = 240 + j\,0$$
$$V_b = 230 \angle -120° = 230(\cos -120° + j\sin -120°) = 230(-0.5 - j\,0.866) = -115 - j\,199.18$$
$$V_c = 245 \angle 120° = 245(\cos 120° + j\sin 120°) = 245(-0.5 + j\,0.866) = -122.5 + j\,212.17$$

**Step 3 — Compute the zero-sequence component $V_0$:**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$$

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3}\big[(240 + j\,0) + (-115 - j\,199.18) + (-122.5 + j\,212.17)\big]$$

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3}(2.5 + j\,12.99) = 0.833 + j\,4.33$$

$$\vert V_0 \vert = \sqrt{0.833^2 + 4.33^2} = \sqrt{0.694 + 18.75} = \sqrt{19.44} \approx 4.41 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the positive-sequence component $V_1$:**

$$V_1 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a V_b + a^2 V_c \big)$$

First compute $a V_b$:
$$a V_b = (-0.5 + j\,0.866)(-115 - j\,199.18)$$
$$= (-0.5)(-115) + (-0.5)(-j\,199.18) + (j\,0.866)(-115) + (j\,0.866)(-j\,199.18)$$
$$= 57.5 + j\,99.59 - j\,99.59 + 172.49 = 230.0 + j\,0.0$$

Then compute $a^2 V_c$:
$$a^2 V_c = (-0.5 - j\,0.866)(-122.5 + j\,212.17)$$
$$= 61.25 - j\,106.085 + j\,106.085 + 183.74 = 245.0 + j\,0.0$$

Now sum:
$$V_1 = \frac{1}{3}\big[240 + j\,0 + 230 + j\,0 + 245 + j\,0\big] = \frac{1}{3}(715) = 238.33 \text{ V}$$

$$\vert V_1 \vert = 238.33 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the negative-sequence component $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = \frac{1}{3}\big( V_a + a^2 V_b + a V_c \big)$$

Compute $a^2 V_b$:
$$a^2 V_b = (-0.5 - j\,0.866)(-115 - j\,199.18)$$
$$= 57.5 + j\,99.59 + j\,99.59 - 172.49 = -115.0 + j\,199.18$$

Compute $a V_c$:
$$a V_c = (-0.5 + j\,0.866)(-122.5 + j\,212.17)$$
$$= 61.25 - j\,106.085 - j\,106.085 - 183.74 = -122.5 - j\,212.17$$

Now sum:
$$V_2 = \frac{1}{3}\big[240 + j\,0 - 115 + j\,199.18 - 122.5 - j\,212.17\big]$$
$$= \frac{1}{3}\big[2.5 - j\,12.99\big] = 0.833 - j\,4.33$$

$$\vert V_2 \vert = \sqrt{0.833^2 + (-4.33)^2} = \sqrt{0.694 + 18.75} \approx 4.41 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Voltage Unbalance Factor:**

$$\text{VUF (\%)} = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100 = \frac{4.41}{238.33} \times 100 \approx 1.85\ \text{\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** VUF = 1.85 % is **just below the IEEE 141 recommended limit of 2 %**. The supply is acceptable for industrial use with no derating. However, it indicates a slight load imbalance that should be investigated by redistributing single-phase loads across the three phases.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing NEMA with IEEE:** Students often apply the NEMA formula to *phase* voltages instead of *line-to-line* voltages. NEMA LVUR is defined strictly on **line-to-line** magnitudes.
> 2. **Forgetting the Fortescue operator $a$:** The complex operator $a = 1 \angle 120°$ is the heart of symmetrical component analysis. Many students treat $V_1$ and $V_2$ formulas as identical — **they are not**! The order of $a$ and $a^2$ is reversed between $V_1$ and $V_2$.
> 3. **Skipping unit conversion:** If line voltages are given in kV, convert to V (or vice versa) before applying the formula. Unit mismatches are an instant mark-deduction trigger.
> 4. **Reporting VUF without percentage sign:** Always write VUF as a percentage; writing "0.0185" instead of "1.85 %" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring zero-sequence in 3-wire systems:** In a 3-wire (no neutral) system, $V_0 = 0$ by Kirchhoff's current law. Many students compute a non-zero $V_0$ in such systems and lose marks.
> 6. **Skipping the angular unit (degrees vs radians):** When using $\sin$ and $\cos$ in rectangular conversion, ensure your calculator is in **DEGREE mode** (since standard KTU problems use degrees, not radians).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Before the KTU Exam — Bookmark This Section!**

- **Voltage unbalance = unequal magnitudes OR unequal phase angles** in a 3-phase system.
- **NEMA definition** uses *line voltage magnitudes only* — simple but approximate.
- **IEEE definition (VUF)** uses *symmetrical components* — accurate and physically meaningful.
- **CIGRE / true definition** uses *both magnitude and angle* of phase voltages.
- **Fortescue operator:** $a = 1 \angle 120°$, $a^2 = 1 \angle 240°$, $a^3 = 1$, $1 + a + a^2 = 0$.
- **Formula recall:**
  * $\text{LVUR} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{max}}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \times 100$
  * $\text{VUF} = \dfrac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$
  * $V_0 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$
  * $V_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + a V_b + a^2 V_c)$
  * $V_2 = \tfrac{1}{3}(V_a + a^2 V_b + a V_c)$
- **NEMA limit:** 5 % (with derating). **IEEE recommended limit:** 2 %.
- **Negative-sequence currents** cause the most damage to motors — heating rises as VUF².
- **Top causes:** unequal single-phase loads, open phase, unbalanced transformer banks, faults.
- **Remedies:** load redistribution, active power filters, delta-wye transformers with tap changers, replacing standard motors with **inverter-duty** motors.
- **Industrial standard for PQ analyzers:** VUF display in real time, alarm at 2 % (IEEE) or 5 % (NEMA).
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry the *Fortescue operator* values in your memory: $a = -0.5 + j\,0.866$, $a^2 = -0.5 - j\,0.866$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Distortion - Notching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Waveform Distortion - Notching**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Notching

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Notching** is a periodic voltage disturbance of relatively short duration superimposed on the fundamental voltage waveform. It is a type of waveform distortion (categorized under the broader family of electromagnetic phenomena in power quality) that appears as a sharp, narrow depression ("notch") on the otherwise sinusoidal voltage trace. It is fundamentally a *switching phenomenon* and is uniquely associated with the normal line-commutation process of power-electronic converters (rectifiers/inverters using thyristors, SCRs, or diodes in bridge configurations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exact Terminology (as per IEEE Std 1159 & KTU PEEET751 Module 1):**
> *"Notching is a periodic disturbance caused by the commutation of current from one phase to another in a three-phase (or higher) power-electronic converter. It is a switching-event phenomenon that occurs whenever direct current is commutated between two or more phases via the AC line."*

The key attributes of a notch are:
1. **Depth** – the magnitude of the voltage dip (measured in volts or % of rated).
2. **Width (Duration)** – the time interval of the notch, expressed in electrical degrees (overlap angle $\mu$) or in microseconds.
3. **Area** – the integral of depth over time; a critical metric for estimating harmonic coupling into the supply network.

## 1.2 Physical Origin – Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for a First-Time Reader):**
> Imagine you are filling three buckets with water from a single hose. You have a rotating valve that switches the water flow from one bucket to the next. Now, the valve does not switch *instantly*; there is a small overlap period when the water is being *shared* between two buckets simultaneously. During this overlap, the pressure in the hose momentarily dips because the load is effectively "double" for that instant. This brief pressure dip, repeating every time the valve switches, is **exactly what notching is** in an electrical system: each time a converter tries to shift DC current from one phase to the next, there is a brief moment when two phases are briefly shorted together, causing a dip in the voltage on those phases.

The technical translation of the analogy:
- The **"hose"** = the AC source with internal impedance.
- The **"rotating valve"** = the firing of thyristors in a 6-pulse bridge.
- The **"water share"** = the commutation of DC current $I_d$ between phases.
- The **"pressure dip"** = the **voltage notch** in the waveform.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

| Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Standard Reference** | **IEEE 519-2014** & **IEEE 1159-2019** |
| **Typical Source** | Three-phase thyristor/diode bridge converters, AC drives, large UPS systems |
| **Disturbance Class** | Waveform distortion (NOT transient, NOT harmonic) |
| **Symptom in Equipment** | Flicker, misfiring of sensitive electronics, insulation stress, protective relay maloperation |
| **Measuring Instrument** | Power Quality Analyser with high sampling rate ($\geq 128$ samples/cycle) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Notching is a *Distortion* and not a *Transient*:** A transient (impulsive/oscillatory) is a one-time, decaying event. A notch is a *recurring*, *steady-state* feature synchronized with the line frequency and the converter's firing pulses. It is essentially a deterministic switching artifact.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Notching in a 6-Pulse Bridge Converter

A 6-pulse converter uses **6 thyristors (T1–T6)** conducting in pairs (T1+T2, T2+T3, T3+T4, etc.) for $60^\circ$ each per cycle. Ideally, when one thyristor pair stops conducting, the *next* pair starts instantly. In reality, the outgoing thyristor pair cannot turn off instantly due to source inductance $L_s$ – the current must transfer gradually. This overlap interval is the **commutation overlap angle $\mu$** (typically $5^\circ$ to $30^\circ$).

### Sequential Logic of a Commutation Event

1. **Step 1 – Pre-Commutation:** Current $I_d$ flows entirely through the outgoing thyristor pair (say T5–T6 from phases c–a). Phase voltage $V_a$ is at nominal sinusoid.
2. **Step 2 – Firing Trigger:** At the firing angle $\alpha$, thyristor T1 (phase a) is fired. Now T5 and T1 both conduct → **line-to-line short between phases a and c through $L_s$**.
3. **Step 3 – Current Transfer:** Current $i_1$ ramps up in T1 while $i_5$ ramps down in T5. The supply sees an effective short across the inductance difference.
4. **Step 4 – Notch Generation:** The voltage $V_{ac}$ (line voltage) is dropped across $2L_s$. Phase voltages $V_a$ and $V_c$ are pulled towards each other – this appears as a **flat bottom / notch** on the waveform.
5. **Step 5 – Completion:** When $i_5$ reaches zero, commutation ends after angle $\mu$. Normal conduction resumes.

## 2.2 The Two Defining Metrics of a Notch

### Metric A – Notch Depth (Magnitude)

The notch depth for a 6-pulse bridge is governed by the **DC link current** and the **per-phase source inductance**:

$$V_{notch} = \frac{2 \cdot I_d \cdot X_s}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \text{(approx., line-line notch effect on phase voltage)}$$

where $X_s = 2\pi f L_s$ is the source reactance at the supply frequency.

### Metric B – Notch Width (Duration)

The notch width is the **commutation angle $\mu$** in electrical degrees, computed as:

$$\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\alpha - \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}}\right] - \alpha$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = firing angle
- $V_{LL}$ = line-to-line RMS voltage
- $I_d$ = DC current
- $X_s$ = source reactance per phase

### Metric C – Notch Area (for harmonic coupling)

The notch area is the **time-integral of the voltage depression** and is the most useful parameter for assessing the propagation of notch-induced high-frequency components into the supply:

$$A_{notch} = \int_{0}^{\Delta t} \Delta V_{notch}(t)\, dt \quad [\text{V}\cdot\text{s}]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{notch} \approx \dfrac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}}$ | Notch depth in a 6-pulse bridge | Volts |
| 2 | $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\alpha - \dfrac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3} V_{LL}}\right] - \alpha$ | Notch width (overlap angle) | Degrees (electrical) |
| 3 | $A_{notch} = \int \Delta V_{notch}\, dt$ | Notch area for HF coupling analysis | V·s |
| 4 | $X_s = 2\pi f L_s$ | Source reactance at fundamental | Ohms |
| 5 | $SCR = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{I_L}$ | Short-Circuit Ratio (governs notch depth) | p.u. (dimensionless) |
| 6 | $\% VTR = \dfrac{V_{notch}}{V_{rated}} \times 100$ | Notch depth in percent | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (often asked in KTU viva):**
> A low short-circuit ratio (weak supply, high $X_s$) → **deep and wide notches**. A stiff supply (high $SCR$) → small, narrow notches. This is why notching is most severe at the *point of common coupling (PCC)* of industrial consumers fed by weak rural grids.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of AC line reactors / DC link chokes:** Inverter notching must be limited to < 40% of peak phase voltage (per IEEE 519). Reactor sizing uses the formulas above.
- **Coordination with Sensitive Loads:** Medical imaging (MRI), PLCs, and zero-crossing detectors are notch-sensitive. Notching can cause *zero-crossing shift errors* in firing circuits.
- **Converter Performance Analysis:** Notch area determines the *effective harmonic voltage distortion* $THD_v$ contribution from the commutation process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Notch Depth in a 6-Pulse Bridge (Exhaustive)

Consider a three-phase source with per-phase inductance $L_s$ feeding a 6-pulse bridge delivering DC current $I_d$. At the moment of commutation from phase c (T5) to phase a (T1):

### Step 1 – Write the Line-to-Line Voltage Equation During Commutation

During commutation, the two phases a and c are shorted through the source inductances. The governing KVL is:

$$V_a - V_c = L_s \frac{di_1}{dt} - L_s \frac{di_5}{dt}$$

### Step 2 – Apply the Constraint of Constant DC Current

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the DC side, the sum of the two phase currents is the DC link current:

$$i_1 + i_5 = I_d$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{di_1}{dt} + \frac{di_5}{dt} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{di_1}{dt} = -\frac{di_5}{dt}$$

### Step 3 – Substitute Back Into the KVL

$$V_a - V_c = 2 L_s \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

Solving for the rate of change of current:

$$\frac{di_1}{dt} = \frac{V_a - V_c}{2 L_s}$$

### Step 4 – Express the Line-to-Line Voltage in Phasor Form

If the source has line-to-line RMS voltage $V_{LL}$ and frequency $\omega$, the instantaneous line voltage during commutation can be expressed. Approximating the notch depth using peak quantities:

$$V_{notch,\,peak} \approx \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \cdot \sin(\mu/2) \quad\text{(geometric interpretation)}$$

For small $\mu$, $\sin(\mu/2) \approx \mu/2$, but a more useful engineering form uses RMS values:

$$V_{notch} \approx \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 5 – Express in Terms of Short-Circuit MVA

The source reactance $X_s$ is related to the available short-circuit MVA $MVA_{sc}$:

$$X_s = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{MVA_{sc}} \quad \text{(per-phase basis, in \Omega)}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{notch} = \frac{2 I_d}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{V_{LL}^2}{MVA_{sc}} = \frac{2 I_d V_{LL}^2}{\sqrt{3}\, MVA_{sc}}$$

### Step 6 – Final Percent-Normalized Form (as a Percentage of Rated Voltage)

$$\%V_{notch} = \frac{V_{notch}}{V_{rated}} \times 100 = \frac{2 I_d V_{LL}^2}{\sqrt{3}\, MVA_{sc}\, V_{rated}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check (Dimensional Check):**
> $I_d$ in A, $V_{LL}^2$ in V², $MVA_{sc}$ in VA → A·V²/VA = A·V = W → dividing by rated V gives per-unit. The dimension is correct.

## 3.2 Derivation of Commutation (Notch) Width – Angle $\mu$

Starting from the commutation differential equation for a thyristor bridge with a purely inductive source:

$$V_{ac}(\theta) = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin(\theta) = 2 L_s \omega \frac{di_1}{d\theta}$$

Integrating over the commutation interval from $\alpha$ to $\alpha + \mu$:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = 2 L_s \omega \int_{0}^{I_d} di_1$$

The right-hand integral simply gives $2 L_s \omega I_d$. The left-hand integral:

$$\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \left[\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right]$$

Setting them equal:

$$\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \left[\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right] = 2 L_s \omega I_d$$

Solving for $\mu$:

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = \cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 L_s \omega I_d}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}} = \cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 X_s I_d}{\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}$$

Since $V_{LL}$ in the formula is already RMS, and the relation between peak and RMS introduces a $\sqrt{2}$, the standard KTU textbook form is:

$$\boxed{\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\alpha - \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}}\right] - \alpha}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For KTU Numerical Problems)

> **Problem:** A 6-pulse, 3-phase converter is connected to a 415 V, 50 Hz supply. The DC current is $I_d = 100$ A. The per-phase source reactance is $X_s = 0.1\,\Omega$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 30^\circ$. Calculate (a) the notch depth, and (b) the notch width $\mu$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify parameters:**

$$V_{LL} = 415\,\text{V},\quad I_d = 100\,\text{A},\quad X_s = 0.1\,\Omega,\quad \alpha = 30^\circ$$

**Step 2 – Compute the notch depth (a):**

$$V_{notch} = \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 0.1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{20}{1.732} = 11.55\,\text{V (RMS)}$$

As a percentage of rated line voltage:

$$\% V_{notch} = \frac{11.55}{415} \times 100 = 2.78\%$$

**Step 3 – Compute the notch width $\mu$ (b):**

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = \cos(30^\circ) - \frac{2 \times 100 \times 0.1}{\sqrt{3} \times 415}$$

$$\cos(30^\circ) = 0.8660,\quad \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3} V_{LL}} = \frac{20}{1.732 \times 415} = \frac{20}{718.78} = 0.02783$$

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = 0.8660 - 0.02783 = 0.8382$$

$$\alpha + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.8382) = 33.04^\circ$$

$$\mu = 33.04^\circ - 30^\circ = 3.04^\circ$$

**Step 4 – Convert $\mu$ to time (electrical):**

$$\Delta t = \frac{\mu}{360^\circ} \times T = \frac{3.04}{360} \times \frac{1}{50} = 168.9\,\mu s$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Recap:** Notch depth = **11.55 V (2.78%)**; Notch width = **3.04° ≈ 169 μs**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Scenario | Source Condition | Notch Depth | Notch Width | Effect on PQ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weak grid, high load | $SCR < 3$ | **Deep (>10%)** | Wide ($>20^\circ$) | Severe – may violate IEEE 519 |
| Stiff grid, low load | $SCR > 20$ | Shallow (<2%) | Narrow ($<5^\circ$) | Negligible |
| Increased firing angle | $\alpha \uparrow$ | Slightly reduced | Slightly increased | Reduced notch severity |
| Adding line reactor | $X_s$ increases artificially | **Increases** | Increases | **Worsens notching** (tradeoff for current smoothing) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – Origin of Notching in a 6-Pulse Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase AC Source\n415 V, 50 Hz] --> B[Source Impedance\nLs and Rs]
    B --> C[6-Pulse Thyristor Bridge\nT1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6]
    C --> D[DC Load\nId constant]
    F[Firing Pulses\nat angle alpha] --> C
    C --> E[Commutation Process\nOverlap angle mu]
    E --> G[Notch on AC Voltage\nDepth V notch, Width mu]
    E --> H[Line-to-Line Short\nduring mu]
    H --> B
    G --> I[IEEE 519 Notching Index\nAssessment]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Notch Waveform Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Sinusoidal Phase Voltage Va] --> N1[Commutation begins\nat angle alpha]
    N1 --> N2[Voltage dip starts\nphase pulled toward Vc]
    N2 --> N3[Notch flat bottom\nduration mu]
    N3 --> N4[Voltage recovers\nafter commutation ends]
    N4 --> P1
    style N1 fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style N2 fill:#f96,stroke:#333
    style N3 fill:#f33,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style N4 fill:#fc6,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram – Propagation Path of Notch Harmonics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Converter 6-pulse] --> N1[Notch Generated]
    N1 --> P1[High Frequency Edge\nrisetime in microseconds]
    P1 --> C1[Couples to Supply\nvia Xs and cable capacitance]
    C1 --> R1[Resonance with\nSystem Capacitors]
    R1 --> E1[Amplified HF Disturbance\nat PCC]
    E1 --> L1[Impact on Sensitive Loads\nPLCs, Drives, Protection]
    N1 --> M1[Notch Area Integral\nA notch for THD calc]
    M1 --> T1[Total THD Assessment]
```

## 4.4 Description of the Notch Waveform (for Mental Picture)

If we draw the phase-a voltage $V_a(t)$ over one cycle, we observe:

- For most of the cycle, the curve is a clean sinusoid.
- At six points per cycle (one per commutation), a brief "cut-out" appears.
- The cut-out begins at the firing instant ($\alpha$) and ends at $(\alpha + \mu)$.
- Inside this interval, $V_a$ is no longer a sinusoid; it is forced to follow the average between $V_a$ and $V_c$.
- The minimum voltage reached (the **notch floor**) is the midpoint of the two original phase voltages at that instant.
- The **depth** = (sinusoidal expected value) − (notch floor value).
- The **width** = $\mu$ in electrical degrees.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Drawing Questions (Memory Aid):**
> Always label three items on your drawn notch:
> 1. The **commutation angle $\mu$** at the base (width).
> 2. The **notch depth $\Delta V$** on the y-axis.
> 3. The **firing angle $\alpha$** from the zero-crossing of the natural commutation point.
> Examiners specifically look for these three annotations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define "notching" in the context of power quality. Mention its primary cause.

**Model Answer:**
Notching is a periodic voltage disturbance appearing as a brief depression (notch) on the sinusoidal voltage waveform, caused by the normal commutation of current between phases in a three-phase (or higher) power-electronic converter. Its **primary cause** is the line-to-line short circuit that occurs for a short duration (the commutation overlap angle $\mu$) when current is being transferred from one thyristor pair to the next across the source inductance. **(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for primary cause)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Differentiate between notching and an impulsive transient. Why is notching classified as a *waveform distortion* and not a *transient*?

**Model Answer:**
- **Impulsive Transient:** A unidirectional, non-recurring, decaying spike of very short duration (microseconds), typically caused by lightning or switching of inductive loads.
- **Notching:** A recurring, deterministic depression in the voltage waveform that is *synchronous* with the line frequency and the converter firing pulses.
- **Classification:** Notching is classified as waveform distortion because it is a *steady-state periodic* phenomenon, not a one-time event. It is caused by the normal operation of power-electronic converters, and its frequency spectrum falls within the waveform-distortion category of IEEE 1159. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) – 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the phenomenon of voltage notching in a three-phase 6-pulse converter. Derive the expression for the commutation overlap angle $\mu$ in terms of the firing angle $\alpha$, the DC current $I_d$, the source reactance $X_s$, and the line voltage $V_{LL}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pulse thyristor converter is fed from a 440 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase supply. The per-phase source inductance is $L_s = 0.3$ mH. The DC current is $I_d = 80$ A and the firing angle is $\alpha = 25^\circ$. Calculate the **(i)** notch depth and **(ii)** commutation overlap angle $\mu$. Also compute the notch width in microseconds. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A clear labelled diagram of a 6-pulse bridge with source, thyristors, and DC load, marking the commutation interval. The waveform showing the notch on phase voltage must be included with $\alpha$, $\mu$, and notch depth labelled.

**Explanation (3 marks):** The detailed mechanism of commutation as described in Section 2.1 of these notes.

**Derivation of $\mu$ (2 marks):**

Starting from the voltage equation during commutation:

$$\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} \left[\cos(\alpha) - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right] = 2 X_s I_d$$

Solving:

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = \cos(\alpha) - \frac{2 X_s I_d}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}}$$

Therefore:

$$\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\alpha - \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}}\right] - \alpha$$

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 440$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $L_s = 0.3$ mH, $I_d = 80$ A, $\alpha = 25^\circ$.

**Step 1 – Compute source reactance:**

$$X_s = 2\pi f L_s = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3} = 0.09425\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 – Notch depth:**

$$V_{notch} = \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 0.09425}{1.732} = \frac{15.08}{1.732} = 8.71\,\text{V}$$

$$\% V_{notch} = \frac{8.71}{440} \times 100 = 1.98\%$$

**Step 3 – Overlap angle:**

$$\frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}} = \frac{15.08}{1.732 \times 440} = \frac{15.08}{762.08} = 0.01979$$

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = \cos(25^\circ) - 0.01979 = 0.9063 - 0.01979 = 0.8865$$

$$\alpha+\mu = \cos^{-1}(0.8865) = 27.55^\circ$$

$$\mu = 27.55^\circ - 25^\circ = 2.55^\circ$$

**Step 4 – Notch width in time:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{\mu}{360^\circ} \times \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2.55}{360} \times \frac{1}{50} = 141.7\,\mu s$$

**Final Answers:** Notch depth = **8.71 V (1.98%)**; $\mu$ = **2.55°**; Notch width = **141.7 μs**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative to Question A) – 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1]**

**(a)** What is a notch? Discuss the effects of notching on sensitive electronic equipment and the limits prescribed by IEEE 519. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pulse converter operates on a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase supply. The DC link current is 150 A. The short-circuit MVA available at the point of common coupling (PCC) is 50 MVA. The firing angle is $\alpha = 45^\circ$. Determine: **(i)** the per-phase source reactance; **(ii)** the notch depth in volts; and **(iii)** the commutation angle. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

- **Definition of notch (2 marks):** As in Q1 of Part A.
- **Effects (3 marks):** Flicker, zero-crossing detection errors in PLCs, misfiring of thyristor-based systems, malfunction of protective relays, accelerated insulation aging, communication interference in PLC carrier systems.
- **IEEE 519 limits (2 marks):** The notch depth should not exceed **20% of peak phase voltage** for single-converter installations, and the notch area (V·s product) is constrained based on the system voltage level and short-circuit capacity. The standard also defines notch depth limits based on the Short-Circuit Ratio (SCR).

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 415$ V, $I_d = 150$ A, $MVA_{sc} = 50$ MVA, $\alpha = 45^\circ$.

**Step 1 – Source reactance (i):**

$$X_s = \frac{V_{LL}^2}{MVA_{sc} \times 10^6} = \frac{415^2}{50 \times 10^6} = \frac{172225}{50 \times 10^6} = 3.4445 \times 10^{-3}\,\Omega$$

Or equivalently: $X_s = 3.44$ m$\Omega$.

**Step 2 – Notch depth (ii):**

$$V_{notch} = \frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 3.4445 \times 10^{-3}}{1.732} = \frac{1.0334}{1.732} = 0.597\,\text{V (RMS)}$$

**Step 3 – Commutation angle (iii):**

$$\frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}} = \frac{1.0334}{1.732 \times 415} = \frac{1.0334}{718.78} = 0.001438$$

$$\cos(\alpha+\mu) = \cos(45^\circ) - 0.001438 = 0.7071 - 0.001438 = 0.7057$$

$$\alpha+\mu = \cos^{-1}(0.7057) = 45.13^\circ$$

$$\mu = 45.13^\circ - 45^\circ = 0.13^\circ$$

> **Note:** A small overlap angle is expected because the grid is very stiff (50 MVA short-circuit MVA) relative to the load.

**Final Answers:** $X_s$ = **3.44 m$\Omega$**; Notch depth = **0.597 V (0.144%)**; $\mu$ = **0.13°**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ in notch-depth formula:** Many students write $V_{notch} = 2 I_d X_s$ instead of $\frac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}}$. This costs full 2 marks in numerical problems. The $\sqrt{3}$ arises from the 3-phase to single-phase conversion.
> 2. **Using $V_{LL}$ where $V_{ph}$ is required:** The formula is derived for line quantities; substituting phase voltage by mistake leads to a $\sqrt{3}$ error.
> 3. **Drawing the notch on a *single-phase* voltage instead of *line voltage*:** The notch is most clearly visible on **phase voltage**. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 4. **Not labelling $\alpha$ and $\mu$ on the waveform diagram:** Even with a correct diagram, missing these labels costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **In part (b) numericals, skipping units in the final answer:** Always state the units (V, $\mu$s, °). A correct number without units loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Quick-Recall Bulletins (For the Last 30 Minutes Before Exam):**

- **Notching** is a *periodic* voltage disturbance caused by **commutation in 6-pulse (or higher) converters**.
- It appears as a sharp dip on the **phase voltage waveform**, repeating **6 times per cycle** in a 6-pulse bridge.
- The **width** of the notch = **commutation overlap angle $\mu$** (in electrical degrees).
- The **depth** of the notch $\propto I_d \cdot X_s / \sqrt{3}$ — proportional to DC link current and source reactance.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  1. $V_{notch} = \dfrac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}}$
  2. $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\cos\alpha - \dfrac{2 I_d X_s}{\sqrt{3}\, V_{LL}}\right] - \alpha$
  3. $X_s = \dfrac{V_{LL}^2}{MVA_{sc}}$ (per-phase from short-circuit MVA)
  4. $\Delta t = \dfrac{\mu}{360^\circ \cdot f}$
- **Weaker grid (lower $MVA_{sc}$, lower SCR) → deeper and wider notches.**
- **Stronger grid (higher $MVA_{sc}$) → smaller, narrower notches.**
- **Classification:** Waveform **distortion** (NOT transient, NOT harmonic – though it *generates* harmonics due to steep edges).
- **IEEE 519 limit:** Notch depth ≤ 20% of peak phase voltage (single-converter case).
- **Detection:** Requires a power quality analyser with **$\geq 128$ samples/cycle**.
- **Mitigation:** Use **active front-end (AFE) converters**, **12-pulse or 18-pulse configurations**, or **line reactors tuned with capacitor banks** to reduce notch depth.
- **For numericals:** Always show the calculation of $X_s$ explicitly; check the units of $V_{LL}$ (line-to-line) and $I_d$ (DC average) carefully.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing the *notch width* $\mu$ (a small angle) with the *firing angle* $\alpha$. Remember: $\alpha$ = delay from natural commutation point; $\mu$ = additional angle during which commutation occurs.
- **Symptom in lab equipment:** If a zero-crossing detector flickers randomly, suspect a high notch-depth scenario at the PCC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harmonics and Voltage Flicker, Power Quality Issues of Grid Connected Renewable Energy Systems - Operating Conflicts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Harmonics and Voltage Flicker, Power Quality Issues of Grid Connected Renewable Energy Systems - Operating Conflicts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Harmonics in Power Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Harmonics** are sinusoidal voltages or currents having frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental power system frequency (**50 Hz in India**). The fundamental component is termed the **first harmonic** ($h=1$), and any waveform distortion can be decomposed using the **Fourier Series** into a sum of sinusoids at these discrete frequencies.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Picture:**
> Imagine the ideal 50 Hz AC waveform as a **perfectly still swimming pool** — clean ripples travelling smoothly at one frequency. Now imagine a mischievous child jumping in (a nonlinear load like a variable-frequency drive). The pool now has ripples of **multiple frequencies simultaneously**: the original 50 Hz wave, plus smaller wavelets at 100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz, etc. Each of those secondary ripples is a **harmonic**. The "jump" represents a load that does not draw current sinusoidally, and the resulting distortion is exactly what we call **harmonic pollution**.

### Standard Metrics for Harmonics

- **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion):** Ratio of RMS of harmonic content to RMS of fundamental.
- **TDD (Total Demand Distortion):** Harmonic current relative to rated load current.
- **Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD):** Magnitude of a specific harmonic relative to fundamental.
- **Crest Factor:** Ratio of peak value to RMS value (1.414 for pure sinewave).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Standard:** The fundamental frequency mandated by the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** and **Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC)** is **50 Hz**, with operational tolerance of **±0.5 Hz** (i.e., 49.5 Hz – 50.5 Hz).

---

## 1.2 Voltage Flicker

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61000-4-15 Standard):**
> **Voltage Flicker** is the impression of unsteadiness of visual sensation induced by a light stimulus whose luminance or spectral distribution fluctuates with time. In engineering terms, it is a **systematic variation of the voltage envelope** (typically in the frequency range **0.5 Hz to 25 Hz**) that causes noticeable luminance changes in incandescent lamps and irritation to human observers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Candle in the Wind" Picture:**
> Picture an incandescent bulb glowing steadily. Now imagine the supply voltage dipping and recovering repeatedly — like a candle flame flickering in a gusty room. The bulb does not blink ON/OFF, but its **brightness pulses rapidly enough** to be annoying, yet **too fast** for the eye to consciously track. That perceptual annoyance, captured quantitatively through the **Flickermeter (IEC 61000-4-15)**, is what we call **flicker**. Wind turbines, arc furnaces, and capacitor switching are classic culprits.

### Standard Flicker Metrics

- **Pst (Short-Term Severity):** Measured over **10 minutes**. A Pst = 1.0 corresponds to the **threshold of irritability** for 50% of observers.
- **Plt (Long-Term Severity):** Computed over **2 hours** from 12 consecutive Pst values.
- **Voltage Fluctuation Magnitude (ΔV/V):** Typically expressed as a percentage.

---

## 1.3 Power Quality Issues in Grid-Connected Renewable Energy (RE) Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Grid-connected RE systems** (wind farms, solar PV plants, micro-hydro) inject power into the utility network through **power-electronic inverters**. Because these inverters are inherently **nonlinear, switching devices**, and because the **primary energy source (wind speed, solar irradiance) is stochastic**, several PQ disturbances arise: **harmonic injection, voltage flicker, DC-link voltage ripple, frequency drift, and islanding**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Connecting a Boat to a Busy River":**
> A river (utility grid) flows steadily in one direction at a constant rate (50 Hz, balanced voltage). Connecting a small boat (an RE system) by rope is easy. But if the boat has its **own engine** (the inverter) that produces irregular pulses, and if the **wind keeps yanking the boat around** (variable wind/solar input), the rope keeps **jerking** — causing disturbances that travel upstream to other boats (other consumers) tied to the same dock (Point of Common Coupling). These disturbances are **harmonic distortion, flicker, and voltage fluctuations**.

---

## 1.4 Operating Conflicts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Operating Conflicts** are the operational incompatibilities that arise when grid-tied RE systems interact unfavorably with the utility network. These include: **(i) anti-islanding protection vs. continued generation, (ii) voltage ride-through (LVRT/HVRT) requirements vs. inverter tripping, (iii) reactive power support vs. real power maximization (e.g., wind MPPT), (iv) harmonic injection limits vs. converter switching schemes, and (v) frequency response vs. inertia-less inverter behavior**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Roundabout Driving" Analogy:**
> Traditional power plants are like **heavy trucks** — they have momentum (rotational inertia) and obey traffic rules reactively. RE systems are like **light electric scooters** — nimble and fast, but with **no inertia** and **different braking response**. When both share the same roundabout (the grid), conflicts arise: who yields? Who accelerates first after a fault? These are **operating conflicts** that grid codes must explicitly resolve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Harmonic Theory — Fourier Decomposition

Any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform $x(t)$ of period $T$ (with fundamental angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T = 2\pi f_1$) can be expanded as:

### General Fourier Series

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_h \cos(h \omega_0 t) + b_h \sin(h \omega_0 t) \right]$$

Alternatively expressed using RMS phasors:

$$x(t) = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \sqrt{2}\, X_h \sin\!\left(h \omega_0 t + \phi_h\right)$$

where:
- $X_h$ = RMS amplitude of the $h^{th}$ harmonic
- $\phi_h$ = phase angle of the $h^{th}$ harmonic
- $h$ = harmonic order (positive integer)

### Why and How — Logic Steps

- **Why Fourier?** Because the power system operates in a linear regime where **sinusoids are eigenvectors** — any sinusoid applied to a linear LTI system remains a sinusoid at the same frequency (with scaled amplitude and shifted phase). Hence, analyzing each harmonic independently using superposition is valid.
- **How?** Step 1: Identify fundamental frequency ($f_1 = 50$ Hz). Step 2: Compute Fourier coefficients. Step 3: Treat each harmonic as an independent phasor. Step 4: Superpose effects.

---

## 2.2 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

### Voltage THD

$$\text{THD}_V = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^{\,2}}}{V_1} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} + V_3^{\,2} + V_4^{\,2} + \cdots}}{V_1}$$

### Current THD

$$\text{THD}_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^{\,2}}}{I_1}$$

### Total Demand Distortion (TDD)

$$\text{TDD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^{\,2}}}{I_L}$$

where $I_L$ is the **maximum demand load current** (15-min or 30-min average at PCC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** IEEE 519-2014 limits THD$_V$ to **8%** at PCC for systems below 1 kV, and **3%** individual harmonic limit on the largest. TDD is preferred over THD for current distortion at PCC.

---

## 2.3 Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD)

$$\text{IHD}_h = \frac{X_h}{X_1} \times 100\,\%$$

where $X_h$ is the RMS value of the $h^{th}$ harmonic and $X_1$ is the fundamental RMS value.

---

## 2.4 Crest Factor (CF)

$$\text{CF} = \frac{X_{\text{peak}}}{X_{\text{RMS}}}$$

For a pure sinusoid: **CF = √2 ≈ 1.414**. A higher CF indicates more peaky distortion (e.g., rectifiers produce CF ≈ 2.0).

---

## 2.5 Voltage Flicker Metrics

### Short-Term Flicker Severity (Pst)

$$\text{Pst} = \sqrt{0.0314\, P_{0.1} + 0.0525\, P_{1s} + 0.0657\, P_{3s} + 0.28\, P_{10s} + 0.08\, P_{50s}}$$

where $P_x$ are percentile exceedance levels from the instantaneous flicker sensation level.

### Long-Term Flicker Severity (Plt)

$$\text{Plt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{12} \sum_{i=1}^{12} (\text{Pst}_i)^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limits (IEC 61000-3-7 / IEEE 1453):** Pst ≤ **1.0** (planning level); Plt ≤ **0.65** for HV/MV systems; Pst ≤ **0.8** for LV systems.

---

## 2.6 DC-Link Voltage Ripple (RE Inverter)

The DC-link voltage ripple for a three-phase PWM inverter is governed by:

$$\Delta V_{\text{dc}} = \frac{I_{\text{load}}}{2 \omega_1 C_{\text{dc}}}$$

where $I_{\text{load}}$ is the inverter-side current ripple, $\omega_1 = 2\pi \times 50$ rad/s, and $C_{\text{dc}}$ is the DC-link capacitance.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Formula** | **Symbol** | **Expression** | **Standard Limit / Value** | **Units** |
|------------------------|------------|----------------|---------------------------|-----------|
| Fourier Series | $x(t)$ | $\sum \sqrt{2}\, X_h \sin(h\omega_0 t + \phi_h)$ | Infinite sum, h integer $\geq 1$ | V or A |
| Harmonic Frequency | $f_h$ | $h \cdot f_1$ | $f_1 = 50$ Hz (India) | Hz |
| Voltage THD | THD$_V$ | $\sqrt{\sum_{h \geq 2} V_h^2} \;/\; V_1$ | $\leq 5\%$ (utility), $\leq 8\%$ (LV) | % |
| Current THD | THD$_I$ | $\sqrt{\sum_{h \geq 2} I_h^2} \;/\; I_1$ | Per IEEE 519 | % |
| TDD | TDD | $\sqrt{\sum_{h \geq 2} I_h^2} \;/\; I_L$ | $\leq 5\% – 20\%$ (per $I_{sc}/I_L$) | % |
| Individual HD | IHD$_h$ | $X_h / X_1 \times 100$ | $\leq 3\%$ (typical) | % |
| Crest Factor | CF | $X_{\text{peak}} / X_{\text{RMS}}$ | $1.414$ (sine); $2.0$ (rectifier) | — |
| Pst (10 min) | Pst | $\sqrt{0.0314 P_{0.1} + \cdots + 0.08 P_{50s}}$ | $\leq 1.0$ | pu |
| Plt (2 hr) | Plt | $\left(\tfrac{1}{12}\sum \text{Pst}_i^3\right)^{1/3}$ | $\leq 0.65 – 0.8$ | pu |
| DC-Link Ripple | $\Delta V_{\text{dc}}$ | $I_{\text{load}}/(2 \omega_1 C_{\text{dc}})$ | $\leq 2\%$ of $V_{\text{dc}}$ | V |
| DC Injection Limit | $I_{\text{DC}}$ | Per IEEE 1547 | $\leq 0.5\%$ of rated output | A |
| LVRT Duration | $t_{\text{LVRT}}$ | 0.15 s @ 0% retained voltage | CBIP / IEEE 1547 | s |
| Frequency Operating Range | $f$ | 47.5 – 52 Hz (India) | IEGC | Hz |

### Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are indispensable in: **(i) harmonic filter design** (passive, active, hybrid), **(ii) flickermeter implementation per IEC 61000-4-15, (iii) inverter control tuning**, **(iv) grid-code compliance certification** for RE plants, and **(v) PQ auditor reports** submitted to electricity regulatory commissions (e.g., KSERC, CERC).

---

## 2.8 Sources of Harmonics in RE Systems

- **PWM Inverters:** Output contains sideband harmonics around switching frequency $f_{sw}$ — primarily $f_{sw} \pm 3f_1$, $2f_{sw} \pm f_1$, etc.
- **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) Chattering:** Causes low-order harmonic currents around the MPPT ripple frequency.
- **DC-DC Boost Converter ripple** propagated through DC-link.
- **Transformer inrush currents** (during energization) — rich in 2nd, 3rd, 4th harmonics.
- **Background grid harmonics** amplified through resonance with RE plant's capacitor bank.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion from Fourier Coefficients

**Objective:** Express the RMS value of a distorted waveform in terms of its fundamental and harmonic RMS components, and derive THD.

### Step 1 — Definition of RMS for a Periodic Waveform

For any periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T$:

$$X_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Parseval's Theorem (Power Orthogonality)

Using $x(t) = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \sqrt{2}\, X_h \sin(h \omega_0 t + \phi_h)$, and substituting into the RMS definition:

$$X_{\text{RMS}}^2 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \left[\sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \sqrt{2}\, X_h \sin(h \omega_0 t + \phi_h)\right]^2 dt$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Square and Apply Orthogonality

When the integral of products of harmonics of different orders is taken over a full period, the cross terms vanish (orthogonality of sinusoids). Only the squared terms survive:

$$X_{\text{RMS}}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 2\, X_h^2 \sin^2(h\omega_0 t + \phi_h)\, dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral

Using the identity $\int_0^T \sin^2(h\omega_0 t + \phi_h)\, dt = T/2$:

$$X_{\text{RMS}}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} 2\, X_h^2 \cdot \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} X_h^2$$

### Step 5 — Separate Fundamental and Harmonic Parts

$$X_{\text{RMS}}^2 = X_1^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} X_h^2$$

### Step 6 — Define Harmonic RMS Content

$$\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} X_h^2} = \sqrt{X_{\text{RMS}}^2 - X_1^2}$$

### Step 7 — Apply the THD Definition

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} X_h^2}}{X_1} = \frac{\sqrt{X_{\text{RMS}}^2 - X_1^2}}{X_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic of Each Step:**
> Step 1 provides the RMS definition. Step 2 substitutes the Fourier expansion. Step 3 exploits orthogonality, meaning harmonics of different orders do not exchange power — only the fundamental interacts with itself. Step 4 evaluates the squared-sine integral using the well-known identity $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$, whose average over one period is $1/2$. Step 5 isolates the fundamental from harmonics. Step 6 rearranges algebraically. Step 7 applies the THD ratio definition directly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pst from Instantaneous Flicker Level

**Objective:** Show how the short-term flicker severity index (Pst) is built from percentile statistics of the instantaneous flicker sensation $P_{inst}(t)$.

### Step 1 — Flickermeter Block Output

The IEC 61000-4-15 flickermeter outputs an **instantaneous flicker sensation** $S(t)$ (normalized), where $S = 1$ corresponds to the **threshold of perception**.

### Step 2 — Statistical Analysis over 10 Minutes

Over a 10-min interval, the cumulative distribution function $F(S)$ is formed. Percentile levels are extracted:

- $P_{0.1}$ = level exceeded for **0.1%** of the time
- $P_{1s}$ = level exceeded for **1%**
- $P_{3s}$ = level exceeded for **3%**
- $P_{10s}$ = level exceeded for **10%**
- $P_{50s}$ = level exceeded for **50%**

### Step 3 — Weighted Sum (the Pst Formula)

The standard equation combines these five percentiles with empirical weights derived from human-factor studies:

$$\text{Pst} = \sqrt{0.0314\, P_{0.1} + 0.0525\, P_{1s} + 0.0657\, P_{3s} + 0.28\, P_{10s} + 0.08\, P_{50s}}$$

### Step 4 — Weighted Cube-Root Averaging for Plt

Over 2 hours, twelve Pst values are cubed, averaged, and cube-rooted (mimicking the **Steeper-Summation Law** of human irritation):

$$\text{Plt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{12}\sum_{i=1}^{12} \text{Pst}_i^{\,3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The cube (in Plt) weights infrequent but high-magnitude flicker events more heavily, since human reaction is not linear but follows a **power-law sensitivity**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: THD Calculation

**Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz supply voltage has the following harmonic spectrum at a residential PCC:

| Harmonic order $h$ | 1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 | 11 |
|---------------------|---|---|---|---|---|----|
| RMS Voltage (V) | 230 | 8 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 1 |

**Find:** THD$_V$, IHD of the 5th harmonic, and Crest Factor (assume $V_{\text{peak}} = 340$ V).

### Solution

#### Step 1 — Compute Harmonic RMS Sum

$$\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2 = 8^2 + 6^2 + 4^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 = 64 + 36 + 16 + 4 + 1 = 121\ \text{V}^2$$

$$\sqrt{\sum V_h^2} = \sqrt{121} = 11\ \text{V}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute THD$_V$

$$\text{THD}_V = \frac{11}{230} = 0.04783 = 4.78\,\%$$

#### Step 3 — Compute IHD of 5th Harmonic

$$\text{IHD}_5 = \frac{V_5}{V_1} \times 100 = \frac{6}{230} \times 100 = 2.61\,\%$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Total RMS Voltage

$$V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum_{h \geq 2} V_h^2} = \sqrt{230^2 + 121} = \sqrt{52900 + 121} = \sqrt{53021} \approx 230.26\ \text{V}$$

#### Step 5 — Compute Crest Factor

$$\text{CF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{RMS}}} = \frac{340}{230.26} = 1.477$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A CF slightly above 1.414 confirms mild peaky distortion. THD$_V$ of 4.78% lies within the IEEE 519 limit of 8% for LV systems. IHD$_5$ of 2.61% is within the 3% individual limit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Harmonic Limits per IEEE 519-2014

| **Bus Voltage $V$ at PCC** | **Individual Harmonic Limit** | **THD$_V$ Limit** | **TDD Limit** |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| $V \leq 1$ kV | **5.0%** | **8.0%** | **5.0% – 20.0%** (per $I_{sc}/I_L$) |
| $1$ kV $< V \leq 69$ kV | **3.0%** | **5.0%** | **2.5% – 15.0%** |
| $69$ kV $< V \leq 161$ kV | **1.5%** | **2.5%** | **1.0% – 7.5%** |
| $V > 161$ kV | **1.0%** | **1.5%** | **1.0% – 5.0%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** As voltage level increases, harmonic tolerance **decreases** because higher-voltage buses serve more consumers and have lower inherent impedance — distortion travels further.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Grid-Connected RE System with PQ Disturbance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Primary Energy Source]
        windA[Wind Turbine]
        solarA[PV Array]
    end

    subgraph CONV[Power Conditioning Unit]
        rectA[Rectifier DC-DC Converter]
        invA[PWM Voltage Source Inverter]
        filtA[LC LCL Filter]
        ctrlA[PLL MPPT Controller]
    end

    src1[Point of Common Coupling PCC]
    gridA[Utility Grid 50 Hz]
    loadA[Local Linear Nonlinear Loads]
    meterA[PQ Meter Harmonics Flicker THD]

    windA --> rectA
    solarA --> rectA
    rectA --> invA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> invA
    invA --> filtA
    filtA --> src1
    src1 --> gridA
    src1 --> loadA
    src1 --> meterA

    classDef source fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef convert fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class windA,solarA source
    class rectA,invA,filtA,ctrlA convert
    class src1,gridA,loadA,meterA grid
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
The diagram traces the path of disturbances from RE source → DC-DC converter → PWM inverter → filter → PCC → grid. The controller block modulates the inverter's switching to manage DC-link voltage and MPPT, while injecting harmonics that the filter must attenuate. The PQ meter block represents the measurement infrastructure (THD, Pst, DC injection) used for compliance reporting.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Operating Conflicts Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    conflicts[Operating Conflicts RE vs Utility Grid]

    conflicts --> c1[Anti Islanding vs Continued Generation]
    conflicts --> c2[LVRT HVRT vs Inverter Trip]
    conflicts --> c3[Reactive Power Support vs MPPT]
    conflicts --> c4[Harmonic Limits vs Switching Scheme]
    conflicts --> c5[Frequency Response vs Inertia Less Inverter]

    c1 --> c1a[IEEE 1547 2 sec Disconnect]
    c1 --> c1b[Intentional Islanding Microgrid]

    c2 --> c2a[CBIP LVRT 0.15 sec 0 percent Voltage]
    c2 --> c2b[Chopper Braking Resistor]

    c3 --> c3a[STATCOM SVC Reactive Source]
    c3 --> c3b[Inverter Q Capability Curve]

    c4 --> c4a[LCL Filter Tuning]
    c4 --> c4b[Active Filter APF]

    c5 --> c5a[Virtual Inertia emulated]
    c5 --> c5b[Droop Control Grid Following vs Forming]

    classDef root fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class conflicts root
    class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5,c1a,c1b,c2a,c2b,c3a,c3b,c4a,c4b,c5a,c5b leaf
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
This taxonomy enumerates the five principal operating conflicts. Each conflict branches into two mitigation strategies: a regulatory/code-based solution and an engineering/control-based solution. For example, anti-islanding conflict splits into **(a) IEEE 1547 mandated 2-second disconnect** (regulatory) and **(b) intentional islanding for microgrids** (engineering choice).

---

## 4.3 Flowchart — Harmonic Distortion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nonlinear[Nonlinear Load RE Inverter Arc Furnace] -->|draws non sinusoidal current| network[Power Network]
    network -->|series impedance Z| pcc[PCC Voltage Distortion]
    pcc -->|propagates to| consumers[Other Consumers]
    pcc -->|resonance with| cap[Capacitor Bank]
    pcc -->|measured by| standards[THD TDD IEEE 519 IEC 61000]

    classDef node1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef node2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef node3 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#333,color:#000
```
