However, **a further symmetry** of the 6-pulse waveform (3-pulse rotation per half-cycle) **nulls all harmonics that are *not* of the form $n = 6k \pm 1$**. A detailed proof of this requires analysing the *triple*-pulse structure; the result (without loss of rigour for the KTU exam) is:

$$
\boxed{\,I_h = \dfrac{I_1}{h}, \quad h = 6k \pm 1,\; k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\,}
$$

with $I_1 = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\, I_d$ for the 6-pulse idealised case (large $L_d$).

## 3.3 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of the 6-Pulse Current

$$
\text{THD}_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^{\,2}}}{I_1} = \frac{\sqrt{I_5^{2} + I_7^{2} + I_{11}^{2} + I_{13}^{2} + \cdots}}{I_1}
$$

Substituting $I_h = I_1 / h$ for $h = 5, 7, 11, 13, \ldots$:

$$
\text{THD}_I = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5^{2}} + \frac{1}{7^{2}} + \frac{1}{11^{2}} + \frac{1}{13^{2}} + \frac{1}{17^{2}} + \frac{1}{19^{2}} + \cdots}
$$

Computing the series term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= 1/25 = 0.040000 \\
\text{Term 2} &= 1/49 = 0.020408 \\
\text{Term 3} &= 1/121 = 0.008264 \\
\text{Term 4} &= 1/169 = 0.005917 \\
\text{Term 5} &= 1/289 = 0.003460 \\
\text{Term 6} &= 1/361 = 0.002770 \\
\text{Term 7} &= 1/529 = 0.001890 \\
\text{Term 8} &= 1/841 = 0.001189 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing the infinite series converges to approximately $\mathbf{0.0887}$. Therefore:

$$
\text{THD}_I = \sqrt{0.0887} = 0.2978 \;\;\text{(p.u.)} = \mathbf{29.78\,\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 6-pulse thyristor bridge always has a current THD of approximately **30 %**, *independent* of the firing angle $\alpha$. This is a fundamental KTU fact — increasing $\alpha$ shifts energy from the fundamental to the harmonics, but the THD remains constant in the ideal, ripple-free case. This is why **passive harmonic filters tuned to the 5th and 7th harmonic** are standard in industrial installations.

## 3.4 Special Case — Single-Phase Full-Bridge (Square Wave Input)

For a single-phase diode bridge with a large DC-side inductance, the input current is an *ideal* $180^\circ$ square wave of amplitude $I_d$:

$$
b_n = \frac{4 I_d}{n\pi},\quad n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_n = \frac{4 I_d}{n \pi}
$$

$$
\text{THD}_{\text{square}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3^{2}} + \frac{1}{5^{2}} + \frac{1}{7^{2}} + \cdots} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} - 1} \approx \mathbf{48.34\,\%}
$$

This is a **classic KTU 2-mark derivation**: "Show that the THD of a square wave is 48.3 %."

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — 6-Pulse vs 12-Pulse vs PWM

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{6-pulse} &: \quad h = 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, \ldots;\quad \text{THD} \approx 29.8\,\% \\
\text{12-pulse} &: \quad h = 11, 13, 23, 25, \ldots;\quad \text{THD} \approx 14.5\,\% \\
\text{PWM (high }m_f\text{)} &: \quad h \approx m_f \pm 2,\, m_f \pm 4, \ldots;\quad \text{THD} < 5\,\% \text{ at PCC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **12-pulse configuration** is obtained by cascading two 6-pulse bridges through a **$\Delta - Y$ phase-shifting transformer** (introducing a $30^\circ$ phase shift). The two sets of $h = 6k \pm 1$ harmonics from each bridge *cancel* in the primary, leaving only $h = 12k \pm 1$. This is the standard HVDC harmonic-reduction technique.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Switching-Device Harmonic Sources

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** from the AC supply through various switching-device topologies to the resulting harmonic spectrum. It is the conceptual map most KTU examiners expect students to draw in Part-B answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACSupply[AC Mains 50 Hz Sinusoidal Source] --> DevCat{Device Category}

    DevCat --> Diode[Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    DevCat --> Thyristor[Thyristor Phase Controlled Bridge]
    DevCat --> IGBT[PWM Inverter IGBT or MOSFET]
    DevCat --> Chopper[Chopper DC to DC]
    DevCat --> Cyclo[Cyclo Converter AC to AC]

    Diode --> DiodeWave[Quasi Square Input Current]
    DiodeWave --> DiodeHarm[Harmonics 3rd 5th 7th THD 80 to 100 percent]

    Thyristor --> ThyWave[120 Degree Quasi Square Pulse]
    ThyWave --> ThyHarm[Harmonics 6k plus minus 1 THD approx 30 percent]

    IGBT --> PwmWave[High Frequency PWM Chopped Current]
    PwmWave --> PwmHarm[Sidebands around f sw Low order negligible THD less than 5 percent]

    Chopper --> ChopWave[DC Side Ripple Reflected to AC]
    ChopWave --> ChopHarm[Switching Frequency Multiples EMI]

    Cyclo --> CycloWave[Modulated AC Output]
    CycloWave --> CycloHarm[Sub Harmonics and Inter Harmonics below 50 Hz]

    DiodeHarm --> Mitigation[Harmonic Mitigation]
    ThyHarm --> Mitigation
    PwmHarm --> Mitigation
    ChopHarm --> Mitigation
    CycloHarm --> Mitigation

    Mitigation --> Filter[Passive L C Filter Tuned to 5th 7th]
    Mitigation --> PhaseShift[12 Pulse Phase Shift Transformer]
    Mitigation --> Active[Active Power Filter APF]
    Mitigation --> Pfc[Power Factor Correction Stage]
```

## 4.2 Six-Pulse Converter Conduction Sequence (Block Map)

The diagram below illustrates the **rotation of conduction** among the six thyristors and the corresponding *120° pulse-train* that forms the line current — the key waveform whose Fourier series was derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PhaseA[Phase A Line Current i a]
        P1[plus I d 30 to 150 deg] --> P2[zero 150 to 210 deg] --> P3[minus I d 210 to 330 deg] --> P4[zero 330 to 360 deg plus 30 deg]
    end

    subgraph Devices[Thyristor Conduction Order]
        D1[T1 conducts] --> D2[T3 conducts] --> D3[T5 conducts] --> D4[T4 conducts] --> D5[T6 conducts] --> D6[T2 conducts] --> D1
    end

    P1 -.-> D1
    P3 -.-> D4
```

## 4.3 Harmonic Order Visualisation

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Fundamental 50 Hz Amplitude 100 percent]
    H5[5th Harmonic 250 Hz Amplitude 20 percent]
    H7[7th Harmonic 350 Hz Amplitude 14.3 percent]
    H11[11th Harmonic 550 Hz Amplitude 9.1 percent]
    H13[13th Harmonic 650 Hz Amplitude 7.7 percent]

    F1 --> H5 --> H7 --> H11 --> H13
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU ESE, examiners often ask students to *sketch the harmonic spectrum* of a 6-pulse converter as a bar chart with amplitude $\propto 1/h$. The bar heights follow the sequence **1.00, 0.20, 0.143, 0.091, 0.077, …** for $h = 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, \ldots$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part-A question carries **3 marks** and expects a precise 4–6 line answer. Total marks per question = 3. Total time allocation per question ≈ 6 minutes.

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three power-electronic switching devices that act as sources of current harmonics, and state the dominant harmonic order produced by each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Diode-bridge rectifier** (used in SMPS, UPS): produces a quasi-square input current whose dominant harmonics are the **3rd, 5th and 7th** (THD ≈ 80–100 % at light load).
2. **Thyristor (SCR) phase-controlled bridge** (used in DC drives, HVDC): produces a 120° quasi-square current with characteristic harmonics of order $\mathbf{h = 6k \pm 1}$ — i.e., 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, … (THD ≈ 30 %).
3. **PWM inverter using IGBTs** (used in VFDs, solar inverters): shifts harmonic energy to the switching frequency sidebands; low-order harmonics are negligible, THD at the PCC is **< 5 %** with proper filtering.

*(1 mark per device + harmonic order; 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why does a 6-pulse thyristor bridge produce only harmonics of order $6k \pm 1$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 6-pulse thyristor bridge has three key symmetries:
- **Half-wave symmetry:** the positive and negative halves of the line current are mirror images $\Rightarrow$ **only odd harmonics** exist.
- **Three-pulse rotation per half-cycle:** the conduction pattern repeats three times in $180^\circ$, meaning any harmonic of order $n$ not satisfying $n = 6k \pm 1$ has zero Fourier coefficient by the **rotational symmetry theorem** of Fourier series.
- **Phase-shift symmetry of the 3-phase set:** harmonics that *are* present in one phase have a $120^\circ$ phase progression, which forces the *triplen* harmonics ($h = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$) to circulate in the zero-sequence and not appear in the line-to-line quantities.

Hence the surviving harmonic orders are $h = 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, \ldots = 6k \pm 1$ *(2 marks for the reasoning, 1 mark for stating the formula)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either A or B). Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. The expected answer length is **6–8 pages** with full mathematical rigour and at least one labelled diagram.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the Fourier series of the input line current of a **6-pulse, 3-phase thyristor bridge rectifier** feeding a highly inductive DC load. Show that only harmonics of order $h = 6k \pm 1$ exist and that $I_h = I_1 / h$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pulse thyristor rectifier draws a fundamental line current of $I_1 = 200\,\text{A}$ (RMS). Calculate the RMS magnitude of the 5th, 7th, 11th and 13th harmonic currents and the **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** in %. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Waveform statement (1 mark):** The line current $i_a(\theta)$ of a 6-pulse thyristor bridge is a $120^\circ$ quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_d$, repeating with period $2\pi$.

$$
i_a(\theta) = I_d \text{ for } \pi/6 + \alpha \le \theta \le 5\pi/6 + \alpha; \quad i_a(\theta) = -I_d \text{ for } 7\pi/6 + \alpha \le \theta \le 11\pi/6 + \alpha; \quad \text{else } 0
$$

2. **Fourier series setup (1 mark):** Using the complex-exponential form,
$C_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} i_a(\theta)\, e^{-jn\theta}\, d\theta$.

3. **Integration (2 marks):** Carry out the two integrals as shown in Section 3.2, Steps 1–4.

4. **Symmetry arguments (2 marks):**
   - Half-wave symmetry $\Rightarrow$ only **odd** harmonics.
   - 3-pulse rotational symmetry in each half-cycle $\Rightarrow$ **only $n = 6k \pm 1$** survives.
   - Conclusion: $I_h = I_1 / h$ for $h = 6k \pm 1$.

5. **Final expression and fundamental calculation (1 mark):**
   $I_1 = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\, I_d$ (ideal, large $L_d$).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given $I_1 = 200\,\text{A}$ (RMS).

$$
I_h = \frac{I_1}{h}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_5 &= \frac{200}{5} = \mathbf{40.0\;A} \\
I_7 &= \frac{200}{7} = \mathbf{28.57\;A} \\
I_{11} &= \frac{200}{11} = \mathbf{18.18\;A} \\
I_{13} &= \frac{200}{13} = \mathbf{15.38\;A}
\end{aligned}
$$

THD calculation:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{I_5^{2} + I_7^{2} + I_{11}^{2} + I_{13}^{2} + \cdots}}{I_1}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{I_5^{2} + I_7^{2} + I_{11}^{2} + I_{13}^{2}} &= \sqrt{40^{2} + 28.57^{2} + 18.18^{2} + 15.38^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1600 + 816.3 + 330.5 + 236.5} \\
&= \sqrt{2983.3} \approx 54.62 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{54.62}{200} \times 100\,\% = \mathbf{27.31\,\%}
$$

> **Note (1 mark):** Including higher-order harmonics ($h = 17, 19, 23, \ldots$) raises the THD to the theoretical limit of **≈ 29.78 %**. The 4-term approximation is acceptable for KTU ESE and yields **27.31 %** (within 2 % of the exact value).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a circuit diagram, how a **6-pulse thyristor bridge rectifier** acts as a harmonic current source. Identify the characteristic harmonics it produces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **12-pulse converter** is constructed by cascading two 6-pulse bridges through a $\Delta - Y$ phase-shifting transformer. With the help of Fourier analysis, show that the 5th and 7th harmonics are eliminated on the primary side and compute the resulting THD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Circuit description (2 marks):** Draw the 6-pulse thyristor bridge (T1–T6) connected to a 3-phase AC supply, feeding DC load $R_L$ with smoothing inductor $L_d$. Each thyristor conducts for $120^\circ$; commutation occurs every $60^\circ$.

2. **Waveform description (2 marks):** The line current is a $120^\circ$ quasi-square wave (amplitude $\pm I_d$, zero for $60^\circ$ in between). Sketch the waveform showing the +ve and -ve pulses.

3. **Harmonic identification (2 marks):** By Fourier analysis, characteristic harmonics are $h = 6k \pm 1 = 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, \ldots$ with magnitudes $I_h = I_1 / h$.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The bridge draws non-sinusoidal current from the mains, acting as a *current harmonic source*. Magnitude of dominant harmonic is 20 % of fundamental (5th).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Phase-shift principle (2 marks):** A $\Delta - Y$ transformer introduces a $30^\circ$ phase shift between the two bridges. Bridge 1 (Y-side) draws harmonics $5\angle 0^\circ, 7\angle 0^\circ, 11\angle 0^\circ, 13\angle 0^\circ$. Bridge 2 (Δ-side) draws $5\angle -150^\circ, 7\angle +150^\circ, 11\angle -330^\circ, 13\angle +330^\circ$ in the *primary* reference.

2. **Cancellation of 5th and 7th (2 marks):** On the primary winding, the 5th harmonics of the two bridges are $150^\circ$ out of phase $\Rightarrow$ they **cancel**. The 7th harmonics also cancel. The 11th and 13th harmonics *add* (they are in phase on the primary).

3. **Surviving harmonics (2 marks):** $h = 12k \pm 1 = 11, 13, 23, 25, \ldots$

4. **THD calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{THD} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{11^{2}} + \frac{1}{13^{2}} + \frac{1}{23^{2}} + \frac{1}{25^{2}} + \cdots} = \sqrt{0.00826 + 0.00592 + 0.00189 + 0.00160 + \cdots} \approx \sqrt{0.0208} \approx \mathbf{14.4\,\%}
$$

> This is the standard **HVDC harmonic-reduction** technique, and the 14.4 % THD is why 12-pulse converters are universally used in modern HVDC stations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Switching-Device Harmonic Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit** (A or %): −0.5 mark.
> 2. **Writing THD in per-unit (0.30) instead of percentage (30 %):** −0.5 mark. KTU 2024 scheme *requires* the % sign.
> 3. **Using the formula $I_h = I_1/h$ for a *non-square* waveform** (e.g., a triangle or trapezoidal PWM current): −1 mark. This formula is valid **only for ideal square / quasi-square** switching waveforms.
> 4. **Stating "5th and 7th harmonics" for a 12-pulse converter:** −2 marks. The 5th and 7th *cancel*; only $12k \pm 1$ remain.
> 5. **Not labelling axes on the harmonic spectrum bar chart:** −1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up $I_1$ (peak) and $I_1$ (RMS):** −1 mark. Always state clearly whether the fundamental is peak or RMS; KTU usually expects RMS for THD computation.
> 7. **Skipping the symmetry argument** for $h = 6k \pm 1$ derivation: −1 mark. The examiner allocates 1–2 marks explicitly for "stating the symmetries used."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Harmonic Sources: Switching Devices (Module 2)**

- ☐ Switching devices are the **most prevalent harmonic source** in modern low-voltage and industrial distribution networks.
- ☐ The general characteristic harmonic order is $\mathbf{h = kp \pm 1}$ where $p$ = pulse number of the converter.
- ☐ For a **6-pulse bridge**: $h = 5, 7, 11, 13, \ldots$ with $I_h = I_1 / h$ and $\text{THD} \approx \mathbf{29.8\,\%}$.
- ☐ For a **12-pulse bridge**: $h = 11, 13, 23, 25, \ldots$ with $\text{THD} \approx \mathbf{14.4\,\%}$.
- ☐ For an **ideal square wave** (single-phase diode bridge): odd harmonics only, $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\pi^{2}/8 - 1} \approx \mathbf{48.34\,\%}$.
- ☐ **Diode rectifiers** (SMPS) → THD 80–100 %; **SCR bridges** (DC drives) → THD ≈ 30 %; **PWM inverters** (VFD) → THD < 5 % with filter.
- ☐ **Symmetry rules:** half-wave symmetry $\Rightarrow$ only odd harmonics; quarter-wave symmetry $\Rightarrow$ only sine/cosine terms; rotational symmetry of 6-pulse $\Rightarrow h = 6k \pm 1$.
- ☐ THD formula: $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum I_h^{2}}}{I_1}$; TDD uses rated load current $I_L$ (per IEEE 519).
- ☐ **Mitigation techniques** (most-favoured KTU 14-mark sub-question): passive L-C filter, 12/24-pulse phase-shift transformer, active power filter (APF), and PFC front-end.
- ☐ PWM inverters shift harmonic energy to $f_{\text{sw}}$ sidebands — switching frequency should be **> 20× fundamental** for effective filtering.
- ☐ Sub-harmonics and inter-harmonics arise from **cyclo-converters and arc furnaces**, not from standard 6/12-pulse rectifiers.
- ☐ **IEEE 519 limits** (must memorise for KTU 2024): individual harmonic voltage $\le 5\,\%$, TDD current $\le 5$–$8\,\%$ (depending on $I_{\text{sc}}/I_L$ ratio).
- ☐ Drawing the **harmonic spectrum bar chart** correctly is worth **1–2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arcing Devices and Saturable Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arcing Devices and Saturable Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Arcing Devices — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arcing Devices** are non-linear electrical loads that intentionally create and sustain an **electric arc** as part of their normal operating principle. The arc is a self-sustaining, low-voltage, high-current plasma discharge across an air gap in which the voltage-current (V-I) characteristic is highly non-linear and time-varying.

In the KTU 2024 Power Quality framework, arcing devices are classified as a major sub-category of **non-linear loads** that pollute the supply network with **harmonics, interharmonics, sub-harmonics, voltage flicker, and unbalance**.

### Common Engineering Examples

| Device | Arc Mechanism | Typical Operating Power |
|--------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **AC Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** | Graphite electrodes melt scrap steel | **5 MW to 100+ MW** |
| **DC Electric Arc Furnace** | Single graphite cathode + conductive hearth | **10 MW to 80 MW** |
| **Arc Welder (Transformer Type)** | Striking/maintaining welding arc | **2 kW to 50 kW** |
| **Arc Lamp / Searchlight** | Carbon electrodes in air | **1 kW to 20 kW** |
| **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps** | Mercury/Sodium vapour arc | **35 W to 1000 W** |
| **Fluorescent Lamps (Magnetic Ballast)** | Arc in low-pressure mercury vapour | **20 W to 200 W** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Water-Pinched Hose Analogy):**
> Imagine a garden hose where you repeatedly squeeze and release the opening. Each time you release, water gushes out in irregular spurts — never in a smooth, continuous stream. An arc behaves similarly: the current "gushes" through the ionized air gap in pulses, never sinusoidally. Because the electrical load the utility "sees" keeps changing in a non-sinusoidal fashion, the supply voltage itself gets distorted, affecting every other customer on the same feeder.

### The Arc — Physical Origin

An electric arc is a **self-sustaining gaseous conduction phenomenon** with temperatures of **3,000 °C to 30,000 °C**. The arc's resistance $R_{arc}$ depends on:
- **Arc length** $l_{arc}$
- **Arc current** $i_{arc}$
- **Electrode material and geometry**
- **Ionization state of the surrounding gas**

A widely used empirical relation is **Ayrton's Equation** for arc voltage:

$$V_{arc} = A + B \cdot l_{arc} + \frac{C + D \cdot l_{arc}}{i_{arc}}$$

where $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ are constants dependent on the electrode material and gas medium. The **negative slope** of the $\frac{C+D \cdot l_{arc}}{i_{arc}}$ term in the **constant-power region** confirms the highly non-linear behaviour.

> [!TIP]
> KTU Examiner Insight: Drawing the **V-I characteristic of an arc** (a hyperbolic curve) and labelling the three operating regions (ohmic, negative resistance, and external-arc regions) almost always scores full marks in a 14-mark question on arcing devices.

---

## 1.2 Saturable Devices — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saturable Devices** are electromagnetic devices whose ferromagnetic core is deliberately or incidentally driven into the **saturation region** of its B-H (flux density vs. magnetic field intensity) characteristic. Once saturated, the incremental permeability drops sharply, magnetizing current spikes occur, and the current waveform becomes distorted (non-sinusoidal) even when the applied voltage is perfectly sinusoidal.

### The Physics of Saturation

A ferromagnetic core follows the **B-H curve** (magnetization curve). In the **linear region**, $B = \mu H$ where $\mu$ is constant. Beyond the **knee point**, further increases in $H$ produce negligible increases in $B$. The flux can no longer "absorb" more magnetization, and additional magnetomotive force (MMF) must be supplied as **harmonic-rich current**.

### Common Engineering Examples

| Device | Saturation Mechanism |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Power Transformer (Inrush/Saturation)** | Transient core saturation at energization or during overvoltage |
| **Ferro-resonant Transformer (CVT)** | Intentional core saturation to provide voltage regulation |
| **Saturated Iron-Core Reactor** | Used in arc-furnace flicker compensation (SVC) |
| **Magnetic Amplifier (Saturable Reactor)** | Control element in legacy power electronics |
| **Choke Ballast (Fluorescent Lamps)** | Magnetic ballast operated near saturation |
| **Neutral Grounding Reactor (NGR)** | Operates near saturation to handle high inrush |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Overflowing Sponge Analogy):**
> Picture a sponge absorbing water. Initially, it soaks up every drop with ease (linear region). As it gets saturated, additional water simply runs off the surface — the sponge cannot absorb any more. A ferromagnetic core behaves the same way: once "full" of magnetic flux, any extra magnetizing force (current) cannot create more flux and instead manifests as a **spike of harmonic current**. This is why saturated transformers are notorious for producing 3rd, 5th, 7th harmonics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception (frequently penalized in KTU exams):**
> Students often assume that a transformer operating at "normal" rated voltage is not saturable. In reality, even healthy transformers experience brief saturation during **energization transients** (inrush), **geomagnetic disturbances**, or following **external fault clearing**. Thus, "saturable" is a *condition*, not a *device type* per se.

---

## 1.3 Why KTU 2024 Emphasizes This Topic

The 2024 Scheme **Course Outcomes (CO2 / CO3)** of PEEET751 explicitly require students to:
1. Identify the source of common power-quality disturbances in industrial systems.
2. Analyze the harmonic and flicker signatures of non-linear loads.
3. Recommend **mitigation techniques** for arcing and saturable devices.

This topic carries an average weightage of **12–15% of ESE Module marks** in recent KTU university examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arcing Devices — Power Quality Impact

### 2.1.1 Voltage Flicker

Arc furnaces are the **single largest source of voltage flicker** in modern power systems. The flicker arises because the arc length varies randomly as scrap metal shifts under the electrodes.

**Flicker Severity (IEC 61000-4-15 standard)**:

The **short-term flicker severity** $P_{st}$ is computed by passing the measured voltage through a chain of filters that simulate:
1. The **lamp-eye-brain response** (a 60 W incandescent bulb, the human visual system).
2. Statistical analysis of the resulting instantaneous flicker sensation.

$$P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314 P_{0.1} + 0.0525 P_{1} + 0.0657 P_{3} + 0.28 P_{10} + 0.08 P_{50}}$$

where $P_x$ are the percentiles of the instantaneous flicker level $F(t)$ over a **10-minute** observation window. The corresponding **long-term severity** $P_{lt}$ uses 2-hour observations:

$$P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_{st,k}^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Limits (IEC 61000-3-7 / IEEE 1453):**
> - $P_{st} \leq 1.0$ (planning level for HV/MV)
> - $P_{lt} \leq 0.8$ (planning level for HV/MV)
> - $P_{st} \leq 0.9$ (emission limit for individual customers)

### 2.1.2 Harmonic & Interharmonic Generation

Arc furnaces produce:
- **Integer harmonics** (2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th…) during stable arc conditions
- **Interharmonics** (non-integer multiples of fundamental) during the scrap-melting phase
- **Sub-harmonics** (frequencies below fundamental) that can excite **subsynchronous torsional interactions** in turbo-generators

### 2.1.3 Voltage Notching and Unbalance

Three-phase arc furnaces are inherently **unbalanced** because each phase has its own independently controlled electrode. Phase-to-phase voltage unbalance of **2–5%** is typical.

---

## 2.2 Saturable Devices — Power Quality Impact

### 2.2.1 Harmonic Spectrum from a Saturated Transformer

The magnetizing current of a saturated transformer can be expanded via **Fourier series**. Because the saturation is symmetric (the positive and negative half-cycles of $B$ are identical), only **odd harmonics** appear in steady state:

$$i_m(t) = I_0 + \sum_{n=1,3,5,7,...}^{\infty} I_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

The relative harmonic content is approximately:

| Harmonic Order | Magnitude (\% of fundamental) |
|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1st (Fundamental) | **100 \%** |
| 3rd | **30 – 60 \%** |
| 5th | **10 – 25 \%** |
| 7th | **3 – 10 \%** |
| 9th | **1 – 5 \%** |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **3-phase transformer with delta primary** (or ungrounded-wye), the 3rd-harmonic currents circulate in the delta and **do not appear on the line** — this is why delta primaries are used as harmonic traps.

### 2.2.2 Transformer Inrush Current

During energization, residual flux plus switching angle drives the core deep into saturation. The inrush waveform is **half-wave asymmetric**, producing:
- **DC component** (up to 60–80% of peak)
- **2nd harmonic** (up to 50% of fundamental)
- **3rd, 4th, 5th harmonics**

The peak inrush current is typically **6 to 8 times** the rated full-load current and can persist for several seconds.

### 2.2.3 Ferro-resonance

When a **saturable inductance** is in series with a **system capacitance** (e.g., cable capacitance, transformer capacitance, capacitor bank), a non-linear resonant condition called **ferro-resonance** can arise. It produces:
- Sustained **overvoltages** (2–5 pu)
- **Subharmonic** currents (typically 1/3 to 1/2 of fundamental)
- Severe **heat and acoustic noise**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU 2024 examiner expects for a 14-mark question on this topic. Memorize units and boundary conditions!

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Standard / Reference | Units |
|---|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Arc Voltage (Ayrton) | $V_{arc} = A + B \cdot l_{arc} + \frac{C + D \cdot l_{arc}}{i_{arc}}$ | Ayrton (1902) | V |
| 2 | Arc Resistance | $R_{arc} = \dfrac{V_{arc}}{i_{arc}}$ | Definition | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Voltage Flicker (quick estimate) | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta Q \cdot X_{sc}}{V^2} \times 100$ | Simplified | \% |
| 4 | Short-term Flicker Severity | $P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314 P_{0.1} + 0.0525 P_{1} + 0.0657 P_{3} + 0.28 P_{10} + 0.08 P_{50}}$ | IEC 61000-4-15 | unitless |
| 5 | Long-term Flicker Severity | $P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_{st,k}^{3}}$ | IEC 61000-4-15 | unitless |
| 6 | Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage) | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h}^{2}}}{V_{1}} \times 100$ | IEEE 519 | \% |
| 7 | Total Harmonic Distortion (Current) | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_{h}^{2}}}{I_{1}} \times 100$ | IEEE 519 | \% |
| 8 | Total Demand Distortion | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum I_{h}^{2}}}{I_{L}} \times 100$ | IEEE 519 | \% |
| 9 | Per-Phase Unbalance (NEMA) | $\%LU = \dfrac{\text{max deviation from avg}}{\text{avg of 3 phase voltages}} \times 100$ | NEMA MG-1 | \% |
| 10 | Zero-Sequence Unbalance | $V_0 = \dfrac{V_a + V_b + V_c}{3}$ | Symmetrical Components | V |
| 11 | Transformer Inrush Peak (approx) | $I_{peak} \approx \dfrac{V_{m}}{\omega L_{sat}} \cdot \left(1 + e^{-R t / L_{sat}}\right)$ | Standard Textbook | A |
| 12 | 2nd Harmonic in Inrush | $I_2 \approx 0.5 \cdot I_{peak}$ (typical) | Empirical | A |
| 13 | Per-Unit Saturation Voltage | $V_{sat,pu} = \dfrac{\omega N A_c B_{sat}}{V_{rated}}$ | Design Equation | pu |
| 14 | Active Power in Non-Linear Load | $P = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h I_h \cos(\phi_h)$ | Parseval's Theorem | W |
| 15 | Reactive Power Distortion (Budeanu) | $D = V_1 \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}$ | Budeanu (1927) | VAr |
| 16 | Inrush Decay Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{sat}}{R_{total}}$ | Transient Analysis | s |
| 17 | Short-Circuit Ratio | $SCR = \dfrac{S_{sc}}{S_{load}}$ | IEC 61000-3-7 | unitless |
| 18 | Harmonic Impedance of System | $Z_h = R_s + j \, h \cdot X_s$ | Network Theory | $\Omega$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Impact of Arcing/Saturable Devices | Mitigation Used |
|-------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------|
| **Steel Industry (EAF)** | Severe flicker, harmonics, unbalance | SVC, STATCOM, series reactors |
| **Welding Workshops** | Voltage dips, 3rd harmonic | Dedicated transformers, surge capacitors |
| **Commercial Lighting (HID)** | Harmonics from magnetic ballasts | Electronic ballasts |
| **HV Substations** | Ferro-resonance after switching | NGRs, surge arresters, damping resistors |
| **Wind Farms (Inrush)** | Transformer saturation during energization | Pre-insertion resistors, controlled switching |
| **Rail Traction** | Severe harmonics from saturable chokes | Active filters, 12-pulse rectifiers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Analysis of a Saturated Magnetizing Current

We model the **magnetizing current waveform** of a transformer in moderate saturation as a **flat-topped sinusoid** (the current is "clipped" on both peaks because the core cannot accept more flux).

### Step 1 — Assume the Waveform

Let the flat-topped magnetizing current be represented as:

$$
i_m(\theta) = 
\begin{cases}
I_p \cdot \sin(\theta), & 0 \le \theta \le \theta_1 \\
I_p \cdot \sin(\theta_1), & \theta_1 \le \theta \le \pi - \theta_1 \\
I_p \cdot \sin(\theta), & \pi - \theta_1 \le \theta \le \pi
\end{cases}
$$

where $\theta = \omega t$ and $\theta_1$ is the **saturation onset angle**. The waveform is **half-wave symmetric**, i.e., $i_m(\theta + \pi) = -i_m(\theta)$.

### Step 2 — State the Fourier Coefficients

For a half-wave symmetric, odd function (no DC, no even harmonics), only **sine terms of odd order** exist:

$$i_m(\theta) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} b_n \sin(n\theta)$$

The Fourier coefficient for the $n$-th harmonic is:

$$b_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} i_m(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$$

### Step 3 — Split the Integral

We split the integration over the three regions defined in Step 1:

$$b_n = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\theta_1} \sin(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta + \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} \sin(\theta_1) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta + \int_{\pi - \theta_1}^{\pi} \sin(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta \right]$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Integral

**Integral 1**: $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\theta_1} \sin(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$

Using the product-to-sum identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$\int_{0}^{\theta_1} \sin(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sin((1-n)\theta)}{1-n} - \frac{\sin((1+n)\theta)}{1+n}\right]_{0}^{\theta_1}$$

For $n \neq 1$:

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sin((1-n)\theta_1)}{1-n} - \frac{\sin((1+n)\theta_1)}{1+n}\right]$$

**Integral 2**: $\displaystyle \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} \sin(\theta_1) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$

$$= \sin(\theta_1) \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\theta)}{n} \right]_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} = \frac{\sin(\theta_1)}{n} \left[ \cos(n\theta_1) - \cos(n(\pi - \theta_1)) \right]$$

For **odd $n$**, $\cos(n(\pi - \theta_1)) = -\cos(n\theta_1)$, hence:

$$= \frac{\sin(\theta_1)}{n} \cdot 2\cos(n\theta_1) = \frac{2\sin(\theta_1)\cos(n\theta_1)}{n}$$

**Integral 3**: $\displaystyle \int_{\pi - \theta_1}^{\pi} \sin(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$

This is symmetric to Integral 1 (with $\theta \to \pi - \theta$), and for odd $n$, gives the **same result** as Integral 1.

### Step 5 — Combine the Results

Adding the three contributions:

$$b_n = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\sin((1-n)\theta_1)}{1-n} - \frac{\sin((1+n)\theta_1)}{1+n} + \frac{2\sin(\theta_1)\cos(n\theta_1)}{n} \right]$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example

Let the **saturation onset angle** be $\theta_1 = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, so $\sin(\theta_1) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

For the **fundamental** ($n = 1$):
The integral reduces to:

$$b_1 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \theta_1 - \frac{\sin(2\theta_1)}{2} + 2\sin(\theta_1) \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} \sin(\theta) \, d\theta \right]$$

After substituting $\theta_1 = \pi/3$ and evaluating:

$$b_1 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi}{3} - \frac{\sin(2\pi/3)}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos(\pi/3) \right]$$

$$b_1 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi}{3} - \frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{2} + \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

$$b_1 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi}{3} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right] = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \right]$$

$$b_1 \approx \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} (1.047 + 0.433) \approx \frac{2 I_p \times 1.480}{\pi} \approx 0.942 I_p$$

So the fundamental is approximately **94.2% of $I_p$**.

For the **3rd harmonic** ($n = 3$):

$$b_3 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\sin(-2\theta_1)}{-2} - \frac{\sin(4\theta_1)}{4} + \frac{2\sin(\theta_1)\cos(3\theta_1)}{3} \right]$$

With $\theta_1 = \pi/3$:
- $\sin(-2\pi/3) = -\sqrt{3}/2$, so first term = $-\sqrt{3}/4$
- $\sin(4\pi/3) = -\sqrt{3}/2$, so second term = $+\sqrt{3}/8$
- $\cos(3\pi/3) = \cos(\pi) = -1$, so third term = $-\frac{2 \cdot \sqrt{3}/2 \cdot 1}{3} = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$

$$b_3 = \frac{2 I_p}{\pi} \left[ -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \right]$$

$$b_3 = \frac{2 I_p \sqrt{3}}{\pi} \left[ -\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} - \frac{1}{3} \right] = \frac{2 I_p \sqrt{3}}{\pi} \left[ \frac{-6 + 3 - 8}{24} \right] = \frac{2 I_p \sqrt{3}}{\pi} \left[ \frac{-11}{24} \right]$$

$$b_3 \approx \frac{2 \times 1.732 \times (-0.458)}{3.1416} \cdot I_p \approx -0.505 I_p$$

The magnitude is approximately **50.5% of $I_p$**, or **53.6% of the fundamental**. This matches field measurements (typical 3rd harmonic of 30–60% in saturated transformers).

> [!TIP]
> KTU Note: For exam purposes, you do **not** need to re-derive this integral in full. Simply stating the **final expression** and computing **one sample value** (e.g., for $n=3$ with $\theta_1 = 60°$) scores full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Approximate Flicker Computation at the PCC

A furnace drawing fluctuating power $\Delta P(t)$, $\Delta Q(t)$ causes a voltage change at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC). The **approximate voltage fluctuation** is:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_{PCC}} \approx \frac{R_{sc} \cdot \Delta P + X_{sc} \cdot \Delta Q}{V_{PCC}^{2}}$$

### Step-by-step Worked Example

**Given Data:**
- Short-circuit MVA at PCC: $S_{sc} = 500 \text{ MVA}$
- Nominal voltage: $V_{PCC} = 33 \text{ kV (line-to-line)}$
- $X/R$ ratio of system: $X/R = 10$
- Furnace load swing: $\Delta P = 20 \text{ MW}$, $\Delta Q = 40 \text{ MVAr}$

**Step 1** — Compute $Z_{sc}$:

$$Z_{sc} = \frac{V^{2}}{S_{sc}} = \frac{(33 \text{ kV})^{2}}{500 \text{ MVA}} = \frac{1089}{500} = 2.178 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2** — Split into R and X using $X/R = 10$:

$$Z_{sc}^{2} = R_{sc}^{2} + X_{sc}^{2} = R_{sc}^{2}(1 + 100) = 101 R_{sc}^{2}$$

$$R_{sc} = \frac{Z_{sc}}{\sqrt{101}} = \frac{2.178}{10.05} \approx 0.2167 \, \Omega$$

$$X_{sc} = 10 \cdot R_{sc} \approx 2.167 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\Delta V/V$:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{(0.2167)(20) + (2.167)(40)}{(33)^{2}} = \frac{4.334 + 86.68}{1089} = \frac{91.014}{1089}$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} \approx 0.0836 \text{ pu} \approx 8.36\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> A voltage change of **8.36%** at the PCC almost always exceeds the IEEE 1453 / IEC 61000-3-7 planning limits and will cause noticeable **lamp flicker** in nearby residential feeders. A **Static Var Compensator (SVC)** is mandatory in such installations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Feature | Arcing Devices | Saturable Devices |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Physical Origin** | Electric arc in ionized gas | Magnetic core in B-H saturation |
| **Waveform Distortion** | Irregular, chaotic | Periodic, repeatable |
| **Dominant Harmonics** | 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th; interharmonics | 3rd, 5th, 7th (odd only in steady state) |
| **DC Component** | Significant (half-wave asymmetry) | Only during transients (inrush) |
| **Flicker Severity** | **Very High** (dominant source) | Low to Moderate |
| **Voltage Unbalance** | **High** (independent electrodes) | Negligible |
| **Time Behaviour** | Random, stochastic | Deterministic / periodic |
| **Subharmonics** | Common | Rare (except ferro-resonance) |
| **Typical Examples** | EAF, welders, HID lamps | Transformers, NGRs, ferro-resonant CVTs |
| **Standard Mitigation** | SVC, STATCOM, series reactors, active filters | Delta windings, K-rated transformers, controlled switching |

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six examples of arcing devices commonly found in industrial power systems.**

**Model Answer:**

Arcing devices are loads that sustain an electric arc as part of their normal operation. Six common examples are:

1. **AC Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** — used in steel melting; electrodes strike an arc on the scrap.
2. **DC Electric Arc Furnace** — single graphite cathode and conductive hearth.
3. **Arc Welder (Transformer Type)** — strikes and maintains a welding arc.
4. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps** — mercury-vapour, sodium-vapour, metal-halide lamps.
5. **Fluorescent Lamps with Magnetic Ballast** — sustain a low-pressure mercury-vapour arc.
6. **Arc Lamps / Searchlights** — carbon-arc lamps used in cinema projectors and signalling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Limit:** KTU expects ~50 words for a 3-mark answer. Use **bullet points** for clarity.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the saturation of a ferromagnetic core? Why does it cause harmonics?**

**Model Answer:**

**Saturation:** A ferromagnetic core is said to be saturated when the magnetic flux density $B$ no longer increases appreciably with further increase in magnetic field intensity $H$, i.e., the core operates on the flat (knee) portion of the B-H curve.

**Reason for Harmonics:** Beyond the knee, the incremental permeability $\mu = \dfrac{dB}{dH}$ drops sharply. To sustain the applied sinusoidal voltage (which demands sinusoidal flux), the magnetizing current must become **peaked and non-sinusoidal**. Fourier analysis of this distorted current shows it contains a **strong 3rd harmonic (30–60%)** along with smaller 5th, 7th, and higher odd-order harmonics.

> [!TIP]
> Drawing a small **B-H curve** with the operating point marked beyond the knee earns 1 extra mark for visual clarity.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the construction and operation of an AC electric arc furnace. Discuss its power quality impacts on the supply system.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) With the aid of waveform diagrams, derive the Fourier series expression for the harmonic content of a typical arc-furnace current. List the typical harmonic spectrum.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A (a) — Construction, Operation & PQ Impacts

**Construction:**
A modern AC EAF consists of:
- A **cylindrical refractory-lined shell** (the furnace body) to hold scrap steel.
- **Three graphite electrodes** (typically 500–750 mm diameter) inserted through the roof.
- **Electrode arms and mast** that allow vertical movement of electrodes via hydraulic actuators.
- A **secondary circuit** of heavy water-cooled copper tubes (called *delta closure*) connecting the electrodes to the furnace transformer secondary.
- A **furnace transformer** (rating 50–150 MVA) with on-load tap changer for primary voltage control.
- A **reactor** in series with the primary to limit short-circuit current during scrap cave-ins.

**Operation (5 Stages):**
1. **Scrap Charging** — Bucket drops scrap from above.
2. **Bore-down** — Electrodes bore holes into the scrap; arc is unstable; large current fluctuations.
3. **Melt-in** — Scrap collapses; arcs are short; very high current and high flicker.
4. **Melt-through** — Pool of molten metal forms at the bottom; arc becomes more stable.
5. **Refining** — Flat bath; arc is stable; power input smooth.

**Power Quality Impacts (with quantitative data):**

| PQ Issue | Magnitude | Cause |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **Voltage Flicker** | $P_{st}$ up to **4–5** | Random arc-length variations during melt-in |
| **Harmonics** | THD_I up to **20–30%** | Arc non-linearity |
| **Interharmonics** | 1–5% | Variable arc impedance |
| **Voltage Unbalance** | 2–5% | Independent electrode control |
| **Subharmonics** | 0.5–2% | Bowl-shaped arc and electrode drift |
| **Negative-Sequence** | Up to 5% | Load asymmetry |

**Diagrams (must be drawn in the exam):**
- Cross-section of an EAF showing shell, electrodes, scrap, and arc.
- V-I characteristic of an arc (negative-slope region).
- Voltage and current waveforms at PCC during melt-in.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A (b) — Fourier Analysis of Arc Current

**Step 1 — Model the Arc Current Waveform:**

The arc current is approximated as a **quasi-square wave** with random amplitude modulation. For analysis, we use a **flat-topped sinusoid** with half-wave symmetry:

$$i_{arc}(t) = I_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

Because of **half-wave asymmetry** (positive and negative half-cycles differ due to arc ignition/extinction), **even harmonics and a DC component** exist, unlike a perfectly symmetric waveform.

**Step 2 — Fourier Coefficient (General):**

$$I_n = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T i_{arc}(t) \cdot e^{-jn\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Final Expression for Flat-Topped Waveform:**

The result (already derived in Section 3) is:

$$I_n = \frac{4 I_p}{\pi} \left[ \frac{\sin(n\theta_1)}{n} \cos(n\theta_1) + \frac{\sin(\theta_1)\cos(n\theta_1)}{n} - \frac{\sin(\theta_1)\cos(n\pi - n\theta_1)}{n} \right]$$

For engineering estimation, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{I_n}{I_1} \approx \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{(for } n \ge 3\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Typical Harmonic Spectrum of Arc Furnace Current (Melt-in Phase):**

| Harmonic Order | Magnitude (\% of fundamental) | Phase Angle |
|----------------|-------------------------------|-------------|
| **1st** (50 Hz) | **100%** | Reference |
| **2nd** | 5–10% | 100–150° |
| **3rd** | 5–15% | 200–250° |
| **4th** | 2–6% | — |
| **5th** | 4–10% | — |
| **7th** | 1–4% | — |
| **Interharmonics (5–45 Hz)** | 1–5% | Random |

**Step 5 — Waveform Diagrams to Draw:**
1. A distorted current waveform showing flat-topping and asymmetry.
2. Magnitude spectrum (bar graph) of harmonics.
3. Phase spectrum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Marks are **deducted** if students:
> - Confuse the harmonic spectrum of an **arc furnace** (has even + odd) with that of a **saturated transformer** (odd only in steady state).
> - Forget to mention **interharmonics** and **subharmonics**, which are unique to arc furnaces.
> - Skip the **mathematical derivation** and only list values — partial credit only.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) What are saturable devices? Explain the phenomenon of transformer inrush current with a waveform diagram and list its harmonic content.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) What is ferro-resonance? With a suitable equivalent circuit, derive the condition for ferro-resonance in a series RLC circuit with a saturable inductance.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B (a) — Saturable Devices & Transformer Inrush

**Definition:**
Saturable devices are electromagnetic devices (transformers, reactors, magnetic amplifiers) whose ferromagnetic core is driven into the **saturation region** of the B-H curve, where the slope $dB/dH$ becomes very small.

**Transformer Inrush Phenomenon:**

When a transformer is energized, the instantaneous core flux depends on:
- The **switching angle** $\alpha$ (the point on the voltage waveform when the breaker closes)
- The **residual flux** $\Phi_r$ left in the core from the previous energization

**Total flux at the instant of switching:**

$$\Phi(t) = -\Phi_m \cos(\omega t + \alpha) + (\Phi_m \cos\alpha + \Phi_r)$$

The worst case occurs when $\alpha = 0$ and $\Phi_r = +0.8 \Phi_m$:

$$\Phi_{max} = 2.8 \Phi_m$$

This drives the core deep into saturation (since rated flux is $\Phi_m$).

**Inrush Current Peak:**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{\Phi_{max} \cdot N}{L_{sat}} = \frac{2.8 \Phi_m N}{L_{sat}}$$

Typical values: $I_{peak} = 6 \text{ to } 8 \times I_{rated}$, lasting **0.5 s to several seconds**.

**Waveform Diagram (to be drawn):**
- A **half-wave asymmetric** waveform with a long positive pulse and a small negative pulse.
- The DC component offset visible as a shifted zero-axis.

**Harmonic Content of Inrush Current:**

| Harmonic | Magnitude (\% of peak) |
|----------|------------------------|
| **DC Component** | **50 – 80%** |
| **1st (Fundamental)** | 100% |
| **2nd** | **40 – 60%** |
| **3rd** | 20 – 30% |
| **4th** | 5 – 15% |
| **5th** | 3 – 10% |

> [!TIP]
> KTU Trick Question: *"Why does the 2nd harmonic dominate in inrush but 3rd harmonic dominates in steady-state saturation?"* Because **half-wave asymmetry** (inrush) generates even harmonics, while **half-wave symmetry** (steady-state) eliminates them.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B (b) — Ferro-resonance

**Definition:**
Ferro-resonance is a **non-linear resonant phenomenon** that occurs when a **saturable inductance** $L(I)$ is in series (or parallel) with a **system capacitance** $C$. It produces **abnormally high voltages, currents, and subharmonic frequencies** that can damage equipment.

**Equivalent Circuit:**

```
   Source V_s ──[R]──[L(I)]──[C]── Neutral
```

where:
- $V_s$ = source voltage
- $R$ = winding/line resistance
- $L(I)$ = saturable inductance (decreases with current)
- $C$ = effective system capacitance (cable, transformer winding, surge capacitor)

**Governing Equation (KVL):**

$$V_s = R \cdot i + \frac{d\lambda}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int i \, dt$$

For a saturable core, the flux linkage $\lambda$ is a **non-linear function of current** $i$:

$$\lambda = L(i) \cdot i$$

**Linearization Approach (for analysis):**

Assume the saturable inductance has the form:

$$L(i) = \frac{L_0}{1 + k i^2}$$

The **characteristic equation** of the linearized circuit is:

$$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L(i)} \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{L(i) C} i = \frac{1}{L(i)} \frac{dV_s}{dt}$$

**Resonance Condition:**

The **ferro-resonant frequency** $\omega_{fr}$ occurs when the inductive and capacitive reactances balance:

$$\omega_{fr} L(I_{fr}) = \frac{1}{\omega_{fr} C}$$

Solving for $I_{fr}$ (and hence the operating point on the B-H curve):

$$I_{fr} = \frac{1}{\omega_{fr} \sqrt{L_0 C - \omega_{fr}^{-2} k C}}$$

**Three Modes of Ferro-resonance:**
1. **Fundamental Mode** — frequency = supply frequency (50 Hz).
2. **Subharmonic Mode** — frequency = $f/2$ or $f/3$ (most common, dangerous).
3. **Chaotic Mode** — broadband noise-like behaviour.

**Practical Occurrences:**
- Energizing an unloaded transformer via a long underground cable.
- Single-phase switching of a 3-phase transformer (neutral displacement).
- Loss of load on a generator feeding a capacitive network.

**Mitigation:**
- Use **Neutral Grounding Reactors (NGRs)** to limit zero-sequence displacement.
- Install **damping resistors** across transformer neutrals.
- Use **controlled switching** to avoid worst-case flux offsets.
- Avoid long cables feeding unloaded transformers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students confuse **ferro-resonance** with **linear resonance**. Emphasize the **non-linear, jump-up behaviour** and the **existence of multiple stable solutions**.
> - Don't forget to draw the **B-H curve** with the operating point and show the **loss of the inductive-capacitive balance** at saturation.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (MUST be revised the night before the exam):**

- [ ] **Arcing devices** include EAFs, arc welders, HID lamps, and fluorescent lamps with magnetic ballasts. They produce **harmonics, interharmonics, subharmonics, voltage flicker, and unbalance**.
- [ ] **Saturable devices** are electromagnetic devices operating in the **B-H saturation region**. They produce predominantly **3rd, 5th, 7th odd harmonics** in steady state and **2nd harmonic + DC offset** during inrush.
- [ ] **Arc voltage** follows **Ayrton's equation**: $V_{arc} = A + B l_{arc} + (C + D l_{arc}) / i_{arc}$.
- [ ] **Arc furnaces** are the **single largest source of voltage flicker** in modern power systems. Standard severity metric is $P_{st}$ (IEC 61000-4-15).
- [ ] **Flicker planning limits** (IEC 61000-3-7): $P_{st} \leq 1.0$, $P_{lt} \leq 0.8$ at HV/MV.
- [ ] **Total Harmonic Distortion**: $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Transformer inrush** can be **6–8 times rated current** with a **DC component of 50–80%** and dominant **2nd harmonic** (40–60%).
- [ ] **Three-phase transformers with delta primary** block 3rd-harmonic currents from reaching the line.
- [ ] **Ferro-resonance** occurs when a saturable inductance is in series with capacitance; produces **subharmonics, overvoltages, and chaotic oscillations**.
- [ ] **Mitigation devices**: SVC, STATCOM, active filters (shunt + series), K-rated transformers, controlled switching, neutral grounding reactors.
- [ ] **IEEE 519** limits current TDD; **IEEE 1453 / IEC 61000-3-7** limit flicker emission.
- [ ] **Equation for voltage fluctuation at PCC** (approximate): $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \approx \dfrac{R_{sc} \Delta P + X_{sc} \Delta Q}{V_{PCC}^{2}}$.
- [ ] **A 33 kV bus with $X/R = 10$ and a 20 MW/40 MVAr furnace swing** produces roughly **8% voltage change** — exceeds flicker limits.
- [ ] **Always** draw the V-I characteristic of an arc and the B-H curve when answering a question on this topic — examiners reward visual clarity.
- [ ] **Watch out for** confusing arcing-device spectrum (has even + odd) with saturable-device spectrum (odd only in steady state).
- [ ] **CO mapped**: This topic covers **CO2** (Identify sources of PQ disturbances) and **CO3** (Analyze harmonic and flicker signatures).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Harmonics on Power System Equipment and Loads - Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of Harmonics on Power System Equipment and Loads - Transformers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Power Quality Engineering**, the **Effects of Harmonics on Transformers** refer to the deleterious impacts caused by the superposition of non-fundamental frequency components (harmonics) on the fundamental sinusoidal voltage and current waveforms within a power transformer. These harmonic currents and voltages, generated primarily by non-linear loads (variable frequency drives, switched-mode power supplies, rectifiers, arc furnaces, etc.), interact with the magnetic core and windings of the transformer, leading to increased thermal loading, elevated losses, mechanical stress, and ultimately a reduction in the transformer's effective service life and operational capacity.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEEET751 – Power Quality (Module 2)**, this topic specifically addresses how harmonic distortion alters the rated operational parameters of transformers, necessitating the use of specialized concepts such as the **K-Factor**, the **Harmonic Loss Factor (HIF)**, and **Transformer Derating**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Harmonics are integer multiples of the fundamental power frequency (typically **50 Hz in India**). A harmonic of order $h$ has a frequency of $f_h = h \cdot f_1$, where $f_1 = 50$ Hz. For example, the 5th harmonic operates at **250 Hz**, and the 7th harmonic at **350 Hz**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a heavy swing in a playground designed to be pushed at a steady, rhythmic pace (the **fundamental frequency**). Now, picture several mischievous children pushing the swing at faster, irregular intervals (the **harmonics**). 

- The swing moves faster and the ropes experience more friction (increased **copper and stray losses**).
- The chains get heated up because the energy transfer becomes chaotic (increased **eddy current losses**).
- The swing is forced to handle forces it was never designed for, so it groans and shakes (audible **noise and vibration**).
- Eventually, the swing can't carry the weight it was originally rated for, so you must reduce the load (transformer must be **derated**).

In electrical terms, harmonics force the transformer's internal components (windings, core, insulation) to dissipate more heat and endure more stress than they would under pure sinusoidal conditions. The transformer, though functioning, operates inefficiently and has a shortened lifespan.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key standard metrics used in KTU-style problems are:

- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_1$):** 50 Hz (Indian standard)
- **K-Factor (UL 1561 / UL 1562 Standard):** A dimensionless weighting factor (1.0 to 50.0) representing the transformer's ability to handle harmonic load currents.
- **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Eddy Current Loss Constant ($W_{ec}$):** Proportional to the square of frequency ($f^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Harmonic Distortion:**
> Harmonic distortion is the deviation of a periodic waveform from a pure sinusoidal shape, caused by the presence of frequencies that are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. In a transformer, this manifests as additional heat, noise, and insulation stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How Harmonics Damage Transformers

When a non-linear load is connected downstream of a transformer, it draws a non-sinusoidal current. By Fourier's theorem, this current is decomposed into a fundamental component ($I_1$) and a series of harmonic components ($I_h$ for $h = 2, 3, 4, \ldots, n$).

The transformer is forced to deliver this composite current, and the harmful interactions occur in the following manner:

### 2.1.1 Increased Copper Losses ($P_{cu}$)
- **Cause:** Harmonic currents flow through the primary and secondary windings. Since copper loss is $I^2R$, the increase is not linear with current but proportional to the **square of the RMS current**.
- The **RMS value of the total current** (fundamental + harmonics) is always higher than the fundamental alone, leading to elevated $I^2R$ losses.

### 2.1.2 Increased Eddy Current Losses ($P_{ec}$)
- **Cause:** Eddy currents are induced in the conducting winding conductors and in the core. These losses are **proportional to the square of the frequency** ($P_{ec} \propto f^2$) and the **square of the flux density** ($B^2$).
- Since harmonic frequencies are higher ($h \cdot f_1$), a 5th harmonic current (250 Hz) causes 25 times more eddy current loss per unit flux than the fundamental.
- This is the **most dominant additional loss** in transformers under harmonic loading.

### 2.1.3 Increased Hysteresis Losses ($P_h$)
- **Cause:** Hysteresis loss is proportional to frequency ($P_h \propto f$). While the increase is linear (not squared), it still contributes to the overall heating.
- This loss is typically dominated by the core's magnetization, and voltage harmonics (not just current) drive this loss.

### 2.1.4 Stray Flux Losses
- **Cause:** Higher frequency harmonic currents create stray magnetic fields in structural parts (tank, clamps, bolts). These induce circulating eddy currents, causing localized **hot spots** that are difficult to cool.

### 2.1.5 Winding Hot Spots
- **Cause:** Due to **skin effect** and **proximity effect** at higher frequencies, current density is non-uniformly distributed in the conductors. The outer layers carry more current, creating localized overheating.
- Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) are especially problematic in **delta-wye** transformers because they circulate in the delta winding and cause localized heating.

### 2.1.6 Audible Noise and Vibration
- **Cause:** Magnetostriction in the core produces vibrations. Higher frequencies produce high-pitched sounds; harmonics can cause mechanical resonance in transformer tank walls, increasing noise levels by **10–20 dB**.

### 2.1.7 Neutral Overloading (in 3-phase systems)
- **Cause:** Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) from each phase add in the neutral conductor (since they are zero-sequence). In a balanced system with fundamental, neutral carries only minor unbalance current, but with triplen harmonics, neutral current can **exceed phase current**, leading to neutral conductor overheating.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all essential formulas required for KTU examination questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_2^2 + \ldots + I_n^2}$ | Total RMS current including harmonics | A |
| 2 | $THD_i = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{n} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (current) | % |
| 3 | $P_{cu,total} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R$ | Total copper loss with harmonics | W |
| 4 | $P_{ec,h} = P_{ec,1} \cdot h^2 \cdot \left(\frac{I_h}{I_1}\right)^2$ | Eddy current loss at harmonic $h$ | W |
| 5 | $P_{ec,total} = P_{ec,1} \sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot \left(\frac{I_h}{I_1}\right)^2$ | Total eddy current loss with harmonics | W |
| 6 | $F_{HL} = \frac{\sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2}$ | Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K\text{-factor} = \frac{\sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2}$ | K-Factor (numerically same as $F_{HL}$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Derating = \sqrt{\frac{1 + F_{HL}}{1 + P_{ec,R}(F_{HL}-1)}}$ | Transformer derating factor (IEEE Std C57.110) | per-unit |
| 9 | $F_{HL} = \frac{\sum (h^2)(I_h/I_1)^2}{\sum (I_h/I_1)^2}$ | HLF in terms of per-unit harmonic currents | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{hyst,h} = P_{hyst,1} \cdot h \cdot \left(\frac{V_h}{V_1}\right)$ | Hysteresis loss at harmonic order $h$ | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Equations 6 and 7:**
> In many textbooks, the Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF, denoted $F_{HL}$) and the K-Factor have **numerically identical formulas**. The distinction is that the K-Factor is a *transformer rating* (a property of the transformer's design), whereas HLF is a *load characteristic* (a property of the connected load).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not merely academic — they are used by practicing electrical engineers to:

- Specify **K-rated transformers** for non-linear loads (data centers, hospitals, office buildings with computers).
- Conduct **harmonics audits** in industrial plants.
- Design **harmonic filters** (passive, active, or hybrid) to protect transformers.
- Determine whether an existing transformer must be **replaced, derated, or supplemented** with harmonic-mitigating equipment.
- Comply with standards like **IEEE 519**, **UL 1561**, and **IEC 61000-3-2/3-12**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total RMS Current with Harmonics

### Step 1: Statement of the Problem
A transformer supplies a non-linear load. The load current contains a fundamental component and multiple harmonic components. We need to find the total RMS current, $I_{rms}$, in terms of the individual harmonic magnitudes.

### Step 2: Definition of RMS Value
The RMS value of any periodic current $i(t)$ is defined as:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T [i(t)]^2 \, dt}$$

### Step 3: Fourier Decomposition
By Fourier's theorem, the load current can be expressed as:

$$i(t) = I_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} I_h \sin(h \omega_1 t + \phi_h)$$

where $I_0$ is the DC component (usually zero in AC systems), $I_h$ is the peak value of the $h^{th}$ harmonic, and $\omega_1 = 2\pi f_1$ is the fundamental angular frequency.

### Step 4: Squaring and Integrating
Substituting the Fourier series into the RMS formula and exploiting the **orthogonality property** of sinusoids (i.e., $\int_0^T \sin(h\omega_1 t)\sin(k\omega_1 t)\, dt = 0$ for $h \neq k$), we obtain:

$$I_{rms}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{n} \left(\frac{I_h}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2$$

### Step 5: Conversion to RMS
Rewriting in terms of the RMS value of each harmonic $I_{h,rms} = I_h/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\boxed{I_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_{h,rms}^2} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_2^2 + I_3^2 + \ldots + I_n^2}}$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** that drives all subsequent harmonic loss calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Copper Loss with Harmonics

### Step 1: Ohm's Law for Losses
Copper loss in a winding of resistance $R$ is $P_{cu} = I_{rms}^2 R$.

### Step 2: Substitution of Total RMS
Substituting the result from Section 3.1:

$$P_{cu,total} = R \cdot \sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2$$

### Step 3: Separation of Fundamental and Harmonic Components
Splitting the sum into the fundamental term and the harmonic term:

$$P_{cu,total} = R \cdot I_1^2 + R \cdot \sum_{h=2}^{n} I_h^2$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
- The first term $R \cdot I_1^2$ is the **rated copper loss** at fundamental frequency.
- The second term $R \cdot \sum_{h=2}^{n} I_h^2$ is the **additional copper loss** caused by harmonic currents.
- This additional loss manifests as heat and reduces the transformer's available capacity for useful work.

## 3.3 Derivation of Eddy Current Loss with Harmonics

### Step 1: Base Eddy Current Equation
The eddy current loss in a conductor exposed to a sinusoidal magnetic field of frequency $f$ and peak flux density $B$ is:

$$P_{ec} = K_e \cdot B^2 \cdot f^2$$

where $K_e$ is a constant that depends on conductor geometry, resistivity, and lamination thickness.

### Step 2: Application to a Single Harmonic
For the $h^{th}$ harmonic, $f_h = h \cdot f_1$ and the eddy current loss becomes:

$$P_{ec,h} = K_e \cdot B_h^2 \cdot (h f_1)^2 = K_e \cdot B_h^2 \cdot h^2 \cdot f_1^2$$

### Step 3: Ratio of Harmonic to Fundamental
Dividing $P_{ec,h}$ by the fundamental eddy current loss $P_{ec,1} = K_e \cdot B_1^2 \cdot f_1^2$:

$$\frac{P_{ec,h}}{P_{ec,1}} = \left(\frac{B_h}{B_1}\right)^2 \cdot h^2$$

### Step 4: Relating Flux to Current
Since the harmonic current drives a proportional flux in the core (assuming linear magnetization), $B_h \propto I_h$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{ec,h} = P_{ec,1} \cdot h^2 \cdot \left(\frac{I_h}{I_1}\right)^2}$$

### Step 5: Total Eddy Current Loss
Summing over all harmonics:

$$P_{ec,total} = P_{ec,1} \sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot \left(\frac{I_h}{I_1}\right)^2$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF)

### Step 1: Definition
The Harmonic Loss Factor is a weighted average of the harmonic orders, weighted by their current contributions:

$$F_{HL} = \frac{\sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2}$$

### Step 2: Physical Meaning
- Numerator: Total eddy current loss (proportional to $h^2$).
- Denominator: Total copper loss (proportional to $I^2$).
- $F_{HL}$ essentially captures how much "extra" eddy current loss is incurred per unit of copper loss.

### Step 3: Special Case — Pure Sinusoidal Load
If only the fundamental is present, $I_h = 0$ for $h \geq 2$. Then $F_{HL} = 1$, which is the reference condition.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)
A 500 kVA transformer supplies a load with the following current spectrum:

| Harmonic Order ($h$) | Current Magnitude (A) |
|---|---|
| 1 (Fundamental) | 100 |
| 3 | 40 |
| 5 | 25 |
| 7 | 15 |
| 11 | 8 |

**Step 4a: Compute $\sum h^2 I_h^2$**

$$\sum h^2 I_h^2 = (1)^2(100)^2 + (3)^2(40)^2 + (5)^2(25)^2 + (7)^2(15)^2 + (11)^2(8)^2$$

$$= 1 \cdot 10000 + 9 \cdot 1600 + 25 \cdot 625 + 49 \cdot 225 + 121 \cdot 64$$

$$= 10000 + 14400 + 15625 + 11025 + 7744 = 58794 \, A^2$$

**Step 4b: Compute $\sum I_h^2$**

$$\sum I_h^2 = 100^2 + 40^2 + 25^2 + 15^2 + 8^2 = 10000 + 1600 + 625 + 225 + 64 = 12514 \, A^2$$

**Step 4c: Compute $F_{HL}$**

$$F_{HL} = \frac{58794}{12514} \approx 4.70$$

**Interpretation:** A transformer supplying this load would need a **K-factor rating of at least 5** to operate without overheating.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Transformer Derating Formula (IEEE C57.110)

### Step 1: Constraint — Equal Total Loss
A transformer can deliver its rated kVA only if its total loss is within thermal limits. Under harmonic loading, total loss increases. To stay within limits, we must **reduce the load current** by a factor $k$ (the derating factor).

### Step 2: Loss Equation at Fundamental
At rated sinusoidal load, total loss = copper loss + eddy current loss:

$$P_{loss,1} = I_1^2 R + P_{ec,1}$$

### Step 3: Loss Equation at Derated Harmonic Load
After derating by $k$ (so the new current is $k \cdot I_{1,rated}$), total loss:

$$P_{loss,derated} = k^2 I_1^2 R + k^2 P_{ec,1} \cdot F_{HL}$$

### Step 4: Equating the Two Loss Equations

$$I_1^2 R + P_{ec,1} = k^2 I_1^2 R + k^2 P_{ec,1} F_{HL}$$

### Step 5: Solving for $k$

$$k^2 = \frac{I_1^2 R + P_{ec,1}}{I_1^2 R + P_{ec,1} F_{HL}} = \frac{1 + (P_{ec,1}/I_1^2 R)}{1 + (P_{ec,1}/I_1^2 R) F_{HL}}$$

### Step 6: Defining $P_{ec,R}$
Let $P_{ec,R} = \dfrac{P_{ec,1}}{I_1^2 R}$ be the ratio of rated eddy current loss to rated copper loss (typically **0.05 to 0.15** for dry-type transformers).

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{\frac{1 + P_{ec,R}}{1 + P_{ec,R} \cdot F_{HL}}}}$$

### Step 7: Worked Example
Using the previous example with $F_{HL} = 4.70$ and assuming $P_{ec,R} = 0.1$:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.1}{1 + 0.1 \times 4.70}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.1}{1.47}} = \sqrt{0.7483} \approx 0.865$$

**Interpretation:** The transformer can be loaded to only **86.5%** of its rated capacity — a derating of **13.5%** — to avoid overheating.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Harmonic Current Flow Path in a Distribution Transformer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how harmonic currents from non-linear loads propagate through a typical distribution system and back to the source, affecting the transformer at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["UTILITY GRID 50 HZ SOURCE"] --> nodeB["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER 11KV TO 415V"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MAIN DISTRIBUTION PANEL"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["BRANCH 1 LIGHTING LOAD"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["BRANCH 2 MOTOR LOAD"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["BRANCH 3 NON LINEAR LOAD VFD SMPS RECTIFIER"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["HARMONIC CURRENT GENERATION 5TH 7TH 11TH 13TH"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["HARMONICS FLOW BACK INTO TRANSFORMER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["INCREASED EDDY CURRENT LOSSES"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["INCREASED COPPER LOSSES"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["WINDING HOT SPOTS"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["NEUTRAL OVERLOAD TRIPLEN HARMONICS"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["TRANSFORMER OVERHEATING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["DERATING REQUIRED OR K RATED TRANSFORMER"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Harmonic Impact on Transformer Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT NON LINEAR LOAD CURRENT"] --> nodeB["FOURIER DECOMPOSITION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FUNDAMENTAL 50 HZ"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["HARMONIC SPECTRUM H EQUAL 2 3 4 N"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["RATED COPPER LOSS I SQUARED R"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["FREQUENCY DEPENDENT LOSSES H SQUARED"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["TOTAL LOSS BUDGET"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH{"LOSS EXCEEDS THERMAL LIMIT"}
    nodeH --> nodeI["APPLY DERATING FACTOR K"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["SELECT K RATED TRANSFORMER"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["SAFE OPERATION"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Harmonic Loss Allocation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["HARMONIC LOAD CURRENT"] --> nodeB["HARMONIC LOSS FACTOR FHL"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["EDDY CURRENT LOSS COMPONENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["COPPER LOSS COMPONENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["STRAY LOSS COMPONENT"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["WINDING HOT SPOTS"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["INSULATION THERMAL AGING"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH["TRANSFORMER FAILURE MODES"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

## 4.4 Description of Physical Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?**
> A physical drawing of a transformer with harmonic flux lines, eddy current loops, and skin effect layers is extremely complex to render as ASCII. The block diagrams above convey the same engineering understanding of *interactions* between harmonics and transformer subsystems, which is what the KTU examiner rewards in answer scripts.

A typical physical schematic would show:
- A **transformer tank** with HV and LV bushings.
- **Laminated core** with flux lines ($\Phi$) at fundamental and harmonic frequencies.
- **LV windings** carrying harmonic currents, with the outer strands carrying higher current density (skin/proximity effect).
- **Stray flux paths** through tank walls, causing eddy current hot spots near the corners and clamping frames.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF). Why is it important for transformer operation? **[3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**
The Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF) is a dimensionless quantity that represents the weighted effect of harmonic currents on the eddy current losses in a transformer. It is defined as:

$$F_{HL} = \frac{\sum_{h=1}^{n} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2}$$

It is important because it quantifies the additional heating caused by harmonic currents and is used directly in the IEEE C57.110 derating formula to determine the safe loading of a transformer supplying non-linear loads. A higher HLF value indicates more harmonic content and a need for greater derating or a K-rated transformer.

### Question 2: What is a K-Factor rated transformer? Mention two applications. **[3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**
A K-Factor rated transformer is a specially designed transformer that can withstand the additional heating effects caused by harmonic currents in non-linear loads. The K-factor quantifies the transformer's ability to handle harmonic load currents and is computed from the harmonic spectrum of the expected load. K-rated transformers use:
- Larger neutral conductors (for triplen harmonics),
- Multiple parallel winding conductors (to reduce skin effect),
- Higher temperature-rated insulation systems.

**Two applications:**
1. Data centers (with large numbers of switched-mode power supplies).
2. Hospitals (with MRI machines, UPS systems, and variable frequency drives).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[Module Choice Option 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various effects of harmonics on power transformers. Discuss the increase in copper losses, eddy current losses, and the impact on transformer insulation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 250 kVA, 11 kV / 415 V, 50 Hz delta-wye distribution transformer has the following load current harmonics measured at the LV side:

| Harmonic Order $h$ | Current $I_h$ (A) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 350 |
| 3 | 90 |
| 5 | 60 |
| 7 | 30 |
| 9 | 20 |
| 11 | 12 |

Assume the eddy current loss at rated fundamental current is 8% of the full-load copper loss. Calculate:
  (i) The Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF).
  (ii) The K-factor of the load.
  (iii) The derating factor using the IEEE C57.110 formula. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Effects of Harmonics on Transformers:**

1. **Increased Copper Losses:** Harmonic currents increase the RMS value of the total current. Since copper loss is $I_{rms}^2 R$, the total copper loss increases by the square of the ratio of total RMS to fundamental RMS.

2. **Increased Eddy Current Losses:** Eddy current losses are proportional to the square of frequency ($h^2 f_1^2$) and the square of the harmonic current. A 5th harmonic (250 Hz) causes 25 times more eddy current loss per unit current than the fundamental, making it the dominant additional loss mechanism.

3. **Increased Hysteresis Losses:** These are proportional to frequency, contributing additional core heating proportional to the voltage harmonics.

4. **Winding Hot Spots:** Skin and proximity effects at higher frequencies cause non-uniform current distribution, with the outer strands carrying higher current density, leading to localized overheating.

5. **Neutral Overloading:** Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) add in the neutral of a wye-connected secondary, potentially causing neutral current to exceed phase current.

6. **Increased Audible Noise:** Magnetostriction and tank resonance at harmonic frequencies raise noise levels by 10–20 dB.

7. **Reduced Transformer Life:** Elevated temperatures accelerate insulation aging, following the **Arrhenius equation** where insulation life halves for every 8–10 °C rise.

8. **Stray Flux Heating:** Harmonics induce circulating currents in structural parts (tank, bolts), creating localized hot spots.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Harmonic Loss Factor:**

First, compute $\sum h^2 I_h^2$:

| $h$ | $I_h$ (A) | $I_h^2$ | $h^2$ | $h^2 \cdot I_h^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 350 | 122500 | 1 | 122500 |
| 3 | 90 | 8100 | 9 | 72900 |
| 5 | 60 | 3600 | 25 | 90000 |
| 7 | 30 | 900 | 49 | 44100 |
| 9 | 20 | 400 | 81 | 32400 |
| 11 | 12 | 144 | 121 | 17424 |
| **Sum** | | **135644** | | **379324** |

$$F_{HL} = \frac{379324}{135644} = 2.797 \approx 2.80$$

**(ii) K-factor:**
The K-factor is numerically equal to the HLF when computed from the current spectrum (with appropriate weighting by the standard K-factor table).

$$\boxed{K = 2.80}$$

**Note on rounding:** Standard K-factor ratings (per UL 1561) are discrete: K-1, K-4, K-9, K-13, K-20, K-30, K-40, K-50. The calculated K-factor of 2.80 rounds to a **K-4** rated transformer.

**(iii) Derating Factor:**
Given $P_{ec,R} = 0.08$ (8% of full-load copper loss):

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{1 + P_{ec,R}}{1 + P_{ec,R} \cdot F_{HL}}}$$

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.08}{1 + 0.08 \times 2.80}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.08}{1.224}} = \sqrt{0.8824} = 0.9394$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 0.94 \text{ p.u.}}$$

**Interpretation:** The transformer must be loaded to no more than **93.94% of its 250 kVA rating** ≈ **234.85 kVA** to avoid overheating. Equivalently, the derating is **6.06%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Module Choice Option 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define K-Factor as applied to transformers. With a neat block diagram, explain how a K-rated transformer differs from a standard transformer. List three design modifications in K-rated transformers. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A three-phase 500 kVA, 50 Hz transformer has measured harmonic currents in the secondary winding as given below. Compute the Harmonic Loss Factor and recommend the appropriate K-factor rated transformer.

| $h$ | $I_h / I_1$ (per unit) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 |
| 5 | 0.180 |
| 7 | 0.120 |
| 11 | 0.060 |
| 13 | 0.040 |

Also, comment on the role of triplen harmonics in delta-wye transformers. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) K-Factor Definition and Design Modifications:**

**Definition:** The K-Factor is a numerical rating (defined in UL Standard 1561) that describes a transformer's suitability for supplying non-linear loads. It quantifies the additional heating effect produced by harmonic currents in the windings. A K-1 rated transformer is suitable only for linear loads, while higher K-ratings (K-4, K-9, K-13, etc.) indicate progressively higher tolerance to harmonic content.

**Block Diagram of K-Rated vs Standard Transformer:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STANDARD TRANSFORMER K EQUAL 1"] --> nodeB["SINGLE STRAND WINDING CONDUCTOR"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["STANDARD NEUTRAL RATED 1.0 X PHASE"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["CLASS 220 DEG C INSULATION"]
    nodeE["K RATED TRANSFORMER K GREATER THAN 1"] --> nodeF["MULTIPLE PARALLEL WINDING CONDUCTORS"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["OVERSIZED NEUTRAL 2 X PHASE FOR TRIPLEN"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["CLASS 220 DEG C OR HIGHER INSULATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["ELECTROSTATIC SHIELD BETWEEN PRIMARY AND SECONDARY"]
```

**Three Design Modifications in K-Rated Transformers:**

1. **Multiple parallel conductors** in windings to reduce skin and proximity effects at harmonic frequencies.
2. **Oversized neutral conductor** (typically 200% of phase conductor size) to handle triplen harmonic currents.
3. **Higher temperature insulation system** (Class H or N, rated up to 220 °C) to withstand the additional heating.
4. *(Bonus)* Use of **electromagnetic shields** between primary and secondary to reduce stray flux losses.
5. *(Bonus)* **Improved core steel** with lower losses per cycle at high frequencies.

**(b) Harmonic Loss Factor and K-Factor Recommendation:**

Compute $F_{HL}$ using $I_h / I_1$ values:

| $h$ | $I_h / I_1$ (p.u.) | $(I_h/I_1)^2$ | $h^2$ | $h^2 \cdot (I_h/I_1)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 | 1.0000 | 1 | 1.0000 |
| 5 | 0.180 | 0.0324 | 25 | 0.8100 |
| 7 | 0.120 | 0.0144 | 49 | 0.7056 |
| 11 | 0.060 | 0.0036 | 121 | 0.4356 |
| 13 | 0.040 | 0.0016 | 169 | 0.2704 |
| **Sum** | | **1.0520** | | **3.2216** |

$$F_{HL} = \frac{3.2216}{1.0520} = 3.062 \approx 3.06$$

**Recommended K-Factor Transformer:** Per UL 1561 standard ratings, $F_{HL} = 3.06$ corresponds to a **K-4 rated transformer** (the next available standard rating above 3.06).

**Role of Triplen Harmonics in Delta-Wye Transformers:**

Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, ...) are zero-sequence components, meaning they are in phase in all three phases of a three-phase system. In a **delta-wye transformer**:
