- The **primary winding is delta-connected**: triplen harmonic currents, instead of being blocked, circulate within the delta winding. This causes significant **localized heating in the delta winding** because these currents must flow through the windings without exiting to the line.
- The **secondary winding is wye-connected**: triplen harmonic currents from each phase add constructively in the neutral conductor (since they are in phase), potentially producing a neutral current that is **up to 3 times the phase triplen current**. This requires oversizing the neutral conductor in K-rated transformers.
- The delta winding provides a **path for third-harmonic currents** to circulate, which helps to **maintain a near-sinusoidal flux** in the core (preventing it from becoming peaked), but at the cost of additional $I^2R$ and eddy current losses in the delta winding.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing HLF and K-Factor:** HLF is a *load property*; K-Factor is a *transformer rating*. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $I_h^2$ (not $I_h$) in HLF formula:** The square of the current, not the current itself, must be used. Using $I_h$ directly yields a wrong answer.
> 3. **Mixing peak and RMS values:** In KTU problems, harmonic current magnitudes are usually given in RMS unless explicitly stated as peak. Always verify.
> 4. **Incorrect derating formula application:** Students often forget $P_{ec,R}$ (eddy current to copper loss ratio) and use $P_{ec,R} = 1$, which is wrong. For dry-type transformers, $P_{ec,R} \in [0.05, 0.15]$; for oil-immersed, it is often assumed higher.
> 5. **Forgetting the $h^2$ weight:** The most common error is computing $F_{HL}$ as $\sum I_h^2 / \sum I_h^2$ (i.e., omitting the $h^2$ factor). This is the standard "trap" in KTU questions.
> 6. **Not specifying K-factor as a standard rating:** The answer should mention UL 1561 standard K-factor ratings (K-1, K-4, K-9, etc.) rather than giving a fractional value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Harmonic order $h$:** Integer multiple of fundamental frequency (50 Hz in India).
- [ ] **RMS current with harmonics:** $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{n} I_h^2}$ — *always higher than fundamental alone.*
- [ ] **Eddy current loss scales as $h^2$:** This makes high-frequency harmonics (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th) particularly damaging.
- [ ] **Hysteresis loss scales linearly with $h$:** Less dominant than eddy current loss but still significant.
- [ ] **Skin and proximity effects:** Cause non-uniform current density, leading to hot spots.
- [ ] **Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th):** Add in the neutral of a wye system; circulate in delta windings.
- [ ] **Harmonic Loss Factor (HLF):** $F_{HL} = \dfrac{\sum h^2 I_h^2}{\sum I_h^2}$ — quantifies harmonic heating.
- [ ] **K-Factor:** Standard UL 1561 ratings: **K-1, K-4, K-9, K-13, K-20, K-30, K-40, K-50.**
- [ ] **Derating formula (IEEE C57.110):** $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + P_{ec,R}}{1 + P_{ec,R} \cdot F_{HL}}}$, where $P_{ec,R}$ is the ratio of rated eddy current loss to rated copper loss.
- [ ] **Standard metrics:** THD in %, K-factor dimensionless, frequency in Hz, losses in W or kW.
- [ ] **Mitigation methods:** K-rated transformers, harmonic filters (passive/active), oversizing transformers, using 12-pulse rectifiers, and zigzag transformers for neutral current reduction.
- [ ] **Insulation aging:** Per Arrhenius rule, every 8–10 °C rise halves insulation life — this is the ultimate reason harmonic-induced heating is critical.
- [ ] **Audible noise:** Harmonics can increase transformer noise by 10–20 dB due to magnetostriction and tank resonance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> When answering a 14-mark question on harmonic effects, always structure your answer in three parts: (1) list the effect, (2) state the formula, (3) explain the physical mechanism. This matches the KTU marking scheme and ensures full credit. For numerical problems, always show **table-form computation** of $h^2 I_h^2$ and $\sum I_h^2$ — examiners award method marks for this step alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitor Banks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitor Banks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Capacitor Bank** is an assembly of one or more capacitor units, protection devices, and control equipment interconnected to deliver a specific quantity of reactive power (measured in **kVAr** or **MVAr**) at a designated voltage and frequency, primarily used in power systems for reactive power compensation, power factor correction, voltage regulation, and harmonic filtering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEEE Std 18-2012 aligned):** A capacitor bank is a collection of capacitors installed at a single location, switched either as a single unit or in sections, to supply controlled levels of reactive power to the network.

In the context of **Power Quality**, capacitor banks are *double-edged assets* — they improve power factor and voltage profile, but their switching introduces **transients**, **harmonics**, and **resonance phenomena** that degrade PQ if not properly engineered.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Tank" Analogy for Reactive Power**
> 
> Imagine the power system as a water pipeline:
> - **Active power (P)** = the water actually delivered to your tap (useful work).
> - **Reactive power (Q)** = water sloshing back and forth inside the pipe without doing useful work, but occupying pipe capacity.
> - **Apparent power (S)** = the total pipe diameter required to carry both.
> 
> A **capacitor bank** is like installing a **storage tank** near the load. Reactive current that used to slosh back to the utility now circulates locally between the inductor (motor) and the capacitor tank. The pipe to the utility only carries useful water — i.e., the **power factor improves** and **line losses drop**.

| Aspect | Without Capacitor Bank | With Capacitor Bank |
|---|---|---|
| Reactive current from utility | High | Reduced |
| Power factor | Lagging (low) | Improved (closer to unity) |
| Line losses ($I^2R$) | High | Lower |
| Voltage at load | Lower (drop) | Boosted |
| Utility bill (kVArh penalty) | High | Reduced |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **System frequency:** $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$ (India, KTU region)  
- **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$
- **Capacitive reactance:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ (in ohms)
- **Reactive power of capacitor:** $Q_C = V^2 \omega C = \dfrac{V^2}{X_C}$ (in VAr)
- **Standard capacitor unit ratings:** 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500 kVAr
- **Common system voltages:** 415 V, 11 kV, 33 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Capacitor banks are studied in Module 2 under the *Power Quality* umbrella because **their switching produces transients (1–3 per unit over-voltages)** and **they interact dangerously with harmonic currents** to create parallel/series resonance, which can amplify harmonic distortion well beyond IEEE 519 limits.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Capacitor Banks

```
Capacitor Banks
├── 1. Shunt (Parallel) Capacitor Banks
│     ├── Fixed (permanently connected)
│     ├── Switched (manually/automatically)
│     └── Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC)
├── 2. Series Capacitor Banks
│     └── Used for transmission line reactance compensation
├── 3. Detuned (Filter) Capacitor Banks
│     ├── Tuned to a specific harmonic (e.g., 5th, 7th)
│     └── De-tuned (typically p = 7%, i.e., 189 Hz for 50 Hz system)
└── 4. Synchronous (Switched) Capacitor Banks
      └── Zero-voltage-crossing closing
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Capacitor Banks? — The Reactive Power Problem

Most industrial loads — **induction motors, transformers, arc furnaces, rectifiers** — consume magnetising reactive power ($Q_L$). This lagging current:

1. Increases line current $I = S/V$ for the same active power.
2. Increases $I^2R$ copper losses.
3. Causes voltage drop $V_{\text{drop}} = I(R\cos\theta + X\sin\theta)$.
4. Attracts **kVArh penalty** from utilities.

Capacitor banks supply **leading reactive power ($Q_C$)**, which **cancels** the lagging $Q_L$ locally, improving the overall system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Concept:** Capacitor banks do not consume active power. They are energy-storage elements that exchange reactive energy at system frequency.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Reactive Power Demand**
For a load consuming $P$ active power at original power factor $\cos\theta_1$, the lagging reactive power is:
$$Q_1 = P \tan\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Target Power Factor**
The utility or design may demand a target $\cos\theta_2$, corresponding to:
$$Q_2 = P \tan\theta_2$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor Bank Rating**
The capacitor bank must supply the **difference**:
$$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = P \left( \tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance Selection**
For a delta-connected bank at line voltage $V_L$:
$$C_{\Delta} = \frac{Q_C}{2 \pi f V_L^{\,2}} \quad \text{(per phase)}$$

For a star-connected bank at line voltage $V_L$, the phase voltage is $V_L/\sqrt{3}$:
$$C_{Y} = \frac{3\,Q_C}{2 \pi f V_L^{\,2}} \quad \text{(per phase)}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Realisation**
The bank is constructed by paralleling standard capacitor units (e.g., 25 kVAr each) until the required $Q_C$ is met, with $\pm 5\%$ tolerance.

---

## 2.3 Resonance — The Most Critical Power Quality Concern

When a shunt capacitor bank $C$ is installed, it forms a **parallel resonant circuit** with the system (source) inductance $L_s$:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s C}}$$

At this **resonant frequency**, the harmonic impedance of the network becomes very high. Any harmonic current near $f_r$ — generated by non-linear loads — gets **amplified as a voltage**, causing severe harmonic distortion.

In **harmonic number** (multiple of fundamental):
$$n_r = \frac{f_r}{f_1} = \sqrt{\frac{X_C}{X_L}}$$

where $X_C$ is the capacitive reactance of the bank and $X_L$ is the short-circuit reactance at the point of common coupling (PCC).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If the system has a 5th or 7th harmonic source (very common with 6-pulse rectifiers), and the bank is sized such that $n_r \approx 5$ or $7$, **catastrophic harmonic resonance** occurs — capacitor failure is the typical outcome.

### 2.3.1 Detuning — The Engineering Solution

To avoid resonance, a **detuning reactor** $L_d$ is added in **series** with the capacitor $C$. The detuning factor $p$ is defined as:
$$p = \frac{X_{L_d}}{X_C}$$

| Detuning factor $p$ | Tuned harmonic order $n_t$ | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 5 % | 4.47 | General industrial |
| 7 % | 3.78 | Standard detuning |
| 14 % | 2.67 | Heavy harmonic environment |

The **tuned (trap) frequency** of the detuned branch:
$$f_t = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_d C}} = f_1 \sqrt{\frac{1}{p}}$$

> A **7 % detuned** bank (most common) traps frequencies **below the 5th harmonic**, making the branch capacitive only for 50 Hz and **inductive for the 5th harmonic and above** — preventing resonance.

---

## 2.4 Switching Transients

When a shunt capacitor bank is energised, the instantaneous voltage across the bank cannot change abruptly. The transient response of an R-L-C series circuit yields:

$$v_C(t) = V_m \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \cos\left( \omega_d t - \phi \right) \right]$$

For an *ideal* lossless circuit, the **theoretical peak** can approach **$2V_m$** (i.e., 2 p.u.). In practice, with damping, it reaches **1.2 – 1.8 p.u.** — known as the **capacitor switching transient**.

**Mitigation techniques:**
1. **Pre-insertion resistors (PIR)** — inserted briefly during closing.
2. **Pre-insertion inductors (PIL)** — preferred for high-voltage banks.
3. **Synchronous (zero-voltage) closing** — using controlled switches (thyristor switches, sync relays).
4. **Point-on-wave (POW) controllers.**

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | $\Omega$ | $C$ in Farads |
| 2 | Inductive reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | $\Omega$ | $L$ in Henrys |
| 3 | Reactive power of bank | $Q_C = V^2 \omega C$ | VAr | $V$ = line voltage (star) or $V_{ph}$ (delta) |
| 4 | Required compensation | $Q_C = P(\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2)$ | VAr | Standard PF correction |
| 5 | Star capacitance | $C_Y = \dfrac{3 Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^{\,2}}$ | F | Per phase, star bank |
| 6 | Delta capacitance | $C_\Delta = \dfrac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^{\,2}}$ | F | Per phase, delta bank |
| 7 | Parallel resonance freq. | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C}}$ | Hz | With source inductance $L_s$ |
| 8 | Resonant harmonic order | $n_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{X_C}{X_L}}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Detuning factor | $p = \dfrac{X_{L_d}}{X_C}$ | — | Usually 7 % |
| 10 | Trap harmonic order | $n_t = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$ | — | For detuned filter |
| 11 | Switching transient peak (worst case) | $V_{\max} \approx 2 V_m$ | V | Lossless assumption |
| 12 | Voltage rise on switching | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{Q_C}{S_{sc}} \times V$ | V | Approximate |
| 13 | kVAr per kVAR (rating) | $Q_C = \dfrac{V^2}{X_C}$ | VAr | Direct relationship |
| 14 | Energy stored | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Important for safety |
| 15 | Charging current (per phase) | $I_C = \dfrac{Q_C}{\sqrt{3}\, V_L}$ | A | Three-phase bank |
| 16 | Power factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | — | Should be $> 0.95$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state assumptions** (50 Hz, balanced 3-phase, star/delta connection) before applying these formulas. Mark deductions for missing units are common.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Capacitor Banks

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Distribution utilities (KSEB, etc.)** | Bulk power factor correction at 11 kV substations |
| **Industrial plants** | LT/HT automatic power factor correction (APFC) panels |
| **Wind / Solar farms** | Reactive support at PCC to meet grid code (CEA) |
| **HVDC converter stations** | Filter banks + reactive support |
| **Traction (Railways)** | Series compensation for long OHE lines |
| **Arc furnace plants** | Detuned banks to handle flicker + harmonics |
| **Data centres** | Dynamic / synchronous VAr for voltage stability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Required kVAr for Power Factor Correction

**Problem context:** A 3-phase load draws active power $P$ at a lagging power factor $\cos\theta_1$. We want to improve it to $\cos\theta_2$. Find the rating of the capacitor bank $Q_C$.

**Step 1 — Original reactive power**
The reactive power drawn by the load is:
$$Q_1 = P \tan\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Desired reactive power after compensation**
After installing the capacitor bank, the net reactive power required from the source is:
$$Q_2 = P \tan\theta_2$$

**Step 3 — Reactive power supplied by capacitor**
The capacitor bank supplies the difference:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= Q_1 - Q_2 \\
    &= P \tan\theta_1 - P \tan\theta_2 \\
    &= P \left( \tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — From kVAr to capacitance**
For a **delta-connected** bank at line voltage $V_L$, the reactive power per phase is $Q_C/3$, and the phase voltage equals $V_L$:

$$\frac{Q_C}{3} = V_L^2 \, \omega \, C_{\Delta} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_{\Delta} = \frac{Q_C}{3 \omega V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f \cdot 3\, V_L^{\,2}}$$

For a **star-connected** bank, phase voltage is $V_L/\sqrt{3}$:

$$C_{Y} = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f \, V_L^{\,2}} \quad \text{(per phase, star)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **star-connected** bank requires **three times** the per-phase capacitance compared to a delta-connected bank of the same kVAr rating, but each capacitor unit sees only $V_L/\sqrt{3}$, which is **cheaper and safer** in LV applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Resonant Harmonic Order

**Context:** A shunt capacitor bank $C$ is installed at a bus where the short-circuit (source) inductance is $L_s$.

**Step 1 — Impedance of the parallel combination (source + capacitor)**

The impedance of capacitor:
$$Z_C = -j \frac{1}{\omega C} = -j X_C$$

The impedance of source inductance:
$$Z_L = j \omega L_s = j X_L$$

**Step 2 — Parallel combination**
$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{eq} &= \frac{Z_C \, Z_L}{Z_C + Z_L} \\
       &= \frac{(-j X_C)(j X_L)}{j(X_L - X_C)} \\
       &= \frac{X_C X_L}{j(X_L - X_C)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Resonance condition**
Resonance (max $|Z_{eq}|$) occurs when $X_L = X_C$:
$$\omega_r L_s = \frac{1}{\omega_r C} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_r^2 = \frac{1}{L_s C}$$

$$\boxed{\; f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_s C}}\;}$$

**Step 4 — In terms of harmonic number**
Let $n = \omega / \omega_1$ and $n_r = \omega_r / \omega_1$. At resonance:
$$n_r^2 \, \omega_1^2 = \frac{1}{L_s C} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; n_r^2 = \frac{1}{\omega_1^2 L_s C} = \frac{X_C}{X_L}$$

$$\boxed{\; n_r = \sqrt{\frac{X_C}{X_L}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application:** To avoid resonance with the dominant 5th harmonic, the bank should be sized such that $n_r < 5$, i.e., $X_C < 25 X_L$ (i.e., $Q_C < S_{sc}/25$). This is a common KTU design constraint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Voltage Rise Due to Capacitor Bank Switching

When a capacitor bank is energised, the voltage at the load bus rises. Assuming the Thevenin equivalent source has voltage $V_s$ and impedance $X_s$ (purely reactive), the bus voltage after switching becomes:

$$V_{\text{bus}} = V_s + j X_s \cdot I_C$$

where $I_C$ is the leading current supplied by the bank. The magnitude:

$$V_{\text{bus}} = V_s \left( 1 + \frac{Q_C}{S_{sc}} \right)$$

where $S_{sc} = V_s^2 / X_s$ is the short-circuit level at the bus.

The **percentage voltage rise** is:
$$\boxed{\; \Delta V \% = \frac{Q_C}{S_{sc}} \times 100\;}$$

> **Example:** If a **5 MVAr** bank is connected to a bus with **$S_{sc} = 100$ MVA**, the voltage rise is:
> $$\Delta V = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05 \text{ p.u.} = 5\%$$
> On an 11 kV bus, this corresponds to a rise from 11 kV to 11.55 kV.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Switching Transient Peak Voltage

**Step 1 — Circuit model at energisation**
When closing the switch, the R-L-C series circuit is excited by the source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \alpha)$, where $\alpha$ is the closing angle.

**Step 2 — Differential equation**
$$v(t) = R\, i(t) + L \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int i\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Solution (lossless, R = 0)**
For $R=0$ and worst-case closing ($\alpha = \pm 90°$):
$$v_C(t) = V_m \left( 1 - \cos \omega_n t \right)$$

The maximum value of $v_C(t)$ approaches $2V_m$, giving the classical **2 p.u. over-voltage**.

**Step 4 — Practical case (with damping)**
With damping factor $\zeta$ and damped natural frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:
$$v_{C,\max} = V_m \left( 1 + e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \right)$$

For typical $\zeta \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ in HV systems, the **practical peak** lies between **1.2 p.u. and 1.8 p.u.**

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Numerical Problem (14-mark style sub-part):**
> A 3-phase, 11 kV industrial substation has a load of 4 MW at 0.72 p.f. (lagging). The utility requires the p.f. to be improved to 0.95 (lagging). Design a star-connected capacitor bank operating at 50 Hz.

**Step 1 — Original reactive power**
$$Q_1 = P \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.72)) = 4 \times \tan(43.95°) = 4 \times 0.963 = 3.852\ \text{MVAr}$$

**Step 2 — Desired reactive power**
$$Q_2 = P \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.95)) = 4 \times \tan(18.19°) = 4 \times 0.3287 = 1.315\ \text{MVAr}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating**
$$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 3.852 - 1.315 = 2.537\ \text{MVAr}$$

**Step 4 — Per-phase capacitance (star)**
$$C_{Y} = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^{\,2}} = \frac{2.537 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 50 \times (11000)^2} = 6.68 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F} = 6.68\ \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 5 — Standard unit selection**
Choose **15 kVAr** units at 11 kV/$\sqrt{3}$ = 6.35 kV. Number of units per phase:
$$N = \frac{2.537 \times 10^3 / 3}{15} = 56.4 \;\Rightarrow\; 57\ \text{units per phase (round up)}$$

> **Answer:** Star-connected capacitor bank, 2.54 MVAr, 57 × 15 kVAr units per phase, capacitance ≈ 6.68 µF per phase.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Capacitor Bank

| Load Profile | Recommended Bank Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Constant load, single PF | **Fixed bank** | Simple, low cost |
| Variable load, 24×7 plant | **Automatic switched (APFC)** | Maintains target PF dynamically |
| Heavy non-linear loads (rectifiers) | **Detuned bank (7%)** | Prevents harmonic resonance |
| Very heavy harmonics (arc furnace) | **Tuned filter (5th/7th)** | Active filtering + PF correction |
| Long transmission line | **Series compensation** | Increases transmittable power, stability |
| Critical voltage support | **Synchronous/static VAr (SVC)** | Dynamic, instantaneous response |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Automatic Power Factor Correction (APFC) System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GridA[Three Phase Grid 415V 50Hz] --> MCCB1[MCCB Main Breaker]
    MCCB1 --> CT1[CT Current Transformers]
    CT1 --> CTRL[APFC Relay Controller]
    CTRL -->|Step Commands| SwitchA[Contactor Bank 1]
    CTRL -->|Step Commands| SwitchB[Contactor Bank 2]
    CTRL -->|Step Commands| SwitchC[Contactor Bank 3]
    CTRL -->|Step Commands| SwitchD[Contactor Bank N]
    SwitchA --> CapA[Capacitor Stage 1]
    SwitchB --> CapB[Capacitor Stage 2]
    SwitchC --> CapC[Capacitor Stage 3]
    SwitchD --> CapN[Capacitor Stage N]
    CapA --> BusB[Compensated Bus]
    CapB --> BusB
    CapC --> BusB
    CapN --> BusB
    BusB --> Load[Industrial Load Motors Heaters]
    Load -->|Back to| GridA
    CT1 -->|Feedback Signal| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Display| HMI[PF Meter and HMI]
```

---

## 4.2 Detuned Capacitor Bank — Detailed Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BusH[HT Bus 11kV] --> VCB[VCB Vacuum Circuit Breaker]
    VCB --> CT2[Current Transformers]
    VCB --> Disc1[Isolator Disconnector]
    Disc1 --> Reactor[Detuning Reactor Ld 7 percent]
    Reactor --> Fuse[HRC Fuses One per Unit]
    Fuse --> CapUnit[Capacitor Units in Series Parallel]
    CapUnit --> NPT[Neutral Potential Transformer]
    NPT --> Earth[Earth Fault Detection]
    CT2 --> RelayA[Numerical Relay]
    RelayA -->|Trip| VCB
    CapUnit --> BusL[Compensated LT Side]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **7 % detuning reactor** is the heart of harmonic-immune capacitor banks. It raises the resonant harmonic order from 5 to 3.78, ensuring that the 5th, 7th, 11th, and 13th harmonics (all typical from 6-pulse rectifiers) see an **inductive** branch — preventing dangerous amplification.

---

## 4.3 Capacitor Bank Switching — Resonant Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Non Linear Load Generates Harmonics] --> Inj[Harmonic Currents Injected into Network]
    Inj --> BusPCC[At PCC Bus]
    BusPCC --> SourceInd[Source Inductance Ls]
    BusPCC --> CapBank[Shunt Capacitor Bank C]
    SourceInd --> ParaRes{Parallel Resonance Condition Ls vs C}
    CapBank --> ParaRes
    ParaRes -->|n_r near 5 or 7| Amp[Voltage Amplification of Harmonic]
    ParaRes -->|Far from harmonics| Safe[No Amplification Bank Functions Normally]
    Amp --> CapFail[Overheating and Capacitor Failure]
    Amp --> EqDamage[Damage to Sensitive Equipment]
    CapFail --> Mitigation[Add Detuning Reactor or Reduce Bank Size]
    EqDamage --> Mitigation
    Mitigation --> Resolved[System Stable]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison: Shunt vs Series vs Detuned — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHUNT
        A1[Shunt Capacitor Bank] --> A2[Connects in Parallel with Load]
        A2 --> A3[Supplies Qc Locally]
        A3 --> A4[Improves PF and Voltage]
    end
    subgraph SERIES
        B1[Series Capacitor Bank] --> B2[Inserts in Series with Line]
        B2 --> B3[Reduces Line Reactance XL]
        B3 --> B4[Increases Power Transfer and Stability]
    end
    subgraph DETUNED
        C1[Detuned Bank] --> C2[Capacitor in Series with Reactor Ld]
        C2 --> C3[Tuned to Frequencies below 5th Harmonic]
        C3 --> C4[PF Correction plus Harmonic Immunity]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Energisation Sequence with Pre-Insertion Resistor

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Source
    participant PIR as Pre Insertion Resistor
    participant MS as Main Switch
    participant CB as Capacitor Bank
    S->>PIR: Step 1 Close Auxiliary Contact T1
    Note over PIR,CB: Current flows through PIR damping transient
    PIR->>CB: Voltage established across bank in 5 to 8 cycles
    S->>MS: Step 2 Close Main Contact T2 after 60 ms
    Note over MS,CB: Main contact bypasses PIR full voltage applied
    S->>PIR: Step 3 Open Auxiliary Contact T3
    Note over CB: Capacitor bank in normal operation
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Aid:** The above sequence reduces the switching transient peak from ~2.0 p.u. (direct energisation) to ~1.2 p.u. — a 40 % reduction, sufficient to comply with IEEE 519 voltage distortion limits.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define a capacitor bank and list any **two** types of capacitor banks used in power systems.**  
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET751, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **capacitor bank** is a group of capacitor units connected in series and/or parallel, switched as a single entity, to deliver a specified reactive power (kVAr) to the network for power factor correction, voltage support, or harmonic filtering.

**Two types:**
1. **Shunt (parallel) capacitor bank** — connected across the supply, supplies leading reactive power to cancel lagging load reactive power.
2. **Series capacitor bank** — connected in series with the line, reduces effective line reactance, increasing power transfer capability.

> *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — types with brief function; 1 mark — clarity/presentation)*

---

### Question 2
**Q: What is detuning of a capacitor bank? Why is it necessary in the presence of non-linear loads?**  
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEET751, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Detuning** is the practice of connecting a small **inductor (reactor) in series** with the capacitor of a bank, so that the tuned (resonant) frequency of the resulting L-C branch lies **below the lowest dominant harmonic order** (typically below 5th harmonic).

It is necessary because:
- Non-linear loads (rectifiers, drives, arc furnaces) inject **harmonic currents** (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th).
- A pure shunt capacitor bank forms a **parallel resonant circuit** with the source inductance at a frequency $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{L_s C})$.
- If $f_r$ coincides with a harmonic frequency, **harmonic voltages are amplified** at the PCC, causing equipment damage.
- A 7 % detuning ensures the branch is **capacitive at 50 Hz** (correcting PF) but **inductive at the 5th harmonic and above** (preventing resonance).

> *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — detuning expression; 1 mark — necessity in non-linear environment)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **construction and working of a switched (automatic) capacitor bank** used for power factor correction in industrial installations. Describe the role of the APFC relay and contactors. **(7 marks)**  
`(a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET751, Module 2]

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A multi-stage LV capacitor panel consists of:
  1. **MCCB / isolator** as main switch.
  2. **Current Transformers (CTs)** for load current sensing.
  3. **APFC (Automatic Power Factor Controller) Relay** — micro-controller-based, samples current & voltage, computes PF, decides capacitor stage switching.
  4. **Capacitor contactors** with **pre-insertion resistors** for transient damping.
  5. **Capacitor units** in steps (e.g., 6 stages: 10-10-20-20-40-50 kVAr).
  6. **HRC fuses** for short-circuit protection.
  7. **Power factor meter / HMI** for display.
  8. **Cooling fans / ventilation.**

**Working:**
- The APFC relay continuously monitors load PF via CTs.
- If PF < target (e.g., 0.95), it switches **ON** the next capacitor stage using a contactor.
- If PF > target (over-compensated, leading), it switches **OFF** a stage.
- Switching is done with a **time delay** (typically 30–120 s) to prevent hunting.
- Capacitors are discharged through **bleed resistors** within 60 s of disconnection (per IEC 60831).

> *(3 marks — construction/diagram; 2 marks — APFC operation; 2 marks — contactor + protection role)*

---

**(b)** A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial load draws 80 kW at 0.70 p.f. (lagging). **(i)** Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank to improve the p.f. to 0.95 (lagging). **(ii)** Calculate the per-phase capacitance if the bank is connected in **delta**. **(iii)** If the source short-circuit MVA at the bus is 2.5 MVA, compute the **percentage voltage rise** at the bus due to bank switching. **(7 marks)**  
`(b) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET751, Module 2]

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Capacitor bank rating**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= P \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.70) = 80 \times \tan(45.57°) = 80 \times 1.020 = 81.6\ \text{kVAr} \\
Q_2 &= P \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.95) = 80 \times \tan(18.19°) = 80 \times 0.3287 = 26.30\ \text{kVAr} \\
Q_C &= Q_1 - Q_2 = 81.6 - 26.3 = \mathbf{55.3\ \text{kVAr}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Delta-connected capacitance (per phase)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f \, V_L^{\,2} \times 3} = \frac{55.3 \times 10^3}{2\pi \times 50 \times (415)^2 \times 3} \\
         &= \frac{55300}{1.626 \times 10^8} = 3.40 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{340\ \mu\text{F}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Voltage rise on switching**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V \% &= \frac{Q_C}{S_{sc}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0553}{2.5} \times 100 \\
            &= \mathbf{2.21\ \%}
\end{aligned}$$

> *(2 marks — Q_C calculation; 2 marks — capacitance; 1 mark — formula for ΔV; 1 mark — numerical evaluation; 1 mark — final answer in V at 415 V bus)*

**Voltage rise in volts:** $\Delta V = 0.0221 \times 415 = 9.17\ \text{V}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **resonant frequency of a shunt capacitor bank** with system source inductance. What is the **resonant harmonic order**? Explain how this leads to **harmonic amplification** and outline a **design guideline** to avoid such resonance. **(7 marks)**  
`(a) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEET751, Module 2]

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**  
At the PCC, the source inductance $L_s$ is in parallel with capacitor $C$ of the bank. The total admittance:
$$Y(\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega L_s} + j\omega C = j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L_s}\right)$$

Resonance occurs when $\text{Im}(Y) = 0$:
$$\omega_r C = \frac{1}{\omega_r L_s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_r = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_s C}}$$

$$\boxed{\; f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_s C}}\;}$$

**Resonant harmonic order:**
$$n_r = \frac{f_r}{f_1} = \sqrt{\frac{X_C}{X_L}}$$

**Harmonic amplification mechanism:**
At $f_r$, the parallel impedance of source and capacitor is **theoretically infinite**. Therefore, a small harmonic current injected by a non-linear load produces an **enormous voltage** at the bus. This voltage adds to the fundamental, distorting the supply, overheating capacitors, and tripping protection.

**Design guideline (KSEB/KTU practice):**
- Compute the **available harmonic orders** in the network.
- Size the bank such that $n_r$ is **at least 20 % below the lowest expected harmonic order**:
$$n_r \le 0.8 \times n_h^{(\text{lowest})}$$
For 5th harmonic: $n_r \le 4$, i.e., $Q_C \le S_{sc}/16$.
- For safety, install a **7 % detuning reactor**, giving $n_t = 3.78 < 5$.

> *(2 marks — derivation; 1 mark — harmonic order expression; 2 marks — amplification explanation; 2 marks — design guideline)*

---

**(b)** With the aid of a **single-line diagram**, explain the working of a **detuned capacitor bank** as a harmonic filter. Why is a 7 % detuning factor preferred in industrial practice? Calculate the **trap frequency** for a 7 % detuned, 50 Hz system. **(7 marks)**  
`(b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEET751, Module 2]

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Working of detuned bank (single-line diagram):**

```
            Bus ───┬──── Capacitor C ───┬──── (Detuning Reactor Ld) ─── Neutral
                  │                     │
                  └── HRC Fuse           │
                                        │
                                      To load side
```

The series combination of $C$ and $L_d$ forms a **tuned L-C trap**. The impedance of this branch is:
$$Z(\omega) = j\left(\omega L_d - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

- At the **trap (resonant) frequency** $f_t$, the impedance is **zero** — it acts as a short circuit for that harmonic.
- At **50 Hz**, $1/(\omega C) \gg \omega L_d$ → branch is **capacitive** → supplies kVAr.
- At **5th harmonic and above**, $\omega L_d > 1/(\omega C)$ → branch is **inductive** → does not amplify harmonics.

**Why 7 % preferred:**
1. **Trap frequency** is well below 5th harmonic (189 Hz vs 250 Hz), so the branch is **inductive at the dominant 5th harmonic**, preventing parallel resonance.
2. The 50 Hz reactive power is only **slightly reduced** (~ 2 %): $Q_{L_d} = p \cdot Q_C = 0.07 Q_C$. Negligible.
3. **Robust** against supply impedance variations — even if $X_L$ varies ± 50 %, the trap stays below 5th.
4. **Standardised by IEC 61642** and adopted by KSEB for industrial consumers.

**Calculation of trap frequency for 7 % detuning at 50 Hz:**
$$f_t = f_1 \sqrt{\frac{1}{p}} = 50 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{0.07}} = 50 \times 3.78 = \mathbf{189\ \text{Hz}}$$

> *(2 marks — single-line + working; 2 marks — 7 % justification; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — numerical 189 Hz; 1 mark — conclusions)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Capacitor Bank Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3** in capacitance formulas for 3-phase systems — students often write $C = Q/(\omega V^2)$ without the phase factor.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$** — for star banks, $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$; for delta banks, $V_{ph} = V_L$.
> 3. **Not specifying the system frequency (50 Hz)** — Indian examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing constant.
> 4. **Confusing "leading" and "lagging" PF** — over-compensation (leading PF) is also harmful; mention it once for full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the detuning reactor's small loss** ($p \cdot Q_C$) — when asked "effect on PF correction", state it explicitly.
> 6. **No mention of IEEE 519 / IEC 61642** standards — KTU values standard references highly.
> 7. **Units** — never write "Farad" without the prefix (µF, mF); same for kVAr vs MVAr.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Capacitor Banks (Module 2, PEEET751)**
> 
> ✅ Capacitor bank = assembly of capacitors, switches, fuses, relays → delivers **reactive power (kVAr)**.
> 
> ✅ Main types: **Shunt (PF correction), Series (line reactance), Detuned (harmonic-immune), Synchronous/Static (dynamic)**.
> 
> ✅ Required kVAr: $\;Q_C = P(\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2)$.
> 
> ✅ Capacitance formulas:  
>   • Star: $C_Y = 3 Q_C / (2\pi f V_L^2)$  
>   • Delta: $C_\Delta = Q_C / (2\pi f V_L^2)$
> 
> ✅ **Resonant harmonic order:** $n_r = \sqrt{X_C / X_L}$ — *critical for harmonic interaction*.
> 
> ✅ **Detuning factor:** $p = X_{L_d}/X_C$ — usually 7 % → trap at 189 Hz.
> 
> ✅ **Switching transients:** theoretical 2 p.u.; practical 1.2–1.8 p.u.; mitigated by **pre-insertion resistors / synchronous closing**.
> 
> ✅ **Voltage rise on switching:** $\Delta V \% = Q_C / S_{sc} \times 100$.
> 
> ✅ **Energy stored in capacitor:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — relevant for discharge circuits.
> 
> ✅ **Standards:** IEEE 18, IEEE 1036, IEEE 519, IEC 60831, IEC 61642.
> 
> ✅ **APFC panel** — micro-controller-based relay + stepped capacitors + contactors + CTs + bleed resistors.
> 
> ✅ **Always state**: 50 Hz, 3-phase, balanced, connection type (star/delta), and target PF in numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motors and Telecommunication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motors and Telecommunication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why Motors and Telecommunication Systems Matter in Power Quality

Power quality (PQ) disturbances rarely affect every load equally. **Electric motors** and **telecommunication (telecom) systems** sit at two opposite ends of the sensitivity spectrum, yet both are deeply vulnerable to voltage and current imperfections. Motors are the **largest energy consumers in industry (≈65-70% of all electrical energy)**, while telecom networks form the **nervous system of the digital economy**. A sub-cycle sag that goes unnoticed in a heater can stall a motor or corrupt a 10 Gbps data stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to map *each* PQ disturbance (sag, swell, harmonic, unbalance, flicker, transient, DC offset) to its *specific* effect on rotating machines and on telecom/computing equipment. Memorising the disturbance table is worth 3 to 7 marks every cycle.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of PQ Impact on Motors

**Definition (KTU-NEP 2020):**
> *"Power quality, with respect to AC induction and synchronous machines, is the conformity of the supplied voltage waveform to the sinusoidal, balanced, rated-magnitude, and rated-frequency reference, such that the machine operates within its nameplate thermal, mechanical, and insulation limits without premature derating or failure."*

In simple terms: a motor expects a **perfect sine wave at rated RMS voltage, rated frequency, with the three phases perfectly balanced**. Anything that deviates from this ideal injects extra losses, torque ripple, vibration, or insulation stress.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower and Turbine" Picture

Imagine a water tower feeding a turbine. The ideal supply is a **smooth, steady column of water at constant pressure** (rated voltage) flowing at a **constant rate** (rated frequency) into **three identical pipes** (balanced three-phase). 

- **Voltage sag** → pressure suddenly drops, turbine slows and may stall. 
- **Voltage swell** → pipe bursts under high pressure. 
- **Harmonics** → pulsating surges mixed with the steady flow, making the turbine vibrate. 
- **Unbalance** → one pipe has lower pressure, the turbine wobbles and overheats on that side. 
- **Transients** → sudden water hammer, damaging the bearings. 

The motor reacts to each disturbance **physically** (heat, torque, vibration), while a telecom system reacts **electronically** (logic errors, data loss).

## 1.3 Formal Definition of PQ Impact on Telecommunication Systems

**Definition:**
> *"Telecommunication equipment and data networks are sensitive electronic systems whose reliable operation depends on a clean, low-impedance, properly grounded power interface that is free from common-mode noise, harmonics, transients, and frequency deviations, in accordance with standards such as IEEE 1100 (Emerald Book), ITIC (CBEMA), and IEC 62018."*

### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Whispering Library"

A telecom cabinet is like a **library where people whisper sensitive data**. Even a small noise — a door slamming (transient), a vibrating floor (harmonic), or uneven lighting (sag) — corrupts the message. Power quality for telecom is essentially **noise hygiene**.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Must Memorise)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Limit | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Sag Magnitude | $V_{sag}$ | 0.1 – 0.9 pu | IEEE 1159 |
| Swell Magnitude | $V_{swell}$ | 1.1 – 1.8 pu | IEEE 1159 |
| Voltage Unbalance Factor | $VUF$ / $LVUR$ | ≤ 2 % (motors) | NEMA MG-1 |
| Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage) | $THD_v$ | ≤ 5 % | IEEE 519 |
| Total Demand Distortion (Current) | $TDD$ | 5 – 8 % | IEEE 519 |
| Flicker Severity (Short-term) | $P_{st}$ | ≤ 1.0 | IEC 61000-4-15 |
| ITI (CBEMA) Tolerance | $V_{min}$, $V_{max}$ | 0.9 pu / 1.1 pu | IEEE 1100 |

> [!NOTE]
> The constants **0.9 pu** and **1.1 pu** are the steady-state tolerance envelope of the **ITI (Information Technology Industry Council) curve**, formerly known as the **CBEMA curve**. Any voltage excursion outside this envelope for longer than 20 ms will typically cause mis-operation or damage to IT and telecom equipment.

## 1.5 Why These Two Loads Are Studied Together

Both categories are **widespread** (motors in every industry; telecom in every office) and both suffer **silent degradation** rather than dramatic failure. KTU examiners frequently set comparison questions asking the student to contrast the *physical* response of a motor versus the *electronic* response of a telecom switch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Quality Disturbances Affecting Motors

### 2.1.1 Voltage Sag (Dip) on Induction Motors

A voltage sag between **0.1 pu and 0.9 pu** lasting **0.5 cycles to 1 minute** causes:

1. **Reduced air-gap torque proportional to $V^2$** — critical for constant-torque loads.
2. **Increased slip** (s = (ns - nr)/ns) because mechanical power demand is unchanged but electrical input drops.
3. **Increased stator current** (initially) as the motor tries to deliver rated torque.
4. **DC link under-voltage trips** in modern VFD-driven motors.
5. **Stalling** if the sag depth × duration exceeds the **motor's sensitivity curve**.

The torque relation:

$$T \propto \frac{s \cdot R_2 / s}{(R_2/s)^2 + (X_2)^2} \cdot V_1^2$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{T_{sag}}{T_{rated}} = \left( \frac{V_{sag}}{V_{rated}} \right)^2 \cdot f(s, V)$$

A **20% sag** (0.8 pu) reduces available torque to **64%** of rated, often insufficient for compressors, conveyors, or elevators.

### 2.1.2 Voltage Swell on Motors

- Causes **insulation stress** and **saturation** in the magnetic core.
- Current may rise **20-40%**, accelerating winding heating.
- Coupled with unbalance, it can excite **negative-sequence** heating.

### 2.1.3 Harmonics in Motors

Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) circulate in the **neutral** (in star-connected machines, they add arithmetically), causing neutral overheating.

Positive-sequence harmonics (5th: actually **negative-sequence**; 7th: positive; 11th: negative; 13th: positive) create:

- **Negative torque** components (5th, 11th) → reduced effective torque.
- **Forward torque** components (7th, 13th) → speed variation.
- **Pulsating torques** at 6n times fundamental frequency (e.g., 300 Hz for 6th harmonic on a 50 Hz system) → shaft fatigue and bearing wear.

The effective RMS current including harmonics:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{H} I_h^2} = I_1 \cdot \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$$

The **harmonic loss factor** is:

$$K_{loss} = \sum_{h=1}^{H} h^{0.5} \left( \frac{I_h}{I_1} \right)^2$$

(per IEEE 519 / P. Pillai derivation). This is critical for motor derating.

### 2.1.4 Voltage Unbalance — The Most Damaging Steady-State PQ Issue

Defined by NEMA MG-1 as **Line Voltage Unbalance Ratio (LVUR)** in percent:

$$LVUR\ (\%) = \frac{\text{Maximum deviation from average line voltage}}{\text{Average line voltage}} \times 100$$

Equivalent **Voltage Unbalance Factor (VUF)** using symmetrical components:

$$VUF\ (\%) = \frac{\vert V_2 \vert}{\vert V_1 \vert} \times 100$$

For small unbalances, $V_2 \approx LVUR \times V_1$.

**The famous NEMA derating rule** for induction motors under voltage unbalance:

$$\Delta T\ (\% \text{ rated temperature rise}) = 2 \cdot (LVUR)^2$$

This means a **2% unbalance causes an 8% rise in temperature** — exceeding the insulation class B limit of 80 K above 40 °C ambient.

### 2.1.5 Transients and Switching Surges

- **Impulsive transients** (1-100 µs, 1.5-5 kV): damage motor insulation, especially in VFD-fed motors with long cable leads (**standing wave / reflected wave phenomenon**). The peak voltage at motor terminals can reach **2 pu** when cable length exceeds critical length:

$$L_{critical} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$$

where $v$ is the cable propagation velocity (~150 m/µs for typical XLPE) and $t_r$ is the IGBT rise time (~0.1 µs). For $t_r = 0.1\,\mu s$, $L_{critical} \approx 7.5$ m.

- **Oscillatory transients** (5-500 µs, 100-4000 Hz): excite winding resonances.

### 2.1.6 DC Offset and Flicker

- **DC offset** causes half-cycle saturation, severe heating, and acoustic noise.
- **Flicker** (rapid voltage fluctuations 0.5-25 Hz) causes visible motor speed oscillation, audible noise, and process variation.

## 2.2 Power Quality Disturbances Affecting Telecommunication Systems

### 2.2.1 Common-Mode vs. Differential-Mode Noise

Telecom racks measure **common-mode noise** (line-to-ground) which is typically 1-50 V, while IT equipment tolerates < 1 V. Standards: **IEEE 1100 (Emerald Book)** recommends < 0.5 V common-mode.

### 2.2.2 Effect of Each PQ Event

| PQ Event | Effect on Telecom Equipment |
|---|---|
| Sag < 0.9 pu for >20 ms | PSU capacitor discharge, logic reset |
| Sag < 0.7 pu | Hard shutdown, call drops |
| Swell | Over-voltage on DC bus, capacitor damage |
| Impulsive transient (kV) | Port blowout, PCB tracking |
| Oscillatory transient | Inter-symbol interference, BER ↑ |
| Harmonics (esp. 3rd, 9th) | Neutral overheating in server rooms |
| High-frequency noise (RF) | Bit error rate degradation |
| Ground potential rise | Equipment damage, safety hazard |
| Frequency deviation | PLL unlock in synchronisation circuits |

### 2.2.3 ITIC (CBEMA) Curve — The Single Most Important Chart

The ITI curve plots **allowable voltage magnitude vs. duration**. It defines four zones:

- **No-effect zone** (steady-state, ±10%).
- **Prohibited zone** (instantaneous, ±30% and beyond).
- **No-damage zone** (brief excursions).
- The curve is **asymmetric** — equipment is more tolerant of sags than swells (due to capacitor over-voltage).

### 2.2.4 Telecom-Specific Standards

- **IEEE 1100 (Emerald Book)** — powering and grounding of telecom/IT.
- **ANSI/TIA-942** — data centre infrastructure.
- **ITU-T K.20/K.21** — resistibility of telecom equipment.
- **IEC 62018** — power consumption of telecom equipment.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** for Part B answers on this topic. Memorise it in the order presented.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T \propto V^2$ | Motor torque vs. voltage | N·m |
| 2 | $LVUR\ \% = \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{\bar{V}} \times 100$ | NEMA line voltage unbalance ratio | % |
| 3 | $V_2 = \dfrac{V_{ab} + a^2 V_{bc} + a V_{ca}}{3}$ | Negative-sequence voltage | V |
| 4 | $I_2 \approx \dfrac{V_2}{Z_2} \approx 5\text{–}7 \cdot I_2^{rated}$ per 1% unbalance | Negative-sequence current | A |
| 5 | $\Delta T\ \% = 2 \cdot (LVUR)^2$ | Temperature rise due to unbalance | % |
| 6 | $K_{derate} = \dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{rated}}$ from NEMA chart | Motor derating factor | pu |
| 7 | $I_{rms} = I_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$ | RMS current with harmonics | A |
| 8 | $THD_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100$ | Voltage THD | % |
| 9 | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100$ | Total demand distortion | % |
| 10 | $f_{pulsation} = 6n \cdot f_1$ (for n-th harmonic in 3-$\phi$) | Torque pulsation frequency | Hz |
| 11 | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$ | VFD reflected wave critical cable length | m |
| 12 | $V_{peak,terminal} = 2 V_{DC,bus}$ (worst case) | Motor terminal peak voltage | V |
| 13 | $P_{st} \le 1.0$ | Short-term flicker severity | pu |
| 14 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}$ | Telecom signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 15 | $BER \propto e^{-SNR}$ | Bit error rate vs. SNR | — |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Industrial plants**: A 2% unbalance in a 100 kW motor wastes ≈ **1.6 kW** as extra heat, costing ~₹1.2 lakh/year (Indian industrial tariff).
- **Data centres**: A 100 ms sag can crash 10,000 servers; downtime costs USD 9,000-25,000 per minute.
- **Telecom towers**: PQ issues cause 30% of all cellular downtime in India (TRAI reports).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NEMA Motor Derating Due to Voltage Unbalance

**Statement:** Show that for a small percentage voltage unbalance $u$ (in pu), the per-unit negative-sequence voltage is approximately $u$, and the resulting per-unit current unbalance is approximately $6u$ to $7u$ in typical induction motors.

**Starting Point (Symmetrical Components):**
Let the three line voltages be $V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}$. Define the unit phasors:

$$a = e^{j120°} = -\tfrac{1}{2} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad a^2 = e^{j240°} = -\tfrac{1}{2} - j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

The positive-sequence voltage:

$$V_1 = \frac{V_{ab} + a V_{bc} + a^2 V_{ca}}{3}$$

The negative-sequence voltage:

$$V_2 = \frac{V_{ab} + a^2 V_{bc} + a V_{ca}}{3}$$

**Step 1 — Assume small unbalance.**
For small deviations from the average line voltage $V_{avg}$, expand to first order. The magnitude of $V_2$ equals the magnitude of the deviation (this is the geometric meaning of $VUF$).

Therefore:

$$\vert V_2 \vert \approx u \cdot \vert V_1 \vert$$

where $u$ is the per-unit unbalance factor.

**Step 2 — Negative-sequence impedance of induction motor.**
At standstill, the negative-sequence impedance equals the positive-sequence impedance (rotor not turning relative to the negative-sequence field):

$$Z_2 \approx Z_{locked-rotor} = R_{locked} + j X_{locked}$$

**Step 3 — Negative-sequence current.**

$$I_2 = \frac{V_2}{Z_2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical motor data.**
For a typical NEMA Design B induction motor at rated slip, the negative-sequence impedance is **6 to 7 times smaller** than the positive-sequence impedance (because $V_1$ sees the magnetising branch in parallel with the rotor, but $V_2$ sees a near-short-circuited rotor through the leakage path only).

$$Z_1 \approx (6 \text{ to } 7) \cdot Z_2$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{V_2 / Z_2}{V_1 / Z_1} = \frac{V_2}{V_1} \cdot \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = u \cdot (6 \text{ to } 7)$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,I_2 \approx 6u \cdot I_1\,}$$

**Engineering consequence:** A 2% unbalance (u = 0.02) gives a 12% negative-sequence current. Since the negative-sequence rotor copper loss scales as $I_2^2 R_2$, the rotor heating rises sharply.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Rise Due to Voltage Unbalance

**Statement:** Derive the NEMA rule $\Delta T\ \% = 2 \cdot (LVUR)^2$.

**Step 1 — Rotor copper loss components.**
Total rotor loss = positive-sequence loss + negative-sequence loss:

$$P_{rotor} = I_1^2 R_2 + I_2^2 R_2$$

(The negative-sequence field is at slip ≈ 2 for the rotor, so rotor frequency for $V_2$ is near standstill frequency; however the resistive component dominates the loss calculation in the NEMA approximation.)

**Step 2 — Substitute the unbalance relation.**

$$P_{rotor} = I_1^2 R_2 \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{I_2}{I_1} \right)^2 \right] = I_1^2 R_2 \left[ 1 + (6u)^2 \right]$$

$$P_{rotor} = I_1^2 R_2 \left[ 1 + 36 u^2 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Temperature-rise linearisation.**
The NEMA standard linearises the rotor heating around rated temperature rise $T_0$ (e.g., 80 K for Class B insulation):

$$\Delta T\ \% = \frac{P_{rotor} - P_{rotor}^{rated}}{P_{rotor}^{rated}} \times 100$$

For a 2% unbalance the empirical rule matches the simplified first-order result:

$$\Delta T\ \% \approx 2 \cdot u^2 \times 100$$

If $u$ is expressed in percent, $u_\% = 100 u$, then:

$$\Delta T\ \% = 2 \cdot \left( \frac{u_\%}{100} \right)^2 \times 100 = 0.02 \cdot u_\%^2 / 10 = \text{...}$$

Let us restate using the textbook NEMA form: **$u$ is already in percent**, then:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta T\ \% = 2 \cdot (u_\%)^2\,}$$

**Numerical example:** For $u_\% = 2\%$, $\Delta T\ \% = 2 \times 4 = 8\%$. The motor's 80 K rise becomes $80 \times 1.08 = 86.4$ K, exceeding the Class B limit.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque Pulsation Frequency from Harmonics

A 3-phase stator MMF with fundamental plus harmonics produces rotating fields whose speeds and directions differ.

For the $h$-th space harmonic (using electrical harmonics, the $h$-th time harmonic creates a 3-phase system with phase sequence $h$):

- $h = 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, \ldots$ → **positive sequence** (forward rotating).
- $h = 2, 5, 8, 11, 14, \ldots$ → **negative sequence** (backward rotating).
- $h = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$ → **zero sequence** (no torque).

The instantaneous torque from a harmonic pair $(h_1, h_2)$ pulsates at:

$$f_{pulsation} = \vert h_1 - h_2 \vert \cdot f_1$$

For the 5th and 7th harmonics acting against the fundamental field:

$$f_{pulsation} = \vert 5 - 1 \vert \cdot 50 = 200\ Hz \quad (\text{or } 240\ Hz \text{ for 60 Hz})$$

A more general and widely used result is the **6n times fundamental** rule for **non-triplen odd harmonics**, derived from the interaction of forward and backward fields of the same harmonic with itself and with the fundamental:

$$f_{pulsation} = 6n \cdot f_1, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

For $f_1 = 50$ Hz, the dominant pulsations are at $300$ Hz (5th & 7th), $600$ Hz (11th & 13th), and $900$ Hz (17th & 19th) Hz.

**Torque amplitude** (approximate, Pillai's analysis):

$$T_h \approx \frac{3 p V_h V_1}{\omega_s Z_h Z_1}$$

where $p$ = pole pairs and $Z_h$ = harmonic impedance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — RMS Current with Harmonics and Derating

For a motor delivering rated mechanical power $P_m$:

$$P_m = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos \phi_1 \cdot \eta$$

When harmonics are present, the **same mechanical power** is delivered, but additional losses occur. The new total RMS current:

$$I_{rms} = I_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$$

The new stator copper loss:

$$P_{cu,new} = I_{rms}^2 R_1 = I_1^2 (1 + THD_i^2) R_1$$

Excess loss fraction:

$$\frac{\Delta P_{cu}}{P_{cu,rated}} = THD_i^2$$

For Class B insulation to stay within temperature limits, the **derating factor** is:

$$K_{derate} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A motor with $THD_i = 20\%$ can deliver only $1 / \sqrt{1.04} = 0.98$ pu of its rated mechanical power without overheating. With $THD_i = 50\%$, it drops to **0.894 pu** — a 10.6% derating.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reflected Wave at VFD-Fed Motor Terminals

**Scenario:** A PWM VFD with DC bus voltage $V_{DC}$ feeds a long motor cable of surge impedance $Z_0 \approx 50\ \Omega$ to a motor with impedance $Z_m \gg Z_0$.

The transmission-line reflection coefficient at the motor end:

$$\Gamma_m = \frac{Z_m - Z_0}{Z_m + Z_0} \approx +1 \quad (\text{open circuit})$$

The coefficient at the VFD end:

$$\Gamma_s = \frac{Z_s - Z_0}{Z_s + Z_0} \approx -1 \quad (\text{IGBT approximates short on turn-off})$$

A wave launched by the IGBT of amplitude $V_{DC}/2$ travels to the motor, reflects with $\Gamma_m = +1$, returns, and re-reflects with $\Gamma_s = -1$. After one round trip:

$$V_{terminal} = V_{DC}/2 + (V_{DC}/2) = V_{DC}$$

After a second constructive round trip:

$$V_{terminal} = 2 V_{DC} = 1.5 \cdot V_{LL} \quad (\text{for 400 V class, } V_{DC} \approx 565 V, V_{peak} \approx 1130 V)$$

**Time for first peak:**
$$t_{peak} = \frac{2 L}{v}$$

For a 50 m cable at $v = 150$ m/µs: $t_{peak} = 667$ ns. If the IGBT rise time is shorter than this, **double-pulsing** occurs, doubling the voltage stress.

**The critical cable length** (Pillai / A. von Jouanne):

$$L_{crit} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$$

Mitigations: **dV/dt filters**, **sine-wave filters**, or **short motor leads (< 7 m)** for 400 V class.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Bit Error Rate in Telecom Channels

For binary signalling over an additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channel:

$$BER = Q\!\left( \sqrt{2 \cdot SNR} \right) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi \cdot SNR}} \cdot e^{-SNR}$$

A 3 dB drop in SNR **roughly doubles** the BER. Power-line harmonics and impulsive noise directly attack the SNR, making power quality a **determinant of digital communication quality** when cables share the same raceway.

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Motor vs. Telecom Response

| PQ Disturbance | Motor Response | Telecom Response |
|---|---|---|
| Sag 0.7 pu, 200 ms | Torque drops, may stall, contactor drops out | PSU regulates; UPS bridges; if UPS absent, hard reset |
| Swell 1.2 pu, 500 ms | Saturation, heating, insulation stress | MOV clamps, capacitor over-voltage |
| Harmonics (5th, 7th) | Torque ripple, bearing damage, neutral heating | Neutral heating, rectifier regulation error, clock jitter |
| Unbalance 3% | 18% extra heating → derate to 0.88 pu | PSU imbalance, component stress |
| Impulse 4 kV | Winding insulation breakdown | Port damage, PCB tracking, modem failure |
| Flicker $P_{st}=1.2$ | Speed oscillation, audible hum | Display flicker, scanner errors |
| DC offset | Half-cycle saturation, vibration | Transformer saturation in PSU |
| Noise 50 V common-mode | Bearing currents (VFD only) | Logic errors, BER ↑, safety hazard |
