# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"List any three effects of voltage unbalance on a three-phase induction motor and state the NEMA rule for temperature rise."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Negative-sequence current** circulates in the rotor, roughly 6-7 times the per-unit voltage unbalance, producing additional $I_2^2 R_2$ copper loss. *(1 mark)*
2. **Torque pulsation** at twice line frequency causes shaft fatigue, audible noise, and bearing wear. *(1 mark)*
3. **Derating** of the motor is required to keep the winding temperature within the insulation class limit. *(1 mark)*

**NEMA rule:**

$$\Delta T\ \% = 2 \times (LVUR\ \%)^2$$

A 2% unbalance causes **8% extra temperature rise**, exceeding Class B limits.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*"Differentiate between the responses of an induction motor and a telecommunication switch-mode power supply to a 200 ms voltage sag of 0.7 pu magnitude."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Induction motor:** The air-gap torque drops to $0.7^2 = 0.49$ pu, the slip increases sharply, and the motor stalls if the load torque exceeds 49% of rated. The stator current rises to 2-3 pu during the sag. *(1½ marks)*
- **Telecom SMPS:** A well-designed SMPS regulates the DC bus for sags down to 0.8 pu; below 0.7 pu the bulk capacitor discharges, the DC bus drops below the under-voltage lockout (~300 V for 400 V class), and the PSU shuts down, dropping the call/connection. UPS bridges the gap in critical sites. *(1½ marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *With the help of symmetrical components, derive the relationship between the percentage voltage unbalance and the negative-sequence current in a three-phase induction motor. Show that a small per-unit unbalance $u$ causes a per-unit negative-sequence current of approximately $6u$ to $7u$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 15 kW, 400 V, 4-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase induction motor operates on a supply with a measured line voltage unbalance of 3.2%. Using the NEMA derating rule and the empirical derating chart, find (i) the percentage temperature rise, (ii) the permissible derated output, and (iii) the new full-load current at the same power factor.* **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Symmetrical Component Definition.**
Define phasors $a = e^{j 120°}$, $a^2 = e^{j 240°}$. The negative-sequence voltage is:

$$V_2 = \frac{V_{ab} + a^2 V_{bc} + a V_{ca}}{3}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Approximation for small unbalance.**
For small deviations, the magnitude $\vert V_2 \vert$ equals the per-unit unbalance factor $u$ times $\vert V_1 \vert$:

$$\vert V_2 \vert \approx u \cdot \vert V_1 \vert$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Positive and negative sequence impedances.**
The positive-sequence impedance $Z_1$ at rated slip includes the magnetising branch in parallel with the rotor. The negative-sequence impedance $Z_2$ is dominated by the rotor leakage inductance (the negative-sequence field sees a near-stationary rotor, so $s \approx 2$, putting the rotor resistance beyond the operating point).

**Step 4 (1 mark): Current ratio.**

$$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \frac{V_2 / Z_2}{V_1 / Z_1} = u \cdot \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Empirical ratio.**
For NEMA Design B motors, $Z_1 / Z_2 \approx 6$ to $7$.

**Step 6 (1 mark): Result.**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{I_2}{I_1} \approx 6u \text{ to } 7u\,}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark): Consequence.**
The rotor copper loss has a term $I_2^2 R_2 \propto 36 u^2$, which dominates the heating effect.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 15$ kW, $V_{LL} = 400$ V, 4-pole, 50 Hz, $LVUR = 3.2\%$.

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute percentage temperature rise.**

$$\Delta T\ \% = 2 \times (3.2)^2 = 2 \times 10.24 = 20.48\ \%$$

Rated Class B temperature rise is 80 K → new rise is $80 \times 1.2048 = 96.4$ K, which **exceeds the limit**.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Read derating factor from NEMA MG-1 / Pillai's table.**
For $LVUR = 3.2\%$, the empirical derating factor is:

$$K_{derate} \approx 0.92 \text{ pu}$$

(Corresponds to a temperature rise limit of 80 K. If the limit were 90 K, $K \approx 0.96$.)

**Step 3 (1 mark): Derated output.**

$$P_{new} = 0.92 \times 15 = 13.8\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): New full-load current.**
Rated FLC:

$$I_{rated} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta} = \frac{15000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.9} = \frac{15000}{530.4} \approx 28.3\ \text{A}$$

(Assuming $\eta = 0.9$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ — both can be stated if not given.)

New FLC at derated output:

$$I_{new} = 0.92 \times 28.3 \approx 26.0\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Conclusion.**
The motor must be derated to **13.8 kW** and **26 A** to avoid insulation failure.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain, with neat sketches, the phenomenon of reflected wave (standing wave) at the terminals of a motor fed by a PWM VFD through a long cable. Derive the critical cable length beyond which the motor insulation is stressed to twice the DC bus voltage. How is this issue mitigated?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss in detail the effect of supply voltage harmonics on a three-phase induction motor. Derive the expression for RMS current including harmonics, and compute the derating factor for a motor with $THD_i = 30\%$.* **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — Reflected Wave (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): PWM VFD output.**
A PWM inverter produces a stepped waveform with rise times of 0.1-1 µs. Each switching edge launches a transmission-line wave into the motor cable.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Reflection mechanism.**
The reflection coefficient at the motor end (open-circuit) is $\Gamma_m \approx +1$. The wave reflects with full voltage doubling. After reflecting back at the VFD ($\Gamma_s \approx -1$, but the next pulse adds), the terminal reaches:

$$V_{peak} = V_{DC} + V_{DC} = 2 V_{DC}$$

For 400 V class: $V_{DC} \approx 565$ V, $V_{peak} \approx 1130$ V → ~1.9 pu of rated RMS.

**Step 3 (1 mark): Critical cable length.**

$$L_{crit} = \frac{v \cdot t_r}{2}$$

With $v = 150$ m/µs (XLPE) and $t_r = 0.1$ µs:

$$L_{crit} = \frac{150 \times 0.1}{2} = 7.5\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Mitigations.**
- **dV/dt filter** at VFD output (reactor + RC snubber) — slows the edge to > 0.5 µs.
- **Sine-wave filter** (LC) — converts PWM to near-sinusoidal output.
- **Short motor leads** (< 7 m) — physically relocate the motor.
- **Inverter-duty motors** with reinforced winding insulation (Class H).
- **Common-mode chokes** to reduce bearing currents.

#### Part (b) — Harmonic Effects (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Harmonic effects on motors.**
- 5th, 11th → **negative-sequence** torque (reverse rotating) reducing net torque.
- 7th, 13th → **positive-sequence** torque (forward rotating) causing speed variation.
- 3rd, 9th → **zero-sequence** currents in neutral causing overheating.
- Torque pulsations at $6n f_1$ (300, 600, 900 Hz for 50 Hz fundamental) → bearing and shaft fatigue.

**Step 2 (2 marks): RMS current derivation.**

$$I_{rms}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{H} I_h^2 = I_1^2 + I_2^2 + I_3^2 + \ldots = I_1^2 \left(1 + \sum_{h=2}^{H} \left( \frac{I_h}{I_1} \right)^2 \right)$$

By definition, $THD_i^2 = \sum_{h=2}^{H} (I_h / I_1)^2$:

$$I_{rms} = I_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Derating formula.**
To keep stator copper loss at rated value with harmonic heating:

$$I_1 = \frac{I_{rated}}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$$

Therefore derating factor:

$$K_{derate} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Numerical calculation.**
For $THD_i = 30\% = 0.3$:

$$K_{derate} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.09}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.09}} = \frac{1}{1.0440} = 0.9578\ \text{pu}$$

The motor can deliver only **95.78%** of its rated mechanical output. For a 15 kW motor:

$$P_{new} = 0.9578 \times 15 = 14.37\ \text{kW}$$

**Conclusion:** A 30% current THD forces an approximately **4.2% derating** to prevent insulation overheating.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Marks Lost):**
> 1. **Confusing LVUR with VUF.** LVUR uses line-to-line voltages with a max-deviation definition; VUF uses symmetrical components. They are *not identical* for asymmetric unbalances. KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark if these are used interchangeably without stating the assumption of small/symmetric unbalance.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V^2$ dependence of torque.** A common error is to say "torque drops by 20% for a 20% sag." It actually drops to 64% because $T \propto V^2$.
> 3. **Omitting units and standards.** Always cite NEMA MG-1, IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, or ITI (CBEMA) where applicable. Examiners award a "professional-engineering" half-mark for citing the standard.
> 4. **In derating problems, students often forget to multiply the new current by the same power factor** when computing the new kW output. Show all intermediate steps.
> 5. **For telecom questions, mention the ITIC (formerly CBEMA) curve by name** — it is a guaranteed 1-2 mark item.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Induction motor torque $\propto V^2$** — a 20% sag = 64% torque.
- [x] **NEMA derating rule:** $\Delta T\ \% = 2 \times (LVUR\ \%)^2$.
- [x] **Negative-sequence current:** $I_2 \approx 6u$ to $7u$ per unit unbalance.
- [x] **Torque pulsation frequency:** $f_p = 6n f_1$ (n = 1, 2, 3, …) for 3-phase machines.
- [x] **RMS current with harmonics:** $I_{rms} = I_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$.
- [x] **Motor derating factor due to harmonics:** $K = 1 / \sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}$.
- [x] **Reflected wave critical cable length:** $L_{crit} = v t_r / 2$ ≈ 7.5 m for typical 400 V VFD.
- [x] **Peak motor terminal voltage:** up to $2 V_{DC}$ ≈ 1130 V for 400 V class.
- [x] **ITI (CBEMA) envelope:** ±10% steady state, ±30% transient; outside ⇒ damage.
- [x] **ITU-T K.20, IEEE 1100 (Emerald Book)** for telecom PQ standards.
- [x] **Common-mode noise limit** for telecom: < 0.5 V (IEEE 1100).
- [x] **Triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th) add in neutral of star-connected motors.
- [x] **Flicker limit:** $P_{st} \le 1.0$, $P_{lt} \le 0.65$ (IEC 61000-4-15).
- [x] **Derating action: reduce mechanical load, not voltage** (always).

### 🔑 Three "Must-Draw" Diagrams for the Exam
1. **NEMA derating curve** (P vs. LVUR) — show K factor fall-off.
2. **ITI/CBEMA curve** — sketch the four zones with magnitudes and durations.
3. **Reflected wave** — VFD → cable → motor with double voltage annotation.

### 🎯 Examiner's Favourite Sub-Topics
1. Derivation of NEMA derating from symmetrical components (asked almost every cycle).
2. Numeric on $THD_i$ derating (one-step easy 7-mark problem).
3. Comparison table: motor vs. telecom response to sags and harmonics.
4. ITIC (CBEMA) curve interpretation (definition + sketch = 3 marks guaranteed).
5. VFD reflected wave and critical cable length (modern KTU favourite).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Triplen Harmonics on Neutral Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Effect of Triplen Harmonics on Neutral Current # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Triplen Harmonics** are odd-order harmonics whose frequency is an odd multiple of the **3rd harmonic** (i.e., the **3rd, 9th, 15th, 21st, 27th,** and so on). The general order is given by the sequence: $$h = 3(2k - 1), \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ That is, $h \in \{3, 9, 15, 21, 27, \ldots\}$. In a **three-phase, four-wire (3-φ, 4-W) system**, triplen harmonics exhibit a unique property — they are **zero-sequence harmonics** and add **constructively (in-phase)** in the common neutral conductor, rather than cancelling out as other harmonic orders do. The mathematical order of the system, when each phase is shifted by $120°$, causes the phase shift for the $h^{th}$ harmonic to become $h \times 120°$. For triplen orders ($h = 3k$), this shift equals a full multiple of $360°$, so all three phases align in phase. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Triplen harmonics are the only harmonic family that flows in the **neutral conductor** of a balanced 3-φ, 4-W system. They are the dominant cause of **neutral conductor overheating** in modern commercial installations rich in non-linear loads (PCs, LEDs, SMPS, VFDs, CFLs). ## 1.2 The Concept in Plain English (Intuitive Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "Three Friends Walking in a Circle":** > > Imagine three friends (Phases A, B, C) walking around a circular track. Normally, they stay 120° apart — when one is at the "12 o'clock" position, the next is at "4 o'clock," and the third is at "8 o'clock." This way, no matter where they are, their positions **balance out** (like a well-balanced three-phase system where neutral current is zero). > > Now, suppose the friends take **giant steps** that are exactly 3 times their normal stride. After the giant step, friend A advances by $3 \times 120° = 360°$ — meaning he has come **full circle** and is back at the **same position** as the others! All three friends are now standing at the **exact same spot** simultaneously. > > When this happens, instead of the three positions **cancelling** (vector sum = 0), they all **pile on top of each other**, and the total magnitude becomes **3×** a single friend. The neutral conductor in a building is like the "ground" that has to carry this piled-up crowd — leading to **overloading, overheating, and fire risk**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Power frequency in India:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (so $3^{rd}$ harmonic = 150 Hz, $9^{th}$ = 450 Hz, $15^{th}$ = 750 Hz) - **Standard neutral sizing rule (IEEE / NEC):** Neutral conductor is normally **equal to** phase conductor in 3-φ, 4-W systems - **With triplen harmonics:** Neutral current can reach **1.5 to 3×** the phase RMS current - **K-rated Transformer:** A transformer designed to handle the additional heating from triplen harmonic currents (K-factor ratings like K-4, K-13, K-20) --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Triplen Harmonics are Zero-Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic) Let the three phase currents of a balanced 3-φ, 4-W system (fundamental) be: $$i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_b(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$i_c(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$ For the $h^{th}$ harmonic, the phase shifts get **multiplied by $h$** because the harmonic completes $h$ cycles in the time the fundamental completes one: $$i_{a,h}(t) = I_h \sin(h\omega t)$$ $$i_{b,h}(t) = I_h \sin(h\omega t - h \cdot 120°)$$ $$i_{c,h}(t) = I_h \sin(h\omega t + h \cdot 120°)$$ The neutral conductor carries the **sum** of the three phase currents (by KCL at the neutral node): $$i_n(t) = i_a(t) + i_b(t) + i_c(t)$$ Using the trigonometric identity for sum of three sinusoids equispaced by $120°$ multiplied by $h$: $$i_{n,h}(t) = I_h \left[\sin(h\omega t) + \sin(h\omega t - h \cdot 120°) + \sin(h\omega t + h \cdot 120°)\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Three Cases of Sequence Rotation for h-th Harmonic:** > > | Harmonic Order ($h$) | $h \times 120°$ Phase Shift | Sequence Type | Neutral Contribution | > |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| > | $1, 7, 13, 19, \ldots$ | $120°, 840°, 1560° \rightarrow 120°$ | Positive (abc) | $I_{n,h} = 0$ | > | $5, 11, 17, 23, \ldots$ | $600°, 1320° \rightarrow 240°$ | Negative (acb) | $I_{n,h} = 0$ | > | **$3, 9, 15, 21, \ldots$** | **$360°, 1080° \rightarrow 0°$** | **Zero (in-phase)** | $I_{n,h} = 3 I_h$ | ## 2.2 Physical Origin of Triplen Harmonics The **3rd harmonic (and its odd multiples)** are generated by single-phase non-linear loads that draw current in **half-wave pulses** (e.g., the half-wave rectifier inside a desktop PC SMPS, laptop chargers, LED drivers, dimmers, CFLs). Because most building distribution is single-phase 230 V, the loads are randomly connected between one phase and neutral, making the **3rd harmonic dominant** in the neutral conductor. A typical desktop computer SMPS current waveform has a **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** of 80–100%, with the **3rd harmonic being the largest single component (often 60–70% of fundamental)**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Triplen harmonic order | $h = 3(2k-1)$ | $h \in \{3, 9, 15, 21, \ldots\}$ | | 2 | Neutral current (single triplen order $h$) | $I_{n,h} = 3 I_h$ | Each triplen harmonic adds 3× its phase value | | 3 | Total RMS neutral current | $I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}$ | Root sum of squares of all triplen components, multiplied by 3 | | 4 | Total RMS phase current | $I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=1}^{H} I_h^2}$ | Includes fundamental + all harmonics | | 5 | Zero-sequence condition | $e^{j0°} + e^{j(-120°h)} + e^{j(+120°h)} = 3$ iff $h = 3k$ | Phasor proof | | 6 | Neutral overloading ratio | $K_n = \dfrac{I_{n,rms}}{I_{ph,rms}}$ | If $K_n > 1$, neutral is **overloaded** | | 7 | 3rd harmonic voltage distortion in neutral | $V_{n,3} = 3 \cdot I_3 \cdot Z_{n,\text{loop}}$ | Can exceed code limits (e.g., 5% per IEEE 519) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For table safety:** the magnitude $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\vert x \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command, never the bare pipe character that breaks markdown tables. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding the triplen-neutral effect is **mandatory** in modern commercial electrical design because: 1. **Modern office buildings** with 80–90% non-linear IT loads show neutral currents up to **2× the phase current** — violating legacy design assumptions where $I_n = 0$. 2. **NEC 210.4 / IEEE C57.110 standards** specifically address oversized neutrals and **K-rated transformers** for such environments. 3. **Hospital-grade and data-center** power systems use **oversized (200% rated) neutrals** or **dual neutral runs** to handle triplen loading. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Phasor Proof of Triplen Harmonic Summation in Neutral We use the **complex exponential (phasor)** representation. For a balanced 3-φ system at harmonic $h$: $$\vec{I}_a = I_h \angle 0°, \quad \vec{I}_b = I_h \angle (-120°h), \quad \vec{I}_c = I_h \angle (+120°h)$$ The neutral current phasor is the vector sum: $$\vec{I}_n = \vec{I}_a + \vec{I}_b + \vec{I}_c$$ Using Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$, we write: $$\vec{I}_n = I_h \left[ e^{j0°} + e^{-j120°h} + e^{+j120°h} \right]$$ Recognize that the second and third terms are complex conjugates: $$e^{-j120°h} + e^{+j120°h} = 2\cos(120°h)$$ Therefore: $$\vec{I}_n = I_h \left[ 1 + 2\cos(120° \cdot h) \right]$$ Now evaluate for the **triplen case** $h = 3, 9, 15, \ldots$, i.e. $h = 3k$: $$\cos(120° \cdot 3k) = \cos(360° \cdot k) = \cos(0°) = 1$$ So: $$\vec{I}_n = I_h [1 + 2(1)] = 3I_h \quad \text{(real, in-phase)}$$ **This proves that every triplen harmonic current in the neutral is exactly 3 times the per-phase value, with all three phases adding constructively.** For non-triplen harmonics, evaluate $\cos(120° h)$: $$\vec{I}_n = I_h \left[ 1 + 2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi h}{3}\right) \right]$$ Let us check $h=1$: $$1 + 2\cos(120°) = 1 + 2(-0.5) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ For $h=5$: $$1 + 2\cos(600°) = 1 + 2\cos(240°) = 1 + 2(-0.5) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ For $h=7$: $$1 + 2\cos(840°) = 1 + 2\cos(120°) = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ So only triplens survive. ## 3.2 Derivation: Total RMS Neutral Current The instantaneous triplen harmonic currents from all three phases are: $$i_{a,triplen}(t) = I_3 \sin(3\omega t) + I_9 \sin(9\omega t) + I_{15}\sin(15\omega t) + \cdots$$ $$i_{b,triplen}(t) = I_3 \sin(3\omega t) + I_9 \sin(9\omega t) + I_{15}\sin(15\omega t) + \cdots$$ $$i_{c,triplen}(t) = I_3 \sin(3\omega t) + I_9 \sin(9\omega t) + I_{15}\sin(15\omega t) + \cdots$$ Add all three (the triplens align in phase, so the magnitudes sum directly): $$i_n(t) = 3I_3 \sin(3\omega t) + 3I_9 \sin(9\omega t) + 3I_{15}\sin(15\omega t) + \cdots$$ The RMS value of a sum of orthogonal sinusoids of different frequencies is the root-sum-square: $$I_{n,rms} = \sqrt{(3I_3)^2 + (3I_9)^2 + (3I_{15})^2 + (3I_{21})^2 + \cdots}$$ Factoring out the 3: $$\boxed{\,I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The factor of **3** multiplies the *whole* RMS sum, not each individual term. This is the dominant effect that can drive $I_{n,rms}$ well above $I_{ph,rms}$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Examination Type) **Problem Statement:** > A balanced 3-phase, 4-wire, 230/400 V system supplies non-linear single-phase loads (PCs and SMPS) with the following per-phase harmonic currents (RMS): > - Fundamental: $I_1 = 30$ A > - 3rd harmonic: $I_3 = 20$ A > - 5th harmonic: $I_5 = 8$ A > - 7th harmonic: $I_7 = 4$ A > - 9th harmonic: $I_9 = 6$ A > > Calculate: **(i)** RMS phase current, **(ii)** RMS neutral current, **(iii)** Neutral-to-phase ratio and comment on conductor sizing. ### Solution — Step 1: RMS Phase Current $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{30^2 + 20^2 + 8^2 + 4^2 + 6^2}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{900 + 400 + 64 + 16 + 36}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{1416}$$ $$\boxed{\,I_{ph,rms} \approx 37.63 \text{ A}\,}$$ ### Solution — Step 2: RMS Neutral Current Only triplens ($h = 3$ and $h = 9$) contribute: $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{20^2 + 6^2}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{400 + 36}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{436}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3 \times 20.881$$ $$\boxed{\,I_{n,rms} \approx 62.64 \text{ A}\,}$$ ### Solution — Step 3: Neutral-to-Phase Ratio $$K_n = \frac{I_{n,rms}}{I_{ph,rms}} = \frac{62.64}{37.63} \approx 1.665$$ **Conclusion:** The neutral current is **66.5% higher** than the phase current. A neutral conductor sized equal to the phase conductor (37.63 A capacity) would be **overloaded** by ~67%. This necessitates: - **Doubling the neutral conductor cross-section** (NEC 310.15(B)(4)(c) for non-linear loads), OR - **Using a separate (dedicated) neutral per phase**, OR - **Installing a K-rated transformer** (K-13 or higher), OR - **Deploying active/passive harmonic filters** to suppress the triplen currents at the load. ### 3.4 The "Worst-Case" Theoretical Bound If the load is **purely 3rd-harmonic current** (a fully rectified single-phase load, like a 6-pulse rectifier with strong DC filter), then $I_1 = 0$ in the ideal zero-crossing sense and only the 3rd harmonic flows. In that case: $$I_{n,rms} = 3 I_3, \quad \text{while} \quad I_{ph,rms} = I_3$$ So $K_n = 3$ — the neutral current is **theoretically three times the phase current**. This is the absolute upper bound of triplen-induced neutral overloading. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Triplen Harmonic Path to the Neutral ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["PHASE A NON-LINEAR LOADS
PCs and SMPS and LED DRIVERS"] --> nodeA3["3rd Harmonic IA3 150 Hz"] node1 --> nodeA9["9th Harmonic IA9 450 Hz"] node1 --> nodeA15["15th Harmonic IA15 750 Hz"] node2["PHASE B NON-LINEAR LOADS
PCs and SMPS and LED DRIVERS"] --> nodeB3["3rd Harmonic IB3 IN PHASE WITH IA3"] node2 --> nodeB9["9th Harmonic IB9 IN PHASE WITH IA9"] node2 --> nodeB15["15th Harmonic IB15 IN PHASE WITH IA15"] node3["PHASE C NON-LINEAR LOADS
PCs and SMPS and LED DRIVERS"] --> nodeC3["3rd Harmonic IC3 IN PHASE WITH IA3"] node3 --> nodeC9["9th Harmonic IC9 IN PHASE WITH IA9"] node3 --> nodeC15["15th Harmonic IC15 IN PHASE WITH IA15"] nodeA3 --> sumNode["NEUTRAL CONDUCTOR
CONSTRUCTIVE SUMMATION"] nodeB3 --> sumNode nodeC3 --> sumNode nodeA9 --> sumNode nodeB9 --> sumNode nodeC9 --> sumNode nodeA15 --> sumNode nodeB15 --> sumNode nodeC15 --> sumNode sumNode --> outNode["In rms equals 3 times sqrt IA3 sq plus IA9 sq plus IA15 sq"] outNode --> effectNode["CONSEQUENCES
Overheating of neutral
Voltage distortion
Breaker nuisance tripping
Fire risk"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mitigation Strategies for Triplen Neutral Overload ```mermaid flowchart LR problem["TRIPLEN HARMONIC POLLUTION
IN NEUTRAL CONDUCTOR"] --> m1["OPTION 1
Oversize Neutral
2x Phase Conductor"] problem --> m2["OPTION 2
K-Rated Transformer
K-13 or K-20"] problem --> m3["OPTION 3
Dedicated Neutral Per Phase
Reduce Shared Coupling"] problem --> m4["OPTION 4
Zero-Sequence Harmonic Filter
Blocks Triplen at Source"] problem --> m5["OPTION 5
Active Harmonic Filter
Injects Anti-Triplen"] problem --> m6["OPTION 6
12-Pulse Rectifier
or 18-Pulse Drive"] m1 --> outC["COMPLIANT AND SAFE INSTALLATION"] m2 --> outC m3 --> outC m4 --> outC m5 --> outC m6 --> outC ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence / Phasor Diagram (Text Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD phA["PHASE A FUNDAMENTAL
Phasor at 0 degrees"]:::pos phB["PHASE B FUNDAMENTAL
Phasor at minus 120 degrees"]:::pos phC["PHASE C FUNDAMENTAL
Phasor at plus 120 degrees"]:::pos phASum["SUM FUNDAMENTAL
Equals ZERO"]:::zero phA3["PHASE A 3rd HARMONIC
at 0 deg"]:::trip phB3["PHASE B 3rd HARMONIC
at minus 360 deg = 0 deg"]:::trip phC3["PHASE C 3rd HARMONIC
at plus 360 deg = 0 deg"]:::trip ph3Sum["SUM 3rd HARMONIC
Equals 3 times I3
MAXIMUM NEUTRAL LOADING"]:::tripmax phA --> phASum phB --> phASum phC --> phASum phA3 --> ph3Sum phB3 --> ph3Sum phC3 --> ph3Sum classDef pos fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef zero fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef trip fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef tripmax fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read the phasor diagram:** In the **top branch** (fundamental), the three phase phasors at 0°, -120°, +120° sum to zero — no neutral current. In the **bottom branch** (3rd harmonic), the phasor shifts become 0°, -360°, +360°, all of which collapse to the **same direction (0°)** — so the three currents add to give $3 I_3$ in the neutral. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **Define triplen harmonics. Why are they called zero-sequence harmonics, and what is their unique effect on the neutral conductor of a 3-phase, 4-wire system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Triplen harmonics are odd multiples of the 3rd harmonic — i.e., the 3rd, 9th, 15th, 21st, … order harmonics. In a balanced 3-φ system, the phase shift of the $h^{th}$ harmonic between successive phases is $h \times 120°$. For triplen orders ($h = 3k$), this becomes a multiple of $360°$, so all three phases are **in phase** (zero phase shift) — hence the name **zero-sequence harmonics**. The unique effect is that all three phase currents add constructively in the neutral, producing a neutral current of magnitude **3 × the per-phase triplen current**, which can severely overload the neutral conductor. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **A balanced 3-phase non-linear load draws a fundamental current of 25 A (RMS) and a 3rd-harmonic current of 15 A (RMS). Calculate the RMS value of the neutral current and the ratio of neutral current to phase current.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Phase RMS current: $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2} = \sqrt{25^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{625 + 225} = \sqrt{850} \approx 29.15 \text{ A}$$ Neutral RMS current (only 3rd harmonic contributes): $$I_{n,rms} = 3 \cdot I_3 = 3 \times 15 = 45 \text{ A}$$ Ratio: $$K_n = \frac{I_{n,rms}}{I_{ph,rms}} = \frac{45}{29.15} \approx 1.544$$ Hence, the neutral current is **~1.54× the phase current**, indicating the need for an oversized neutral conductor. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (with KTU Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** write the neutral current as $3 I_{n,total}$ where $I_{n,total}$ is the sum of all phase triplen RMS values. The correct formula is the **root-sum-square** multiplied by 3, not the arithmetic sum times 3. > 2. **Do NOT** include non-triplen harmonics (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th) in the neutral current formula — they cancel out. > 3. **Do NOT** confuse the triplen order sequence: it is **3, 9, 15, 21** (NOT 6, 12, 18 — those are even and not present in typical balanced loads). > 4. **Show the phase-shift multiplication** ($h \times 120°$) explicitly to get full marks on derivation questions. > 5. In numerical problems, students often forget the factor of **3** in the neutral formula. Always write the formula before substituting. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with mathematical analysis why triplen harmonics are zero-sequence and add constructively in the neutral conductor of a 3-φ, 4-wire system. Derive the general formula for neutral current due to all triplen harmonic components. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Phasor representation of balanced 3-φ currents at harmonic order $h$:** For a balanced 3-φ load, the $h^{th}$ harmonic current phasors are: $$\vec{I}_a = I_h \angle 0°, \quad \vec{I}_b = I_h \angle(-120°h), \quad \vec{I}_c = I_h \angle(+120°h)$$ **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the neutral node:** $$\vec{I}_n = \vec{I}_a + \vec{I}_b + \vec{I}_c = I_h\left[e^{j0°} + e^{-j120°h} + e^{+j120°h}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Simplify using Euler's identity:** Since $e^{-j\theta} + e^{+j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$: $$\vec{I}_n = I_h\left[1 + 2\cos(120°h)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Test for triplen harmonics ($h = 3k$):** For $h = 3$: $$\vec{I}_n = I_3[1 + 2\cos(360°)] = I_3[1 + 2(1)] = 3I_3$$ For $h = 9$: $$\vec{I}_n = I_9[1 + 2\cos(1080°)] = I_9[1 + 2(1)] = 3I_9$$ In general for $h = 3k$: $$\vec{I}_n = 3 I_{3k} \angle 0° \quad \text{(in-phase, zero-sequence)}$$ **Step 5 — Total RMS neutral current:** Adding all triplen components (which are at different frequencies and therefore orthogonal): $$\boxed{\,I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}\,}$$ This proves mathematically that triplen harmonics are zero-sequence and add constructively in the neutral with a 3× factor. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 4-wire commercial building has the following per-phase harmonic currents (RMS) due to a large cluster of PCs and LED lighting: - $I_1 = 40$ A, $I_3 = 22$ A, $I_5 = 6$ A, $I_7 = 4$ A, $I_9 = 5$ A, $I_{11} = 2$ A Compute: **(i)** RMS phase current, **(ii)** RMS neutral current, **(iii)** Neutral-to-phase ratio, and **(iv)** Recommend the neutral conductor sizing per NEC 210.4 / IEEE guidelines. **Model Solution:** **(i) RMS Phase Current:** $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2 + I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_9^2 + I_{11}^2}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{40^2 + 22^2 + 6^2 + 4^2 + 5^2 + 2^2}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{1600 + 484 + 36 + 16 + 25 + 4}$$ $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{2165} \approx 46.53 \text{ A}$$ **(ii) RMS Neutral Current (only $h = 3$ and $h = 9$ contribute):** $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2} = 3\sqrt{22^2 + 5^2}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{484 + 25} = 3\sqrt{509}$$ $$I_{n,rms} = 3 \times 22.56 \approx 67.68 \text{ A}$$ **(iii) Neutral-to-Phase Ratio:** $$K_n = \frac{67.68}{46.53} \approx 1.454$$ The neutral current is **45.4% higher** than the phase current. **(iv) Recommendation:** A standard neutral sized equal to the phase conductor (rated for ~46.53 A) will be **overloaded by 45%**. Recommended actions: - **Double the neutral cross-section** (use 200%-rated neutral as per NEC 210.4(B)), OR - **Use a K-13 rated transformer** for the building distribution, OR - **Install a passive zero-sequence harmonic filter** tuned to 150 Hz to shunt the 3rd harmonic current. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **effects of triplen harmonics on the neutral conductor** of a 3-φ, 4-wire power distribution system. Explain at least **five adverse consequences** and **four mitigation techniques** with their working principles. **Model Solution:** **Adverse Effects of Triplen Harmonics on Neutral:** 1. **Overloading of Neutral Conductor:** Triplen harmonics add constructively, so neutral RMS current can reach $1.5\times$ to $3\times$ the phase current. Since neutrals are typically sized equal to phase conductors, this causes **chronic overloading**. 2. **Overheating and Fire Risk:** Continuous overloading of the neutral causes $I^2R$ losses to rise by factors of 2.25× to 9×. Insulation degrades, leading to **short-circuit faults and fire hazards**. 3. **Voltage Distortion on the Neutral:** The non-zero neutral current flowing through the neutral wire impedance produces a **neutral-to-ground voltage** at harmonic frequencies, raising the neutral potential above ground — a serious safety issue (touch voltage violation). 4. **Nuisance Tripping of Protective Devices:** Neutral current may cause 4-pole breakers, residual current devices (RCDs/RCCBs), and neutral-overload relays to trip, even when phase currents are within limits. 5. **Transformer Overheating:** Triplen currents circulate in the **delta winding** of a Dy transformer (zero-sequence trap), causing **extra copper losses** and overheating. Standard transformers are not designed for this. 6. **Capacitor Bank Failures:** Power factor correction capacitors, sized for 50 Hz, draw excessive current at 150 Hz and beyond, leading to capacitor failures. **Mitigation Techniques:** 1. **Oversized Neutral Conductor (200%):** Double the cross-section of the neutral. Simple, passive, no electronics. Limitation: adds cost and weight. 2. **K-Rated Transformers (K-13, K-20):** Special transformer with reduced eddy-current losses in the core and oversized neutral bus, designed to handle triplen heating. Works on the principle of robust thermal design. 3. **Zero-Sequence Harmonic Filter:** A passive LC trap tuned to 150 Hz (3rd harmonic) connected between neutral and ground provides a low-impedance path, shunting triplen currents away from the system neutral. 4. **Active Harmonic Filter (AHF):** Power-electronic inverter that injects **anti-phase triplen currents** to cancel the harmonic pollution. Modern, fast, and effective for variable loads. 5. **Dedicated Neutral per Phase (3 Separate Neutrals):** Prevents coupling of triplen currents across phases. Recommended for IT and data-center environments. 6. **12-Pulse or 18-Pulse Rectifier Front-End:** For VFDs and large SMPS, multi-pulse rectifiers naturally cancel 5th, 7th, 11th, and 13th harmonics and reduce triplen content significantly. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression showing that **in the worst case, the neutral current can be three times the per-phase current** due to triplen harmonics. Illustrate with a numerical example where a building's neutral conductor experiences dangerous overloading. **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Consider a balanced 3-φ, 4-wire system where the load is **purely a single-phase non-linear rectifier** (e.g., a heavily filtered SMPS drawing current only in narrow pulses near the voltage peak). The current waveform can be represented as primarily the **3rd harmonic** (plus its odd multiples). If the fundamental component is **negligible** and only the 3rd harmonic flows with RMS value $I_3$ per phase, then: $$I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + I_3^2} = I_3 \quad \text{(when } I_1 = 0\text{)}$$ The neutral current (only 3rd harmonic contributes): $$I_{n,rms} = 3 I_3$$ The ratio becomes: $$K_n = \frac{I_{n,rms}}{I_{ph,rms}} = \frac{3 I_3}{I_3} = 3$$ **Thus, in the theoretical worst case, the neutral current is exactly 3 times the phase current.** **Numerical Illustration:** Consider a small office wing with 30 desktop PCs, each drawing 2 A RMS (3rd-harmonic dominated). The loads are balanced 10 per phase, so per-phase triplen RMS current is: $$I_3 = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ A}$$ Phase RMS current (assuming $I_1 \approx 0$ for highly distorted SMPS): $$I_{ph,rms} \approx I_3 = 20 \text{ A}$$ Neutral current: $$I_{n,rms} = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ A}$$ The neutral conductor (sized for 20 A, equal to the phase conductor) carries **60 A — three times its rated capacity**. The $I^2R$ losses become $9\times$ the design value, leading to rapid insulation failure and high risk of **electrical fire within hours**. **Conclusion:** Building codes (NEC 210.4, IEEE C57.110) require the neutral to be **doubled (200%)** whenever non-linear loads exceed 50% of the total load, precisely to mitigate this 3× worst-case triplen overloading scenario. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — KTU Module 2** - ✅ **Triplen harmonics = odd multiples of 3rd** → orders $\{3, 9, 15, 21, 27, \ldots\}$ - ✅ **Zero-sequence property:** $h \times 120° = $ multiple of $360°$ for $h = 3k$ → all three phases in phase - ✅ **Neutral current formula:** $I_{n,rms} = 3\sqrt{I_3^2 + I_9^2 + I_{15}^2 + I_{21}^2 + \cdots}$ - ✅ **Phase current formula:** $I_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{\sum I_h^2}$ (all harmonic orders) - ✅ **Worst-case ratio:** $I_{n,rms} \le 3 \cdot I_{ph,rms}$ (theoretical upper bound) - ✅ **Typical real-world ratio:** $K_n \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ in modern offices - ✅ **Sources:** Single-phase non-linear loads — PC SMPS, LED drivers, CFLs, dimmers, laptop chargers - ✅ **Consequences:** Neutral overheating, voltage distortion, breaker tripping, transformer overheat - ✅ **Solutions:** Oversized neutral (200%), K-rated transformer (K-13/K-20), zero-sequence filter, AHF, 12-pulse rectifier - ✅ **Standard reference:** NEC 210.4(B) for neutral sizing, IEEE C57.110 for transformer K-factor, IEEE 519 for harmonic limits - ✅ **Sequence classification:** 1st, 7th, 13th → positive; 5th, 11th, 17th → negative; **3rd, 9th, 15th → zero** - ✅ **Memory trick:** "Triplens are the **Tri-umphants** of the neutral — they multiply by 3" - ✅ **Key exam insight:** In KTU, always check whether the problem is balanced — for unbalanced loads, the analysis must include KCL per phase and is more complex (not covered in this single-topic note) - ✅ **India frequency:** $f = 50$ Hz, so $3^{rd}$ = 150 Hz, $9^{th}$ = 450 Hz, $15^{th}$ = 750 Hz ---

topics.title: Line and Phase Voltages, Harmonic Analysis Using Fourier Series and Fourier Transforms - Simple Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Line and Phase Voltages, Harmonic Analysis Using Fourier Series and Fourier Transforms - Simple Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Line and Phase Voltages — Formal Academic Definition

In any **three-phase (3-φ) power system**, two distinct voltage levels coexist at every instant. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Power Quality – Module 2) defines them precisely as:

> **Phase Voltage ($V_{Ph}$):** The voltage measured across a single phase winding (between any one line terminal and the neutral point) of the source or load. Units: **Volts (V)** or **kV**.

> **Line Voltage ($V_L$):** The voltage measured between any two line conductors (line-to-line) of the three-phase source or load. Units: **Volts (V)** or **kV**.

The relationship between $V_L$ and $V_{Ph}$ is governed entirely by the **connection topology** of the source/load:

| Configuration | $V_L$ vs $V_{Ph}$ | $I_L$ vs $I_{Ph}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Star (Y)** | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{Ph}$ | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ |
| **Delta ($\Delta$)** | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \, I_{Ph}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The factor $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ is the *single most tested constant* in three-phase power problems. Always state it to **three decimal places (1.732)** for full marks.

---

### 1.2 Harmonic Analysis — Formal Definition (Fourier Series & Fourier Transform)

> **Fourier Series:** Any *periodic*, piecewise-continuous signal $f(t)$ of fundamental period $T$ can be decomposed into an infinite sum of pure sinusoids (harmonics) at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/T$.

> **Fourier Transform:** The continuous-frequency generalization that expresses a *non-periodic* (transient or aperiodic) signal as a continuous spectrum of sinusoidal components — the density function of Fourier coefficients over frequency.

$$f(t) = \underbrace{\frac{a_0}{2}}_{\text{DC}} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

$$F(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Orchestra" of Power:**
> Imagine your three-phase AC supply as an **orchestra**. The **fundamental (50 Hz) tone** is the dominant melody (violins). When you plug in non-linear loads like **rectifiers, SMPS, VFDs, or LED drivers**, they act like a *trombone section* blowing unintended extra notes — the **harmonics (100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz, ...)**. The **Fourier Series is the musical analyst** who breaks the distorted waveform into a sum of clean pure tones, telling you *exactly* which "notes" are present and how loud each one is. The **Fourier Transform** is the same analyst, but for a *single drum hit* — a one-time transient event with no repeating rhythm.

Similarly, **Line Voltage is the voltage between two "main pipes"** carrying water (current), while **Phase Voltage is the pressure inside a single pipe** measured against a common reference (neutral). The two are not independent — they are 120° apart in phase, creating the famous $\sqrt{3}$ geometric relationship.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why $V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{Ph}$ in Star — Geometric Reasoning

Consider a balanced 3-phase star system with phase voltages (as phasors) separated by 120°:

$$\vec{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0^\circ, \quad \vec{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120^\circ, \quad \vec{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle +120^\circ$$

The line voltage $\vec{V}_{RY}$ is the **phasor difference**:

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \vec{V}_{RN} - \vec{V}_{YN}$$

Geometrically, the three phase phasors form a closed equilateral triangle (rotating at $\omega$). Subtracting any two phase phasors yields the side of that triangle, whose length is $\sqrt{3}$ times the radius ($V_{Ph}$). The $30°$ lead between line and phase voltage is also derivable:

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph} \angle +30^\circ$$

### 2.2 Why $V_L = V_{Ph}$ in Delta — Physical Reasoning

In a delta connection, each phase winding is connected **directly across two line conductors**. Therefore, the voltage across a phase winding is, by definition, the line-to-line voltage. No transformation occurs.

### 2.3 Why Fourier Analysis is the Backbone of Power Quality

Any **distorted, non-sinusoidal** voltage or current waveform $f(t)$ in a power system can be expressed as:

$$f(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sqrt{2}\, F_n \sin(n\omega_0 t + \phi_n)$$

- $n = 1$ → **Fundamental component** (50 Hz in India).
- $n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots$ → **Harmonic components**.
- The Total Harmonic Distortion (**THD**) quantifies the "amount" of distortion:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} F_n^2}}{F_1} \times 100\,\%$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}$ | Star line-to-line voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ | Delta line-to-line voltage | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ | Star line current | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $I_L = \sqrt{3}\, I_{Ph}$ | Delta line current | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T f(t) \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | Fourier cosine coefficient | V or A |
| 6 | $b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T f(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | Fourier sine coefficient | V or A |
| 7 | $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}$ | Amplitude of $n^{th}$ harmonic | V or A |
| 8 | $F_{rms} = \sqrt{F_1^2 + \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} F_n^2}$ | True RMS of distorted wave | V or A |
| 9 | $THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} F_n^2}}{F_1}$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | Unitless (%) |
| 10 | $F(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ | Fourier Transform | Spectrum |
| 11 | $f(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} F(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$ | Inverse Fourier Transform | Time signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Never use the vertical bar $\vert$ inside KTU markdown tables.** This rule prevents markdown parser crashes. In the table above, all magnitudes are denoted by subscripted variables ($F_n$, $F_1$, $F_{rms}$) for that reason.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Star (Y):** Used in **transmission systems, distribution transformers, and motor windings** — the neutral allows single-phase loads (households) to be tapped safely.
- **Delta ($\Delta$):** Used in **transmission lines, motor starting, and rectifier transformers** — provides a path for triplen (3rd) harmonic currents to circulate, preventing them from appearing in the line.
- **Fourier Analysis:** Foundation of every **Power Quality meter, harmonic analyzer, and oscilloscope** (FFT mode). Mandated by **IEEE 519-2014** for assessing utility-grade waveform purity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Star-Connected $V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}$

**Step 1 — Define the three phase phasors** (RMS form, balanced system):

$$\vec{V}_R = V_{Ph} \angle 0^\circ, \quad \vec{V}_Y = V_{Ph} \angle -120^\circ, \quad \vec{V}_B = V_{Ph} \angle +120^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Express line voltage $\vec{V}_{RY}$ as a phasor difference:**

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \vec{V}_R - \vec{V}_Y$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the polar forms and expand using Euler's formula:**

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph} - V_{Ph}\left[\cos(-120^\circ) + j\sin(-120^\circ)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute trigonometric values** $\cos(-120^\circ) = -1/2$ and $\sin(-120^\circ) = -\sqrt{3}/2$:

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph} - V_{Ph}\left[-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the real and imaginary parts:**

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph} + \frac{V_{Ph}}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}}{2}$$

$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \frac{3V_{Ph}}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the magnitude** by applying $|a + jb| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$:

$$\vert \vec{V}_{RY} \vert = \sqrt{\left(\frac{3V_{Ph}}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Expand the squares:**

$$\vert \vec{V}_{RY} \vert = \sqrt{\frac{9 V_{Ph}^2}{4} + \frac{3 V_{Ph}^2}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{12 V_{Ph}^2}{4}} = \sqrt{3 V_{Ph}^2}$$

**Step 8 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the phase angle** for completeness:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}/2}{3V_{Ph}/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = +30^\circ$$

Thus: $\vec{V}_{RY} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph} \angle +30^\circ$ — the line voltage **leads** the R-phase voltage by 30°.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Star-Connected Load (KTU-Standard)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Style — Dec 2024 Pattern]:**
> A balanced star-connected 3-phase load has a phase voltage of **$230 \text{ V}$** and draws a phase current of **$10 \text{ A}$** at a power factor of **0.8 lagging**. Calculate:
> (a) Line voltage and line current
> (b) Total active power consumed

**Solution:**

Given: $V_{Ph} = 230\,\text{V}$, $I_{Ph} = 10\,\text{A}$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ (lag).

**(a) Line quantities** (using the Star formula table above):

$$V_L = \sqrt{3} \times 230 = 1.732 \times 230 = 398.36 \text{ V}$$

$$I_L = I_{Ph} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**(b) Total 3-phase active power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L\, I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 398.36 \times 10 \times 0.8$$

$$P = 1.732 \times 398.36 \times 8 = 5520.2 \text{ W} \approx 5.52 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{total} \approx 5.52 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Harmonic Analysis of a Square Wave (KTU-Standard)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Style — June 2024 Pattern]:**
> A **square wave** of amplitude $A = 100\,\text{V}$ and fundamental frequency $f_0 = 50\,\text{Hz}$ is measured across a load. Using Fourier series analysis, determine:
> (a) The expression for $v(t)$ in terms of its harmonics
> (b) The RMS value of the fundamental
> (c) Total RMS voltage
> (d) THD (%)

**Solution:**

**(a) Fourier expansion** — A symmetric square wave (odd function, zero DC) has the well-known expansion:

$$v(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega_0 t)$$

Substituting $A = 100$ V:

$$v(t) = \frac{400}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots \right] \text{ V}$$

The amplitude of the $n^{th}$ harmonic (odd $n$ only):

$$V_n = \frac{4A}{n\pi} = \frac{400}{n\pi} \text{ V}$$

**(b) Fundamental RMS** (peak / $\sqrt{2}$):

$$V_{1,peak} = \frac{4 \times 100}{\pi} = \frac{400}{\pi} = 127.32 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{1,rms} = \frac{127.32}{\sqrt{2}} = 90.03 \text{ V}$$

**(c) Total RMS voltage** (sum of squares of all harmonics):

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2 + \cdots}$$

$$V_1 = 127.32,\ V_3 = 42.44,\ V_5 = 25.46,\ V_7 = 18.19,\ V_9 = 14.15, \ldots$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{127.32^2 + 42.44^2 + 25.46^2 + 18.19^2 + 14.15^2 + \cdots}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{16211.7 + 1801.2 + 648.3 + 330.9 + 200.2 + \cdots} = \sqrt{20000} = 100 \text{ V}$$

> (This matches the known result: $V_{rms} = A = 100$ V for a square wave — a great self-check!)

**(d) THD calculation:**

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{1801.2 + 648.3 + 330.9 + 200.2 + \cdots}}{127.32} \times 100\,\%$$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{3788.3}}{127.32} \times 100\,\% = \frac{61.55}{127.32} \times 100\,\%$$

$$\boxed{THD \approx 48.34\,\%}$$

(As $n \to \infty$, the well-known theoretical limit is $THD_{square} = \sqrt{\pi^2/8 - 1} \approx 48.34\%$.)

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Fourier Coefficient from a Given Waveform

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Style]:**
> A **periodic triangular wave** with peak amplitude $V_m = 50\,\text{V}$ and period $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ is expressed as:

$$v(t) = a_1 \sin(\omega_0 t) + a_3 \sin(3\omega_0 t) + a_5 \sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots$$

> with coefficients $a_n = \frac{8 V_m}{n^2 \pi^2}$ for odd $n$ and $a_n = 0$ for even $n$. Calculate the amplitude of the **1st, 3rd, and 5th harmonics** and the **THD (%)**.

**Solution:**

For odd $n$:

$$a_n = \frac{8 \times 50}{n^2 \pi^2} = \frac{400}{n^2 \pi^2}$$

- **Fundamental (n=1):** $a_1 = 400/\pi^2 = 40.53\,\text{V}$
- **3rd harmonic:** $a_3 = 400/(9\pi^2) = 4.50\,\text{V}$
- **5th harmonic:** $a_5 = 400/(25\pi^2) = 1.62\,\text{V}$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{a_3^2 + a_5^2 + a_7^2 + \cdots}}{a_1} \times 100\,\%$$

$$THD \approx \frac{\sqrt{4.50^2 + 1.62^2 + 0.74^2 + \cdots}}{40.53} \times 100\,\%$$

$$\boxed{THD \approx 11.96\,\%}$$

(Triangular waves are much "cleaner" than square waves — about 4× less distortion!)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Star (Y) Connection — Phasor & Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sourceA["SOURCE PHASE R"] --> neutralA["NEUTRAL N"]
    sourceB["SOURCE PHASE Y"] --> neutralA
    sourceC["SOURCE PHASE B"] --> neutralA
    sourceA --> lineA["LINE CONDUCTOR R"]
    sourceB --> lineB["LINE CONDUCTOR Y"]
    sourceC --> lineC["LINE CONDUCTOR B"]
    lineA --> loadA["LOAD PHASE R"]
    lineB --> loadB["LOAD PHASE Y"]
    lineC --> loadC["LOAD PHASE B"]
    loadA --> loadN["LOAD NEUTRAL N"]
    loadB --> loadN
    loadC --> loadN
    neutralA -.-> loadN
    note1["V_RY = LINE VOLTAGE BETWEEN R AND Y = sqrt3 x V_Ph"]:::annot
    note2["V_RN = PHASE VOLTAGE = V_Ph"]:::annot
    note1 -.- lineA
    note2 -.- loadA
    classDef annot fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#daa520,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Delta ($\Delta$) Connection — Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["END OF R WINDING"] --> p2["START OF Y WINDING"]
    p2 --> p3["END OF Y WINDING"]
    p3 --> p4["START OF B WINDING"]
    p4 --> p5["END OF B WINDING"]
    p5 --> p1
    p1 --> lineR["LINE R"]
    p2 --> lineY["LINE Y"]
    p3 --> lineB["LINE B"]
    noteA["V_L = V_Ph (NO TRANSFORMATION)"]:::annot
    noteB["I_L = sqrt3 x I_Ph"]:::annot
    noteA -.- p1
    noteB -.- p2
    classDef annot fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Fourier Analysis — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input["DISTORTED WAVEFORM f of t"] --> sampler["SAMPLE THE WAVEFORM OVER ONE PERIOD T"]
    sampler --> fft["APPLY FOURIER SERIES OR FFT ALGORITHM"]
    fft --> extract["EXTRACT a_n AND b_n COEFFICIENTS"]
    extract --> harm1["FUNDAMENTAL n EQUALS 1, 50 HZ"]
    extract --> harm2["3RD HARMONIC n EQUALS 3, 150 HZ"]
    extract --> harm3["5TH HARMONIC n EQUALS 5, 250 HZ"]
    extract --> harmN["HIGHER HARMONICS n EQUALS 7, 9, 11 ..."]
    harm1 --> rms["COMPUTE V_RMS USING SQRT OF SUM OF SQUARES"]
    harm2 --> rms
    harm3 --> rms
    harmN --> rms
    rms --> thd["CALCULATE THD PERCENTAGE"]
    thd --> ieee["COMPARE WITH IEEE 519 2014 LIMITS"]
    ieee --> report["GENERATE POWER QUALITY REPORT"]
    classDef process fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#fff0f5,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:2px
    class sampler,fft,extract,rms,thd process
    class ieee,report output
```

### 4.4 Harmonic Spectrum Visualization (Schematic Bar Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    h1["FUNDAMENTAL 100 PERCENT REFERENCE"]:::base
    h3["3RD HARMONIC ABOUT 33 PERCENT"]:::h3
    h5["5TH HARMONIC ABOUT 20 PERCENT"]:::h5
    h7["7TH HARMONIC ABOUT 14 PERCENT"]:::h7
    h9["9TH HARMONIC ABOUT 11 PERCENT"]:::h9
    h11["11TH HARMONIC ABOUT 9 PERCENT"]:::h11
    h13["13TH HARMONIC ABOUT 7 PERCENT"]:::h13
    h1 --- h3 --- h5 --- h7 --- h9 --- h11 --- h13
    classDef base fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef h3 fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef h5 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef h7 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef h9 fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef h11 fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef h13 fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Spectrum:** The bar values drop off as $1/n$ for square waves and $1/n^2$ for triangular waves. This is the *fingerprint* of a waveform's harmonic content — the same principle used by audio codecs (MP3/AAC) to compress music.
