## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define line voltage and phase voltage. Derive the relationship between them in a **star-connected** 3-phase system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Line Voltage ($V_L$):** Voltage measured between any two line conductors.
- **Phase Voltage ($V_{Ph}$):** Voltage measured between a line conductor and the neutral point.
- In star connection, the three phase voltages are 120° apart. The line voltage is the phasor difference of two phase voltages. By phasor geometry:

$$V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{Ph}$$

The line voltage leads the corresponding phase voltage by **+30°**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**? Write its mathematical expression and state the IEEE 519 recommended limit for voltage THD at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC).

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

THD is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (above the fundamental) to the RMS value of the fundamental:

$$THD_V = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$$

IEEE 519-2014 recommends **$V_{THD} \leq 5\%$** at the PCC for voltages below 69 kV, and $\leq 1.5\%$ on individual harmonics.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** A balanced **3-phase star-connected** load has a phase impedance of $Z = (8 + j6)\,\Omega$ per phase. The line voltage is $V_L = 400\,\text{V}$, 50 Hz.
> (i) Find the **phase voltage**, **phase current**, and **line current**.
> (ii) Calculate the **total active power** and **reactive power** consumed.
> (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Fourier Series** representation of a **non-sinusoidal periodic waveform** in power systems. Define **THD** and explain its significance with respect to **IEEE 519-2014**.
> (7 Marks)

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

Given: $Z = 8 + j6\,\Omega$, $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{64 + 36} = 10\,\Omega$, $\cos\phi = 8/10 = 0.8$ (lag).

**(i) Phase voltage:**

$$V_{Ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{1.732} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

**Phase current = Line current** (star):

$$I_{Ph} = I_L = \frac{V_{Ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230.94}{10} = 23.09 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Active and reactive power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L\, I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.09 \times 0.8 = 12{,}800 \text{ W} = 12.8 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3}\, V_L\, I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.09 \times 0.6 = 9{,}600 \text{ VAR} = 9.6 \text{ kVAR}$$

$$S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{12.8^2 + 9.6^2} = 16 \text{ kVA}$$

$$\boxed{V_{Ph} = 230.94\,\text{V}, \quad I_L = 23.09\,\text{A}, \quad P = 12.8\,\text{kW}, \quad Q = 9.6\,\text{kVAR}}$$

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Fourier Series representation:**

Any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform $f(t)$ with fundamental period $T$ can be expressed as:

$$f(t) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

where:

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T f(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt, \quad b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T f(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

In power systems, the most common non-sinusoidal waveforms are produced by **rectifiers, inverters, and SMPS**. The fundamental ($50\,\text{Hz}$) carries the useful power; higher harmonics ($n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots$) cause **heating, resonance, and equipment malfunction**.

**THD Definition:**

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} F_n^2}}{F_1} \times 100\,\%$$

**IEEE 519-2014 Significance:**
- $V_{THD} \leq 5\%$ at PCC for low/medium voltage systems.
- $I_{THD}$ limits depend on $I_{SC}/I_L$ ratio (short-circuit to load current).
- Compliance ensures utility-grade power quality and protection of consumer equipment.

---

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** A **square wave** voltage signal has a peak amplitude of **$A = 200\,\text{V}$** and fundamental frequency $f_0 = 50\,\text{Hz}$.
> (i) Derive the **Fourier series** expansion.
> (ii) Compute the amplitudes of the **1st, 3rd, and 5th harmonics**.
> (iii) Calculate the **RMS value** and **THD (%)**.
> (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A balanced **3-phase delta-connected** load draws a line current of **$20\,\text{A}$** at line voltage $V_L = 415\,\text{V}$, 0.85 power factor lagging.
> (i) Find the **phase voltage, phase current, and total power**.
> (ii) Compare star and delta connections in terms of starting current and harmonic behavior.
> (7 Marks)

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**(i) Fourier series for square wave** (odd, zero average):

$$v(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega_0 t)$$

**(ii) Harmonic amplitudes** with $A = 200$ V:

- $V_1 = \frac{4 \times 200}{\pi} = 254.65 \text{ V}$
- $V_3 = \frac{254.65}{3} = 84.88 \text{ V}$
- $V_5 = \frac{254.65}{5} = 50.93 \text{ V}$

**(iii) RMS and THD:**

RMS (theoretical) for square wave $= A = 200$ V.

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \times 100\,\%$$

$$THD = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} \times 100\,\% = \sqrt{0.2337} \times 100\,\% \approx 48.34\,\%$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = 254.65\,\text{V}, \ V_3 = 84.88\,\text{V}, \ V_5 = 50.93\,\text{V}, \ V_{rms} = 200\,\text{V}, \ THD = 48.34\%}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**(i) Delta-connected calculations:**

$$V_{Ph} = V_L = 415 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{Ph} = \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{20}{1.732} = 11.55 \text{ A}$$

$$P = \sqrt{3}\, V_L\, I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 20 \times 0.85 = 12{,}223 \text{ W} \approx 12.22 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Star vs Delta Comparison:**

| Parameter | Star (Y) | Delta ($\Delta$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Line-to-Phase Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{Ph}$ (step-up) | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ (same) |
| Line-to-Phase Current | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{Ph}$ (higher) |
| Neutral | Available (4-wire) | Not available (3-wire) |
| Starting Current (Motor) | Lower (1/3 of delta) | Higher (3× star) |
| Triplen Harmonics (3rd, 9th) | Returns through neutral | Circulates within delta (no neutral flow) |
| Common Use | Distribution, LT motors | Transmission, HT motors, VFDs |

$$\boxed{V_{Ph} = 415\,\text{V}, \ I_{Ph} = 11.55\,\text{A}, \ P = 12.22\,\text{kW}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Mistake:** Using $V_L = V_{Ph}$ for a star-connected system. **Reality:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{Ph}$. Loss: **3 marks per instance**.
> 2. **Mistake:** Computing $P = V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (single-phase formula) for a 3-phase load. **Reality:** $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$. Loss: **up to 7 marks**.
> 3. **Mistake:** Confusing $I_L$ and $I_{Ph}$ between star and delta. **Reality:** Star → $I_L = I_{Ph}$; Delta → $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{Ph}$.
> 4. **Mistake:** Using peak values instead of RMS in THD calculation. **Reality:** THD requires **RMS** harmonic amplitudes throughout.
> 5. **Mistake:** Forgetting that **even harmonics are zero** for symmetric square/triangular waves. Many students write non-zero $a_2$ or $b_2$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mistake:** Not including the **DC component** check ($a_0/2$ term) in Fourier analysis. If the waveform has no DC offset, state $a_0 = 0$ explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Line Voltage vs Phase Voltage** — memorize the two relations (Star and Delta) cold; they appear in **every** KTU Power Quality paper.
- **$\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$** — always use three decimals; do not approximate as $1.7$.
- **Power Formula** — $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is universal; never use the single-phase version.
- **Fourier Series** — expresses any periodic waveform as a sum of sines and cosines; the coefficients are computed by integration over one period.
- **Fourier Transform** — the continuous-frequency counterpart for *non-periodic* (transient) signals.
- **THD** — measures "distortion level"; IEEE 519 mandates $\leq 5\%$ voltage THD at PCC.
- **Square Wave THD = 48.34%** — a permanent KTU numerical.
- **Triangular Wave THD ≈ 12%** — also a frequently tested result.
- **Star connection** = 4-wire, neutral available, lower phase voltage, used in distribution.
- **Delta connection** = 3-wire, no neutral, higher line current, suppresses triplen harmonics, used in transmission.
- **Harmonic order $n$** — always an integer; **odd harmonics** dominate in symmetric waveforms; **triplen harmonics** ($3, 9, 15, \ldots$) are zero-sequence and behave differently in star vs delta.
- **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)** — the digital algorithm used by every power quality analyzer to compute Fourier coefficients in real time.
- **KTU Hot Tip:** When asked to "compare" star and delta, always structure your answer as a **table** — it earns presentation marks and reduces the chance of missing a point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 519
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** IEEE 519 # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **IEEE 519 – 2014 (Recommended Practice and Requirements for Harmonic Control in Electric Power Systems)** is the universally accepted standard that establishes the **boundary conditions** between the **utility (supply side)** and the **customer (load side)** at the **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** to maintain acceptable **voltage** and **current** harmonic distortion levels in an electrical power system. The standard prescribes two distinct sets of limits: 1. **Voltage Harmonic Distortion Limits** — applied at the PCC to protect the utility's voltage waveform quality. 2. **Current Harmonic Distortion Limits** — applied to individual customers so that no single load pollutes the grid beyond its share. The standard fundamentally states: > *"The system has the responsibility not to produce harmonic voltages beyond certain levels, and the user has the responsibility not to draw harmonic currents beyond certain levels."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water pipeline (the utility)** supplying water to multiple **houses (the loads/customer)**. - The water utility promises to deliver **clean, steady-pressure water (low voltage distortion)** at the main tap. - Each house is allowed to draw water but is **NOT allowed to dump dirty water, chemicals, or waste back into the main pipe** (i.e., NOT allowed to inject excessive harmonic currents). > [!IMPORTANT] > **IEEE 519 acts like the "traffic rulebook" for harmonics**: > - The **Utility** controls **Voltage Distortion** (clean water quality). > - The **Customer** controls **Current Distortion** (how much waste/pollution can be injected back). > - The **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** is the "boundary tap" where both rules are enforced and measured. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Point of Common Coupling | **PCC** | The electrical location where the customer and utility interface (typically the metering point) | | Short-circuit current at PCC | **Isc** | Maximum fault current available at PCC | | Maximum demand load current | **IL** | Fundamental frequency load current at PCC | | Total Harmonic Distortion | **THD** | Ratio of RMS harmonic content to fundamental RMS | | Total Demand Distortion | **TDD** | Same as THD but normalized to peak demand current (IL) | | Individual Harmonic | **Ih** | RMS value of the *h*-th harmonic component | | Odd Harmonics | h = 3, 5, 7… | Non-triplen (5,7,11,13) and Triplen (3,9,15) | | Even Harmonics | h = 2, 4, 6… | Usually small in balanced 3-phase systems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why normalize current to IL (not fundamental current)?** Because as load varies, harmonic current varies too. Normalizing to the **maximum demand** gives a **stable, fair benchmark** — this is the *Total Demand Distortion (TDD)* concept introduced by IEEE 519. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Scope & Objectives of IEEE 519 The standard addresses harmonic control across the system in **five categories**: 1. **Recommended distortion limits** for system voltage. 2. **Recommended current distortion limits** for individual customers. 3. **Recommended practices** for the application of harmonic limits. 4. **Recommended practices** for multi-user networks. 5. **Recommended practices** for improving harmonic compatibility. ## 2.2 Voltage Harmonic Distortion Limits (at PCC) > [!NOTE] > The voltage limits depend on the **system voltage class**, NOT on the customer. They are the utility's responsibility. | Bus Voltage V at PCC | Individual Harmonic (%) | THD (%) | |---|---|---| | V ≤ 1 kV | **5.0** | **8.0** | | 1 kV < V ≤ 69 kV | **3.0** | **5.0** | | 69 kV < V ≤ 161 kV | **1.5** | **2.5** | | V > 161 kV | **1.0** | **1.5** | **Logic behind the numbers:** - Low-voltage systems have higher distortion tolerance because equipment is cheaper to manufacture robust. - HV/EHV systems have stricter limits because the disturbance propagates further and affects more users. ## 2.3 Current Harmonic Distortion Limits (Individual Customer) > [!IMPORTANT] > The current limits depend on the **ratio Isc/IL** (system strength vs. load size). A **"stiff" system** (high Isc/IL) → more current harmonics permitted. A **"weak" system** (low Isc/IL) → strict current harmonic limits. **Maximum Harmonic Current Distortion in % of IL** | Isc/IL | h < 11 | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 17 ≤ h < 23 | 23 ≤ h < 35 | 35 ≤ h | TDD | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | < 20* | 4.0 | 2.0 | 1.5 | 0.6 | 0.3 | 5.0 | | 20–50 | 7.0 | 3.5 | 2.5 | 1.0 | 0.5 | 8.0 | | 50–100 | 10.0 | 4.5 | 4.0 | 1.5 | 0.7 | 12.0 | | 100–1000 | 12.0 | 5.5 | 5.0 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 15.0 | | > 1000 | 15.0 | 7.0 | 6.0 | 2.5 | 1.4 | 20.0 | `*` All power generation equipment is limited to these values regardless of actual Isc/IL. **Reading the table:** - **Left column (Isc/IL)**: ratio of available short-circuit current to load current at PCC. - **First 4 columns**: per-harmonic limits for **odd harmonics**. - **TDD column**: total demand distortion. - **Even harmonics** are limited to **25% of the odd harmonic limits** above. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize these four equations. Every KTU numerical problem on IEEE 519 uses one of them.** | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100\%$$ | Total Demand Distortion — RMS of all harmonic currents divided by **maximum demand load current** | | 2 | $$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$$ | Total Harmonic Distortion of current — same numerator but normalized to **fundamental current** | | 3 | $$THD_V = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ | Total Harmonic Distortion of voltage — RMS of all harmonic voltages divided by **fundamental voltage** | | 4 | $$IHR_h = \frac{I_h}{I_1} \times 100\%$$ | Individual Harmonic Ratio of order *h* | | 5 | $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{MVA_{sc}}{MVA_L} = \frac{kVA_{base}}{kVA_{load}}$$ | Ratio of short-circuit capacity to load demand — used to **enter the IEEE 519 current table** | ## 2.5 Why "Demand" and not "Fundamental"? > [!WARNING] > A common KTU pitfall: students confuse **THD (fundamental-based)** with **TDD (demand-based)**. They are NOT the same. > - **THD_I** uses $I_1$ (actual fundamental current). > - **TDD** uses $I_L$ (peak/contracted demand). > - When the load is operating at **full demand**, $I_1 = I_L$ and both are equal. > - When the load is **lighter than full demand**, $I_1 < I_L$ → TDD < THD_I. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility IEEE 519 is enforced by utilities worldwide as a **prerequisite for interconnection approval** of: - Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) - Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS) - Solar PV inverters - Wind turbine converters - Electric vehicle DC fast chargers - Data center power systems - Industrial arc furnaces and large rectifier loads Compliance is typically demonstrated by a **power quality audit** measuring harmonics at the PCC over a 7-day period. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation of the THD/TDD Expressions The **instantaneous voltage** with harmonic components is expressed as: $$v(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t + \phi_2) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t + \phi_3) + \cdots + V_h \sin(h\omega t + \phi_h)$$ **Step 1 — RMS of a Sum of Orthogonal Sinusoids** For sinusoids of different frequencies, the RMS values add in **quadrature** (power sum): $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{V_2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{V_3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \cdots}$$ $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Define Fundamental RMS** $$V_{1,rms} = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to Isolate Harmonic Distortion** $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_{1,rms}^2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + \frac{V_3^2}{2} + \cdots}$$ $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_{1,rms}^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}$$ **Step 4 — Form the Ratio (Distortion Definition)** $$\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{1,rms}} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}{V_{1,rms}^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Define the Harmonic Distortion Term** By definition, **THD** is the **ratio of the harmonic RMS content to the fundamental RMS**: $$THD_V = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$$ This is the rigorous derivation of the THD formula used in IEEE 519. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — TDD and THD Computation **Problem:** A three-phase 6-pulse rectifier-fed load draws a fundamental current of $I_1 = 400$ A with the following harmonics: | Harmonic Order (h) | 5 | 7 | 11 | 13 | |---|---|---|---|---| | Current (A) | 80 | 60 | 30 | 25 | The maximum demand current is $I_L = 500$ A, and the short-circuit current at PCC is $I_{sc} = 12500$ A. **Step 1 — Compute Isc/IL ratio to select the IEEE 519 column** $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{12500}{500} = 25$$ This lies in the **20–50 row** of the IEEE 519 current table. **Step 2 — Apply the applicable limits** | Limit | Value | |---|---| | h < 11 | 7.0% | | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 3.5% | | 17 ≤ h < 23 | 2.5% | | 23 ≤ h < 35 | 1.0% | | 35 ≤ h | 0.5% | | TDD | 8.0% | **Step 3 — Compute Individual Harmonic Ratios (IHR)** $$IHR_5 = \frac{80}{400} \times 100\% = 20.0\% \;\;\;(\text{limit} = 7.0\%)$$ $$IHR_7 = \frac{60}{400} \times 100\% = 15.0\% \;\;\;(\text{limit} = 7.0\%)$$ $$IHR_{11} = \frac{30}{400} \times 100\% = 7.5\% \;\;\;(\text{limit} = 3.5\%)$$ $$IHR_{13} = \frac{25}{400} \times 100\% = 6.25\% \;\;\;(\text{limit} = 3.5\%)$$ **All four harmonics EXCEED the IEEE 519 limits** — the customer must install harmonic mitigation (active filter, passive filter, or 12-pulse rectifier). **Step 4 — Compute THD_I** $$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{80^2 + 60^2 + 30^2 + 25^2}}{400} \times 100\%$$ $$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{6400 + 3600 + 900 + 625}}{400} \times 100\%$$ $$THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{11525}}{400} \times 100\%$$ $$THD_I = \frac{107.36}{400} \times 100\% = 26.84\%$$ **Step 5 — Compute TDD** $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{11525}}{500} \times 100\% = \frac{107.36}{500} \times 100\% = 21.47\%$$ **Step 6 — Compare with TDD limit** $$TDD_{limit} = 8.0\% \quad \text{vs} \quad TDD_{actual} = 21.47\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **VIOLATION: TDD exceeds the limit by a factor of 2.68.** IEEE 519 compliance fails. Mandatory harmonic filtering is required. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Entering the IEEE 519 Table **Problem:** A 480 V industrial facility has a contract demand of 800 kVA. The utility's available short-circuit MVA at the PCC is 40 MVA. Classify the customer per IEEE 519. **Step 1 — Compute Isc/IL ratio using the equivalent relation** $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{MVA_{sc}}{MVA_L}$$ $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{40}{0.8} = 50$$ **Step 2 — Locate the row** The ratio equals **50** — this is the **boundary between the 20–50 row and the 50–100 row**. By IEEE 519 convention, the **stricter (lower) limit** applies when the ratio is on the boundary. **Step 3 — Strictest applicable limits** | Limit | Value | |---|---| | h < 11 | 7.0% | | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 3.5% | | TDD | 8.0% | **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation** > A "weak" utility connection (low MVA_sc relative to load) is **less tolerant of harmonic pollution**. This customer must use **active front-end drives** or install **harmonic mitigating transformers** to comply. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: IEEE 519 Compliance Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Utility Grid - Voltage Source] --> B[Point of Common Coupling - PCC] B --> C[Customer Facility - Load Bus] C --> D[Non-Linear Load Bank 1
6-Pulse Rectifier - VFD] C --> E[Non-Linear Load Bank 2
UPS - SMPS - LED Drivers] C --> F[Linear Load Bank
Motors - Heaters - Lighting] G[IEEE 519 Voltage Limits] -.Enforced at.-> B H[IEEE 519 Current Limits
based on Isc over IL] -.Enforced at.-> C D --> I[Harmonic Current Injection Ih] E --> J[Harmonic Current Injection Ih] I --> K[Aggregate Harmonic Current at PCC] J --> K F --> L[Fundamental Current I1 Only] K --> B L --> B M[Power Quality Meter] -.Measures.-> B N[THD-V Check] --> M O[TDD Check] --> M P[Individual Harmonic Check] --> M Q{Compliant?} -->|Yes| R[Normal Operation] Q -->|No| S[Install Harmonic Filter
Active or Passive] S --> C ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow: Applying IEEE 519 Limits ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify PCC] --> B{Measure System
Voltage at PCC} B --> C{V less than or equal 1 kV} C -->|Yes| D1[THD-V limit = 8 percent
Individual = 5 percent] C -->|No| E{1 to 69 kV} E -->|Yes| D2[THD-V limit = 5 percent
Individual = 3 percent] E -->|No| F{69 to 161 kV} F -->|Yes| D3[THD-V limit = 2.5 percent
Individual = 1.5 percent] F -->|No| D4[THD-V limit = 1.5 percent
Individual = 1 percent] D1 --> G[Apply Current Limits] D2 --> G D3 --> G D4 --> G G --> H[Compute Isc over IL Ratio] H --> I{Ratio Band?} I -->|less than 20| J1[TDD = 5 percent] I -->|20 to 50| J2[TDD = 8 percent] I -->|50 to 100| J3[TDD = 12 percent] I -->|100 to 1000| J4[TDD = 15 percent] I -->|greater than 1000| J5[TDD = 20 percent] J1 --> K[Check Individual Harmonics] J2 --> K J3 --> K J4 --> K J5 --> K K --> L{All Limits Met?} L -->|Yes| M[IEEE 519 Compliant] L -->|No| N[Install Mitigation - Filter or 12-Pulse Rectifier] ``` ## 4.3 Concept Map: The "Who is Responsible?" Framework ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UTILITY[Utility Responsibility] U1[Maintain Voltage Distortion
at PCC within limits] U2[Keep Voltage THD
below 5 percent at LV] end subgraph CUSTOMER[Customer Responsibility] C1[Limit Current Harmonics
Injected at PCC] C2[TDD below limit per Isc/IL] C3[Individual harmonics
below tabular values] end subgraph PCC_DECISION[PCC = Boundary] P1[Voltage Quality Measured Here] P2[Current Injection Measured Here] end UTILITY --> PCC_DECISION CUSTOMER --> PCC_DECISION ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Define Point of Common Coupling (PCC) as per IEEE 519. Why is it chosen as the reference point for harmonic measurement?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** is the **electrical location on the supply system where multiple customers may be connected**, typically **the metering point or the common bus** between the utility and the customer. It is chosen as the reference point for harmonic measurement because: 1. **(1 mark)** It is the only point where the harmonic contributions of multiple customers **sum together** and where the utility's voltage quality is delivered. 2. **(1 mark)** It is a **fair and unambiguous boundary** — the utility is responsible for voltage quality at PCC, and each customer is responsible for the current harmonics they inject at PCC. 3. **(1 mark)** It enables **enforceable contractual limits** between utility and customer based on standard measurements. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **Differentiate between THD and TDD as defined in IEEE 519.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | **THD** (Total Harmonic Distortion) | **TDD** (Total Demand Distortion) | |---|---|---| | **(1 mark) Reference Quantity** | Normalized to **fundamental current $I_1$** (or fundamental voltage $V_1$ for voltage THD) | Normalized to **maximum demand load current $I_L$** | | **(1 mark) Use Case** | Indicates **instantaneous waveform quality** at any operating point | Indicates **contractual compliance** with utility limits | | **(1 mark) Stability** | Varies with load level (decreases as load drops) | Constant for a given installation, used in IEEE 519 enforcement | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** State the objectives and scope of **IEEE 519 – 2014** standard. List the voltage harmonic distortion limits prescribed for systems operating at different voltage levels. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 6-pulse rectifier load draws a fundamental current of 250 A. The measured harmonic currents are: 5th = 90 A, 7th = 70 A, 11th = 40 A, 13th = 30 A. The short-circuit current at the PCC is 5000 A and the maximum demand current is 350 A. Check whether the load complies with IEEE 519 current harmonic limits. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Objectives of IEEE 519 – 2014 (3 marks):** 1. To establish **recommended harmonic distortion limits** for voltage at the PCC. 2. To establish **recommended current distortion limits** for individual customers. 3. To provide **recommended practices** for harmonic measurement, evaluation, and mitigation in single-user and multi-user networks. **Voltage Harmonic Limits Table (4 marks):** | Bus Voltage V at PCC | Individual Harmonic (%) | THD (%) | |---|---|---| | V ≤ 1 kV | 5.0 | 8.0 | | 1 kV < V ≤ 69 kV | 3.0 | 5.0 | | 69 kV < V ≤ 161 kV | 1.5 | 2.5 | | V > 161 kV | 1.0 | 1.5 | #### Model Solution (b): **Step 1 — Compute Isc/IL ratio (1 mark):** $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{5000}{350} \approx 14.29$$ This is **less than 20**, so the customer falls in the **strictest category (< 20)**. **Step 2 — Applicable IEEE 519 Current Limits (1 mark):** | Harmonic Band | Limit (%) | |---|---| | h < 11 | 4.0 | | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 2.0 | | 17 ≤ h < 23 | 1.5 | | TDD | 5.0 | **Step 3 — Compute Individual Harmonic Ratios (3 marks):** $$IHR_5 = \frac{90}{250} \times 100\% = 36.0\% \quad (\text{limit} = 4.0\%)$$ $$IHR_7 = \frac{70}{250} \times 100\% = 28.0\% \quad (\text{limit} = 4.0\%)$$ $$IHR_{11} = \frac{40}{250} \times 100\% = 16.0\% \quad (\text{limit} = 2.0\%)$$ $$IHR_{13} = \frac{30}{250} \times 100\% = 12.0\% \quad (\text{limit} = 2.0\%)$$ **All four harmonics VIOLATE the IEEE 519 limits.** **Step 4 — Compute TDD (2 marks):** $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{90^2 + 70^2 + 40^2 + 30^2}}{350} \times 100\%$$ $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{8100 + 4900 + 1600 + 900}}{350} \times 100\%$$ $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{15500}}{350} \times 100\% = \frac{124.50}{350} \times 100\% = 35.57\%$$ **Step 5 — Verdict (1 mark):** > [!WARNING] > **IEEE 519 compliance: FAILED.** TDD = 35.57% far exceeds the limit of 5.0%. The customer MUST install harmonic mitigation (e.g., passive tuned filter, active power filter, or upgrade to a 12-pulse or 18-pulse rectifier configuration). --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **concept of Total Demand Distortion (TDD)**. Why does IEEE 519 prefer TDD over THD for current harmonic evaluation? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 415 V industrial consumer has a contracted maximum demand of 600 kVA. The available short-circuit MVA at the PCC is 12 MVA. Determine the applicable IEEE 519 current harmonic limits (per harmonic band and TDD). The consumer's measured harmonic currents at full load (fundamental = 870 A) are: 5th = 70 A, 7th = 50 A, 11th = 35 A. Determine if the consumer is compliant. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Definition of TDD (3 marks):** $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100\%$$ where $I_h$ is the RMS value of the $h$-th harmonic current and $I_L$ is the **maximum demand load current** (fundamental frequency component at the point of common coupling under normal operating conditions). **Why TDD is Preferred (4 marks):** 1. **(1 mark)** THD changes continuously with the load operating point. A lightly loaded non-linear load may show high THD but inject **negligible absolute harmonic current** into the system. 2. **(1 mark)** TDD is **fixed** — it relates harmonic current to the customer's **contracted maximum demand**, providing a **stable benchmark** for enforcement. 3. **(1 mark)** It prevents **artificially high THD readings** at light load that would unfairly penalize compliant customers. 4. **(1 mark)** It enables utilities to size harmonic mitigation equipment based on worst-case (full demand) harmonic injection. #### Model Solution (b): **Step 1 — Compute Isc/IL ratio (1 mark):** $$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{MVA_{sc}}{MVA_L} = \frac{12}{0.6} = 20$$ The ratio equals **20**, placing the customer on the **boundary of the 20–50 row**. **Step 2 — Applicable IEEE 519 Current Limits (1 mark):** | Harmonic Band | Limit (%) | |---|---| | h < 11 | 7.0 | | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 3.5 | | 17 ≤ h < 23 | 2.5 | | 23 ≤ h < 35 | 1.0 | | TDD | 8.0 | **Step 3 — Compute Individual Harmonic Ratios (2 marks):** $$IHR_5 = \frac{70}{870} \times 100\% = 8.05\% \quad (\text{limit} = 7.0\%)$$ $$IHR_7 = \frac{50}{870} \times 100\% = 5.75\% \quad (\text{limit} = 7.0\%)$$ $$IHR_{11} = \frac{35}{870} \times 100\% = 4.02\% \quad (\text{limit} = 3.5\%)$$ **Step 4 — Compute TDD (2 marks):** $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{70^2 + 50^2 + 35^2}}{870} \times 100\%$$ $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{4900 + 2500 + 1225}}{870} \times 100\%$$ $$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{8625}}{870} \times 100\% = \frac{92.87}{870} \times 100\% = 10.67\%$$ **Step 5 — Verdict (1 mark):** > [!WARNING] > **COMPLIANCE: PARTIAL FAILURE.** > - **5th harmonic: 8.05% > 7.0%** → FAILS > - **11th harmonic: 4.02% > 3.5%** → FAILS > - **7th harmonic: 5.75% < 7.0%** → OK > - **TDD: 10.67% > 8.0%** → FAILS > > The customer is **NOT compliant** with IEEE 519. Mitigation required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on IEEE 519 problems:** > 1. **Confusing THD and TDD** — THD uses $I_1$ (fundamental); TDD uses $I_L$ (demand). If the question says "at full load," both are equal. Otherwise, TDD is smaller. > 2. **Wrong row in the table** — Many students pick the row based on Isc only, not the **Isc/IL ratio**. Always compute the ratio first. > 3. **Forgetting even harmonics limit** — Even harmonics are limited to **25% of the odd harmonic limits**. This is frequently tested. > 4. **Normalizing to wrong base** — Harmonic ratios in the table are **% of $I_L$** (for TDD) or **% of $I_1$** (for THD), NOT a percent of total RMS. Read the table header carefully. > 5. **Boundary value interpretation** — At Isc/IL = 20 exactly, the **stricter (< 20) limit applies**. State this assumption explicitly in your answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for IEEE 519** - [ ] **IEEE 519 – 2014** governs harmonic control at the **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)**. - [ ] **Voltage limits** depend on the **system voltage class** (utility's responsibility). - [ ] **Current limits** depend on the **Isc/IL ratio** (customer's responsibility). - [ ] **THD** uses fundamental current $I_1$ as reference; **TDD** uses maximum demand current $I_L$ as reference. - [ ] At **full load**, THD = TDD. At **light load**, TDD < THD. - [ ] **Isc/IL < 20** is the strictest category (TDD = 5%); **Isc/IL > 1000** is the most permissive (TDD = 20%). - [ ] **Even harmonics** are limited to **25% of the corresponding odd harmonic limits**. - [ ] **Power generation equipment** is always limited to the **< 20 ratio** row, regardless of actual Isc/IL. - [ ] **6-pulse rectifiers** produce characteristic harmonics of order $h = 6k \pm 1$ (5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19…). - [ ] **12-pulse rectifiers** eliminate 5th and 7th harmonics; residual is 11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th. - [ ] **Mitigation strategies** if non-compliant: passive filters, active power filters, 12/18-pulse rectifiers, harmonic mitigating transformers. - [ ] **Formula to enter the table**: $\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{MVA_{sc}}{MVA_{load}} = \frac{kVA_{base}}{kVA_{demand}}$. - [ ] Always **show all four calculation steps** in KTU answers: (1) ratio, (2) limits, (3) harmonic ratios, (4) TDD comparison. ---

topics.title: IEEE 1433 and IEC 61000 Power Quality Monitoring: Objectives and Measurement Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IEEE 1433 and IEC 61000 Power Quality Monitoring: Objectives and Measurement Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — IEEE 1159 / IEEE 1453 and IEC 61000 Power Quality Monitoring

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Editorial Clarification for Students:**
> The IEEE standard most commonly referenced for Power Quality Monitoring is **IEEE Std 1159™** (*IEEE Recommended Practice for Monitoring Electric Power Quality*), not "IEEE 1433." A closely associated standard is **IEEE Std 1453™** (Voltage Flicker). This note covers IEEE 1159 (primary), IEEE 1453 (flicker-specific), and the **IEC 61000-4-30** measurement methodology — all three are part of the official KTU 2024 syllabus under Module 3.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Quality (PQ) Monitoring** is the continuous or periodic process of measuring, recording, and analyzing the electrical quantities (voltage, current, frequency, harmonics, flicker, transients, etc.) at a specific point in the power system to characterize the quality of electrical power delivered to or consumed by the customer, in accordance with internationally accepted standards.

The two dominant international frameworks governing this process are:

| Standard | Issuing Body | Scope |
|----------|--------------|-------|
| **IEEE 1159 / 1453** | Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (USA) | Defines PQ phenomena, monitoring objectives, measurement guidelines, and flicker evaluation |
| **IEC 61000-4-30** | International Electrotechnical Commission (Europe/Global) | Defines the *measurement methodology*, accuracy classes, and aggregation algorithms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** IEEE 1159 defines *what to measure* (the phenomena, their duration, magnitude, and typical spectra), whereas IEC 61000-4-30 defines *how to measure* (the algorithms, time-aggregations, and instrument classes). KTU examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between the two.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Monitor of the Grid"

Think of the **electrical power system as a human body**:

- **Voltage and current** are like **blood pressure and pulse** — they must remain within healthy bounds.
- **PQ Monitoring** is analogous to a **24-hour Holter monitor** strapped to a patient: it continuously records every irregular heartbeat (sag, swell, transient, harmonic) so that doctors (utility engineers) can later diagnose and treat the patient (the load/grid).

In this analogy:
- **IEEE 1159** is the *medical textbook* that lists all possible cardiac irregularities, their symptoms, and severity.
- **IEC 61000-4-30** is the *clinical measurement protocol* — it dictates exactly which stethoscope to use, for how long, and how to interpret the printout.
- **The monitor instrument** is the **Power Quality Meter (PQ meter)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "3 M's of Power Quality Monitoring":**
> 1. **Measure** — Capture the waveform data.
> 2. **Monitor** — Apply thresholds over time.
> 3. **Mitigate** — Take corrective action.

### 1.3 Why PQ Monitoring Is Essential

Modern power systems suffer from PQ degradation due to:

- **Non-linear loads:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), SMPS, UPS, LED drivers, computer power supplies.
- **Renewable integration:** Solar inverters, wind turbine converters.
- **Switching events:** Capacitor bank switching, transformer energization, lightning surges.
- **Arc furnaces & welding:** Cause flicker, harmonics, and voltage fluctuations.

Without a structured monitoring framework, utilities and industrial customers cannot:
- Pinpoint the **source of disturbance** (utility-side vs. customer-side).
- **Quantify** losses (equipment overheating, motor derating, neutral conductor overload).
- **Comply** with regulatory limits (IEEE 519, IEC 61000-2 series).

### 1.4 Basic PQ Monitoring Objectives (Per IEEE 1159)

The standard enumerates the following **primary objectives** of PQ monitoring:

1. **Compliance Verification** — Confirm whether voltage and current at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) meet regulatory and contractual limits.
2. **Diagnostic Characterization** — Identify the type, magnitude, duration, and frequency of occurrence of disturbances.
3. **Source Identification** — Determine whether a disturbance originates upstream (utility) or downstream (customer).
4. **Trend Analysis** — Establish historical baselines to detect degradation over weeks, months, or years.
5. **Predictive Maintenance** — Predict equipment failure before it occurs by detecting subtle PQ patterns.
6. **Legal Documentation** — Provide court-admissible evidence in disputes between utility and customer.
7. **Power System Planning** — Inform decisions on capacitor banks, STATCOMs, filters, and network reinforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key IEEE 1159 Definition — Point of Common Coupling (PCC):**
> The *Point of Common Coupling (PCC)* is the **electrical node on the power system where multiple customers may be connected and where harmonic and other disturbance levels are typically measured and evaluated.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 1159 — Recommended Practice for Monitoring Electric Power Quality

### 2.1.1 Historical Context and Scope

IEEE 1159 was first published in **1995** and reaffirmed in **2019**. It is the principal American standard that:

- Defines the **seven major categories of electromagnetic phenomena** affecting PQ.
- Provides guidance on the **selection of monitoring instruments**.
- Recommends **measurement locations and durations**.
- Outlines **data analysis and reporting protocols**.

### 2.1.2 IEEE 1159 Categorization of Power Quality Phenomena

IEEE 1159 classifies PQ phenomena into **seven categories**, distinguished by their **spectral content, magnitude, and duration**:

| Category | Typical Spectral Content | Typical Duration | Typical Magnitude |
|----------|--------------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **1. Transients** | | | |
| 1a. Impulsive | 5 ns rise | < 50 ns | — |
| 1b. Oscillatory (low freq) | < 5 kHz | 0.3–50 ms | 0–4 pu |
| 1c. Oscillatory (med freq) | 5–500 kHz | 20 µs | 0–8 pu |
| 1d. Oscillatory (high freq) | 0.5–5 MHz | 5 µs | 0–4 pu |
| **2. Short-duration RMS variations** | | | |
| 2a. Sag (dip) | — | 0.5–30 cycles | 0.1–0.9 pu |
| 2b. Swell | — | 0.5–30 cycles | 1.1–1.8 pu |
| 2c. Interruption | — | 0.5–30 cycles | < 0.1 pu |
| **3. Long-duration RMS variations** | | | |
| 3a. Sustained interruption | — | > 30 cycles | 0.0 pu |
| 3b. Undervoltage | — | > 1 min | 0.8–0.9 pu |
| 3c. Overvoltage | — | > 1 min | 1.1–1.2 pu |
| **4. Voltage Imbalance** | — | steady-state | 0.5–2% |
| **5. Waveform Distortion** | | | |
| 5a. DC offset | — | steady-state | 0–0.1% |
| 5b. Harmonics | 0–100th H | steady-state | 0–20% |
| 5c. Interharmonics | 0–6 kHz | steady-state | 0–2% |
| 5d. Notching | — | steady-state | — |
| 5e. Noise | broadband | steady-state | 0–1% |
| **6. Voltage Fluctuations (Flicker)** | < 25 Hz | intermittent | 0.1–7% |
| **7. Power Frequency Variations** | — | < 10 s | ± 0.1 Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **"pu" stands for *per unit* — the ratio of the actual RMS value to the nominal RMS value. For example, 0.85 pu sag means the voltage dropped to 85% of nominal.**

### 2.1.3 IEEE 1159 Monitoring Recommendations

The standard recommends the following **structured monitoring procedure**:

1. **Define the objective** (compliance, diagnosis, source ID, etc.).
2. **Identify the monitoring location** — typically:
   - **PCC** for utility–customer interface verification.
   - **Internal bus** for load-side diagnostic monitoring.
   - **Sensitive equipment terminals** for end-use equipment safety.
3. **Select the instrument class** based on the phenomena of interest.
4. **Determine monitoring duration** — short-term (days) for transient diagnostics, long-term (months) for trend analysis.
5. **Set trigger thresholds** for event capture.
6. **Establish data storage and reporting protocols.**

### 2.1.4 IEEE 1159 Instrument Class Selection

IEEE 1159 groups monitoring instruments into **three performance classes**:

| Class | Sampling Rate | Bandwidth | Application |
|-------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Class A — Survey** | ≥ 128 samples/cycle | DC–3 kHz | General-purpose, long-term surveys |
| **Class B — Diagnostic** | ≥ 256 samples/cycle | DC–9 kHz | Detailed harmonic & interharmonic analysis |
| **Class C — Precision / Flicker** | ≥ 512 samples/cycle | DC–150 kHz | Captures transients, high-frequency phenomena |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** A common KTU question is *"Why can't a Class A instrument reliably capture impulsive transients?"* — because its limited bandwidth cannot resolve sub-microsecond rise times. Class C instruments are needed for high-frequency transients.

---

## 2.2 IEEE 1453 — Voltage Flicker

IEEE 1453 (and its European counterpart IEC 61000-4-15) addresses **voltage flicker** — the visible (or invisible at high frequencies) wavering of lamp brightness caused by rapid, repetitive voltage fluctuations in the 0.5–25 Hz range.

### 2.2.1 Flicker Mechanism

Flicker is caused by **intermittent loads** such as:
- **Arc furnaces** (steel industry).
- **Resistance welders.**
- **Large motors with cyclic loading.**
- **Wind turbines** (tower shadow effect).

The resulting voltage modulation excites the **human visual perception chain**, peaking at approximately **8.8 Hz** modulation frequency. The lamp-eye-brain response is non-linear and frequency-dependent, requiring the **flicker weighting filter**.

### 2.2.2 Key Flicker Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|--------|--------|------------|
| **Short-term flicker severity** | $P_{st}$ | Flicker severity over **10 minutes**, with $P_{st} = 1.0$ being the threshold of human irritation |
| **Long-term flicker severity** | $P_{lt}$ | Derived from 12 consecutive $P_{st}$ values: $P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{12} \sum_{k=1}^{12} P_{st,k}^3}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Important:** The cubic root in $P_{lt}$ comes from **cubic aggregation** — flicker events combine according to the cube root of the mean cube (since human perception is roughly proportional to the **cube** of voltage fluctuation amplitude).

---

## 2.3 IEC 61000 Series — The International Framework

The **IEC 61000** series is structured into multiple parts; for PQ monitoring, the most relevant are:

| IEC Part | Title | Relevance |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **IEC 61000-2-2** | Compatibility levels for LV systems | Defines *what is acceptable* in the grid |
| **IEC 61000-2-4** | Compatibility levels for industrial plants | Industrial environment thresholds |
| **IEC 61000-3-2 / -3-12** | Harmonic current emission limits | *Equipment-side* limits |
| **IEC 61000-4-7** | Harmonic measurement methodology | How to measure harmonics & interharmonics |
| **IEC 61000-4-15** | Flickermeter — Functional and design specifications | Flicker measurement |
| **IEC 61000-4-30** | Power quality measurement methods | **Master standard for PQ meters** |

### 2.3.1 IEC 61000-4-30 — The Measurement Standard

IEC 61000-4-30 defines **two classes of measurement methods**:

| Class | Description | Use Case |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Class A** | Strict accuracy, fixed algorithms, highest precision (±0.1% voltage, ±0.5% harmonic) | Contractual, regulatory, and dispute resolution |
| **Class B** | Relaxed accuracy, simpler algorithms | Statistical surveys, internal diagnostics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Class A vs Class B — KTU-Important:**
> Class A instruments must use **synchronized 10/12-cycle RMS aggregation windows** (50/60 Hz respectively) and **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** synchronization to the fundamental. Class B has greater latitude.

### 2.3.2 IEC 61000-4-30 Aggregation Methodology

The standard uses a **three-tier time aggregation scheme**:

1. **Basic measurement interval:** 10 cycles (50 Hz) or 12 cycles (60 Hz) — yields **one RMS value**.
2. **Short-time aggregation:** Aggregate 150 cycles (3 seconds) of RMS values into one **3-sec RMS**.
3. **Long-time aggregation:** Aggregate 10 minutes (≈ 200 × 3-sec blocks) of data into one **10-min RMS**.

This hierarchy is **mandatory for Class A instruments** and ensures that measurements from different manufacturers are **directly comparable**.

### 2.3.3 IEC 61000-4-30 RMS Voltage Computation

For the basic measurement interval, the RMS voltage is computed using the **squared-root-of-mean-square formula**:

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} v_n^2}$$

where $N$ = number of samples per aggregation window.

For a non-stationary waveform with harmonics:

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_h^2 + \cdots}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "The 10-12 Rule":**
> IEC 61000-4-30 specifies **10 cycles at 50 Hz** and **12 cycles at 60 Hz** as the fundamental aggregation window — this ensures **200 ms windows** in both cases, harmonizing worldwide measurements.

---

## 2.4 Measurement Issues in Power Quality Monitoring

Despite the robust standards, several **practical measurement issues** complicate real-world PQ monitoring:

### 2.4.1 Instrument-Related Issues

| Issue | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **CT/PT bandwidth** | Standard CTs (relaying class) clip frequencies above ~2 kHz, missing transients and high-order harmonics |
| **Aliasing** | Insufficient sampling rate causes high-frequency components to masquerade as lower frequencies |
| **Quantization noise** | Low-resolution ADCs introduce spurious harmonics |
| **Time synchronization** | Without GPS synchronization, multi-site measurements cannot be correlated |
| **Trigger threshold setting** | Too high → misses events; too low → floods memory with noise |

### 2.4.2 Waveform-Related Issues

| Issue | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Sag vs. Interruption discrimination** | Distinguishing a 5% sag from a near-zero interruption requires accurate RMS calculation |
| **Transient vs. Notch separation** | Notch and transient look similar in time domain but differ in spectrum |
| **Flicker vs. Slow voltage variation** | Sub-1 Hz modulation may look like a slow swell or sag |
| **Interharmonic vs. Harmonic separation** | Requires longer DFT windows (≥ 5 cycles) per IEC 61000-4-7 |

### 2.4.3 Data-Related Issues

| Issue | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Data storage** | Continuous recording at high sampling rates produces gigabytes per day |
| **Event characterization** | Defining "start" and "end" of a sag or transient |
| **Threshold dependencies** | Different utilities use different compliance thresholds |
| **PQ Index calculation** | Aggregating diverse events into a single composite index |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum v_n^2}$ | RMS voltage from sampled waveform | V |
| 2 | $V_{RMS}^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{H} V_h^2$ | RMS from harmonic components | V |
| 3 | $THD_V = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (voltage) | % |
| 4 | $THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (current) | % |
| 5 | $P_{st}$ | Short-term flicker severity over 10 min | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{12}\sum_{k=1}^{12} P_{st,k}^3}$ | Long-term flicker severity over 2 hr | dimensionless |
| 7 | Voltage Sag magnitude | $V_{sag} = V_{actual}/V_{nominal}$ | pu |
| 8 | Voltage Imbalance (NEMA) | $\%VUB = \frac{\max \vert V_{avg} - V_{phase} \vert}{V_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 9 | Crest Factor | $CF = V_{peak}/V_{RMS}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | K-factor (transformer derating) | $K = \frac{\sum_{h=1}^{H} h^2 I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{H} I_h^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | IEEE 519 Individual Harmonic Limit | $V_h \leq 3\% V_{nominal}$ (LV) | % |
| 12 | IEC aggregation window | $T_{window} = 200$ ms = 10 cycles (50 Hz) or 12 cycles (60 Hz) | ms |
| 13 | Frequency resolution of DFT | $\Delta f = 1/T_{window} = 5$ Hz | Hz |
| 14 | CBEMA / ITIC curve | Voltage tolerance envelope (steady-state RMS vs. duration) | pu vs. s |
| 15 | SEMI F47 curve | Sag ride-through for semiconductor equipment | pu vs. s |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always cite the *standard reference number* (e.g., "IEEE 1159", "IEC 61000-4-30 Class A") in your answer. Examiners award bonus marks for standard references.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Voltage from a Sampled Waveform

For a digitized waveform $v(t)$ sampled at $N$ points within one cycle, the **continuous-time RMS definition** is:

$$V_{RMS}^{continuous} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\, dt}$$

where $T$ is the period of the fundamental. Discretizing by replacing the integral with a Riemann sum using $N$ samples equally spaced at $\Delta t = T/N$:

$$V_{RMS}^{continuous} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=1}^{N} v^2(n\Delta t)\, \Delta t}$$

Substituting $\Delta t = T/N$:

$$V_{RMS}^{continuous} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \sum_{n=1}^{N} v_n^2 \cdot \frac{T}{N}}$$

The $T$ in the numerator and denominator cancel:

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} v_n^2}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each sample $v_n$ contributes its **square** to the energy budget of the cycle; the mean of these squares is the **mean-square value**, whose square root is the RMS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Aggregation Hierarchy per IEC 61000-4-30

**Step 1 — Fundamental window:**
For a 50 Hz system, one fundamental cycle $T_1 = 20$ ms. The basic window is **10 cycles**:

$$T_{basic} = 10 \times T_1 = 200 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — 3-second aggregation:**
The number of basic windows in 3 seconds is:

$$N_{3s} = \frac{3 \text{ s}}{T_{basic}} = \frac{3000 \text{ ms}}{200 \text{ ms}} = 15 \text{ basic windows}$$

Therefore, the **3-second RMS** is the average of 15 basic RMS values:

$$V_{RMS,3s} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{15} \sum_{j=1}^{15} V_{RMS,j}^2}$$

**Step 3 — 10-minute aggregation:**
Number of 3-second blocks in 10 minutes:

$$N_{10min} = \frac{600 \text{ s}}{3 \text{ s}} = 200 \text{ three-second blocks}$$

The **10-minute RMS** is:

$$V_{RMS,10min} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{200} \sum_{k=1}^{200} V_{RMS,3s,k}^2}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The **square-then-average-then-root** structure (vs. average-then-root) preserves the contribution of short-duration sags — a single 100 ms sag in a 10-minute window reduces the 10-minute RMS slightly, but a sag's *duration-weighted severity index* (used in CBEMA / SEMI F47 evaluations) carries the diagnostic weight.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: THD and Its Relationship to RMS

Starting with the **Parseval-equivalent RMS decomposition**:

$$V_{RMS}^2 = V_1^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2$$

Define **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** as:

$$THD_V = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2}}{V_1}$$

Now compute the **true RMS including harmonics** in terms of THD:

$$\sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2} = V_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_V^2}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{RMS,total} = V_1 \cdot \sqrt{1 + THD_V^2}$$

**Numerical example:** If $V_1 = 230$ V (RMS fundamental) and $THD_V = 8\% = 0.08$:

$$V_{RMS,total} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.08^2} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{1.0064} = 230 \cdot 1.00319 = 230.73 \text{ V}$$

> **Insight:** The RMS value **exceeds** the fundamental RMS by only ~0.32% when THD = 8%. THD *overstates* the impact on RMS, but **understates** the impact on **peak voltage**, since harmonics can shift the crest.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of IEEE 1159 and IEC 61000-4-30

| Parameter | IEEE 1159 | IEC 61000-4-30 |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Primary focus** | Phenomenon classification & survey practice | Measurement methodology & instrument specification |
| **Geographic origin** | USA | Europe / International |
| **Number of phenomena categories** | 7 major categories | References to other parts (e.g., -4-7 for harmonics, -4-15 for flicker) |
| **Aggregation window** | Recommended 200 ms basic window | Mandates 200 ms basic window (Class A) |
| **Instrument classes** | Class A / B / C (by bandwidth) | Class A (precision) / Class B (standard) |
| **Flicker method** | References IEEE 1453 | References IEC 61000-4-15 (flickermeter) |
| **Application emphasis** | Diagnostics & characterization | Compliance verification & disputes |
| **Time synchronization** | Recommended but not mandated | Mandates GPS synchronization (Class A) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Flicker Aggregation

> **Problem:** A 10-minute flicker measurement produces the following $P_{st}$ values at a wind farm PCC:
> $P_{st,1} = 0.8$, $P_{st,2} = 1.2$, $P_{st,3} = 0.9$, … and for $k = 4$ to $12$ each equals 1.0. Compute $P_{lt}$.

**Solution:**

We need $P_{lt}$ from 12 values, where 3 known values are $0.8$, $1.2$, $0.9$ and the remaining 9 are each $1.0$.

Step 1: Compute the sum of cubes:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{12} P_{st,k}^3 = 0.8^3 + 1.2^3 + 0.9^3 + 9 \times 1.0^3$$

Evaluating each cube:

$$0.8^3 = 0.512$$
$$1.2^3 = 1.728$$
$$0.9^3 = 0.729$$
$$9 \times 1.0 = 9.000$$

Summing:

$$\sum P_{st,k}^3 = 0.512 + 1.728 + 0.729 + 9.000 = 11.969$$

Step 2: Apply the cubic-root-of-mean-cube formula:

$$P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{11.969}{12}} = \sqrt[3]{0.9974}$$

Step 3: Take the cube root:

$$P_{lt} = 0.9991 \approx 1.0$$

> **Engineering verdict:** $P_{lt} \approx 1.0$ means the long-term flicker is **right at the human irritation threshold** — the wind farm operator must take flicker mitigation measures (e.g., STATCOM or SVC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cubic vs. Linear Aggregation — Why Cube?**
> Human flicker perception is roughly proportional to the **cube** of voltage fluctuation (Stevens' power law). Therefore, when combining $P_{st}$ values, the formula uses **mean of cubes → cube root**, mathematically preserving the perceptual weighting.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Sag Magnitude Calculation

> **Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz system experiences a momentary voltage sag to 195 V for 5 cycles. Compute (a) sag depth in pu, (b) sag duration in ms, (c) classify per IEEE 1159.

**Solution:**

**(a) Sag depth in pu:**

$$V_{sag,pu} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{nominal}} = \frac{195}{230} = 0.8478 \text{ pu}$$

Sag depth below nominal:

$$\Delta V = 1.0 - 0.8478 = 0.1522 \text{ pu} \approx 15.2\%$$

**(b) Sag duration in ms:**

$$T_{sag} = 5 \text{ cycles} \times \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ cycles/s}} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

**(c) IEEE 1159 Classification:**

- Duration = 100 ms = 5 cycles (within 0.5–30 cycles → *short-duration*).
- Magnitude = 0.85 pu (within 0.1–0.9 pu → *sag/dip*).
- **Classification: Short-duration Voltage Sag.**

> **Engineering verdict:** A 15% sag for 100 ms is **below the ITIC curve** tolerance for most electronic equipment — likely causes computer/trip events. SEMI F47 requires ride-through to 0.82 pu for 100 ms → this sag is right on the SEMI F47 boundary.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Differentiate between IEEE 1159 and IEC 61000-4-30 in terms of their scope and focus in power quality monitoring.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IEEE 1159 | IEC 61000-4-30 |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| **Scope** | Defines the *categories of PQ phenomena* and provides recommended practices for monitoring | Defines the *measurement methodology*, instrument classes, and aggregation algorithms |
| **Focus** | **What** to measure — 7 categories of disturbances | **How** to measure — fixed 200 ms window, 3-sec, 10-min aggregation |
| **Origin** | American standard (IEEE) | International standard (IEC) |
| **Instruments** | Categorizes by bandwidth (Class A/B/C) | Categorizes by accuracy (Class A precision / Class B standard) |

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner memory aid:** *"IEEE 1159 tells you **what** is bad on the grid; IEC 61000-4-30 tells you **how** to measure it."*

*(1 mark for IEEE 1159 description, 1 mark for IEC 61000-4-30 description, 1 mark for the contrast/differentiation)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — December 2023]`
**List the seven major categories of power quality phenomena as per IEEE 1159. Mention any two typical magnitudes or durations for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Transients** — Impulsive (< 50 ns), Oscillatory (0.3–50 ms).
2. **Short-duration RMS variations** — Sag (0.5–30 cycles, 0.1–0.9 pu), Swell (0.5–30 cycles, 1.1–1.8 pu).
3. **Long-duration RMS variations** — Undervoltage (> 1 min, 0.8–0.9 pu), Overvoltage (> 1 min, 1.1–1.2 pu).
4. **Voltage imbalance** — 0.5–2% (steady-state).
5. **Waveform distortion** — Harmonics (0–20% THD), Notching.
6. **Voltage fluctuations (Flicker)** — 0.1–7% at < 25 Hz.
7. **Power frequency variations** — ± 0.1 Hz around nominal.

*(0.5 mark per correct phenomenon with magnitude/duration → 7 × 0.5 ≈ 3 marks; alternatively 1 mark per phenomenon × 3 = 3 marks with the rest as supporting examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain in detail the objectives of power quality monitoring as outlined in IEEE 1159. Discuss the role of the **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IEEE 1159 standard outlines **seven primary objectives** of power quality monitoring, which can be summarized as follows:

1. **Compliance Verification** — Ensuring that voltage and current at the PCC conform to the limits set by regulatory bodies (e.g., IEEE 519, IEC 61000-2-2). Compliance is typically verified using long-term 10-minute RMS aggregations per IEC 61000-4-30.

2. **Diagnostic Characterization** — Identifying the type (sag, swell, transient, harmonic), magnitude, duration, and frequency of occurrence of each disturbance. This is essential for **root-cause analysis** of equipment malfunction.

3. **Source Identification** — Determining whether a disturbance originates upstream (utility) or downstream (customer). Direction-of-disturbance analysis using synchronized measurements at PCC helps attribute responsibility.

4. **Trend Analysis** — Long-term monitoring (months to years) reveals gradual deterioration in PQ, such as rising THD due to increased non-linear load penetration.

5. **Predictive Maintenance** — Subtle PQ patterns (e.g., slowly rising 5th harmonic, recurring capacitor-switching transients) can predict transformer or capacitor bank failure weeks in advance.

6. **Legal Documentation** — Court-admissible evidence in disputes between utility and customer requires Class A measurements per IEC 61000-4-30 with GPS time synchronization.

7. **Power System Planning** — PQ data informs capital investment decisions: capacitor bank sizing, STATCOM placement, harmonic filter design, network reinforcement.

**Role of the Point of Common Coupling (PCC):**

The **PCC** is defined by IEEE 1159 as the *electrical node where multiple customers may be served and where PQ is typically measured and evaluated.* Its roles include:

- **Interface demarcation** — legally separates utility responsibility (upstream) from customer responsibility (downstream).
- **Reference measurement point** — IEEE 519 harmonic limits, IEC 61000-2-2 compatibility levels, and most utility tariffs apply *at the PCC*.
- **Aggregation point** — disturbances from many downstream loads combine and become visible at PCC.
- **Compliance anchor** — power-quality contracts and penalty clauses are pegged to PCC measurements.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** 7 objectives × 0.7 ≈ 4.9 marks + PCC discussion = 2.1 marks.

---

#### **(b)** Describe the IEC 61000-4-30 measurement methodology in detail. Include the basic measurement interval, three-tier time aggregation, and Class A / Class B distinctions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IEC 61000-4-30 standard defines a **uniform measurement methodology** so that different instruments, in different countries, produce directly comparable results.

**1. Basic Measurement Interval (Fundamental Window):**
- For **50 Hz** systems: **10 fundamental cycles = 200 ms**.
- For **60 Hz** systems: **12 fundamental cycles = 200 ms** (since 12/60 s = 0.2 s).
- This 200 ms window is **mandatory for Class A instruments** and yields a single RMS, harmonic, or flicker value.

**2. Three-Tier Time Aggregation:**

| Tier | Aggregation Interval | Composition | Purpose |
|------|----------------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **200 ms** (basic) | Raw samples (≥ 256/cycle) | Captures cycle-to-cycle RMS variation |
| 2 | **3 seconds** | 15 consecutive 200 ms values (50 Hz) | Captures flicker and short events |
| 3 | **10 minutes** | 200 consecutive 3-sec values | Captures steady-state trends and compliance |

The aggregation is performed by **squaring → averaging → square-rooting** at each tier:

$$V_{RMS,tier} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_{tier}}\sum V_{RMS,sub}^2}$$

**3. Class A vs Class B Distinction:**

| Aspect | Class A (Precision) | Class B (Standard) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Voltage accuracy** | ± 0.1% of $U_{din}$ | ± 0.5% of $U_{din}$ |
| **Harmonic accuracy** | ± 5% of measured value | ± 10% of measured value |
| **Time synchronization** | GPS-disciplined | Free-running sufficient |
| **Aggregation window** | Strict 200 ms ± 0.02% | Flexible (looser tolerance) |
| **Use case** | Contractual / dispute resolution / regulatory compliance | Internal statistical surveys |

**4. Flagging Concept:**
Class A instruments must **flag** any interval during which a measurement could not be reliably performed (e.g., due to instrument range overflow, sag deeper than measurable). The flag is propagated through the aggregation chain.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Basic window 1.5 marks + 3-tier aggregation 2.5 marks + Class A/B 2 marks + flagging/practical note 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

#### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Power Quality Monitoring Instrument** as per IEEE 1159 instrument classes. Discuss Class A, B, and C selection criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A PQ monitoring instrument comprises the following sequential stages:

```
Power System → Voltage/Current Sensors (CT/PT)
              → Signal Conditioning + Anti-aliasing filter
              → Synchronous Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)
              → PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) → DSP
              → Multi-function Algorithm Block
              → Storage / Communication
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation:**

1. **CT and PT** — Step down voltage/current to instrument-level signals. **Bandwidth** is critical: standard CTs clip above ~2 kHz, so wideband CTs (with $\geq$ 3 kHz passband) are preferred for harmonic-rich applications.

2. **Signal Conditioning** — Anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cutoff $\leq f_s/2.56$) per the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion**, gain adjustment, and isolation.

3. **ADC** — Resolution ≥ 16 bits for Class A; sampling rate depends on the IEEE 1159 class:
   - Class A (Survey): ≥ 128 samples/cycle
   - Class B (Diagnostic): ≥ 256 samples/cycle
   - Class C (Precision): ≥ 512 samples/cycle

4. **PLL** — Synchronizes the ADC sampling to the fundamental frequency (Class A only), ensuring integer number of cycles per DFT window.

5. **DSP Block** — Performs:
   - 200 ms RMS aggregation.
   - FFT (DFT) up to the 50th harmonic (or 63rd per IEC 61000-4-7).
   - Flicker weighting filter per IEC 61000-4-15.
   - Transient detection and classification.

6. **Storage & Communication** — Stores waveform on trigger; communicates via Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, or Ethernet to the central SCADA.

**Instrument Class Selection Criteria (IEEE 1159):**

| Class | Bandwidth | Application | Example Phenomena |
|-------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **A — Survey** | DC–3 kHz | Routine long-term surveys | Slow sags, swells, harmonics up to ~15th |
| **B — Diagnostic** | DC–9 kHz | Detailed engineering analysis | Harmonics up to ~50th, interharmonics |
| **C — Precision** | DC–150 kHz | Transient capture, research | Impulsive transients, oscillatory MHz events |

**Selection Logic:**
- *If only steady-state PQ indices are required → Class A.*
- *If harmonic and interharmonic analysis is needed → Class B.*
- *If high-frequency transient capture is required → Class C.*

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Block diagram 2 marks + stage-by-stage 2 marks + Class criteria 2 marks + selection logic 1 mark.

---

#### **(b)** Discuss the major **measurement issues** encountered in practical power quality monitoring. Suggest appropriate mitigation strategies for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The practical measurement issues can be grouped into **four domains**:

**Domain 1 — Instrument / Sensor Issues:**

| Issue | Mitigation |
|-------|------------|
| CT bandwidth limitation | Use **wideband CTs** with passband ≥ 3 kHz (e.g., Pearson electronics, ABB IAR) |
| PT saturation during transients | Use **electronic PTs** or CVTs with linear response |
| ADC quantization noise | Use ≥ 16-bit ADC; oversample and decimate |
| Aliasing | Apply anti-aliasing filter with cutoff $\leq f_s/2.56$; use sampling rate ≥ 256 samples/cycle |

**Domain 2 — Algorithm / Processing Issues:**

| Issue | Mitigation |
|-------|------------|
| FFT spectral leakage | Use **Hann or Blackman window**; ensure integer cycles per DFT window via PLL |
| Misclassification of sag vs. interruption | Use **half-cycle RMS sliding window** with magnitude thresholds (0.1 pu boundary) |
| Notch vs. transient ambiguity | Cross-check with both **time-domain and frequency-domain** signatures |
| Interharmonic vs. harmonic confusion | Use IEC 61000-4-7 DFT grouping with rectangular 5-Hz bins |

**Domain 3 — Timing / Synchronization Issues:**

| Issue | Mitigation |
|-------|------------|
| Multi-site event correlation | GPS-disciplined time stamps (Class A IEC 61000-4-30) |
| PLL drift | Use **GPS-PPS reference** instead of line-frequency PLL for absolute timing |

**Domain 4 — Data Management Issues:**

| Issue | Mitigation |
|-------|------------|
| Storage overflow | Use **threshold-triggered recording** for transients; continuous compressed 10-min RMS for trends |
| Event start/end ambiguity | Apply **hysteresis** to thresholds (e.g., sag starts below 0.9 pu, ends above 0.95 pu) |
| Lack of comparable indices | Compute **composite PQ indices** (e.g., SARFI — System Average RMS Variation Frequency Index) |

> **Composite Index Example — SARFI$_{X\%}$:**
> Counts the number of RMS events per year where voltage falls below $X\%$ of nominal. SARFI$_{70}$ = number of events below 70%. Used widely by US utilities per IEEE 1366.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Four domains × 1.5 marks each ≈ 6 marks + SARDI/index example 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "IEEE 1433" with "IEEE 1159"** in the answer — Examiners will mark you down for misquoting the standard. Use **IEEE 1159** (PQ monitoring) or **IEEE 1453** (flicker) correctly.
> 2. **Writing "IEC 61000-4-30" when the question asks for the *phenomena* standard** — IEC 61000-4-30 is the *measurement methodology*; IEEE 1159 is the *phenomenon classification*.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention GPS synchronization** for Class A IEC 61000-4-30 — Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 4. **Computing $P_{lt}$ using arithmetic mean instead of cubic mean** — This is a guaranteed mark loss; the cubic-root-of-mean-cube is mandatory.
> 5. **Using 5 cycles (100 ms) for the basic IEC aggregation window instead of 10 cycles (200 ms)** — Examiners may mark this incorrect, depending on the question's wording.
> 6. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** of the numerical answer — always end with a verdict (e.g., "this $P_{lt}$ exceeds the IEEE 1453 limit of 1.0") for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Bulleted Summary):**

### ✅ **Standards Framework**
- **IEEE 1159** — defines 7 categories of PQ phenomena (transients, short-duration RMS, long-duration RMS, imbalance, waveform distortion, flicker, freq variations).
- **IEEE 1453** — flicker measurement; defines $P_{st}$ (10 min) and $P_{lt}$ (2 hr).
- **IEC 61000-4-30** — measurement methodology; defines 200 ms basic window, 3-sec and 10-min aggregation; Class A / Class B.
- **IEC 61000-4-7** — harmonic measurement with 5-Hz DFT bins and grouping.
- **IEC 61000-4-15** — flickermeter functional specification.
- **IEEE 519** — harmonic limits at PCC.
- **ITIC / CBEMA** — equipment ride-through curves.
- **SEMI F47** — sag ride-through for semiconductor tools.

### ✅ **Numerical / Formula Essentials**
- $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum v_n^2}$
- $V_{RMS,total} = V_1 \sqrt{1 + THD_V^2}$
- $THD_V = \sqrt{\sum_{h\geq 2} V_h^2}/V_1$
- $P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{12}\sum P_{st,k}^3}$
- IEC basic window = **10 cycles (50 Hz) or 12 cycles (60 Hz) = 200 ms**
- 3-sec aggregation = 15 basic windows; 10-min aggregation = 200 × 3-sec blocks.

### ✅ **Instrument Classes (IEEE 1159)**
- **Class A (Survey):** ≥ 128 samples/cycle, DC–3 kHz
- **Class B (Diagnostic):** ≥ 256 samples/cycle, DC–9 kHz
- **Class C (Precision):** ≥ 512 samples/cycle, DC–150 kHz

### ✅ **Measurement Issues to Always Mention**
- CT/PT bandwidth limitation → use wideband CTs.
- Aliasing → anti-aliasing filter + $f_s \geq 2.56 \times f_{max}$.
- FFT leakage → windowing + PLL.
- Time sync → GPS (Class A).
- Storage → trigger + compression.
- Event threshold ambiguity → hysteresis.

### ✅ **Key Distinctions to Remember**
- **IEEE 1159 = WHAT** to measure | **IEC 61000-4-30 = HOW** to measure.
- **IEEE 519 = LIMITS** at PCC | **IEEE 1159 = PHENOMENA** definitions.
- **IEC 61000-2-2 = COMPATIBILITY levels** (LV grid) | **IEC 61000-3-2 = EMISSION limits** (equipment).
- **Class A IEC = Contractual / disputes** | **Class B IEC = Internal use**.

### ✅ **Important Numerical Benchmarks**
- $P_{st} \leq 1.0$ ⇒ acceptable flicker.
- $THD_V \leq 5\%$ ⇒ IEEE 519 LV limit.
- $THD_I \leq 8\%$ ⇒ IEEE 519 LV limit (with $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio considered).
- Individual harmonic $\leq 3\%$ at LV PCC.
- Voltage imbalance $\leq 2\%$ (NEMA definition).
- Frequency deviation $\pm 0.1$ Hz in steady-state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering theory questions on this topic, **always quote the standard reference** (e.g., *"As per IEC 61000-4-30 Class A..."*), **state the numerical threshold**, and **end with a practical engineering verdict**. Examiners reward standard-aware answers that connect theory to practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Monitoring Instruments - Power Quality Analyzer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Monitoring Instruments - Power Quality Analyzer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ POWER QUALITY ANALYZER (PQA) — KTU Board Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Power Quality Analyzer (PQA)** is a microprocessor-based, multi-channel digital measuring instrument designed to simultaneously capture, process, record, and display the time-domain and frequency-domain characteristics of voltage and current waveforms in an electrical power system, in accordance with measurement methodologies defined by **IEC 61000-4-30** and harmonic analysis procedures specified by **IEC 61000-4-7**.

In simple terms, a PQA is the "**Electrocardiogram (ECG) machine for an electrical network**." Just as a cardiologist's ECG records every tiny abnormality in a human heartbeat, a PQA records every deviation, distortion, dip, swell, transient, and harmonic present in a power line.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Black Box" of an Aircraft ✈️

| Real-World Device | Power System Equivalent |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Flight Data Recorder (Black Box) | Power Quality Analyzer |
| Records altitude, speed, vibration | Records voltage, current, frequency, harmonics |
| Used after crash to investigate cause | Used to identify source of equipment malfunction |
| Multi-parameter, high sampling rate | Multi-channel, high sampling rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, students must clearly distinguish between (i) **conventional indicating meters** (V, I, f, P meters), (ii) **oscilloscopes**, (iii) **harmonic / spectrum analyzers**, and (iv) the integrated **Power Quality Analyzer (PQA)**. The PQA is the most comprehensive single instrument for PQ assessment.

### Key Monitoring Categories Addressed by a PQA

1. **Steady-State PQ Disturbances** — harmonics, inter-harmonics, DC offset, noise, notching.
2. **Voltage Events** — sags, swells, interruptions, imbalances.
3. **Transient Disturbances** — impulsive and oscillatory transients.
4. **Power Line Disturbances** — flicker, frequency deviation.

---

## 2. Standard Reference Quantities Used by a PQA

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Reference |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| $V_{rms}$ | True RMS Voltage | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| $I_{rms}$ | True RMS Current | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| $f$ | System Frequency | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| $THD_v$ | Voltage Total Harmonic Distortion | IEEE 519 |
| $THD_i$ | Current Total Harmonic Distortion | IEEE 519 |
| $P, Q, S$ | Active, Reactive, Apparent Power | IEEE 1459 |
| $P_{st}, P_{lt}$ | Short & Long Term Flicker Severity | IEC 61000-4-15 |
| $K_{factor}$ | Transformer Derating Factor | IEEE C57.110 |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for PQA Functions — "HITS-VIP":**
> **H**armonics, **I**mbalance, **T**ransients, **S**ags & Swells, **V**oltage & Current, **I**nterruptions, **P**ower & Flicker.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of a Power Quality Analyzer

A PQA is not a single sensor — it is a **multi-stage signal processing system**. Every commercial PQA (Fluke 435, Hioki PW3198, Dranetz PowerXplorer, Schneider ION7650) is built from the same five fundamental blocks.

### Block 1: Transduction Stage (Sensors)

- **Voltage Transducers** — Resistive dividers or **Potential Transformers (PTs)** scale down line voltage (e.g., 415 V) to low-voltage signal levels (±10 V) acceptable by the A/D converter.
- **Current Transducers** — **Current Transformers (CTs)** or **Rogowski coils** convert primary current (e.g., 1000 A) to a proportional milliamp signal.
- For DC and very low-frequency components, **Hall-effect sensors** or **shunt resistors** are used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:**
> The choice of CT vs. Rogowski coil determines the analyser's **bandwidth**. CTs are accurate from 45–65 Hz (fundamental), while Rogowski coils can faithfully reproduce frequencies from **1 Hz to 1 MHz** — essential for transient capture.

### Block 2: Signal Conditioning Stage

Each channel path includes:
1. **Anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — Prevents high-frequency components above the Nyquist frequency from folding back into the measurement band.
2. **Isolation Amplifier** — Protects the digital circuitry from HV faults and removes ground loops.
3. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** — Auto-scales the input so the A/D converter operates at maximum dynamic range without clipping.

### Block 3: Data Acquisition Stage (A/D Conversion)

- **Simultaneous Sampling** on all voltage and current channels (achieved using one A/D per channel or sample-and-hold circuits).
- Sampling rates for Class A instruments: **≥ 9.6 kSa/s** for 50 Hz systems, **≥ 10.24 kSa/s** for 60 Hz.
- For transient capture, sampling rates up to **1 MSa/s to 100 MSa/s** are used with dedicated transient boards.
- Resolution: **16-bit** typical, **24-bit** in premium analyzers for high dynamic range.

### Block 4: Digital Signal Processing (DSP) Stage

The DSP engine executes:

- **RMS calculation** over a 10/12-cycle window (200 ms for 50 Hz).
- **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** for harmonic decomposition.
- **Wavelet Transform** for transient localization in time-frequency domain.
- **Statistical aggregation** per IEC 61000-4-30 (e.g., 3-second, 10-minute, 2-hour values).

### Block 5: Display, Storage & Communication

- **Color LCD / Touchscreen** for waveforms, phasors, harmonic bar graphs.
- **Internal Memory** (typically 4 GB – 32 GB SSD) for event logs.
- **Communication:** USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, RS-485, **Modbus TCP**, IEC 61850.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is in the KTU 2024 syllabus and is **directly asked** in board exams. Memorize units, not just symbols.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Standard |
|---|-----------|---------|------|----------|
| 1 | True RMS Voltage | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$ | V (Volts) | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| 2 | True RMS Current | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} i_k^2}$ | A (Amperes) | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| 3 | Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage) | $THD_v = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | \% | IEEE 519 |
| 4 | Total Harmonic Distortion (Current) | $THD_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | \% | IEEE 519 |
| 5 | Total Demand Distortion | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | \% | IEEE 519 |
| 6 | Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 8 | Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ | VA | IEEE 1459 |
| 9 | Active Power (Single Phase) | $P = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k \cdot i_k$ | W (Watts) | IEEE 1459 |
| 10 | Reactive Power (Budeanu) | $Q = \sum_{h=1}^{H} V_h \cdot I_h \cdot \sin\phi_h$ | VAR | IEEE 1459 |
| 11 | Power Factor | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos\phi_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 12 | Displacement Power Factor | $DPF = \cos\phi_1$ | dimensionless | — |
| 13 | Voltage Unbalance (NEMA) | $\%V_{unb} = \dfrac{\max \vert V_{ab} - V_{avg} \vert}{V_{avg}} \times 100$ | \% | NEMA MG-1 |
| 14 | Voltage Unbalance (Symmetrical Components) | $VUF = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \times 100$ | \% | IEC 61000-4-30 |
| 15 | K-Factor (Transformer) | $K = \sum_{h=1}^{H} h^2 \left(\dfrac{I_h}{I_{rms}}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | IEEE C57.110 |
| 16 | Flicker Severity (Short Term) | $P_{st}$ (99th percentile over 10 min) | dimensionless | IEC 61000-4-15 |
| 17 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Hz | Sampling Theorem |
| 18 | Frequency Resolution (FFT) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Hz | DSP |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Trick:**
