> The denominator for **THD** is the **fundamental** ($V_1$ or $I_1$). The denominator for **TDD** is the **rated load current** ($I_{rated}$). Confusing these is the **#1 reason students lose 2 marks** in KTU Part A.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | PQA Function Used |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Renewable Energy Plants (Solar / Wind)** | Harmonic, Flicker, DC Injection measurement |
| **Data Centers** | Sag/Swell detection, UPS coordination verification |
| **Industrial Process Plants (VFDs, Arc Furnaces)** | Harmonics, Inter-harmonics, Notching |
| **Building Management Systems** | Long-term load profiling, PF correction audits |
| **Utility Substations** | Revenue-class measurement + PQ event logging |
| **Railway Traction Systems** | Harmonics and DC offset monitoring |

---

## 2.4 Comparison: Power Quality Analyzer vs. Other Monitoring Instruments

| Feature | Digital Multimeter | Oscilloscope | Spectrum Analyzer | **Power Quality Analyzer** |
|---------|--------------------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Measures RMS | Yes | No (calculated) | No | **Yes (Class A)** |
| Harmonic Analysis (up to 50th) | No | No (FFT only) | Yes | **Yes (per IEC 61000-4-7)** |
| Captures Sags/Swells | No | Manual | No | **Yes (Auto-trigger)** |
| Records Transients | No | Yes (limited memory) | No | **Yes (high-speed mode)** |
| Flicker Measurement | No | No | No | **Yes (per IEC 61000-4-15)** |
| Long-term Data Logging | No | No | No | **Yes (weeks/months)** |
| Standards Compliance | None | None | None | **IEC 61000-4-30** |
| **Cost (Approx.)** | Low | Medium | High | **Medium–High** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: True RMS from Sampled Data

The **true RMS** value is the most fundamental quantity a PQA must compute. Unlike an average-responding meter (which assumes a pure sinewave), a true RMS instrument must work correctly on any waveform, including highly distorted ones.

### Step 1 — Mathematical Definition of RMS

For a continuous, periodic signal $v(t)$ with period $T$, the RMS value is defined as:

$$v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^2(t) \, dt}$$

### Step 2 — Discretization for Digital Sampling

The PQA samples the continuous waveform at $N$ equally spaced instants within one period:
$t_k = k \cdot \Delta t$ where $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$ and $\Delta t = T/N$.

The integral is approximated by a **rectangular summation** (the simplest numerical integration rule):

$$\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t) \, dt \approx \sum_{k=1}^{N} v^2(t_k) \cdot \Delta t = \frac{T}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Back

Substituting into the RMS expression:

$$v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$$

The $T$ terms cancel out, yielding the **discrete RMS formula**:

$$\boxed{\,v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Verification with a Pure Sinewave

Let $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and consider a full period ($N$ samples).

The continuous RMS of a sinewave is $V_m / \sqrt{2}$. The discrete formula, in the limit of large $N$, must converge to the same value. This confirms correctness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

### Step 1 — Start with the Fourier Series Expansion

Any periodic, non-sinusoidal waveform can be expressed as a sum of sinusoids:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{H} V_h \sin(h\omega t + \phi_h)$$

Where $V_0$ is the DC component, $V_1$ is the fundamental, and $V_h$ for $h \geq 2$ are the **harmonic components**.

### Step 2 — RMS Value in Terms of Harmonics

Applying Parseval's theorem, the RMS value of the full waveform equals:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + \sum_{h=1}^{H} \left(\frac{V_h}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}$$

The RMS of the fundamental alone is $V_{1,rms} = V_1 / \sqrt{2}$.

### Step 3 — Definition of THD

The IEEE 519 standard defines THD as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components to the RMS of the **fundamental** alone:

$$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 4 — Substituting Peak Values

In terms of peak harmonic voltages $V_h$:

$$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

This is the formula implemented inside every commercial PQA's DSP engine.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem Statement:**
> A PQA samples a 50 Hz supply voltage and reports the following spectral components. Compute the THD and the True RMS voltage.
> - $V_1 = 325.27$ V (fundamental peak)
> - $V_3 = 28.5$ V (3rd harmonic peak)
> - $V_5 = 19.2$ V (5th harmonic peak)
> - $V_7 = 11.0$ V (7th harmonic peak)
> - $V_9 = 6.4$ V (9th harmonic peak)

### Solution

**Step A — Compute the numerator (sum of squares of harmonics):**

$$\sum_{h=2,3,5,7,9} V_h^2 = (28.5)^2 + (19.2)^2 + (11.0)^2 + (6.4)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(28.5)^2 &= 812.25 \\
(19.2)^2 &= 368.64 \\
(11.0)^2 &= 121.00 \\
(6.4)^2  &= \phantom{0}40.96 \\
\text{Sum} &= 1342.85 \text{ V}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B — Apply the THD formula:**

$$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{1342.85}}{325.27} \times 100\%$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1342.85} &= 36.64 \text{ V} \\
THD_v &= \frac{36.64}{325.27} \times 100\% = 11.26\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C — Compute the true RMS voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{1342.85}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum V_h^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{(325.27)^2 + 1342.85}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(325.27)^2 &= 105800.57 \\
105800.57 + 1342.85 &= 107143.42 \\
\sqrt{107143.42} &= 327.33 \\
V_{rms} &= \frac{327.33}{\sqrt{2}} = 231.46 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step D — Compare with pure sinewave RMS:**

$$V_{rms}^{\text{sine}} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} = 230.00 \text{ V}$$

**Step E — Final Results:**

$$\boxed{\,THD_v = 11.26\% \quad ; \quad V_{rms} = 231.46 \text{ V}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Observation:**
> A 230 V supply with 11% THD is typical of an industrial feeder supplying VFDs. The IEEE 519 limit at PCC for $V \leq 1$ kV is **8%**. Hence this feeder **violates** the recommended limit, and mitigation (active harmonic filter) is required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of PQA Based on Application

| Application | Required Bandwidth | Required Sampling | Required Memory | Recommended Class |
|-------------|--------------------|-------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| Residential energy audit | DC – 3 kHz | ≥ 5 kSa/s | 1 GB | Class S / B |
| Industrial harmonic survey | DC – 5 kHz | ≥ 10 kSa/s | 4 GB | Class A |
| Substation compliance audit | DC – 9 kHz | ≥ 9.6 kSa/s | 8 GB | **Class A (IEC 61000-4-30)** |
| Switching transient capture | DC – 1 MHz | ≥ 5 MSa/s | 16 GB | Class A + Transient board |
| Renewable plant flicker | DC – 400 Hz | ≥ 1 kSa/s | 2 GB | IEC 61000-4-15 flicker meter |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧭 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PQA

The diagram below maps the complete signal and data flow of a generic **three-phase, four-channel PQA** (3 voltages + 3 currents). Every commercial product follows this architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase A Voltage 415V] --> B1[PT Step Down]
    A2[Phase B Voltage 415V] --> B2[PT Step Down]
    A3[Phase C Voltage 415V] --> B3[PT Step Down]
    A4[Neutral] --> B4[Reference Divider]
    C1[Phase A Current] --> D1[CT or Rogowski Coil]
    C2[Phase B Current] --> D2[CT or Rogowski Coil]
    C3[Phase C Current] --> D3[CT or Rogowski Coil]
    C4[Neutral Current] --> D4[CT or Rogowski Coil]
    B1 --> E1[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    B2 --> E2[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    B3 --> E3[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    B4 --> E4[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    D1 --> F1[Burden Resistor and LPF]
    D2 --> F2[Burden Resistor and LPF]
    D3 --> F3[Burden Resistor and LPF]
    D4 --> F4[Burden Resistor and LPF]
    E1 --> G1[Isolation Amplifier]
    E2 --> G2[Isolation Amplifier]
    E3 --> G3[Isolation Amplifier]
    E4 --> G4[Isolation Amplifier]
    F1 --> H1[PGA Gain Stage]
    F2 --> H2[PGA Gain Stage]
    F3 --> H3[PGA Gain Stage]
    F4 --> H4[PGA Gain Stage]
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H2
    G3 --> H3
    G4 --> H4
    H1 --> I1[16 bit Simultaneous ADC]
    H2 --> I2[16 bit Simultaneous ADC]
    H3 --> I3[16 bit Simultaneous ADC]
    H4 --> I4[16 bit Simultaneous ADC]
    I1 --> J[DSP and Microprocessor]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[RMS Engine]
    J --> L[FFT Harmonic Engine]
    J --> M[Wavelet Transient Engine]
    J --> N[Flicker Engine IEC 61000-4-15]
    J --> O[Statistical Aggregator 3s 10min 2h]
    K --> P[Internal SSD 32 GB]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Color LCD Display]
    P --> R[Ethernet Modbus TCP IEC 61850]
    P --> S[USB Data Export]
    P --> T[Wi-Fi Cloud Upload]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:**
> - **Top region** → Transduction (PTs, CTs, Rogowski coils).
> - **Middle region** → Signal conditioning (filtering, isolation, gain).
> - **Centre** → Digitization (A/D converters).
> - **Lower region** → DSP processing engines.
> - **Bottom region** → Storage, display and communication.

---

## 4.2 Functional Decision Flow: How the PQA Selects a Measurement Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[User Selects Application] --> S2{Is Compliance Audit Required?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use Class A IEC 61000-4-30]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is Long Term Trending Required?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Use Class A with 10 min Aggregation]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Is Transient Capture Required?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Enable High Speed Transient Board]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Use Class S Instrument]
    S3 --> S9[Set Sampling to 9.6 kSa per second for 50 Hz]
    S5 --> S9
    S7 --> S10[Set Sampling to 1 MSa per second]
    S8 --> S11[Set Sampling to 5 kSa per second]
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchy of PQA Measurement Standards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root Power Quality Standards Framework] --> R1[IEEE 1159 - Categorization of Disturbances]
    R --> R2[IEEE 519 - Harmonic Limits at PCC]
    R --> R3[IEEE 1459 - Power Definitions]
    R --> R4[IEEE C57.110 - K Factor]
    R --> R5[IEC 61000-4-30 - Measurement Methods]
    R5 --> R51[IEC 61000-4-7 - Harmonic Measurement]
    R5 --> R52[IEC 61000-4-15 - Flicker Meter]
    R5 --> R53[IEC 61000-4-27 - Unbalance]
    R5 --> R54[IEC 62586 - Power Quality Instrument Classes]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:**
> **IEEE** = "**I**nstalled **E**ngineering **E**xpectations" (system-level limits).
> **IEC 61000-4-30** = "**I**nstrument **E**valuation **C**riteria" (how to measure).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3, PEEET751):**
> - Part A: 3-mark short answer (definitions, simple computations).
> - Part B: 14-mark descriptive with internal choice (sub-parts a, b typically 7 + 7).
> - Cognitive Levels: Apply (40%), Analyze (30%), Understand (20%), Remember (10%).

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Power Quality Analyzer. List any four parameters it measures.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Power Quality Analyzer (PQA)** is a microprocessor-based, multi-channel digital instrument that captures, processes, and records voltage and current waveforms in a power system, computing parameters in accordance with **IEC 61000-4-30**.
>
> Four parameters measured:
> 1. **True RMS voltage and current** ($V_{rms}$, $I_{rms}$).
> 2. **Total Harmonic Distortion** (THD %) and individual harmonics.
> 3. **Voltage sag, swell, and interruption** events.
> 4. **Flicker severity** ($P_{st}$, $P_{lt}$) per IEC 61000-4-15.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for parameters)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - July 2023]`
**Distinguish between THD and TDD. Why are both required?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | THD | TDD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Full Form | Total Harmonic Distortion | Total Demand Distortion |
| Denominator | Fundamental current ($I_1$) | Rated / maximum demand current ($I_{rated}$) |
| Indicates | Waveform shape | Actual harmonic stress on the system |
| Standard | Universal | IEEE 519 |

> **Reason both are required:** THD becomes meaningless at no-load (denominator $I_1 \to 0$, THD $\to \infty$ %). TDD provides a meaningful measure tied to the system's **load capacity**, making it the legally relevant metric in IEEE 519.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **Power Quality Analyzer**. Describe the role of **Anti-aliasing filter**, **A/D converter**, and **DSP engine** in the measurement chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz industrial bus is monitored by a PQA. The following harmonic spectrum is obtained for phase A voltage:

| Harmonic Order $h$ | Magnitude (V peak) |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 (Fundamental) | 338.0 |
| 3 | 25.0 |
| 5 | 18.0 |
| 7 | 9.0 |
| 11 | 5.0 |

Compute:
- (i) Total Harmonic Distortion ($THD_v$).
- (ii) True RMS voltage.
- (iii) Verify whether the installation complies with the **IEEE 519 limit of 8% THD** for $V \le 1$ kV systems. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Architecture Explanation **(7 marks)**

The PQA is organized in **five sequential stages**:

1. **Transduction Stage (1.5 marks):** PTs step down 415 V to ±10 V; CTs or Rogowski coils convert primary current to proportional mA. Rogowski coils offer wider bandwidth (1 Hz – 1 MHz) — preferred for transients.

2. **Signal Conditioning Stage (1.5 marks):** Anti-aliasing **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** removes frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent spectral aliasing. Isolation amplifiers provide galvanic isolation and reject common-mode noise. **Programmable Gain Amplifiers (PGA)** auto-scale the signal to the A/D's full-scale range.

3. **A/D Conversion Stage (1 mark):** 16-bit, simultaneous-sampling A/Ds convert all 4 channels at $\ge 9.6$ kSa/s for Class A IEC 61000-4-30 compliance.

4. **DSP Engine (1.5 marks):** Computes RMS over 10-cycle (200 ms) windows, executes FFT for harmonic decomposition, applies wavelet transform for transient detection, and runs flicker algorithms per IEC 61000-4-15.

5. **Storage, Display & Communication (1.5 marks):** 32 GB SSD stores aggregated 3-second, 10-minute, and 2-hour values. Color LCD shows waveforms, phasors, harmonic bars. Communication: Ethernet, Modbus TCP, IEC 61850, Wi-Fi.

> *(Block diagram — 1 mark should be awarded separately within the 7-mark allocation; reference the Mermaid architecture provided in Section 4.1.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Step (i) — Compute THD (3 marks):**

Sum of squares of harmonic peak voltages:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{h=2,3,5,7,11} V_h^2 &= (25.0)^2 + (18.0)^2 + (9.0)^2 + (5.0)^2 \\
&= 625 + 324 + 81 + 25 \\
&= 1055 \text{ V}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$THD_v = \frac{\sqrt{1055}}{338.0} \times 100\% = \frac{32.48}{338.0} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\,THD_v = 9.61\%\,}$$

**Step (ii) — Compute True RMS Voltage (2 marks):**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum V_h^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{(338.0)^2 + 1055}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(338.0)^2 &= 114244 \\
114244 + 1055 &= 115299 \\
\sqrt{115299} &= 339.56 \text{ V} \\
V_{rms} &= \frac{339.56}{\sqrt{2}} = 240.12 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{rms} = 240.12 \text{ V}\,}$$

**Step (iii) — Compliance Check (2 marks):**

IEEE 519 limit for $V \le 1$ kV at PCC = **8% THD**.

Measured $THD_v = 9.61\% > 8\%$.

$$\boxed{\,\text{The installation is NON-COMPLIANT. Harmonic mitigation (active/passive filter) is required.}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between a **Class A** and a **Class S** Power Quality Analyzer as per **IEC 62586-2**. Why are Class A instruments mandatory for **regulatory compliance** audits? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz line supplying an LED driver load is monitored. The PQA samples the voltage waveform at 5 kSa/s and the following readings are recorded at one instant:

- $V_{rms} = 230.0$ V
- Fundamental voltage $V_1 = 228.5$ V (rms)
- Harmonic RMS content: $V_h^{rms} = 24.3$ V (combined 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th)
- Crest factor $CF = 1.62$
- Active Power $P = 1.80$ kW
- Apparent Power $S = 2.16$ kVA

Compute:
- (i) Total Harmonic Distortion $THD_v$.
- (ii) Power Factor $PF$ and Displacement Power Factor $DPF$.
- (iii) Comment on the crest factor and its practical implication. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Class A vs Class S **(7 marks)**

| Aspect | Class A | Class S |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Purpose | **Regulatory, contractual, compliance** audits | **Statistical surveys**, troubleshooting |
| Accuracy | Strict (≤ 0.1% voltage, ≤ 0.5% current) | Lower (≤ 0.5% voltage) |
| Measurement method | **Pinned** to IEC 61000-4-30 algorithms — same result on any Class A instrument | Non-pinned; manufacturer-defined |
| Time aggregation | Fixed 10/12-cycle, 150/180-cycle, 10-min | Adjustable |
| Cost | High | Lower |
| Typical use | **Utility-grade**, **court-admissible** PQ data | Plant engineering surveys |

**Why Class A is mandatory for compliance (2 marks):**
- Results from different Class A instruments are **directly comparable** because the algorithms, time windows, and aggregation rules are standardized.
- A dispute between utility and consumer (e.g., "is the voltage THD within limits?") can be **legally resolved** only with Class A data.
- Class S results are for internal diagnostics, not for cross-organizational compliance.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Step (i) — THD (2 marks):**

$$THD_v = \frac{V_h^{rms}}{V_1^{rms}} \times 100\% = \frac{24.3}{228.5} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\,THD_v = 10.63\%\,}$$

**Step (ii) — PF and DPF (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,PF = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{1.80}{2.16} = 0.833 \text{ (lagging)}\,}$$

The relation between PF, DPF, and THD for a non-sinusoidal current (voltage nearly sinusoidal) is:

$$PF = DPF \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$$

Approximating $THD_i \approx THD_v \cdot (V_1/I_1) \cdot (I_1/V_1)$ (since load is non-linear, current is the dominant distorter — assume $THD_i \approx 60\%$ as typical for LED drivers):

$$PF = DPF \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.60)^2}} = DPF \cdot 0.857$$

$$DPF = \frac{PF}{0.857} = \frac{0.833}{0.857}$$

$$\boxed{\,DPF = 0.972 \text{ (i.e., displacement angle } \phi_1 \approx 13.7^\circ \text{ lagging)}\,}$$

**Step (iii) — Crest Factor (2 marks):**

- Pure sinewave: $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- Observed: $CF = 1.62$ → **above normal**.

**Implication:** The waveform has a sharper peak than a pure sine — typical of **capacitive input rectifier loads (LED drivers, SMPS)**. Such CF imposes extra stress on:
- **Insulation** (higher peak voltage stress).
- **Neutral conductors** in 3-phase systems (high triplen harmonics).
- **Capacitors** in UPS / PFC stages (higher RMS current).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — *Read This Before Writing Your Exam!***
>
> 1. **"THD violates limit"** vs **"Voltage is non-compliant"** — Use the correct phrasing. A system **is** non-compliant if its measured $THD > 8\%$, even if individual equipment is functioning.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{peak}$ and $V_{rms}$** in the THD formula is a 2-mark deduction. **Peak** in numerator and denominator (for the same waveform type), or **RMS** in both.
> 3. **Forgetting $\sqrt{2}$ conversion** when using peak harmonic values to compute RMS voltage — this gives 0.707 vs. 1.414 of the correct value.
> 4. **Stating "Class S = less accurate"** without mentioning the **pinned vs non-pinned** algorithm concept loses the 2-mark KTU key phrase.
> 5. **Drawing only a "black box"** for the architecture in 7-mark Q(a) — Examiner allocates **1 mark for the diagram** and **6 marks for the labelled explanation**. A labelled block diagram with PT, CT, LPF, A/D, DSP, Display is mandatory.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [x] **PQA** = integrated digital instrument compliant with **IEC 61000-4-30** (measurement) and **IEC 61000-4-7** (harmonics).
- [x] Five functional blocks: **Transduction → Conditioning → A/D → DSP → Storage/Display**.
- [x] **Class A** instruments: regulatory, pinned, $\le 0.1\%$ accuracy. **Class S**: statistical, non-pinned, troubleshooting only.
- [x] **PTs** for voltage (low bandwidth, accurate); **Rogowski coils** for current (high bandwidth, transient-capable).
- [x] **Anti-aliasing LPF** cutoff $\le f_s/2$ (Nyquist theorem).
- [x] **Minimum Class A sampling rate**: 9.6 kSa/s for 50 Hz; 10.24 kSa/s for 60 Hz.
- [x] **THD** uses $V_1$ (fundamental) as denominator; **TDD** uses $I_{rated}$ (demand) as denominator.
- [x] **IEEE 519 voltage THD limit** at PCC for $V \le 1$ kV = **8%**.
- [x] **IEC 61000-4-15** defines $P_{st}$ (10-min short-term) and $P_{lt}$ (2-hour long-term) flicker.
- [x] **RMS formula** (discrete): $V_{rms} = \sqrt{(1/N) \sum v_k^2}$.
- [x] **Crest Factor** for sinewave = $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Higher CF → peaky waveform (capacitive loads).
- [x] **Communication** protocols: Modbus TCP, IEC 61850, Ethernet, USB, Wi-Fi.
- [x] **Memory** in PQA stores 3-second, 10-minute, and 2-hour aggregated values for trend analysis.
- [x] PQA **must use simultaneous sampling** on all channels to preserve phase relationships.
- [x] **K-Factor** indicates transformer derating due to harmonic losses (IEEE C57.110).
- [x] For **transients**, separate high-speed (1–100 MSa/s) capture module is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Golden Rule:**
> *A Power Quality Analyzer is not just a "fancy multimeter" — it is a time-synchronized, standards-compliant, multi-channel data acquisition system that turns a power system into a fully observable, diagnosable, and reportable engineering asset.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer # MODULE 3: HARMONIC SPECTRUM ANALYZER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer (HSA):** A precision measurement instrument engineered to quantify, decompose, and graphically display the **harmonic frequency components** embedded within a distorted periodic power system waveform. It transforms a complex time-domain signal into a discrete **amplitude-versus-frequency spectrum** using the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** or, more commonly, the computationally efficient **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Power Quality (PEEET751)**, the Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer is classified as the **primary diagnostic tool** for power quality auditing. It directly supports **Course Outcome CO3** by enabling the measurement of **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**, individual harmonic magnitudes, and phase angles up to the **50th – 100th harmonic order** as mandated by **IEEE 519-2014 / IEC 61000-4-7** standards. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Cocktail Glass Analogy":** > Imagine you walk into a party and the music is a horrible jumbled mess — vocals, drums, guitars, and a screeching trumpet all playing at once. Your **ear** hears only "noise," but a **spectrum analyzer** is like a superhuman ear that can instantly pick out **each instrument** and tell you *exactly* how loud each one is playing. > > - The **distorted electrical waveform** = the jumbled party music. > - The **spectrum analyzer** = the superhuman ear. > - The **individual harmonics** (50 Hz fundamental, 150 Hz 3rd, 250 Hz 5th, etc.) = individual instruments. > - The **bar graph on the screen** = the volume level of each instrument. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Fundamental Frequency (India) | **50 Hz** | Base frequency for harmonic index calculation | | Standard Harmonic Range | **2nd to 50th** (IEEE 519) | Up to 2.5 kHz | | Nyquist Sampling Frequency | **≥ 2 × f_max** | Minimum 5 kHz for 50 harmonics | | Anti-aliasing Filter Cutoff | **2.5 kHz – 5 kHz** | Prevents spectral folding | | Display Resolution | **5 Hz bins** | Standard for power quality | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** A single 14-mark question in the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** frequently asks students to draw and explain the **block diagram of a digital harmonic spectrum analyzer**. Memorize the signal path: *Transducer → Anti-aliasing Filter → S/H → ADC → FFT Processor → Display.* --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Block-Level Breakdown A digital harmonic spectrum analyzer executes its measurement task in **six sequential stages**. Each stage is essential to obtain a faithful frequency-domain representation of the power signal. ### Stage 1: Signal Acquisition (Transducer / Sensor) - A **Current Transformer (CT)** or **Potential Transformer (PT)** scales down the high-voltage/current power signal to a low-level analog signal (typically ±5 V or ±10 V). - **Why?** Direct connection to a digital circuit would destroy it. Isolation and scaling are mandatory. ### Stage 2: Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter) - A **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency **f_c** set just above the highest harmonic of interest. - **Why?** Without it, frequency components above half the sampling rate fold back into the spectrum (aliasing), producing **false harmonics**. ### Stage 3: Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit - Captures the instantaneous amplitude of the signal at uniform time intervals Δt = 1/f_s. - **Why?** The ADC requires a stable input voltage during conversion; a continuously varying signal would produce erroneous codes. ### Stage 4: Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) - Quantizes the held analog sample into a **binary digital code** (e.g., 12-bit or 16-bit resolution). - **Why?** FFT operates on discrete numerical sequences, not continuous voltages. ### Stage 5: FFT Processor (The Mathematical Core) - Applies the **Fast Fourier Transform** algorithm to convert the time-domain sequence x(n) into frequency-domain bins X(k). - **Why?** A real-world distorted waveform is the sum of many sinusoids; FFT mathematically separates them. ### Stage 6: Display & Calculation Engine - Plots the **magnitude spectrum |X(k)|** and **phase spectrum ∠X(k)** versus frequency. - Calculates derived quantities: **THD, TDD, individual harmonic distortion, K-factor, crest factor.** > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The order of blocks from left to right in the diagram is *always* analog-first, digital-last. The most frequently tested diagram question requires you to label **"Anti-aliasing Filter"** before the **"Sample & Hold"** circuit — reversing their order is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn / N}$ | **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** | $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ | | 2 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | **Nyquist Frequency** | Hz | | 3 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max,h}$ | **Sampling Theorem** | $f_{max,h}$ = highest harmonic | | 4 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | **Frequency Resolution** | Hz per bin | | 5 | $h = \dfrac{f_h}{f_1}$ | **Harmonic Order** | Integer ≥ 2 | | 6 | $I_h = \dfrac{\vert X(k_h) \vert}{N/2}$ | **Harmonic Magnitude (RMS)** | A or V | | 7 | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | **Total Harmonic Distortion** | Percentage (%) | | 8 | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | **Total Demand Distortion** | Percentage (%) | | 9 | $K\text{-factor} = \dfrac{\sum_{h=1}^{H} h^2 \cdot I_h^2}{\sum_{h=1}^{H} I_h^2}$ | **Transformer K-Factor** | Dimensionless | | 10 | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | **Crest Factor** | Dimensionless | | 11 | $N_{FFT} = 2^m$ | **FFT Length (Power of 2)** | Typically 1024 or 2048 | | 12 | $t_{window} = \dfrac{N}{f_s}$ | **Capture Window Time** | Seconds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Escape Rule for Tables:** The vertical pipe character `|` inside table cells would break markdown table syntax. In this cheat sheet, all absolute values (e.g., $|X(k)|$) have been rewritten as `\vert X(k) \vert` to maintain table integrity. **Use the same convention in your own answer sheets to avoid formatting collapse during online KTU valuation portals.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compliance Auditing:** Verifying that industrial plants comply with **IEEE 519-2014** harmonic limits (typically 5% THD at PCC). - **Filter Sizing:** Identifying the dominant harmonic (e.g., 5th or 7th) to design correctly tuned passive/active harmonic filters. - **Transformer Derating:** Calculating the **K-factor** to specify K-rated transformers for non-linear loads (data centers, VFDs). - **Capacitor Bank Protection:** Detecting resonance conditions that amplify harmonic voltages and destroy power factor correction capacitors. - **Renewable Integration:** Analyzing harmonic pollution from solar inverters and wind turbine converters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) from Continuous Fourier Series We start with the **Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** representation of a periodic power signal $x(t)$ with fundamental period $T_0$: $$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_h \cos(2\pi h f_0 t) + b_h \sin(2\pi h f_0 t) \right]$$ where $f_0 = 1/T_0 = 50$ Hz is the fundamental frequency. **Step 1:** The Fourier Series coefficients are obtained by integration over one period: $$C_h = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} x(t) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi h f_0 t} \, dt$$ **Explanation:** Each coefficient $C_h$ represents the *complex amplitude* of the $h$-th harmonic, encoding both magnitude and phase information. The exponential form compactly combines the sine and cosine terms. **Step 2:** Convert the continuous integral into a discrete summation by sampling $x(t)$ at $N$ uniformly spaced points $t_n = n \Delta t$ where $\Delta t = T_0 / N$: $$t_n = \frac{n T_0}{N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$ **Explanation:** Since the analyzer can only store discrete digital samples, the continuous integral is approximated by Riemann summation. Each sample is separated by a time interval $\Delta t$. **Step 3:** Replace the differential $dt$ with the sampling interval $\Delta t$: $$C_h \approx \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n\Delta t) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi h f_0 (n T_0 / N)} \cdot \Delta t$$ **Explanation:** This is the standard Riemann-sum approximation: the integral becomes a sum of rectangular areas of width $\Delta t$. **Step 4:** Substitute $f_0 T_0 = 1$ and $\Delta t = T_0 / N$: $$C_h \approx \frac{1}{T_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi h n / N}$$ **Explanation:** The factor $f_0 (n T_0 / N) = h n / N$ simplifies the exponent. The leading $T_0$ cancels with the $1/T_0$ prefactor. **Step 5:** The $T_0$ terms cancel, leaving the standard DFT definition: $$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$ **Explanation:** Replacing the harmonic index $h$ with the standard DFT bin index $k$ (where $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$) gives the canonical DFT formula. The amplitude of the $k$-th bin is $\vert X(k) \vert$, and the phase is $\angle X(k) = \tan^{-1}\left( \dfrac{\text{Im}\{X(k)\}}{\text{Re}\{X(k)\}} \right)$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Examiners:** Notice that the DFT inherently assumes the signal is **periodic** with period $N$ samples. If the captured window is not an *integer multiple* of the fundamental period, **spectral leakage** occurs — a frequent KTU sub-question. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Frequency Resolution Equation **Step 1:** The sampling frequency is defined as: $$f_s = \frac{1}{\Delta t} = \frac{N}{T_0}$$ **Step 2:** The DFT produces $N$ frequency bins spanning from $0$ Hz up to $f_s$ Hz (with only the first $N/2$ bins being unique due to symmetry). **Step 3:** Therefore, the spacing between adjacent bins is: $$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{N / T_0}{N} = \frac{1}{T_0} = f_0 = 50 \text{ Hz}$$ **Explanation:** For a perfectly synchronized capture window of exactly one fundamental cycle, the frequency resolution equals the fundamental frequency itself. To achieve finer resolution (e.g., 5 Hz bins), the capture must span $N/10$ fundamental cycles. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — THD Calculation from a Spectrum **Problem Statement:** A harmonic spectrum analyzer produces the following readings on a 230 V, 50 Hz supply: | Harmonic Order $h$ | 1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 | 11 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Voltage Magnitude (V) | 230 | 18.4 | 11.5 | 6.9 | 4.6 | 2.3 | **Calculate the THD.** **Step 1:** Square each harmonic magnitude (excluding the fundamental): $$ \begin{aligned} V_3^2 &= (18.4)^2 = 338.56 \\ V_5^2 &= (11.5)^2 = 132.25 \\ V_7^2 &= (6.9)^2 = 47.61 \\ V_9^2 &= (4.6)^2 = 21.16 \\ V_{11}^2 &= (2.3)^2 = 5.29 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum the squared magnitudes: $$\sum_{h=2}^{11} V_h^2 = 338.56 + 132.25 + 47.61 + 21.16 + 5.29 = 544.87 \text{ V}^2$$ **Step 3:** Take the square root: $$\sqrt{\sum V_h^2} = \sqrt{544.87} = 23.343 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the THD formula: $$THD = \frac{23.343}{230} \times 100\% = 10.15\%$$ **Conclusion:** The measured THD of **10.15%** exceeds the IEEE 519 voltage limit of **5%** at the PCC, indicating that harmonic mitigation is required. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Digital Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PowerLine
Voltage Current] --> B[PT or CT
Transducer] B --> C[Signal
Conditioner] C --> D[Anti-Aliasing
Low Pass Filter] D --> E[Sample and
Hold Circuit] E --> F[Analog to
Digital Converter] F --> G[Memory
Buffer N samples] G --> H[FFT
Processor] H --> I[Harmonic
Magnitude Calculator] I --> J[Display
Spectrum Plot] H --> K[Phase Angle
Calculator] K --> J I --> L[THD and K-Factor
Calculator] L --> J M[Crystal
Clock Oscillator] --> E M --> F M --> G ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The **left-to-right signal flow** represents the analog front-end (yellow) and the digital back-end (blue). - The **crystal clock** synchronizes sampling to a precise timebase, ensuring $\Delta t$ is exact. - The **FFT processor** is the computational heart, branching outputs to magnitude, phase, and distortion calculators in parallel. - The **display** unifies all calculated quantities into a bar-graph spectrum plus numerical THD readout. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the FFT Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start Acquisition] --> win[Apply Window Function
Hanning or Hamming] win --> fft[Compute N point FFT] fft --> mag[Compute Magnitude
Absolute value of X k] fft --> phs[Compute Phase
Arctangent of Imag by Real] mag --> leak[Check Spectral Leakage
Energy Spread Check] phs --> out[Display Bar Graph] leak --> calcthd[Calculate THD Formula] calcthd --> out out --> export[Export Data
CSV or PDF Report] export --> stop[End] ``` **Functional Stage Explanation:** 1. **Window Function:** Multiplies the sampled data by a tapering function (Hanning, Hamming, Blackman-Harris) to reduce spectral leakage caused by non-integer cycle truncation. 2. **FFT Computation:** The 1024 or 2048-point radix-2 FFT is executed in $O(N \log_2 N)$ operations. 3. **Magnitude & Phase Extraction:** Each complex output bin $X(k)$ is decomposed into $\vert X(k) \vert$ and $\angle X(k)$. 4. **Leakage Check:** Energy is summed across adjacent bins to detect smeared harmonic peaks. 5. **THD Calculation:** The formula from the cheat sheet is applied using the magnitudes of bins corresponding to multiples of the fundamental. 6. **Export:** Data is downloaded to a USB drive or transmitted to a central SCADA system for archiving. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is a window function used?" in a 7-mark sub-part, answer in **three layers:** > 1. **Problem:** DFT assumes the signal repeats forever; truncation creates discontinuities. > 2. **Effect:** Discontinuities spread harmonic energy into adjacent bins (leakage). > 3. **Solution:** Multiplying by a smooth window (e.g., Hanning) tapers the ends to zero, suppressing leakage by 30–40 dB. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer. List its main functional blocks.** **Model Answer:** A Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer is an instrument that decomposes a distorted power system waveform into its constituent harmonic frequencies and displays the amplitude (and often phase) of each harmonic as a bar graph. Main functional blocks: 1. Transducer (CT/PT) 2. Anti-aliasing filter 3. Sample-and-Hold circuit 4. Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) 5. FFT processor 6. Display unit *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for blocks)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is spectral leakage? How is it minimized in a harmonic spectrum analyzer?** **Model Answer:** **Spectral leakage** is the phenomenon where the energy of a single harmonic frequency spreads into adjacent frequency bins in the FFT output, causing inaccurate amplitude readings. **Causes:** The captured signal window is not an exact integer multiple of the fundamental period, creating artificial discontinuities at the window boundaries. **Minimization:** Apply a **window function** such as Hanning, Hamming, or Blackman-Harris to the sampled data before computing the FFT. This tapers the signal to zero at both ends of the window, reducing leakage by 30–40 dB. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for cause, 1 for solution)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **(i) Draw the block diagram of a digital harmonic spectrum analyzer and explain the function of each block.** **Model Solution:** Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The functional explanation for each block: | Block | Function | Key Specification | |---|---|---| | PT/CT Transducer | Scales HV/LV signals to safe ADC levels; provides galvanic isolation | 110 V / 5 A output typical | | Anti-aliasing filter | Removes frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing | 4th-order Butterworth, $f_c = 2.5$ kHz | | Sample-and-Hold | Freezes the analog voltage for stable ADC conversion | Aperture time < 50 ns | | ADC | Quantizes the analog sample into a binary code | 12-bit or 16-bit, $f_s = 128 \cdot f_0$ | | FFT Processor | Computes $X(k)$ from $x(n)$ using radix-2 algorithm | N = 1024 or 2048 points | | Display | Plots $\vert X(k) \vert$ vs frequency; shows THD numerically | LCD / LED bar graph | **(4 marks for diagram, 3 marks for explanation)** **(ii) Why must the anti-aliasing filter be placed *before* the Sample-and-Hold circuit? What happens if its cutoff frequency is set too high or too low?** **Model Solution:** The anti-aliasing filter must precede the S&H circuit because the S&H samples the signal at discrete instants, and **any frequency above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist limit) that passes through the S&H will be irrevocably folded back** into the lower spectrum as a false low-frequency component. Once aliased, the distortion is mathematically irreversible. - **Cutoff too high:** High-frequency noise and harmonics above the 50th order will alias into the measured band, producing false harmonic readings. - **Cutoff too low:** The filter will attenuate legitimate high-order harmonics (e.g., 47th, 49th) that the IEEE 519 standard requires to be measured, leading to under-reported THD. **(3 marks: 1 for placement justification, 1 each for too-high/too-low effects)** **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A 415 V, 50 Hz three-phase industrial bus is measured by a harmonic spectrum analyzer. The harmonic current spectrum is given below. Calculate the THD and determine whether the plant complies with IEEE 519 limits (5% THD at PCC).** | Harmonic Order $h$ | 1 | 5 | 7 | 11 | 13 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Current (A) | 100 | 18 | 12 | 7 | 4 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Square each harmonic magnitude (excluding fundamental): $$ \begin{aligned} I_5^2 &= 18^2 = 324 \\ I_7^2 &= 12^2 = 144 \\ I_{11}^2 &= 7^2 = 49 \\ I_{13}^2 &= 4^2 = 16 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Sum the squares: $$\sum I_h^2 = 324 + 144 + 49 + 16 = 533 \text{ A}^2$$ **Step 3:** Take the square root: $$\sqrt{\sum I_h^2} = \sqrt{533} = 23.086 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the THD formula: $$THD = \frac{23.086}{100} \times 100\% = 23.09\%$$ **Step 5:** Compare with the IEEE 519 limit of **5% THD**: Since **23.09% ≫ 5%**, the plant **does NOT comply** with IEEE 519. **Mitigation is required**: install a 5th-harmonic passive filter or an active harmonic filter (AHF). **(7 marks: 2 for calculation steps, 1 for substitution, 1 for THD value, 2 for compliance conclusion, 1 for recommendation)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithms as applied to harmonic analysis. State and justify the minimum sampling frequency required to measure up to the 50th harmonic on a 50 Hz system.** **Model Solution:** **DFT Definition:** The Discrete Fourier Transform converts a finite discrete time-domain sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$ into a finite discrete frequency-domain sequence $X(k)$: $$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ **FFT Definition:** The FFT is an *efficient algorithm* to compute the DFT, exploiting the symmetry and periodicity of the twiddle factor $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$. It reduces computational complexity from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$. For $N = 1024$, DFT requires ~1 million multiplications; FFT requires only ~10,000 — a **100× speedup**. **Sampling Frequency Calculation:** The 50th harmonic has a frequency of: $$f_{50} = 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ Hz} = 2.5 \text{ kHz}$$ By the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**, the minimum sampling frequency is twice the highest frequency component of interest: $$f_{s,min} = 2 \times f_{50} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000 \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ kHz}$$ **Justification:** In practice, a safety factor of 2.5×–4× is applied to accommodate the anti-aliasing filter roll-off. Hence, commercial power quality analyzers typically sample at **128 × 50 Hz = 6.4 kHz** or **256 × 50 Hz = 12.8 kHz** to give a 2.56 kHz or 5.12 kHz Nyquist limit. **(7 marks: 2 for DFT, 2 for FFT, 1 for calculation, 2 for safety-factor justification)** **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A 6-pulse rectifier load draws a current waveform of:** $$i(t) = 100 \sin(314 t) + 30 \sin(942 t) + 15 \sin(1570 t) + 8 \sin(2198 t) \text{ A}$$ **Identify the harmonic orders present and calculate the THD.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify each frequency component and the corresponding harmonic order: - $314 = 2\pi \times 50$ → **1st harmonic (fundamental)** = 100 A - $942 = 2\pi \times 150$ → **3rd harmonic** = 30 A - $1570 = 2\pi \times 250$ → **5th harmonic** = 15 A - $2198 = 2\pi \times 350$ → **7th harmonic** = 8 A **Characteristic:** This is the **classic 6-pulse rectifier signature** — only odd non-triplen harmonics (6k±1) appear. The missing 3rd, 9th, 15th... (triplen harmonics) is consistent with a balanced 3-phase 6-pulse converter. **Step 2:** Square each harmonic magnitude: $$ \begin{aligned} I_3^2 &= 30^2 = 900 \\ I_5^2 &= 15^2 = 225 \\ I_7^2 &= 8^2 = 64 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Sum and take the square root: $$\sqrt{900 + 225 + 64} = \sqrt{1189} = 34.48 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4:** Compute THD: $$THD = \frac{34.48}{100} \times 100\% = 34.48\%$$ **Conclusion:** A THD of **34.48%** is severely distorted, typical of an unfiltered 6-pulse rectifier. A 12-pulse rectifier or active filter is required for compliance. **(7 marks: 2 for harmonic identification, 1 for missing triplen explanation, 2 for calculation, 2 for conclusion)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\times 100\%$ factor in THD** — A student writing "THD = 0.2348" instead of "23.48%" loses **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing RMS and peak values** — The DFT output bin magnitude equals the *peak* amplitude. To convert to RMS, divide by $\sqrt{2}$ for sinusoidal components. Mixing these is a **2-mark trap**. > 3. **Forgetting to exclude the fundamental in THD** — Summing $I_1^2$ in the numerator inflates the THD incorrectly. The summation must run from $h = 2$ to $H$. > 4. **Reversing the analog/digital block order** — Placing the ADC *before* the anti-aliasing filter is a **3-mark deduction** because it makes the system physically impossible. > 5. **Stating Nyquist frequency as $f_s$ (not $f_s/2$)** — Off by a factor of 2; an automatic 1-mark loss. > 6. **Ignoring the symmetry property of FFT** — Stating that all $N$ bins are independent is wrong; only $N/2 + 1$ bins are unique for real-valued inputs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📋 KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Harmonic Spectrum Analyzer** - **Definition:** An instrument that decomposes a distorted waveform into individual harmonics using FFT. - **Block sequence (memorize verbatim):** Transducer → Anti-aliasing Filter → S/H → ADC → FFT → Display. - **Core formula:** $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ - **Nyquist limit:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$. For 50th harmonic on 50 Hz → **$f_s \geq 5$ kHz**. - **Frequency resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N$. For one-cycle capture, $\Delta f = f_0 = 50$ Hz. - **THD formula:** $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{H} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ - **TDD vs THD:** TDD denominator is $I_{rated}$; THD denominator is $I_1$ (fundamental). - **Spectral leakage:** Caused by non-integer cycle capture → minimized using **Hanning/Hamming windows** (30–40 dB suppression). - **Aliasing:** Caused by frequencies above $f_s/2$ → prevented by **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** *before* S&H. - **6-pulse rectifier signature:** Harmonic orders = $6k \pm 1$ = 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, 17th, 19th, ... (no triplens). - **12-pulse rectifier signature:** Harmonic orders = $12k \pm 1$ = 11th, 13th, 23rd, 25th, ... (eliminates 5th and 7th). - **IEEE 519 voltage THD limit at PCC:** **5%**; individual harmonic limit: **3%**. - **K-factor:** Used to specify transformers for non-linear loads; $K = \sum h^2 I_h^2 / \sum I_h^2$. - **Crest Factor:** $CF = V_{peak} / V_{rms}$. Pure sine = 1.414. Distorted waveforms can exceed 2.0. - **Common ADC resolutions in PQA:** 12-bit (4096 levels) or 16-bit (65536 levels). - **Typical FFT length:** $N = 1024$ or $N = 2048$ (powers of 2 for radix-2 algorithm).

topics.title: Flicker Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flicker Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 3: FLICKER METERS — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (IEC 61000-4-15 / IEEE 1453-2015)**  
> A **Flicker Meter** is a precision instrument specifically designed to measure the *short-term* ($\boldsymbol{P_{st}}$) and *long-term* ($\boldsymbol{P_{lt}}$) flicker severity indices caused by rapid, repetitive fluctuations in the RMS supply voltage. It quantitatively models the human visual perception of luminance changes in incandescent lamps connected to the power network, producing a unitless perceptibility index (where **1.0 represents the threshold of irritability** for the average human observer).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEEET751 – Power Quality**, the Flicker Meter is classified as a **Power Quality Indexing Instrument** that converts a time-varying voltage waveform into a single perceptibility number that can be directly compared against grid-code compatibility limits (typically $\boldsymbol{P_{st} \leq 1.0}$ and $\boldsymbol{P_{lt} \leq 0.8}$ for 99% probability of non-irritation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flicker Intuit"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Candle in a Windy Room"**  
> Imagine an incandescent bulb (old-style tungsten filament) glowing steadily in a calm room. Now imagine a gentle but **rhythmic breeze** keeps puffing the filament's temperature up and down — perhaps 8 times a second. Your eyes will perceive a subtle but annoying "shimmer." Even though the *average* energy delivered to the bulb is the same as before, the *rate of change* of brightness is what your retina finds irritating. A Flicker Meter is essentially an **"electronic human eye"** — it mathematically pretends to be a real observer looking at a 60 W bulb and decides: *"Is this shimmer annoying enough to file a complaint? If yes, by how much?"*

### 1.3 Physical Origin of Flicker

Flicker is **not** caused by a steady-state voltage sag or swell. It is triggered specifically by:

- **Interharmonic voltages** generated by:
  - Arc furnaces (steel melting)
  - Cycloconverters and load-commutated inverters
  - Welding machines
  - Wind turbine power-electronic interfaces
- **Step voltage changes** in the 0.2 – 10 Hz modulation band
- **Sub-synchronous oscillations** injected into the grid

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why we cannot simply use a true-RMS meter:**  
> A standard true-RMS voltmeter will report a *time-averaged* value that hides the modulation. For example, a 230 V nominal signal fluctuating between 228 V and 232 V at 8.8 Hz has a perfectly acceptable RMS value, but a flicker meter will return $\boldsymbol{P_{st} > 1.0}$. This is because the human eye–brain system acts as a **band-pass filter for light intensity**, not a mean-square detector.

### 1.4 Standardization Bodies and Compliance

| Standard | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 61000-4-15** | Flickermeter – Functional and Design Specifications | **Primary design standard** for the instrument |
| **IEEE Std 1453-2015** | Recommended Practice for Measurement and Limits | North American counterpart |
| **IEC/TR 61000-3-7** | Assessment of emission limits for fluctuating loads | Defines $\boldsymbol{P_{st}, P_{lt}}$ planning levels |
| **EN 50160** | Voltage characteristics of electricity supplied by public distribution systems | Sets $\boldsymbol{P_{lt} \leq 1.0}$ for 95% of the week |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the UIE/IEC Flicker Meter

The standard IEC Flicker Meter (Block F in IEC 61000-4-15) is composed of **two main sections**, each containing multiple sub-blocks. Each block faithfully replicates a specific physiological or physical phenomenon.

### **Section 1 — Simulation of the Lamp-Eye-Brain Response Chain**

| Block | Name | Function | Physical Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Input Voltage Adapter | Scales the input voltage to an internal reference level. For $\boldsymbol{230\,\text{V}}$ nominal systems, the reference is $\boldsymbol{V_{ref} = 230\,\text{V}}$ (or its 1-minute averaged value) | The voltage delivered to the test lamp socket |
| **2** | Squaring Multiplier | Squares the scaled voltage, then subtracts the DC component (via a 1st-order high-pass filter) to obtain a signal proportional to the **relative luminance fluctuation** $\boldsymbol{\Delta V/V}$ | The non-linear response of a tungsten filament (intensity $\propto V^2$) |
| **3** | Band-Pass Filter (Weighting Filter 1) | Implements the **eye–brain sensitivity** to modulation frequency. Passband: $\boldsymbol{0.05\,\text{Hz} \leq f \leq 33.3\,\text{Hz}}$, with a peak gain at $\boldsymbol{\approx 8.8\,\text{Hz}}$ | The photoreceptor's frequency-dependent response |
| **4** | Weighting Filter 2 + Range Adapter | A 1st-order low-pass filter ($\boldsymbol{6\,\text{dB/octave}}$) and a sliding-mean squaring unit, producing the **instantaneous flicker sensation** $\boldsymbol{S(t)}$ | Brain's perception memory (low-pass time response) |

### **Section 2 — Statistical Analysis of the Flicker Sensation**

| Block | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **5** | Statistical Classifier | Sorts $S(t)$ into 64+ logarithmic classes and produces the **Cumulative Probability Function (CPF)** |
| **6** | Flicker Severity Calculator | Computes the 5 specific percentiles from the CPF, which feed the $\boldsymbol{P_{st}}$ formula |

## 2.2 The Standard Short-Term Flicker Severity Formula

The short-term flicker severity index, evaluated over a 10-minute observation window, is given by the **quadratic mean (root-sum-square) of 5 percentile values** of the instantaneous flicker sensation $\boldsymbol{S(t)}$:

$$
P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314\,P_{0.1} + 0.0525\,P_{1} + 0.0657\,P_{3} + 0.28\,P_{10} + 0.08\,P_{50}}
$$

Where the constants are **pre-computed weights** such that a rectangular voltage fluctuation of **0.32% (or 0.732 V on 230 V)** at a modulation frequency of **8.8 Hz (1 change per minute)** yields $\boldsymbol{P_{st} = 1.00}$. This calibration is the *primary design anchor* of the entire instrument.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Significance of the 8.8 Hz Peak:**  
> The human eye is most sensitive to luminance flicker at approximately **8.8 Hz**. At this frequency, even a **0.3% voltage fluctuation** is perceptible and annoying. The eye's sensitivity rolls off dramatically at frequencies below 1 Hz (long, slow dimming is not perceived as flicker) and above 25 Hz (the filament's thermal inertia "averages out" the fluctuation).

## 2.3 Long-Term Flicker Severity

For assessment over longer periods (typically 2 hours), a cubic-mean combination is used:

$$
P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{st,i}^{\,3}}
$$

Typically, $\boldsymbol{N = 12}$ consecutive $\boldsymbol{P_{st}}$ readings (12 × 10 min = 2 hours).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Units | Boundary / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\boldsymbol{V_{ref}}$ | Reference voltage for input adapter | $\text{V}$ | 230 V (EU) / 120 V (US) |
| $\boldsymbol{f_{m}}$ | Modulation frequency (flicker) | $\text{Hz}$ | Sensitivity peak at $\boldsymbol{8.8\,\text{Hz}}$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\Delta V / V}$ | Relative RMS voltage fluctuation | p.u. or % | $\boldsymbol{0.32\%}$ at 8.8 Hz $\Rightarrow P_{st}=1$ |
| $\boldsymbol{P_{0.1}, P_{1}, P_{3}, P_{10}, P_{50}}$ | 0.1%, 1%, 3%, 10%, 50% percentiles of $\boldsymbol{S(t)}$ | unitless | Read from CPF |
| $\boldsymbol{P_{st}}$ | Short-term severity (10 min) | unitless | Planning limit $\boldsymbol{\leq 1.0}$ |
| $\boldsymbol{P_{lt}}$ | Long-term severity (2 hr) | unitless | Planning limit $\boldsymbol{\leq 0.8}$ (99% of week) |
| $\boldsymbol{S(t)}$ | Instantaneous flicker sensation | unitless | Continuous, output of Block 4 |
| $\boldsymbol{N}$ | Number of $P_{st}$ samples averaged | integer | Typically 12 |
| $\boldsymbol{d(t)}$ | Relative voltage change in block 2 | p.u. | Squared then DC-stripped |
| $\boldsymbol{G(s)}$ | Lamps-eye-brain transfer function | – | Bode plot with 8.8 Hz peak |
| $F(s),\, A(s),\, B(s)$ | Composite weighting filter constants | – | Defined by IEC tables |
| $\boldsymbol{K}$ | Scaling constant (Block 6) | – | Calibrated to $\boldsymbol{P_{st}=1}$ reference |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compliance verification** for arc furnace installations (EAFs are the *single largest flicker source* globally).
- **Grid-code enforcement** for renewable integration — wind farms with back-to-back converters can be significant flicker emitters under specific wind conditions.
- **Customer dispute resolution** when residential consumers report light annoyance.
- **Emission limit allocation** in harmonic/flicker planning studies (IEC/TR 61000-3-7).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptual Output from a Modulated Sinusoid

We consider a sinusoidal voltage that is *amplitude-modulated* by a small sinusoidal envelope. This is the canonical test waveform used in IEC 61000-4-15 to characterize the flicker meter's response.

### Step 1 — Set up the time-domain voltage

Let the supply voltage be:

$$
v(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}\left[1 + m\,\cos(2\pi f_{m}\,t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_{s}\,t)
$$

Where:
- $\boldsymbol{V_{rms}}$ is the nominal RMS supply voltage (e.g., 230 V)
- $\boldsymbol{m}$ is the modulation depth (a small number, e.g., 0.0032 for 0.32%)
- $\boldsymbol{f_{m}}$ is the modulation (flicker) frequency
- $\boldsymbol{f_{s}}$ is the fundamental supply frequency (50 or 60 Hz)

### Step 2 — Apply the squaring multiplier (Block 2)

Inside Block 2, the input is first squared:

$$
v^{2}(t) = 2V_{rms}^{2}\left[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_{m}\,t)\right]^{2}\cos^{2}(2\pi f_{s}\,t)
$$

Using the identity $\cos^{2}(x) = \frac{1+\cos(2x)}{2}$ and expanding $\left[1 + m\cos\theta\right]^{2} = 1 + 2m\cos\theta + m^{2}\cos^{2}\theta$:

$$
v^{2}(t) = V_{rms}^{2}\left[1 + 2m\cos(2\pi f_{m}\,t) + \frac{m^{2}}{2} + \frac{m^{2}}{2}\cos(4\pi f_{m}\,t)\right]\left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_{s}\,t)\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Expand and identify terms

Expanding the product, we obtain terms of the form $\cos(4\pi f_{s}\,t)$ (a 100/120 Hz carrier) and baseband terms. The subsequent **DC-blocking high-pass filter** (Block 2 second stage) removes the DC component, leaving only the fluctuating terms. After filtering, the dominant low-frequency component is:

$$
d(t) \approx 2m\,V_{rms}^{2}\cos(2\pi f_{m}\,t)
$$

Since the input adapter normalizes by the DC term ($V_{rms}^{2}$), the **relative luminance fluctuation** at the output of Block 2 is:

$$
\frac{d(t)}{V_{rms}^{2}} \approx 2m\cos(2\pi f_{m}\,t)
$$

This result confirms that the output of the squaring block is **proportional to the modulation depth** $m$, scaled by a factor of 2.

### Step 4 — Apply the band-pass weighting filter (Block 3)

The 8.8 Hz band-pass filter has a transfer function gain $G(f_m)$ whose magnitude is the *normalized eye-sensitivity curve*. The output is:

$$
y(t) = 2m \cdot G(f_{m})\cos\!\left(2\pi f_{m}\,t - \phi(f_{m})\right)
$$

where $\phi(f_m)$ is the small phase shift introduced by the filter.

### Step 5 — Low-pass smoothing and squaring (Block 4)

The 1st-order low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 0.53 Hz) is followed by a sliding-mean square. For sinusoidal input, the result is a constant amplitude:

$$
S(t) \approx \left[2m\,G(f_{m})\right]^{2} = 4\,m^{2}\,G^{2}(f_{m})
$$

(after the sliding-mean averages $\cos^{2}\theta \to 1/2$ and the scale factor of 2 is absorbed into the meter gain).

### Step 6 — The 8.8 Hz, 0.32% Calibration Anchor

By international standard, the meter gain is fixed so that for the calibration case:

$$
m = 0.0032 \quad (0.32\%),\qquad f_{m} = 8.8\,\text{Hz}
$$

the instantaneous flicker sensation must yield $S(t) = 1.0$ at the 50th percentile. This is the **single anchor point** that defines all other sensitivity weights throughout the instrument.

### Step 7 — The Percentile Combination

Over 10 minutes, the instrument collects thousands of $S(t)$ samples and sorts them into percentile bins. The standard $P_{st}$ formula is constructed so that for *rectangular* voltage fluctuations (the worst-case shape), the combination:

$$
P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314\,P_{0.1} + 0.0525\,P_{1} + 0.0657\,P_{3} + 0.28\,P_{10} + 0.08\,P_{50}}
$$

reproduces the perceptibility curve with a maximum error below 5% across the entire 0.5 – 25 Hz range.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Note — Why a cubic root for $P_{lt}$?**  
> Because flicker annoyance accumulates non-linearly. A single 10-minute period with $P_{st}=3$ is roughly three times as annoying as ten periods with $P_{st}=1$. The cubic-mean (norm-3 average) was empirically found by UIE to best correlate with customer complaint statistics for long-term exposure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the IEC Flicker Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Voltage Adapter (Block 1): V ref 230 V, 1-min averaging"] --> B["Squaring Multiplier (Block 2a): v t squared"]
    B --> C["DC Blocker (Block 2b): 1st-order HP filter f c 0.05 Hz"]
    C --> D["Band-pass Weighting Filter (Block 3): peak gain at 8.8 Hz, range 0.05 to 33.3 Hz"]
    D --> E["Low-pass Filter and Range Adapter (Block 4a): 1st-order LP f c 0.53 Hz"]
    E --> F["Squaring and Smoothing (Block 4b): produces S t"]
    F --> G["Statistical Classifier (Block 5): 64-bin CPF, percentiles 0.1 to 50"]
    G --> H["Flicker Severity Calculator (Block 6): P st formula"]
    H --> I["Long-term Aggregator: P lt cube-root mean over N samples"]
    I --> J["Compliance Output: Pass or Fail vs grid code limits"]
```

## 4.2 Eye-Sensitivity Frequency Response (Bode Plot Approximation in Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["0.05 Hz: Low sensitivity (slow drift)"] --> B["1 Hz: Rising response"]
    B --> C["5 Hz: Strong response"]
    C --> D["8.8 Hz: PEAK sensitivity G max"]
    D --> E["12 Hz: Declining response"]
    E --> F["20 Hz: Low but non-zero response"]
    F --> G["33.3 Hz: Filter cutoff, sensitivity 0"]
```

## 4.3 Signal Path State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Block1_Adapter
    Block1_Adapter --> Block2_Squarer: Voltage scaled to V ref
    Block2_Squarer --> Block2b_DCBlock: v squared
    Block2b_DCBlock --> Block3_Weighting: d t, modulation extracted
    Block3_Weighting --> Block4a_LowPass: Eye-sensitivity filtered
    Block4a_LowPass --> Block4b_SquareSmooth: Brain time-response
    Block4b_SquareSmooth --> Block5_Classifier: S t, instantaneous flicker
    Block5_Classifier --> Block6_Calculator: CPF percentiles
    Block6_Calculator --> Output_Pst: 10-min severity index
    Output_Pst --> Aggregator_Plt: 2-hour cube-root mean
    Aggregator_Plt --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Modulation-to-Perception Mapping (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Modulation frequency f m"] --> N{"Is f m in 0.5 to 25 Hz band?"}
    N -- No --> X["Low S t, low flicker annoyance"]
    N -- Yes --> O["Compute d t, weight by G f m"]
    O --> P["S t 4 m squared times G f m squared"]
    P --> Q["Bin into CPF"]
    Q --> R["Apply P st formula"]
    R --> S["Compare with limit 1.0"]
    S --> T{"P st greater than 1.0?"}
    T -- Yes --> U["Non-compliant, action required"]
    T -- No --> V["Compliant"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## **Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define flicker and state the standard that specifies the functional design of a flicker meter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Flicker is the **visual perception of unsteady brightness** of a light source caused by rapid, repetitive fluctuations in the supply voltage, typically in the 0.5 – 25 Hz modulation band. The instrument designed to quantify this perception is called a **Flicker Meter**, and its functional design and test procedures are standardized by **IEC 61000-4-15** (with North American equivalent being **IEEE Std 1453-2015**). The instrument models the lamp–eye–brain response chain in five functional blocks and outputs the unitless severity indices $P_{st}$ (10-minute) and $P_{lt}$ (2-hour). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the standard, 1 for the indices.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Why is the human eye's peak flicker sensitivity at 8.8 Hz, and what is the standard calibration point of a flicker meter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The human visual system, comprising the retina and the visual cortex, acts as a **band-pass filter for luminance**. At low frequencies (<1 Hz), the brain interprets slow dimming as a steady change; at high frequencies (>25 Hz), the filament's thermal inertia "smooths out" the fluctuation. The peak sensitivity occurs at **8.8 Hz** because at this rate the thermal inertia of a 60 W tungsten filament is too slow to follow the voltage modulation, while the brain still perceives the change as a discrete event. The flicker meter is **calibrated** so that a **rectangular voltage fluctuation of 0.32% at 8.8 Hz, occurring once per minute**, produces $P_{st} = 1.0$. *(3 marks: 1 for band-pass explanation, 1 for 8.8 Hz peak, 1 for 0.32% calibration anchor.)*

---

## **Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks] With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the operation of the IEC 61000-4-15 flicker meter. Explain the role of each block in simulating the lamp–eye–brain chain.**

**Model Solution:**

The IEC 61000-4-15 flicker meter is conceptually divided into **two sections**, with **five functional blocks** in total:

**Section 1 – Simulation of Lamp–Eye–Brain Response:**

1. **Block 1 – Input Voltage Adapter (1 mark):** Scales the incoming voltage to an internal reference of 230 V (or 120 V). It uses a 1-minute RMS averaging to track slow supply variations. This block ensures the meter behaves correctly regardless of slow steady-state voltage drift.

2. **Block 2 – Squaring Multiplier and DC Blocker (2 marks):** The scaled voltage is squared, simulating the **non-linear power-to-light conversion** of an incandescent filament (where light intensity $\propto V^2$). A 1st-order high-pass filter (cutoff 0.05 Hz) then removes the DC component, leaving a signal proportional to the **relative voltage fluctuation** $\Delta V/V$. This block effectively "demodulates" the envelope from the supply carrier.

3. **Block 3 – Band-pass Weighting Filter (2 marks):** This is the **eye-sensitivity filter**. Its transfer function is designed so that the gain peaks at **8.8 Hz** and rolls off symmetrically on either side. The passband is 0.05 Hz to 33.3 Hz. This block converts the dimensionless modulation into a perceptually-weighted signal.

4. **Block 4 – Low-pass Filter and Range Adapter (2 marks):** A 1st-order low-pass filter (6 dB/octave, cutoff 0.53 Hz) simulates the **brain's memory and perception time-constant**, after which a squaring-and-smoothing stage produces the **instantaneous flicker sensation** $S(t)$.

**Section 2 – Statistical Analysis:**

5. **Block 5 + 6 – Statistical Classifier and $P_{st}$ Calculator (1 mark for both):** $S(t)$ is digitized, sorted into 64+ logarithmic bins to form a Cumulative Probability Function (CPF), and the percentiles $P_{0.1}, P_{1}, P_{3}, P_{10}, P_{50}$ are read. The standard formula then produces $P_{st}$. *(Total: 8 conceptual points distributed across 7 marks.)*

#### **(b) [7 marks] A 230 V, 50 Hz supply experiences rectangular voltage fluctuations of amplitude $\pm 0.4\%$ at 8.8 Hz. The flicker meter records the following percentile values from $S(t)$: $P_{0.1} = 1.42$, $P_{1} = 1.30$, $P_{3} = 1.21$, $P_{10} = 1.05$, $P_{50} = 0.85$. Compute $P_{st}$ and comment on grid-code compliance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the standard formula:

$$
P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314\,P_{0.1} + 0.0525\,P_{1} + 0.0657\,P_{3} + 0.28\,P_{10} + 0.08\,P_{50}}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute each percentile and compute each term:

- $0.0314 \times 1.42 = 0.04459$
- $0.0525 \times 1.30 = 0.06825$
- $0.0657 \times 1.21 = 0.07950$
- $0.28 \times 1.05 = 0.29400$
- $0.08 \times 0.85 = 0.06800$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sum the five contributions:

$$
\text{Sum} = 0.04459 + 0.06825 + 0.07950 + 0.29400 + 0.06800 = 0.55434
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take the square root:

$$
P_{st} = \sqrt{0.55434} = 0.7446 \approx 0.74
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Comment on compliance:**  
- IEC 61000-3-7 and EN 50160 specify that the planning level for $P_{st}$ is **≤ 1.0** (with 95% probability).  
- Since $P_{st} = 0.74 < 1.0$, the installation is **compliant** with short-term flicker limits.  
- However, the fluctuation amplitude of 0.4% exceeds the **0.32% reference at 8.8 Hz**, so it produces elevated perceptual severity. The customer is in the "irritability" range; an even small increase in load could push $P_{st}$ above 1.0. The operator should monitor the long-term index $P_{lt}$.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Derive the output of the squaring multiplier block of an IEC flicker meter when the input is an amplitude-modulated sinusoid $v(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)]\cos(2\pi f_{s}t)$. Comment on why the squaring block is essential to flicker measurement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the input voltage and note that $m \ll 1$:

$$
v(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}\left[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_{s}t),\qquad m \ll 1
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Square the signal:

$$
v^{2}(t) = 2V_{rms}^{2}\left[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)\right]^{2}\cos^{2}(2\pi f_{s}t)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply $\cos^{2}\theta = (1+\cos 2\theta)/2$ and expand the bracket (ignoring $m^{2}$ term since $m \ll 1$):

$$
v^{2}(t) \approx V_{rms}^{2}\left[1 + 2m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)\right]\left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_{s}t)\right]
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Expand and identify baseband vs. carrier terms. The DC-blocker (1st-order HP, cutoff 0.05 Hz) removes the DC term, leaving the dominant low-frequency component:

$$
d(t) \approx 2m\,V_{rms}^{2}\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Normalize by $V_{rms}^{2}$ (performed implicitly by the input adapter) to obtain the **relative luminance fluctuation**:

$$
\frac{d(t)}{V_{rms}^{2}} \approx 2m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The squaring block is essential because flicker is a property of the **modulation envelope**, not the carrier. True-RMS meters average over many cycles and cannot recover the envelope. The squaring operation, combined with the DC blocker, effectively performs an *envelope detection*, extracting the flicker component $m\cos(2\pi f_{m}t)$ that is later weighted by the eye-sensitivity filter. Without this block, the meter could not distinguish a steady 230 V supply from a fluctuating 228 V–232 V supply.

#### **(b) [7 marks] Explain the difference between $P_{st}$ and $P_{lt}$. An industrial site has twelve consecutive 10-minute $P_{st}$ values as follows: 0.95, 1.10, 1.20, 0.85, 0.90, 1.05, 1.30, 1.15, 0.95, 1.00, 0.80, 0.90. Calculate $P_{lt}$ and interpret the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Distinguish $P_{st}$ and $P_{lt}$:**  
- $P_{st}$ is the **short-term flicker severity** evaluated over **10 minutes**. It quantifies the immediate annoyance potential of flicker.  
- $P_{lt}$ is the **long-term flicker severity** evaluated over typically **2 hours** (12 consecutive $P_{st}$ values). It captures cumulative annoyance and uses a **cubic-mean (norm-3)** combination because annoyance grows non-linearly with severity.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the cubic-mean formula:

$$
P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}P_{st,i}^{\,3}},\qquad N = 12
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $P_{st,i}^{3}$ for each value and sum:

| $P_{st,i}$ | $P_{st,i}^{3}$ |
|---|---|
| 0.95 | 0.857375 |
| 1.10 | 1.331000 |
| 1.20 | 1.728000 |
| 0.85 | 0.614125 |
| 0.90 | 0.729000 |
| 1.05 | 1.157625 |
| 1.30 | 2.197000 |
| 1.15 | 1.520875 |
| 0.95 | 0.857375 |
| 1.00 | 1.000000 |
| 0.80 | 0.512000 |
| 0.90 | 0.729000 |

**Sum** = 13.233375

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Divide by $N = 12$ and take the cube root:

$$
P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{13.233375}{12}} = \sqrt[3]{1.10278} = 1.0330
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Interpretation:**  
The long-term flicker severity is $P_{lt} \approx 1.03$. The planning limit from EN 50160 and IEC 61000-3-7 is $P_{lt} \leq 0.8$ for 95% of the week. Hence, this site **exceeds the planning level** and is likely causing flicker complaints. Mitigation measures — such as installing a **Static VAR Compensator (SVC)** or **Active Flicker Mitigator** — are recommended. The cubic-mean emphasizes the impact of the worst 10-minute windows (here, $P_{st}=1.30$), which is why $P_{lt} > 1$ even though some windows have $P_{st} < 1$.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**  
> 1. **Confusing percentiles with probabilities:** $P_{0.1}$ is the 0.1%-exceeded level, *not* a 0.1% probability of compliance. Wrong sign = full marks lost.  
> 2. **Forgetting the cube root in $P_{lt}$:** Students often compute an arithmetic or RMS mean. The UIE/IEC standard explicitly mandates a **cubic root (norm-3)** for long-term aggregation.  
> 3. **Using peak voltage $V_{peak}$ instead of $V_{rms}$** in the calibration anchor. The 0.32% reference is on the **RMS** value, and 230 V (or 120 V) is the *nominal RMS*, not the peak.  
> 4. **Ignoring the input adapter:** Forgetting the $V_{rms}^{2}$ normalization factor when computing $d(t)$ leads to dimensional errors in the output.  
> 5. **Wrong units for the percentile inputs:** $P_{0.1}$ etc. are *unitless*; substituting them into the $P_{st}$ formula yields a unitless $P_{st}$. Do not write "volts" or "Hz" in the answer.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Flicker** = perception of unsteady light intensity due to voltage modulation, primarily 0.5 – 25 Hz.
- 📏 **Standards:** IEC 61000-4-15 (design), IEEE 1453 / IEC 61000-3-7 (limits), EN 50160 (compliance).
- 🧠 **Eye-sensitivity peak:** 8.8 Hz; 0.32% rectangular voltage fluctuation at 8.8 Hz ⇒ $P_{st} = 1.0$ (calibration anchor).
- 🧱 **Five functional blocks:** Input adapter → Squarer → DC blocker → Band-pass weighting → Low-pass + range → Statistical classifier.
- 📊 **Statistical block:** Sorts $S(t)$ into 64+ bins to extract percentiles $P_{0.1}, P_{1}, P_{3}, P_{10}, P_{50}$.
- 🔢 **$P_{st}$ formula:** Weighted square root of 5 percentiles, with weights $0.0314, 0.0525, 0.0657, 0.28, 0.08$.
- ⏱️ **$P_{lt}$ formula:** Cubic-root mean (norm-3) of N = 12 consecutive $P_{st}$ values over 2 hours.
- 🏭 **Major flicker sources:** Arc furnaces, cycloconverters, welding sets, wind turbine converters.
- ✅ **Compliance limits:** $P_{st} \leq 1.0$ (95% of week), $P_{lt} \leq 0.8$ (99% of week) per EN 50160.
- 🎯 **Killer formula to memorize (KTU high-priority):**
$$
P_{st} = \sqrt{0.0314\,P_{0.1} + 0.0525\,P_{1} + 0.0657\,P_{3} + 0.28\,P_{10} + 0.08\,P_{50}}
$$
- 🔁 **$P_{lt}$ formula:**
$$
P_{lt} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}P_{st,i}^{3}}
$$
- 🛠️ **Mitigation devices:** SVC, STATCOM, Active Flicker Compensator (AFC), detuned filters, load re-scheduling.
- 🚫 **Do not** use a true-RMS voltmeter to assess flicker — RMS averaging hides the modulation envelope.
- 📚 **Frequency-memorize:** DC-blocker cutoff = 0.05 Hz; Band-pass peak = 8.8 Hz; Band-pass upper = 33.3 Hz; Low-pass cutoff = 0.53 Hz.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mitigation of Power Quality Problems - Harmonic Elimination - Design Simple Problems and Analysis of Passive Filters to Reduce Harmonic Distortion - Demerits of Passive Filters - Description of Active Filters - Shunt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mitigation of Power Quality Problems - Harmonic Elimination - Design Simple Problems and Analysis of Passive Filters to Reduce Harmonic Distortion - Demerits of Passive Filters - Description of Active Filters - Shunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Harmonic Mitigation?

**Harmonic Mitigation** refers to the set of techniques and engineered devices used to reduce or eliminate unwanted harmonic currents and voltages in a power system, ensuring that the supply remains within the distortion limits prescribed by standards such as **IEEE 519-2014** and **IEC 61000-3-6**. In KTU parlance, mitigation is the *corrective* arm of power quality, sitting alongside prevention (which uses K-rated transformers, line reactors, etc.).

**Harmonic Elimination** is a stricter subset of mitigation that targets the complete cancellation (theoretically, $\infty$ attenuation) of one or more specific harmonic orders, typically the dominant low-order ones (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** Examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish* between harmonic elimination (complete removal) and harmonic reduction (attenuation). Always quote the harmonic order you are eliminating in your answer.

### 1.2 The Two Engineering Solutions — Passive vs. Active

Think of a noisy highway next to a residential colony:

- **Passive Filters** = *Soundproofing walls* built into the colony side. They are fixed, brick-and-mortar structures that absorb specific noise frequencies. Once built, they only block the design frequency — if the noise changes, the wall is useless.
- **Active Filters** = *Active Noise Cancellation (ANC) headphones*. They sense the incoming noise, generate an *anti-noise* waveform, and inject it so the two cancel out in real time. They adapt to whatever frequency appears.

| Aspect | Passive Filter | Active Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Components | L, C, R (passive) | Power electronics (VSI/CSI) + DSP |
| Power Rating | Tuned for fixed load | Adapts to load changes |
| Response Time | Instantaneous | Few switching cycles (µs–ms) |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High |
| Size | Bulky (especially at 50 Hz) | Compact |

### 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Passive Power Filter (PPF):** A combination of inductance, capacitance, and resistance, arranged in shunt or series, whose impedance is very low at one or more harmonic frequencies, thereby providing a low-impedance bypass path for harmonic currents and preventing them from flowing into the supply system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Active Power Filter (APF):** A power-electronic converter-based device (typically a Voltage Source Inverter, VSI) that injects compensating currents/voltages equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (or 180° phase-shifted) to the harmonic distortion present in the load, achieving cancellation at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC).

### 1.4 Classification of Active Filters

Active filters are classified by the *type of converter* and by the *connection topology*:

1. **By Converter Type:**
   - **VSI-based APF** (Voltage Source Inverter) — most common, uses DC link capacitor.
   - **CSI-based APF** (Current Source Inverter) — uses DC link inductor, more robust but bulky.

2. **By Topology (KTU focus):**
   - **Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF)** — injects compensating current *in parallel* with the load. Used for current-type harmonics (non-linear current-drawing loads like rectifiers).
   - **Series Active Power Filter** — injects compensating voltage *in series* with the supply. Used for voltage-type harmonics (distorted supply, e.g., from upstream).
   - **Hybrid APF** — combination of passive + active to reduce rating of the active part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
