> For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the syllabus explicitly demands a **description of Shunt Active Filters**. Memorize the block diagram: *non-linear load → PCC → VSI → DC bus → Control algorithm (p-q theory / instantaneous reactive power theory)*.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Fundamental frequency:** $f_1 = 50\,\text{Hz}$ (India, KTU region)
- **Tuning order for industrial drives:** typically 5th harmonic ($f_5 = 250\,\text{Hz}$)
- **IEEE 519 THD limit at PCC (≤ 69 kV):** $I_{THD} \le 5\%$, $V_{THD} \le 5\%$
- **DC bus voltage of typical SAPF:** $V_{dc} \ge \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{LL}$ (line-to-line RMS)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Harmonic Mitigation Techniques

```
Harmonic Mitigation
├── Passive Methods
│   ├── Passive Filters (LC, RLC tuned)
│   ├── Line Reactors
│   ├── K-rated Transformers
│   └── Zig-zag Grounding Transformers
└── Active Methods
    ├── Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF)
    ├── Series Active Power Filter
    ├── Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC)
    └── Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR)
```

### 2.2 Passive Filters — Types and Operation

Passive filters are the **most widely deployed** harmonic mitigation solution in industry (estimated >80% of installed base). They offer simplicity, robustness, and zero control complexity.

#### 2.2.1 Single-Tuned (Notch) Passive Filter

This is the **most common KTU-exam topic**. It is a series RLC circuit tuned to resonate at a specific harmonic frequency $f_n = n \cdot f_1$, where $n$ is the harmonic order to be eliminated.

- **At resonance:** $X_L = X_C \Rightarrow \omega_n L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n C}$
- The filter presents a **very low impedance** (ideally $R$ only) at $f_n$, shunting harmonic current to ground.
- For $f \ne f_n$, the filter impedance rises sharply, leaving the fundamental unaffected.

#### 2.2.2 Dampers (Damped / High-Pass Filters)

Used when multiple high-order harmonics must be attenuated, or as a broadband safety net to absorb residual distortion.

- **First-order** (R + L in parallel with C) — simple, lossy.
- **Second-order** (R in series with L, parallel with C) — better damping, most common.
- **Third-order** (R + C in series with L) — reduced losses at fundamental.

#### 2.2.3 Band-Pass / C-Type Filters

Used at high-voltage levels to reduce fundamental-frequency losses; $C_2$ blocks the fundamental, $L$ + $C_1$ tune to the harmonic.

### 2.3 Why Passive Filters "Trap" Harmonics — The Underlying Physics

The harmonic current from a non-linear load (e.g., a 6-pulse rectifier) splits at the PCC between the **supply** (high impedance) and the **filter** (low impedance at $f_n$). Since current takes the path of least impedance (Kirchhoff's Current Law + Ohm's Law analogy), almost all the $n$-th harmonic current flows into the filter branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The filter does **not** absorb the harmonic energy into a useful form — it dissipates it as heat in $R$ (or stores it in $L$ and $C$, but ultimately $R$). Thus a passive filter must be rated for the **full harmonic current**, not just the fundamental.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE** — questions on filter design and analysis are almost always variations of these formulas.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant (tuning) frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz; $n = f_n/f_1$ |
| 2 | Quality factor (sharpness) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{X_0}{R}$ | Dimensionless; high $Q$ = sharp notch |
| 3 | Characteristic impedance | $X_0 = \sqrt{L/C} = \omega_n L$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{f_n}{Q}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Reactance at harmonic $h$ | $X_L(h) = h \cdot X_{L1}$, $X_C(h) = X_{C1}/h$ | $\Omega$; subscripts denote fundamental |
| 6 | Filter impedance at $f_n$ | $Z_f(f_n) = R$ | Purely resistive (minimum) |
| 7 | Reactive power supplied by $C$ at fundamental | $Q_C = V_{ph}^2 \,\omega_1 C$ | VAR; capacitor rating |
| 8 | Reactive power of inductor at $f_n$ | $Q_{L,n} = I_{h}^2 \cdot \omega_n L$ | VAR |
| 9 | DC bus voltage (SAPF) | $V_{dc} \ge 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}$ | V; for hysteresis control |
| 10 | THD definition | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1}\times 100\%$ | % |

> **Pro-Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to "design" a passive filter, examiners expect: (1) choice of $n$ (typically 5), (2) assumed $Q$, (3) calculation of $C$ from reactive-power requirement, (4) calculation of $L$ from $f_n$, (5) calculation of $R$ from $Q$, (6) verification of impedance at $f_n$.

### 2.5 Demerits of Passive Filters (High-Weightage in KTU)

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **board-favourite 7-mark question**. List the demerits with one-line engineering justification — never just "it is bad."

1. **Fixed tuning** — once installed for 5th harmonic, cannot adapt if the dominant harmonic shifts to 7th or 11th.
2. **Resonance risk with source impedance** — series resonance between filter capacitor and source inductance can **amplify** voltages at the $f_n$, sometimes causing catastrophic insulation failure. This is the *worst* demerit.
3. **Overloading on load change** — if harmonic content increases (load expansion), the filter is forced to absorb more current, leading to overheating.
4. **Fundamental-frequency reactive power injection** — the filter capacitor supplies leading VARs, which can push the system into leading power factor (over-voltage, self-excitation of generators).
5. **Bulky and heavy** — at 50 Hz, the inductor and capacitor values are large (especially for low-frequency 5th harmonic).
6. **No harmonic cancellation** — provides only a low-impedance path; does not actively cancel.
7. **Cannot mitigate voltage harmonics, only current harmonics** — requires separate series filter for voltage distortion.

### 2.6 Active Filters — Why They Exist (the Motivation)

Active filters were developed in the 1980s–90s specifically to overcome the demerits of passive filters. The fundamental principle:

$$
i_{filter}(t) = -i_{harm}(t)
$$

That is, the APF injects a current that is **exactly equal and opposite** to the harmonic current drawn by the load. By superposition at the PCC:

$$
i_{source}(t) = i_{load}(t) + i_{filter}(t) = i_{1}(t) + i_{harm}(t) - i_{harm}(t) = i_1(t)
$$

The source sees only the fundamental — a clean sinusoid.

### 2.7 Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF) — Detailed Description

**Shunt Active Filter** = "Current Compensator in Parallel with the Load."

**Components of a typical SAPF:**

1. **Non-linear Load** (rectifier, ASD, arc furnace) — source of harmonics.
2. **VSI (Voltage Source Inverter)** with IGBT/MOSFET switches — synthesises the compensating current.
3. **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ — maintains a stiff DC voltage (energy storage).
4. **Coupling/interface inductor** $L_f$ — smooths the injected current, filters switching ripple.
5. **Control algorithm** — senses load current, extracts harmonic component, generates reference $i_{filter}^*$.
6. **PWM / hysteresis modulation** — converts reference into gate pulses for the IGBTs.
7. **PCC (Point of Common Coupling)** — the node where filter, load, and source meet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Leading Control Strategies (KTU Frequently Tested):**
> 1. **p-q Theory (Instantaneous Reactive Power Theory)** by Akagi (1983) — operates in $\alpha\beta$ frame; computes $p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta$ and $q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$; then $p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p}$ where $\tilde{p}$ is the oscillating (harmonic) part. Filter compensates $\tilde{p}$ and $q$.
> 2. **Synchronous Reference Frame (SRF / d-q Theory)** — transforms $i_{abc}$ to $i_{dq0}$ using Park's transform; low-pass filter extracts DC component (fundamental); harmonic = $i_{dq} - i_{dq,DC}$.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Renewable integration:** SAPFs at the PCC of solar/wind farms to comply with IEEE 519.
- **Data centres** with dense IT loads (SMPS-heavy, harmonic-rich environments).
- **Electric vehicle charging stations** (Level 2/3 chargers are major harmonic sources).
- **Industrial drives** (6-pulse and 12-pulse rectifier-fed VFDs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Tuning Condition of a Series RLC Filter

A series RLC branch has the impedance:

$$
Z_f(\omega) = R + j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)
$$

For the imaginary part to vanish (purely resistive, minimum impedance):

$$
\omega_n L - \dfrac{1}{\omega_n C} = 0
$$

$$
\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
$$

At this frequency:

$$
Z_f(\omega_n) = R
$$

Since $R$ is small (typically 1–5% of $X_0$), the filter offers a near-short-circuit at $f_n$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality Factor in Terms of Components

Quality factor $Q$ is defined as the ratio of reactive energy stored to energy dissipated per cycle at resonance:

$$
Q = \dfrac{\omega_n \cdot (\text{Peak Energy Stored})}{(\text{Energy Dissipated per Cycle})}
$$

For a series RLC, the peak energy stored in $L$ (or equivalently $C$) is:

$$
W_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_{peak}^2
$$

Energy dissipated per cycle in $R$:

$$
W_{diss} = \pi R I_{peak}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q = \omega_n L / R = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{X_0}{R}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Source-Filter Interaction (Why Resonance is Dangerous)

Consider the Thevenin equivalent of the source: $V_s$ with impedance $Z_s = R_s + j\omega L_s$, and a shunt filter at the PCC.

Total impedance seen by harmonic current:

$$
Z_{total}(h) = \dfrac{Z_s(h) \cdot Z_f(h)}{Z_s(h) + Z_f(h)}
$$

**Case 1 — Anti-resonance (parallel resonance, BAD):** This occurs at frequency $f_a$ where $X_{L_s}(f_a) = X_{C_f}(f_a)$ but the two are *not* in series with $R$ — instead, source $L$ and filter $C$ form a parallel tank. At $f_a$:

$$
Z_{total}(f_a) \to \infty
$$

This **amplifies** any harmonic near $f_a$ instead of filtering it. Mitigation: ensure $f_n < f_a$ by safety margin, e.g., tune the filter slightly below 5th harmonic (e.g., 4.7th or 4.8th).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-resonance frequency** is given by:
> $$f_a \approx f_n \sqrt{\dfrac{X_{C,n}}{X_s'}}$$
> where $X_{C,n}$ is the capacitor reactance at $f_n$ and $X_s'$ is the source reactance scaled to $f_n$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Style (14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz industrial feeder supplies a 6-pulse rectifier load that draws 100 A fundamental current with 25% 5th harmonic and 12% 7th harmonic. Design a single-tuned passive filter to eliminate the 5th harmonic. Assume quality factor $Q = 30$ and that the capacitor supplies 30 kVAR of reactive power at fundamental frequency. Then calculate the filter impedance at the 5th harmonic.

#### Step 1: Identify the target harmonic

The dominant harmonic is 5th. Tuning frequency:

$$
f_5 = 5 \times 50 = 250\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\omega_5 = 2\pi f_5 = 2\pi \times 250 = 1570.8\,\text{rad/s}
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the capacitor value from reactive power

For a Y-connected (phase) filter capacitor, per-phase reactive power is $Q_C/3$:

$$
Q_C/3 = V_{ph}^2 \cdot \omega_1 C
$$

Where $V_{ph} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{3} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94\,\text{V}$ and $\omega_1 = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$.

$$
C = \dfrac{Q_C/3}{V_{ph}^2 \cdot \omega_1} = \dfrac{30\,000/3}{(230.94)^2 \times 314.16}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{10\,000}{53\,333.3 \times 314.16} = \dfrac{10\,000}{16\,755\,160} = 5.968 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 597\,\mu\text{F (per phase)}}
$$

#### Step 3: Calculate the inductor value

Using $f_n = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$:

$$
L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_5^2 \cdot C} = \dfrac{1}{(1570.8)^2 \times 5.968 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{1}{2\,467\,412 \times 5.968 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{1472.9} = 6.79 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 0.679\,\text{mH (per phase)}}
$$

#### Step 4: Calculate the resistance from Q

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{30}\sqrt{\dfrac{6.79 \times 10^{-4}}{5.968 \times 10^{-4}}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{30} \times \sqrt{1.138} = \dfrac{1.067}{30} = 0.0356\,\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 0.0356\,\Omega \text{ (per phase)}}
$$

#### Step 5: Verify filter impedance at 5th harmonic

At resonance, $Z_f(f_5) = R$:

$$
\boxed{Z_f(f_5) = 0.0356\,\Omega \text{ (purely resistive)}}
$$

This is a near-short-circuit at 250 Hz — the harmonic current will preferentially flow into this branch instead of the supply.

#### Step 6: Verify filter impedance at fundamental (50 Hz) — should be high

$$
X_{L1} = 2\pi \times 50 \times 6.79 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2134\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_{C1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 5.968 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.333\,\Omega
$$

$$
Z_f(f_1) = R + j(X_{L1} - X_{C1}) = 0.0356 + j(0.2134 - 5.333) = 0.0356 - j5.12\,\Omega
$$

$$
\vert Z_f(f_1) \vert \approx 5.12\,\Omega \quad (\text{purely capacitive — supplies VARs, as required})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms a properly designed filter: **low impedance at $f_5$**, **high (capacitive) impedance at $f_1$**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Passive vs. Active Filter Trade-off

| Performance Criterion | Passive Filter | Shunt Active Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Adaptation to load changes | ❌ Poor (fixed tuning) | ✅ Excellent (real-time DSP) |
| Resonance risk | ⚠️ High (anti-resonance) | ✅ None (does not use L-C tank) |
| Compensation of reactive power | ⚠️ Yes, but fixed | ✅ Dynamic and controllable |
| Size and weight | ❌ Bulky (50 Hz) | ✅ Compact (high-freq switching) |
| Cost (small loads) | ✅ Low | ❌ High |
| Cost (large, variable loads) | ❌ Multiple stages needed | ✅ Single unit suffices |
| Mitigation of voltage sags/swells | ❌ No | ⚠️ Possible (with series-shunt) |
| Response to sudden load change | ✅ Instantaneous | ⚠️ Few cycles (PWM latency) |
| IEEE 519 compliance under varying load | ❌ Difficult | ✅ Easily maintained |

### 3.6 p-q Theory — Mathematical Skeleton (for SAPF Control)

The instantaneous power theory (Akagi, 1983) operates in the $\alpha\beta$ reference frame using Clarke's transform:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}
$$

Similarly for currents. Then:

$$
p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta \quad \text{(real/instantaneous power)}
$$

$$
q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta \quad \text{(imaginary power)}
$$

Decompose:

$$
p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p}, \quad q = \bar{q} + \tilde{q}
$$

The filter compensates the **oscillating** parts $\tilde{p}$ and $\tilde{q}$ (and all of $q$ if reactive power compensation is also desired). The reference currents are:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} i_{\alpha}^* \\ i_{\beta}^* \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & v_\beta \\ v_\beta & -v_\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{p} \\ \tilde{q}^* \end{bmatrix}
$$

Inverse Clarke transform then yields $i_a^*, i_b^*, i_c^*$ — the gate-pulse references for the VSI.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Tuned Passive Filter — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[3-Phase 50 Hz Supply 400 V] --> node2[PCC Point of Common Coupling]
    node2 --> node3[Non-Linear Load 6-Pulse Rectifier 100 A]
    node2 --> node4[Single-Tuned Passive Filter Branch]
    node4 --> node5[Inductor L 0.679 mH]
    node5 --> node6[Resistor R 0.0356 ohm]
    node6 --> node7[Capacitor C 597 microfarad]
    node7 --> node8[Neutral / Ground Reference]
    node4 -. "Tuned to 5th harmonic 250 Hz" .- node5
    node3 -. "Injects 5th and 7th harmonic currents" .- node2
    node4 -. "Provides low-impedance path Zf = R" .- node2
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **left side** is the utility source.
- The **center node (PCC)** is the harmonic current splitter.
- The **right branch** is the non-linear load (the harmonic source).
- The **bottom branch** is the LC filter — note that at 250 Hz it offers a near-short-circuit, attracting the harmonic current away from the supply.

### 4.2 Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[3-Phase Mains Vsa Vsb Vsc] --> node2[PCC Bus Bar]
    node2 --> node3[Non-Linear Load Harmonic Source]
    node2 --> node4[Shunt Active Power Filter SAPF]
    
    subgraph ControlSection [Control and Sensing Block]
        node5[Load Current Sensor iLa iLb iLc] --> node6[Clarke Transform alpha beta]
        node6 --> node7[Instantaneous Power pq Calculation]
        node7 --> node8[Low Pass Filter to Extract DC Components]
        node8 --> node9[Reference Current Generator iStar a b c]
        node9 --> node10[PWM Hysteresis Modulator]
    end
    
    node4 --> node5
    
    subgraph PowerSection [Power Converter Block]
        node11[DC Link Capacitor Cdc] --> node12[Voltage Source Inverter VSI IGBT]
        node12 --> node13[Coupling Inductor Lf]
    end
    
    node10 --> node12
    node13 --> node2
    
    node14[DC Voltage Regulator PI Controller] --> node9
    
    style ControlSection fill:#fff4e1
    style PowerSection fill:#e1f0ff
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Top half = Sensing & Control** (DSP / microcontroller domain).
- **Bottom half = Power Conversion** (VSI, IGBT switches, DC capacitor).
- The **dashed feedback line** carries the reference currents to the modulator.
- The DC voltage regulator compensates for inverter losses by adjusting the active power reference.

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Harmonic Mitigation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1{Is the load fixed\nand known?} -->|Yes| node2{Is the harmonic\norder stable?}
    node1 -->|No| node6[Choose Active Filter SAPF]
    node2 -->|Yes 5th dominant| node3{Site has space\nand budget?}
    node2 -->|No multiple orders| node6
    node3 -->|Yes| node4[Use Single-Tuned\nPassive Filter]
    node3 -->|No| node5[Use Hybrid Filter\nPassive + Active]
    node4 --> node7[Verify anti-resonance\nmargin with source X]
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node8[Apply p-q or d-q control\nEnsure Vdc regulation]
    node7 --> node9[Compliance Check\nIEEE 519 THD Limits]
    node8 --> node9
```

### 4.4 Harmonic Current Path (Before and After SAPF Compensation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BeforeFilter [Before SAPF Compensation]
        A1[Source Current] --> A2[Distorted Sinusoid\nContains 5th 7th 11th 13th]
    end
    
    subgraph AfterFilter [After SAPF Compensation]
        B1[Source Current] --> B2[Clean Sinusoid\nFundamental Only]
    end
    
    subgraph LoadSide [Load Side Constant]
        C1[Non-Linear Load] --> C2[Same Distorted Current]
    end
    
    C2 -.-> A2
    C2 -.-> B2
    A2 -. "Ifilter = - Iharm" .-> B2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Style]**
Define a **passive harmonic filter**. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-tuned shunt passive filter.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A passive harmonic filter is a combination of inductance ($L$), capacitance ($C$), and resistance ($R$) connected in shunt (or series) with the load, tuned to offer a **low-impedance path** to a specific harmonic frequency.

A single-tuned shunt filter is connected across the load at the PCC. At its **resonant frequency** $f_n = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$, the filter impedance is minimum (ideally equal to $R$), causing the harmonic current of order $n$ to flow preferentially into the filter branch rather than into the supply. The filter thus "traps" the harmonic, isolating the supply from the polluting load. For other frequencies, the filter presents high impedance and has negligible effect. *(Diagram: 1 mark; working: 2 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**
List any **three demerits** of passive filters.

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark each):**
1. **Fixed tuning** — cannot adapt to changes in dominant harmonic order as the load varies.
2. **Series/parallel resonance with source impedance** can amplify harmonic voltages, leading to insulation failure.
3. **Overloading** — increased harmonic content causes filter overheating and eventual failure.

*(Other valid demerits: bulkiness, leading-power-factor injection, no voltage harmonic compensation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Design & Analysis of Passive Filter

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted from Past Patterns]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **single-tuned passive filter** for eliminating the 5th harmonic in a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz industrial system. Derive the expressions for the tuning frequency, characteristic impedance, and quality factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pulse rectifier load draws a fundamental current of 200 A with 28% 5th harmonic. Design a single-tuned passive filter (per phase, Y-connected) to eliminate the 5th harmonic, assuming the filter capacitor supplies 50 kVAR per phase at fundamental and $Q = 25$. Hence calculate the filter impedance at 5th harmonic and verify the design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working & Derivations (7 marks):**
- Diagram: Supply → PCC → [Non-linear load] and [Filter branch] → (3 marks)
- At resonance $X_L = X_C$:
  $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}, \quad X_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}, \quad Q = \dfrac{X_0}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$
- Working: filter offers $Z_f(f_n) = R$ (very small) → harmonic current flows into filter branch instead of supply. (4 marks)

**(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):**
Step 1: $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94\,\text{V}$, $f_5 = 250\,\text{Hz}$, $\omega_1 = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$.

Step 2: Capacitor:
$$C = \dfrac{Q_C}{V_{ph}^2 \cdot \omega_1} = \dfrac{50\,000}{(230.94)^2 \times 314.16} = \dfrac{50\,000}{16\,755\,160} = 2.984 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{F} \approx 2984\,\mu\text{F}$$

Step 3: Inductor:
$$L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_5^2 C} = \dfrac{1}{(1570.8)^2 \times 2.984 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{7365.6} = 1.358 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{H} \approx 0.136\,\text{mH}$$

Step 4: Resistance:
$$R = \dfrac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{25}\sqrt{\dfrac{1.358 \times 10^{-4}}{2.984 \times 10^{-3}}} = \dfrac{1}{25}\sqrt{0.0455} = \dfrac{0.2133}{25} = 8.53 \times 10^{-3}\,\Omega$$

Step 5: Verification:
- At 250 Hz: $Z_f = R = 8.53\,\text{m}\Omega$ (very small — harmonic flows into filter) ✓
- At 50 Hz: $X_{L1} = 0.0427\,\Omega$, $X_{C1} = 10.66\,\Omega$, $\vert Z_f \vert \approx 10.66\,\Omega$ (high, capacitive — supplies 50 kVAR) ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error** — converting $kVAR$ to $VAR$ (multiply by 1000). Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 3** for Y-connected filter — $Q_C$ given is total, must convert to per-phase. Loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $V_{LL}$ instead of $V_{ph}$** in the $C$ formula. Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the "verify at fundamental" step** — KTU evaluators check whether the student confirmed the design does not short-circuit the fundamental. Loss of 1 mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Active Filters & Shunt Topology

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Adapted from Past Patterns]**

**(a)** Explain the **limitations of passive filters**. How do active filters overcome these limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **shunt active power filter (SAPF)**. Explain the **p-q theory** (instantaneous reactive power theory) used for reference current generation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Limitations & Motivation (7 marks):**

*Limitations of Passive Filters (4 marks):*
1. Fixed tuning — cannot adapt to changing harmonic spectrum.
2. Anti-resonance with source impedance can amplify harmonic voltages.
3. Overloading under increased harmonic content.
4. Bulky at 50 Hz, especially for low-order harmonics.
5. Provides no compensation for voltage harmonics or reactive power fluctuations.
6. Leading power factor injection can over-voltage the bus.

*How Active Filters Overcome (3 marks):*
1. **Adaptive compensation** — DSP senses load in real time; reference current changes with the load.
2. **No anti-resonance** — does not use passive L-C tank, so no risk of resonance with source.
3. **Dynamic VAR control** — can supply/absorb reactive power as needed.
4. **Compact** — high-frequency switching allows small magnetic components.
5. **Selective compensation** — can target specific harmonics or total harmonic distortion.

---

**(b) Shunt Active Filter Block Diagram & p-q Theory (7 marks):**

*Block Diagram (3 marks):*
The SAPF consists of: (i) 3-phase VSI with IGBT switches, (ii) DC-link capacitor, (iii) coupling inductor $L_f$, (iv) current/voltage sensors, (v) control block (DSP/FPGA). The filter is connected in **shunt** (parallel) at the PCC. The non-linear load draws harmonic-rich current. The control block computes the harmonic component using p-q theory and generates a reference current $i_{filter}^*$ that is **equal in magnitude and 180° phase-shifted** to the harmonic component. The VSI synthesizes this reference via PWM/hysteresis modulation, and the coupling inductor injects it into the PCC. By superposition, the source sees only the fundamental.

*p-q Theory (4 marks):*
1. **Clarke transform** of $v_{abc}$ and $i_{abc}$ to $v_{\alpha\beta}$ and $i_{\alpha\beta}$.
2. Compute instantaneous real power $p = v_\alpha i_\alpha + v_\beta i_\beta$ and imaginary power $q = v_\beta i_\alpha - v_\alpha i_\beta$.
3. Decompose: $p = \bar{p} + \tilde{p}$, $q = \bar{q} + \tilde{q}$.
4. **Low-pass filter** extracts $\bar{p}$ (average = fundamental component).
5. Reference compensating power: $p^* = \tilde{p}$ (or $-\bar{q} + \tilde{p}$ if reactive compensation also needed).
6. Compute reference currents in $\alpha\beta$ frame: $[i_\alpha^*, i_\beta^*]^T = \frac{1}{v_\alpha^2 + v_\beta^2} \begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha & v_\beta \\ v_\beta & -v_\alpha \end{bmatrix} [p^*, q^*]^T$.
7. Apply **inverse Clarke transform** to get $i_{a}^*, i_{b}^*, i_{c}^*$ — the gate-pulse references.
8. Hysteresis or SPWM modulator drives the VSI IGBTs to track these references.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Confusing shunt with series topology** — SAPF compensates *current* harmonics (load-generated), series APF compensates *voltage* harmonics (supply-side). Wrong topology = 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Missing the role of DC-link capacitor** — must be charged to $V_{dc} \ge 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}$ for proper PWM operation. Loss of 1 mark if omitted.
> 3. **Not stating the fundamental equation $i_{filter} = -i_{harm}$** in the working description. Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the inverse Clarke transform** in p-q theory explanation. Loss of 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — One-Page Revision Punchlist**

- **Harmonic mitigation** = *corrective* approach; harmonic *elimination* = complete cancellation of one order.
- **Passive filter** is a series RLC (or R-L||C) tuned to $f_n = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$; offers $Z = R$ at $f_n$.
- **Q-factor** controls sharpness: high $Q$ → narrow notch, low $Q$ → broadband damping.
- **Anti-resonance** is the *biggest demerit* of passive filters — occurs between source $L$ and filter $C$.
- **Passive filters provide VARs** at fundamental because the filter is capacitive at $f_1$.
- **Active filter** is a VSI + DSP that injects $i_{filter} = -i_{harm}$.
- **SAPF** is connected in *parallel* with the load — used for *current* harmonics.
- **Series APF** is connected in *series* with the supply — used for *voltage* harmonics.
- **p-q theory (Akagi)** uses Clarke transform → instantaneous $p$, $q$ → extract oscillating parts → reference currents → inverse Clarke → PWM.
- **SRF/d-q theory** uses Park transform → low-pass filter extracts DC → harmonic = AC part.
- **DC bus voltage** of SAPF: $V_{dc} \ge 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}$ (hysteresis) or $\ge \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL}$ (SPWM).
- **Hybrid APF** = passive + active; reduces active filter rating by 50–70%.
- **IEEE 519 limits** (low-voltage ≤ 1 kV): $I_{THD} \le 8\%$, individual harmonic $\le 5\%$.
- **Typical filter design order:** choose $n$ → fix $Q$ → calc $C$ from $Q_C$ → calc $L$ from $f_n$ → calc $R$ from $Q$ → verify at fundamental & harmonic.
- **Filter rating** = full harmonic current, not just fundamental (frequent student error).
- **Coupling inductor** $L_f$ in SAPF limits switching ripple and protects IGBTs.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Always end your filter-design answer with a verification — examiners reward students who close the loop by checking $Z_f$ at both $f_1$ and $f_n$."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET751 - POWER QUALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Power Quality (PQ)** engineering, the term **"Series"** refers to a class of **Series Compensation Devices** (also called **Series Custom Power Devices** or **Series Active Power Filters**) that are connected in **series** with the supply and the load. These devices **inject a compensating voltage ($V_{inj}$)** through an injection transformer in such a way that the load-side voltage remains a pure, balanced, undistorted sinusoid, regardless of any distortion, sag, swell, or unbalance present on the source side.

The most prominent series device studied at the KTU level is the **Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR)**, which is a solid-state power electronic converter connected in **series** with the load terminals through a **series injection transformer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEET751 – Module 4):**
> *Series active power filters are voltage-source-inverter-based compensating devices connected in series between the source and the load. They protect the sensitive load from supply-side disturbances such as voltage sags, swells, harmonics, flicker, and unbalance by dynamically injecting a compensating voltage through a series injection transformer.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drinking water from a tap, but the water pressure keeps fluctuating — sometimes too weak, sometimes too strong, and occasionally mixed with muddy particles.

You place a **water-pressure regulator with a built-in purifier** in the pipe between the tap and your glass.

- If the tap water is too weak, the regulator **adds pressure**.
- If the tap water is too strong, the regulator **absorbs excess pressure**.
- If the water is muddy, the regulator **filters it out**.

The glass always receives **clean, steady, perfect water**, no matter what the tap does.

**In electrical terms:**
- **Tap** = Polluted / disturbed supply (sag, swell, harmonics)
- **Regulator** = Series Compensator / DVR
- **Glass** = Sensitive critical load (computers, PLCs, medical equipment)
- **Regulator action** = Injection of compensating voltage $V_{inj}$ to maintain $V_{load}$ = ideal

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Shunt vs. Series):**
> - **Shunt devices** compensate **current-related** problems (current harmonics, reactive current, load unbalance) by injecting current **in parallel**.
> - **Series devices** compensate **voltage-related** problems (voltage harmonics, sags, swells) by injecting voltage **in series**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nominal System Voltage:** Standard LV ratings — **230 V (single-phase)**, **415 V (three-phase)**, **11 kV / 22 kV (distribution)**.
- **Operating Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India / KTU standard).
- **Power Quality Index (THD) Limit (IEEE 519):** Voltage THD $\leq$ **5%** at PCC.
- **Voltage Sag Magnitude:** Typically **0.1 pu to 0.9 pu** of nominal.
- **Voltage Swell Magnitude:** Typically **1.1 pu to 1.8 pu** of nominal.
- **Sag Duration:** **0.5 cycles to 1 minute** (per IEEE 1159).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Series Injection Concept

Let us consider a simple single-phase representation. The source, the series compensator, and the load are connected in a **series loop**.

The fundamental equation governing series compensation is the **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** of the compensated feeder:

$$V_{source}(t) \;+\; V_{inj}(t) \;=\; V_{load}(t)$$

To keep the load voltage **purely sinusoidal and at rated magnitude**, the compensator must dynamically compute and inject the **difference voltage**:

$$V_{inj}(t) \;=\; V_{load,desired}(t) \;-\; V_{source,actual}(t)$$

The compensator must:
1. **Sense** the source voltage in real time.
2. **Extract** the fundamental positive-sequence component (using PLL — Phase-Locked Loop).
3. **Compare** the actual source voltage with the reference.
4. **Compute** the required injection voltage.
5. **Generate** it using a **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with PWM.
6. **Inject** it through a **series injection transformer**.

### 2.2 Operating Modes of a Series Compensator

The series device operates in one of the following modes depending on the source condition:

- **Normal Mode (Bypass Mode):** When the source is healthy, the series device is bypassed (or injects a near-zero voltage), and the load is fed directly.
- **Voltage Sag Compensation:** When $V_{source} < V_{load,desired}$, the device injects $V_{inj}$ in phase with the source to **boost** the voltage.
- **Voltage Swell Compensation:** When $V_{source} > V_{load,desired}$, the device injects $V_{inj}$ in **anti-phase** to the source to **buck** the voltage.
- **Harmonic Compensation:** The device injects harmonic voltages equal in magnitude but **opposite in phase** to the harmonic distortion present in the source.
- **Unbalance Compensation:** Positive, negative, and zero sequence components are separated; only the positive sequence fundamental is passed to the load.

### 2.3 Block Diagram of Series Compensation System

The standard functional architecture of a series compensator / DVR consists of:

1. **Source-side voltage sensing** (PT — Potential Transformer).
2. **Load-side voltage sensing** for closed-loop feedback.
3. **Load current sensing** (CT — Current Transformer) for reference generation.
4. **DSP / Controller** running the compensation algorithm (e.g., $d$-$q$ frame / SRF / instantaneous $p$-$q$ theory).
5. **PWM Signal Generator** generating gate pulses for the VSI.
6. **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with IGBT / IGCT switches and DC link capacitor.
7. **LC / LCL Filter** to remove high-frequency switching ripple.
8. **Series Injection Transformer** providing isolation and voltage matching.
9. **DC Energy Storage** (capacitor bank, BESS, or SMES).
10. **Bypass Switch / Thyristor Crowbar** for protection during faults.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{inj} = V_{load,ref} - V_{source}$ | Required injection voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{source} + V_{inj} = V_{load}$ | KVL of series-compensated feeder | V |
| 3 | $V_{sag,\%} = \dfrac{V_{nom} - V_{dip}}{V_{nom}} \times 100$ | Percentage voltage sag | % |
| 4 | $V_{inj,pu} = 1 - V_{sag,pu}$ | Per-unit injection during sag | pu |
| 5 | $E_{DC,min} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{DC}^2$ | DC link energy storage | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $I_{inj,rated} = \dfrac{S_{load}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{inj,rated}}$ | Rated injection transformer current | A |
| 7 | $S_{DVR} = V_{inj} \cdot I_{load}$ | Apparent power rating of DVR | VA |
| 8 | $E_{DC} = \int_{0}^{T_{sag}} V_{inj}(t) \cdot i_{load}(t) \, dt$ | Energy drawn from DC storage during sag | J |
| 9 | $V_{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion of voltage | % |
| 10 | $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{control}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | PWM modulation index | dimensionless |
| 11 | $V_{LL,inj,max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,inj,max}$ | Line-to-line injection voltage limit | V |
| 12 | $T_{sag,max} = \dfrac{E_{DC}}{V_{inj,rms} \cdot I_{load,rms}}$ | Maximum sag compensation duration | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Injection Transformer:**
> The series injection transformer performs **dual function** — (a) voltage level matching (step-up or step-down), and (b) **galvanic isolation** between the VSI and the power line. The rating of this transformer directly dictates the maximum sag depth the DVR can compensate.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Series compensators are deployed in industries where the **load is highly sensitive to voltage variations**:
- Semiconductor fabrication plants (cleanroom tools).
- Hospitals (MRI, CT scanners, life-support systems).
- Data centers (server farms, UPS-fed critical IT).
- Steel rolling mills (PLC and drive protection).
- Process control industries (DCS / SCADA systems).
- Renewable integration (wind farm grid-side voltage support).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Injection Voltage for Voltage Sag Compensation

**Problem Statement:**
A three-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz industrial feeder experiences a balanced three-phase voltage sag of **20%** (i.e., the source voltage drops to **0.8 pu**). The load is rated at **415 V, 50 kVA, 0.85 pf lagging**. The DVR uses a series injection transformer with turns ratio such that the VSI DC link voltage and modulation index allow maximum injection of **0.25 pu**.

**Find:** Whether the DVR can fully compensate the sag, and compute the magnitude and phase of the required injection voltage $V_{inj}$.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{LL,nom} = 415 \, V$
- $V_{sag} = 20\% = 0.20 \, pu$
- $V_{s,pu} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 \, pu$
- $S_{load} = 50 \, kVA$
- $pf = 0.85 \, lagging$
- $V_{inj,max} = 0.25 \, pu$

**Step 1 — Compute the per-unit source voltage during the sag:**

The sag causes the source voltage magnitude to drop by 20%, so the source per-unit voltage is:

$$V_{s,pu} = 1.0 - 0.20 = 0.80 \, pu$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required per-unit injection voltage:**

For the load to receive the rated voltage of **1.0 pu**, applying KVL to the compensated feeder:

$$V_{s,pu} + V_{inj,pu} = V_{load,pu} = 1.0 \, pu$$

Solving for $V_{inj,pu}$:

$$V_{inj,pu} = 1.0 - 0.80 = 0.20 \, pu$$

**Step 3 — Check feasibility against the DVR rating:**

Compare the required injection with the maximum DVR injection capability:

$$V_{inj,required} = 0.20 \, pu \;\leq\; V_{inj,max} = 0.25 \, pu$$

Since $0.20 < 0.25$, the DVR has **sufficient capacity** to fully compensate this sag.

**Step 4 — Compute the actual line-to-line injection voltage:**

Convert per-unit to actual voltage:

$$V_{inj,LL} = 0.20 \times 415 \, V = 83.0 \, V \, (line\text{-}to\text{-}line)$$

The per-phase (line-to-neutral) injection voltage is:

$$V_{inj,ph} = \frac{V_{inj,LL}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{83.0}{\sqrt{3}} = 47.92 \, V$$

**Step 5 — Determine the phase angle of the injection voltage:**

For a **balanced sag with no phase jump** (most common in three-phase faults), the source voltage retains its original phase angle, and the injection must be **in phase with the pre-sag source voltage** (i.e., same phase as $\underline{V}_s$).

The phasor representation becomes:

$$\underline{V}_{load} \;=\; \underline{V}_s \;+\; \underline{V}_{inj} \quad \text{(all in phase)}$$

$$\underline{V}_{inj} = 0.20 \angle 0^\circ \, pu \quad \text{relative to } \underline{V}_s$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{V_{inj} = 83.0 \, V \, (L\text{-}L) = 47.92 \, V \, (L\text{-}N), \text{ in phase with } V_s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Required from DC Storage for a Given Sag Duration

**Problem Statement:**
The DVR from the previous example is required to compensate a **balanced three-phase sag of 20% lasting for 0.6 seconds** (i.e., 30 cycles at 50 Hz). The load draws **50 kVA at 0.85 pf lagging** and remains constant during the sag.

**Find:** The minimum DC link energy that the storage device (capacitor) must supply.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{inj,LL} = 83.0 \, V$
- $I_{load,LL} = \dfrac{S_{load}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415} = 69.56 \, A$
- $T_{sag} = 0.6 \, s$
- $pf = 0.85$, so $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\sin\phi = 0.527$

**Step 1 — Express the injection voltage as instantaneous phasor:**

The DVR injects a voltage that is **in phase** with the source. The active power delivered by the DVR during the sag is:

$$P_{DVR} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{inj,LL} \cdot I_{load,LL} \cdot \cos\phi_{inj}$$

Assuming $\cos\phi_{inj} = 1$ (since $V_{inj}$ is aligned with $I_{load}$ for in-phase injection), the DVR supplies only **active power** if injection is in phase with current. (Note: for a **purely in-phase injection**, the DVR exchanges only real power with the system.)

**Step 2 — Compute the active power rating of the DVR:**

$$P_{DVR} = \sqrt{3} \times 83.0 \times 69.56 \times 1.0$$

$$P_{DVR} = 9999.6 \, W \approx 10.0 \, kW$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total energy required from the DC link:**

For a sag of duration $T_{sag}$, the energy delivered is:

$$E_{DC} = P_{DVR} \cdot T_{sag} = 10{,}000 \times 0.6 = 6000 \, J = 6.0 \, kJ$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required DC link capacitance:**

Using the energy storage formula for a capacitor:

$$E_{DC} = \frac{1}{2} C_{DC} \left( V_{DC,max}^2 - V_{DC,min}^2 \right)$$

Assume $V_{DC,max} = 700 \, V$ and allow voltage dip of $\Delta V = 100 \, V$ (i.e., $V_{DC,min} = 600 \, V$):

$$6000 = \frac{1}{2} C_{DC} \left( 700^2 - 600^2 \right)$$

$$6000 = \frac{1}{2} C_{DC} \times (490000 - 360000)$$

$$6000 = \frac{1}{2} C_{DC} \times 130000$$

$$6000 = 65000 \cdot C_{DC}$$

$$C_{DC} = \frac{6000}{65000} = 0.0923 \, F = 92{,}300 \, \mu F$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{C_{DC} \approx 92{,}300 \, \mu F \;\; \text{at} \;\; 700 \, V \, DC}$$

This is the energy storage capacitor size required to compensate a **20% sag for 0.6 seconds** while feeding a **50 kVA load**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU Exam:** Notice that the energy storage requirement is the **limiting factor** for DVRs in practical deployment. For long-duration deep sags, capacitor banks become impractically large, motivating the use of **BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems)**, **super-capacitors**, or **grid-side active rectifiers** that draw power from the AC source to maintain the DC link during prolonged disturbances.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compensation of Harmonic Voltage Using Series Filter

**Problem Statement:**
The supply voltage feeding a critical load contains a **5th harmonic component of magnitude 8% of the fundamental**. Compute the magnitude and phase of the harmonic voltage the series compensator must inject to ensure a **clean sinusoidal voltage at the load terminals**.

**Given Data:**
- $V_1 = V_{nom}$ (fundamental)
- $V_5 = 0.08 \, V_1$ (5th harmonic)
- Phase of 5th harmonic: $\angle V_5 = 30^\circ$ (assumed)

**Step 1 — Source voltage expression in time domain:**

$$v_s(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + 0.08 V_1 \sin(5\omega t + 30^\circ)$$

**Step 2 — Required load voltage (clean fundamental only):**

$$v_{load}(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Required injection voltage:**

By KVL:

$$v_{inj}(t) = v_{load}(t) - v_s(t)$$

$$v_{inj}(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) - \left[ V_1 \sin(\omega t) + 0.08 V_1 \sin(5\omega t + 30^\circ) \right]$$

$$v_{inj}(t) = -0.08 V_1 \sin(5\omega t + 30^\circ)$$

This can be written equivalently as:

$$v_{inj}(t) = 0.08 V_1 \sin(5\omega t + 30^\circ + 180^\circ)$$

$$v_{inj}(t) = 0.08 V_1 \sin(5\omega t + 210^\circ)$$

**Step 4 — Final harmonic injection requirement:**

$$\boxed{\underline{V}_{inj,5} = 0.08 \angle 210^\circ \; pu \quad \text{(at 250 Hz, i.e., 5th harmonic)}}$$

The series filter injects a **5th harmonic voltage of magnitude 8% and phase $210^\circ$** to cancel the harmonic present in the source.

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Compensation Strategy

| Load Sensitivity | Sag Depth | Sag Duration | Recommended Series Topology |
|------------------|-----------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Low (motors, lighting) | < 10% | < 5 cycles | Passive LC trap + surge arrester |
| Medium (drives, PLCs) | 10% – 30% | 5–30 cycles | DVR with capacitor DC link |
| High (semiconductor fab) | 30% – 50% | 30–180 cycles | DVR with BESS / super-capacitor |
| Critical (data center) | > 50% | > 180 cycles | DVR + UPS / Flywheel storage |
| Ultra-sensitive (medical) | Any | Any | DVR + UPQC (combined series-shunt) |
